1	---
2	title: "Antitrust as industrial policy: Government-Sponsored Mergers as Passive Industrial Policy in Brazil, 1995-2002"
3	author:
4	  - name: AndrГ© Vereta-Nahoum
5	  - name: Tales ManГ§ano
6	abstract: |
7	  This article analyzes how antitrust policy can function as passive industrial policy, promoting competitiveness and internationalization in economies under fiscal constraint. Drawing on government-supported mergers in Brazil during the 1990s, the study examines how flexibility in the enforcement of competition law was justified by the need to strengthen national sectors in the face of economic liberalization. Antitrust and industrial policy are traditionally regarded as antagonistic. At the turn of the century, however, Brazil's antitrust authority allowed the consolidation of major corporate groups, such as Gerdau and Ambev, on the grounds of efficiency gains and global competitiveness вҖ” even as the Cardoso government prioritized monetary stability and denied adopting sectoral industrial policies. The Brazilian case illustrates how fiscally conservative governments can strategically use antitrust policy to drive economic development and the internationalization of firms, aligning indirectly and passively with objectives traditionally associated with classic, strengthening-oriented industrial policy.
8	keywords:
9	  - antitrust policy
10	  - industrial policy
11	  - institutional conversion
12	  - Brazil
13	  - mergers and acquisitions
14	format:
15	  pdf: default
16	  html: default
17	  docx: default
18	---
19	
20	## 1. Introduction
21	
22	Can governments pursue industrial policy while explicitly denying doing so? This article examines how Brazil's antitrust enforcement in the 1990s operated as a form of industrial policy under conditions of fiscal constraint and liberalizing reform, despite the official rejection of such policy by leading policymakers. During the administrations of Fernando Henrique Cardoso, economic policy was publicly organized around macroeconomic stabilization, market liberalization, and a commitment to limiting state intervention in markets. As the then Finance Minister Pedro Malan put it, "the best industrial policy is to have no industrial policy" [@2004-07-03_news_fsp_autores-apontam-caminhos-para-a-politica-industrial]. Yet, at the same time, major mergers involving leading Brazilian firms were approved with explicit support from the executive branch and justified in terms of the need to compete internationally. This apparent contradiction constitutes the central puzzle of the paper. In the absence and even explicital denial of specific industrial policy mechanisms, antitrust can serve as an indirect regulatory means with similar effects of benefitting specific firms and industries with firm-level competitiveness or sectoral concentration. In this sense, industrial policy may operate not through visible interventions such as subsidies or protection, but through the reinterpretation and selective enforcement of existing legal frameworks. This case also emphasizes the importance of analytically disentangling state discourses, policy instruments, and policy effects.
23	
24	Passive industrial policy, as defined in this article, refers to the promotion of sectoral or firm-level competitiveness through regulatory practices that do not involve fiscal transfers or direct state coordination, but instead operate by enabling market restructuring. When such policies are pursued under explicit discursive denial, they take the form of covert industrial policy. This passivity should not be mistaken for state absence. Rather, it reflects a form of indirect and strategically mediated intervention, in which the state shapes market outcomes by exercising discretion over enforcement, interpretation, and institutional design. In the Brazilian case, this occurred through the lenient application of antitrust law, which allowed firms to consolidate their domestic market positions and subsequently expand internationally.
25	
26	The mechanism through which this process unfolds can be understood as a form of institutional conversion, in the sense developed by @Mahoney-Thelen2010. Existing rules are not formally changed but are reinterpreted in ways that produce outcomes that diverge from their original intent. In the context of antitrust policy, two legal concepts are particularly central to this process: efficiency and the definition of the relevant market. While efficiency was formally tied to consumer welfare in the new competition regime, it could be expanded in practice to include considerations of firm competitiveness and technological upgrading. More importantly, the concept of the relevant market proved highly malleable. By defining markets as global rather than national, actors were able to dilute measures of concentration and justify mergers as necessary responses to international competition. This ambiguity created room for interpretation that enabled the conversion of antitrust policy into an instrument of industrial policy.
27	
28	The empirical analysis is based on a comparative study of two emblematic merger cases in Brazil during the 1990s: the acquisition of Pains by the Gerdau Group in the steel sector and the merger of Antarctica and Brahma into Ambev in the beverage sector. These cases are particularly revealing because they were highly contested, involved extensive documentation, and generated significant political debate. The study employs process tracing based on administrative rulings by CADE, technical reports produced by government agencies, corporate statements, and media coverage. In order to manage and analyze a large volume of textual material, the research also relied on computational assistance using large language models, namely NotebookLM. These tools were used to reconstruct procedural timelines, identify recurring argumentative patterns, and compare the positions of different actors across documents. Their use was strictly supportive, facilitating the organization and interrogation of the material, while all analytical interpretations were carried out by the authors.
29	
30	The Brazilian case is especially illuminating because it unfolds in a context where industrial policy was not only absent but explicitly disavowed. Following the enactment of the 1994 competition law, the Administrative Council for Economic Defense (CADE) was strengthened as an autonomous authority tasked with promoting competition and supporting macroeconomic stabilization. The broader policy framework emphasized the reduction of state intervention and the disciplining of markets, in part as a strategy to control inflation. At the same time, trade liberalization exposed domestic firms to increased foreign competition, raising concerns about their capacity to survive and expand in a more open economy. It is within this context that antitrust policy became a site of strategic reinterpretation.
31	
32	Taken together, the two cases reveal the process of institutional conversion that allowed the manipulation of antitrust regulatory principles to serve as a passive industrial policy. In the Gerdau case, the reinterpretation of antitrust principles required direct political intervention and generated visible conflict within the regulatory framework. By the time of the Ambev merger, similar interpretive moves were more readily accommodated within the logic of the competition regime itself. The ambiguity surrounding the definition of the relevant market played a central role in this transformation, providing the legal and analytical flexibility necessary to reconcile high levels of domestic concentration with the formal requirements of competition law. Through this process, antitrust policy came to function as a mechanism for enabling firm consolidation and promoting international competitiveness, even as industrial policy continued to be publicly disavowed.
33	
34	These findings have broader implications for debates on industrial policy and competition in international political economy. They suggest that industrial policy need not take the form of explicit intervention or fiscal expenditure, but can be embedded in the operation of regulatory regimes typically associated with market discipline. They also challenge the view of antitrust as a neutral and purely technocratic instrument, highlighting its susceptibility to political reinterpretation and strategic use. Finally, the Brazilian case resonates with contemporary debates in the United States and the European Union, where policymakers have increasingly sought to reconcile antitrust enforcement with the need to foster globally competitive firms in sectors such as digital platforms, artificial intelligence, and green technologies. These debates are often framed as a choice between maintaining strict competition or relaxing enforcement to enable scale [@Khan2016; @Khan-Vaheesan2017; @JuhaszEtAl2023].
35	
36	The Brazilian case suggests that this dichotomy may be misleading. Rather than formally suspending antitrust principles, states may pursue industrial objectives through the internal reinterpretation of competition law, allowing concentration while preserving the formal language of market efficiency and consumer welfare. In this sense, contemporary efforts to align antitrust with industrial strategy may already involve forms of covert and passive industrial policy, even where such policies are not explicitly acknowledged.
37	
38	The article proceeds as follows. The next section situates the argument within existing debates on antitrust and industrial policy. The subsequent sections analyze the Gerdau and Ambev cases in detail, followed by a comparative discussion of the mechanisms at work and their broader implications.
39	
40	## 2. Antitrust and Industrial Policy: Reframing the Relationship
41	
42	The relationship between antitrust and industrial policy has long been framed as one of tension, if not outright incompatibility. Early antitrust traditions in the United States, associated with Louis Brandeis and progressive-era reformers, viewed concentration as both an economic and political threat, opposing large-scale corporate organization on the grounds that it undermined democracy and fair competition [@Berk2009; @Hovenkamp2005]. Within this perspective, industrial policy---understood as state support for specific sectors or firms---appeared as a dangerous extension of concentrated power, further entrenching dominant actors at the expense of smaller competitors.
43	
44	This skepticism persisted, albeit in different form, within the Chicago School and transaction-cost approaches that came to dominate antitrust thinking in the late twentieth century. While these approaches rejected Brandeisian hostility to large firms, they nonetheless opposed industrial policy interventions on the grounds that they distorted market signals and reduced efficiency [@Posner1979; @Bork1978; @Williamson1975]. Antitrust, in this view, should focus narrowly on consumer welfare, allowing concentration where it produces efficiency gains and intervening only where clear harm can be demonstrated.
45	
46	In recent years, the resurgence of industrial policy in advanced economies has reopened these debates. Scholars and policymakers have increasingly questioned whether strict adherence to competition principles is compatible with the need to foster innovation, build strategic industries, and compete in global markets [@Khan2016; @Khan-Vaheesan2017; @Rodrik2021; @JuhaszEtAl2023]. Yet even in this renewed context, the relationship between antitrust and industrial policy is often framed as a trade-off, with governments facing a choice between maintaining competitive markets and enabling the emergence of large, globally competitive firms.
47	
48	This binary framing obscures the possibility that antitrust itself may serve as a vehicle for industrial policy. Historical institutionalist accounts have pointed in this direction, showing that regulatory regimes can shape market structures in ways that resemble industrial policy outcomes, even in the absence of explicit state planning [@Dobbin1994; @Prasad2012]. However, these accounts have focused primarily on earlier periods and have not systematically examined how such processes unfold in contemporary liberalizing contexts.
49	
50	The Brazilian case suggests that the opposition between antitrust and industrial policy is not only overstated but analytically misleading. Rather than choosing between competition and intervention, governments may pursue industrial objectives through the internal reinterpretation of competition law. This requires shifting attention away from formal policy categories and toward the ways in which legal concepts are mobilized and redefined in practice. In particular, it calls for examining how ambiguities embedded in antitrust frameworks---such as those surrounding efficiency and market definition---create opportunities for strategic reinterpretation.
51	
52	By foregrounding these dynamics, the paper shows that antitrust policy, far from being a neutral or purely technical domain, is deeply embedded in broader struggles over economic strategy and state intervention. In doing so, it opens up a new perspective on the relationship between liberalization and industrial policy, suggesting that the former may not displace the latter but rather transform its modalities.
53	
54	## 3. Context: Liberalization, Institutional Reform, and the Emergence of a New Antitrust Regime
55	
56	The transformation of Brazil's political economy in the 1990s provides a critical backdrop for understanding the cases analyzed in this paper. Following decades of state-led development characterized by import substitution, state-owned enterprises, and corporatist arrangements, the country embarked on a process of economic liberalization that reshaped the relationship between state and market. Trade barriers were reduced, privatization programs were implemented, and macroeconomic stabilization became the central objective of economic policy.
57	
58	Brazil's competition-defense framework did not emerge from this liberalization all at once. Regulation against the abuse of economic power has its origins in the Vargas era, with the decree-law on the "protection of the popular economy" and, later, the so-called *Lei Malaia*, subsequently incorporated into the constitution that succeeded the Estado Novo [@Onto2017]. CADE itself was created in 1962, under the government of JoГЈo Goulart, inspired by the U.S. antitrust authority (ibid.). Although the 1962 Competition Law established the combating of "abuse of economic power" as its aim, CADE operated with limited effectiveness between 1962 and 1994, owing to judicialization and, notably, an intentional hollowing-out of the agency during the military dictatorship, when it was instrumentalized mainly to oversee the administration and finances of state-owned enterprises [@Onto2017].
59	
60	After the promulgation of the 1988 Constitution, debate over the contours of state regulation of economic activity gained momentum in Brazil. The period was marked by trade opening and domestic market liberalization, alongside a transition from a developmentalist outlook to a liberal economic order [@Bresser-Pereira-Diniz2009]. The state was seen as abandoning "its role as a protective shell against external competition and as a lever for industrial development and domestic private enterprise" [@SallumJr1999, p. 28]. The privatization of state-owned enterprises, measures to increase the autonomy of the Central Bank, and the creation of sectoral regulatory agencies were all expressions of this shift.
61	
62	Within this broader transformation, the reform of the competition regime played a key role. The enactment of the 1994 antitrust law strengthened the institutional capacity and autonomy of CADE, positioning it as the central authority responsible for enforcing competition policy. The new framework aimed not only to promote efficiency and consumer welfare but also to support macroeconomic stabilization by preventing price distortions and anti-competitive practices that could undermine inflation control.
63	
64	The 1994 law, in particular, made CADE a federal autarchy and created the Brazilian System for the Defense of Competition (SBDC), understanding the defense of competition as a fundamental instrument for the "efficient and fair functioning of the market economy" [@Brasil1993]. Drafted by a commission of jurists and economists, the law required "notorious legal or economic expertise" [@Onto2017, p. 68] of CADE's members, which drove the entry of economists onto the council and the growing use of economic theories and concepts вҖ” such as "relevant market" and efficiency analysis вҖ” to inform the agency's decisions. As an autarchy, CADE was able to expand its technical staff and legal counsel, and its remit was considerably broadened: the law instituted mandatory review of corporate concentration acts (mergers, acquisitions, associations) meeting certain revenue thresholds, and gave CADE the resources to investigate and repress anticompetitive conduct, causing the number of cases before the agency to grow substantially from 1994 onward [@Onto2017]. The law also specified that there would be no appeal to the Executive branch, and that CADE's decisions were to be judicially enforced without delay. It defined as violations of the economic order any acts that had as their object, or could produce effects such as, limiting free competition or free enterprise, dominating a relevant market for goods or services, arbitrarily increasing profits, or abusively exercising a dominant position [@Brasil1994; @Carvalho-Ragazzo2012]. President Itamar Franco, for his part, was especially keen to mobilize the legislation against what he considered abusive pricing вҖ” particularly in the pharmaceutical sector вҖ” and against a broader culture of inflation, at a moment when competition was seen as helping to prevent abuses following economic opening [@Onto2017].
65	
66	At the same time, the ideological orientation of the government emphasized a retreat from direct state intervention in the economy. Policymakers sought to distance themselves from the developmentalist strategies of previous decades, framing liberalization as both a technical necessity and a normative commitment to market principles. This orientation was reflected in public statements that rejected industrial policy and in efforts to limit the role of the state in coordinating economic activity.
67	
68	Yet this apparent withdrawal of the state coincided with new challenges. The opening of the economy exposed domestic firms to increased competition from foreign producers, raising concerns about their ability to survive and expand in a more integrated global market. In this context, the consolidation of domestic firms emerged as a potential strategy for enhancing competitiveness, even as the formal policy framework discouraged direct intervention.
69	
70	As liberalization advanced, antitrust policy вҖ” strongly shaped by this liberal tradition вҖ” regained centrality. Under this new orientation, only industries capable of adapting to compete in an internationalized economy would be preserved, as advocated by the new, liberal and internationalizing political bloc led by Cardoso [@Onto2017].
71	
72	It is within this tension between ideological commitment and economic necessity that the reinterpretation of antitrust policy took place. The new competition regime, while designed to promote market discipline, also contained ambiguities that could be mobilized to accommodate consolidation. The following sections examine how these ambiguities were exploited in practice through the Gerdau and Ambev cases.
73	
74	## 4. The Gerdau--Pains Case: Conflict, Intervention, and the Reinterpretation of Antitrust
75	
76	Gerdau was founded in Porto Alegre in 1901 as a nail manufacturer and expanded significantly after the Second World War amid strong demand for its products and import restrictions. Instability in steel supply led the firm to integrate vertically, acquiring SiderГәrgica Riograndense in 1948 to secure its raw materials; it then expanded its operations in Rio Grande do Sul and the Southeast with the support of the military regime, which facilitated the acquisition of a mill in Pernambuco in 1969. During the so-called Brazilian Economic Miracle, Gerdau continued to expand: in 1973, in a joint venture with the German group Thyssen ATH and bank financing, it acquired the state-owned Cosigua mill in Rio de Janeiro, making Sepetiba Bay a hub of its operations. The company also purchased SiderГәrgica GuaГӯra (1972) and Cia. SiderГәrgica de Alagoas (1974), built SiderГәrgica Cearense (1981) with support from the CearГЎ state government, and acquired Usina SiderГәrgica Paraense (1986); its first operation in Minas Gerais, Usina BarГЈo de Cocais, was purchased in 1988 following its privatization. In short, Gerdau had received substantial federal support for its ventures since the dictatorship, and grew considerably through the privatization of state-owned steel mills and the acquisition and construction of mills across the country, much of it financed with public loans and tax incentives. In the steel sector, the 1990s economic opening saw state-owned and even foreign mills being sold off as the state's role in certain economic sectors was redefined [@Nassif1995; @Teixeira-etal2001, p. 186], with the developmentalist tradition giving way to a liberal vision of the state's role [@Schneider2015; @Onto2017]. Non-tariff barriers to imports were suspended, and import tariffs were gradually reduced.
77	
78	The Gerdau--Pains case provides an early and revealing instance of the tensions inherent in the new competition regime. In the mid-1990s, Gerdau's acquisition of assets associated with the German group Korf, including the Pains steel mill, raised concerns within CADE about excessive concentration in the domestic market for long steel products, particularly rebar.[^gerdau-marketshare] Central to the dispute were the definition of the relevant market and the assessment of potential efficiency gains, which could justify, in the terms of the antitrust law of the period, increased market concentration. The case unfolded over a protracted period marked by legal disputes, political intervention, and institutional transformation.
79	
80	CADE vetoed the acquisition, finding that it would excessively concentrate control over the production of certain steel products [@1995-03-23_news_fsp_cade-adia-decisao-sobre-fusao]. The Pains acquisition, together with Colgate-Palmolive's acquisition of Kolynos, was among the first post-1994 CADE cases to generate widespread controversy: "the purchase of SiderГәrgica Pains by the Gerdau Group generated significant political conflict with repercussions in the media and in the political sphere" [@Onto2017, p. 59]. Rather than pursue the matter through the courts, Gerdau pressured FHC's Justice Minister, Nelson Jobim вҖ” who immediately took the firm's side against the regulator вҖ” despite the recently enacted Law 8,884/94, under which no Executive interference was foreseen [@Ribeiro1995]. Jobim stated that "the Brazilian steel market is open to international products, and the concentration act does not eliminate competition in a substantial part of the market" [@Ribeiro1995]. In the minister's view, since steel products were a commodity, it made no sense to treat the roughly 40-percent market concentration at stake as grounds for a veto, since the product could easily be imported. Gerdau's public response was forceful: according to the firm, CADE's opposition to the Pains purchase "does not follow the government's political strategy, nor does it track the evolution of the international scenario" [@1995-10-14_news_fsp_contra-a-mare], suggesting that the firm counted on active government support for the acquisition. Gerdau widened its attacks on CADE, arguing that the decision was anticompetitive and undermined the country's economic opening, and that the agency's definition of the relevant market was inappropriate вҖ” a rhetorical move resembling that of Chicago School economists against the earlier antitrust tradition. As chairman Jorge Gerdau Johannpeter put it, CADE was "yet another chapter in the delay of Brazil's economic opening," arguing that antitrust law was anachronistic because it was tied to a closed-market vision, and that the country should either adopt structural solutions for competitiveness or watch businesses turn, out of desperation, to protectionist ones вҖ” "which would be wrong." He argued that mergers and acquisitions were the path to competitiveness and efficiency in an era of economic globalization, and that CADE interfered with this process through legislation far more restrictive than that of First World and Asian countries. Gerdau also argued, in an official note, that the steel sector was inherently oligopolistic вҖ” profitable only at large production scales вҖ” pointing to a single steel group operating in France and only two in England, and to a merger trend already under way among U.S. steelmakers; the note added that the sector was open to international competition, since import tariffs stood at around 10 percent [@Seidl1996].
81	
82	In its initial assessment, CADE adopted a relatively strict interpretation of competition principles. The authority emphasized that the merger would substantially increase concentration in the domestic market and questioned the validity of the efficiency claims advanced by the firms. CADE's technical analysis suggested that, despite trade liberalization, domestic prices for steel products remained largely decoupled from international benchmarks, indicating that the market retained a predominantly national character. This finding implied that the merger would create significant market power within Brazil, raising concerns about potential anti-competitive effects. Once an acting counselor took over CADE's presidency, he ordered an intervention in the mill to undo the deal, threatening to remove Jorge Gerdau after the businessman stated publicly that he intended to defy the agency; the counselor, Rodrigues-Chaves, grounded his position in an opinion by Elizabeth Farina, arguing that international steel prices were decoupled from domestic ones вҖ” rebutting Gerdau's claim that the local market would import less because the firm was already exposed to international competition вҖ” and that the cost savings from reduced competition would not be passed on to consumers but would instead increase Gerdau's profits (Votos AC 16-1994, p. 947вҖ“949).
83	
84	The authority initially issued a partial approval, requiring the deconstitution of part of the operation. Gerdau and its allies contested this interpretation, arguing that steel should be understood as a global commodity subject to international competition. By framing the relevant market in global terms, they sought to demonstrate that domestic concentration was less problematic and that the merger was necessary to achieve the scale required to compete internationally [@Seidl1996].
85	
86	The firms challenged the decision, refused to comply, and appealed to the Minister of Justice, arguing that the legal framework granting CADE final authority was unconstitutional. The Minister accepted the appeal and suspended enforcement, effectively intervening in the process. The argument raised by Gerdau's legal team was supported by broader claims about the need for consolidation in the context of globalization, with political actors assuming steel is a global commodity and emphasizing that mergers and acquisitions among Brazilian firms in the industry were essential for competitiveness in an open economy. As one CADE counselor of the period recalled: "The company requested reconsideration of the decision, but the veto was upheld. Then something unprecedented in CADE's history happened: Gerdau appealed directly to the Minister of Justice, Nelson Jobim, asking him to overturn the decision... We were stunned, because we never imagined CADE could be undermined from within the Ministry of Justice itself," recalled counselor Neide Mallard. "We, the counselors, met and concluded that we would not send the case to the minister, but rather to the Public Prosecutor's Office, so that it would enforce the decision." According to CADE's president at the time, Ruy Coutinho, the episode caused "tremendous political unease": "Jorge Gerdau went to President Fernando Henrique Cardoso, who contacted me to find out what had happened" [@Basile2009].
87	
88	The case was then reconsidered by a reconfigured CADE, which ultimately approved the acquisition with conditions, including divestments and commitments regarding supply and designed to mitigate anti-competitive effects. However, the outcome of the case extended beyond the specific operation. It had significant institutional consequences and contributed to the redefinition of antitrust policy in Brazil. In response to the tensions generated by the decision, President Cardoso altered the composition of CADE, replacing members with legal backgrounds [from the AGU]{.mark} with economists trained in transaction-cost approaches to antitrust [@Onto2017]. President Cardoso reportedly warned the newly appointed counselors: "Don't cause me any problems!" [@Onto2017, p. 70]. According to LГәcia Helena Salgado, one of the economists who joined CADE shortly after the Gerdau-Pains case, this period marked the beginning of a search for "rationality," or the "rationalization," of the agency's activity вҖ” a search that, for the Presidency, was meant to avoid the political problems CADE could generate. As sociologist Carlos Bello recounts it, Finance Minister Pedro Malan and Justice Minister Nelson Jobim publicly stated that the new counselors should be technocrats; shortly before the appointments were announced, the two ministers met with Minister JosГ© Serra and President Cardoso and agreed to nominate specialized technical experts with legal or economic training. For Bello, the president was "very concerned that political balkanization could generate new conflicts with CADE ... the economists would serve to block CADE," rather than to make it more effective [@Onto2017, p. 70].
89	
90	This shift in expertise contributed to a reorientation of the authority toward a more permissive stance regarding mergers and acquisitions, emphasizing efficiency and conduct remedies over structural constraints. The case established a precedent for the acceptance of mergers justified in terms of international competitiveness. This transformation can be understood as part of a broader process of institutional conversion [@Mahoney-Thelen2010], in which the meaning and application of competition law were reshaped without formal legislative change.
91	
92	The reconsideration process stretched over several years. Gerdau was ultimately allowed to keep the Pains mill after complying with ten conditions set by the council to prevent market concentration in the long-steel sector, including reconstituting and selling Pains's rolling unit in Contagem (MG), selling its transport subsidiary (Transpains), and rehiring previously dismissed workers [@1998-03-19_news_fsp_gerdau-fica-com-a-pains-cade-aprova]. The group also redirected more than half of Pains's output toward special long steel вҖ” reducing its share of the common long-steel market while helping to deconcentrate the special long-steel segment, then dominated by three other firms вҖ” and committed to additional investments of US$78 million, along with further measures on production reorientation, distribution, technology, and periodic reporting to CADE. The settlement marked the saturation of the firm's domestic growth and the acceleration of its international expansion across the Americas вҖ” Uruguay, Chile, Canada, and Argentina вҖ” and, from the late 1990s onward, particularly the United States: in 1999 Gerdau acquired 75 percent of AmeriSteel Corporation, entering the U.S. market with a production capacity of two million tonnes of steel per year [@Teixeira-etal2001, p. 204].
93	
94	[^gerdau-marketshare]: The acquisition would have raised Gerdau's share of the long-steel market from 39.6 to 46.2 percent, and its overall share of the national steel market to roughly 43 percent [@1995-03-23_news_fsp_cade-adia-decisao-sobre-fusao].
95	
96	## 5. The Ambev Case: Consolidation, Legitimization, and the Stabilization of Conversion
97	
98	The Ambev case, decided at the end of the decade, reflects the consolidation of this new orientation. The merger between Antarctica and Brahma, announced in 1999, brought together the two largest beer producers in the country, creating a firm that controlled approximately 70 percent of the beer market and a substantial share of distribution networks.
99	
100	Throughout the 1990s economic opening, Brazil's brewers had been courted by foreign multinationals for acquisitions or distribution partnerships, seen by many as a prelude to the entry of the sector's leading global firms into the domestic market [@Abdallah2020]. In any eventual competition with these foreign entrants, domestic firms would be at a serious disadvantage: with access only to the costlier domestic credit market, they would have to pay higher interest rates, raising the cost of investment and limiting what they could offer to acquire other firms in Brazil or abroad, while foreign firms вҖ” able to borrow at significantly lower rates, as in the U.S. credit market вҖ” could offer more for the same targets. As Antarctica's chairman put it: "If an American company wanted to acquire a Brazilian brewery, it could pay twice what a domestic competitor offered, since over eight years the rate of return would be identical in both cases" [@Abdallah2020, p. 136]. Since the 1980s, both Brahma and Antarctica had been approached by multinationals such as Anheuser-Busch, Miller, Kirin, Coca-Cola, and Pepsi interested in partnering with them; distribution agreements and joint ventures were repeatedly attempted, but these either failed to materialize or were curtailed by CADE, which at times demanded additional investment commitments that discouraged the foreign firms [@Abdallah2020, p. 140]. Facing the prospect of eventual acquisition by one of these larger, better-capitalized foreign rivals, Brahma's controllers вҖ” Jorge Paulo Lemann and Marcel Telles вҖ” instead pursued a different ambition: to become the world's largest brewer, including through the acquisition of Anheuser-Busch itself [@Abdallah2020, p. 148]. The alternative that emerged from informal conversations between Brahma's and Antarctica's leadership was to merge the country's two largest brewers instead, in order to build a firm strong enough to expand abroad. As Antarctica's Victorio De Marchi recalled of the conversations preceding the merger: "should we leave the foreigners out, and think about building a company strong enough to secure ourselves domestically and allow us to grow abroad?" [@Abdallah2020].
101	
102	From the outset, the merger was framed by its proponents in terms that went beyond conventional efficiency arguments.
103	
104	The operation was announced by its proponents as essential for the creation of a "green-and-yellow multinational" capable of competing with global industry leaders and warned that failure to achieve sufficient scale would result in the denationalization of the sector. As Ambev co-president Marcel Telles put it: "If you have a huge German shepherd that barks at your visitors, you have to keep it under control, not kill it. Plausible restrictions need to be created for the merger of two large companies, but the chance for the country to have a global competitor вҖ” a green-and-yellow multinational that will already be born as the world's third-largest brewery вҖ” should not be eliminated" [@Seidl2000]. This framing resonated with political actors, who publicly endorsed the merger as part of a broader strategy to strengthen domestic firms in the face of international competition. The merger was in fact announced to the press from the PalГЎcio da Alvorada, with President Cardoso present, following a meeting between the businessmen and the president [@LoPrete1999]; the new firm's international ambitions were championed by Cardoso from the very start of the negotiations.
105	
106	The procedural trajectory of the case reveals both continuity and change relative to the Gerdau case. Following the announcement of the merger in July 1999, CADE issued a precautionary measure suspending its implementation pending review. Technical bodies within the government, including the Secretary for Economic Monitoring, challenged the efficiency claims advanced by the firms, arguing that projected cost reductions were overstated and unlikely to benefit consumers. At the same time, debates over the definition of the relevant market reemerged, with the firms advocating for a broad definition encompassing all beverages, while critics insisted on focusing on the beer market, where concentration was extreme and exports remained minimal.
107	
108	Opposition to the merger was led by Kaiser, one of Ambev's rival brewers,[^kaiser-coke] which ran a series of media campaigns arguing that concentrating roughly two-thirds of the country's beer production and sales in the new firm could lead to anticompetitive conduct, including price increases contrary to the national interest: "Kaiser, enemy number one of the union, put out yet another campaign yesterday вҖ” the third вҖ” to prevent the association, which is under review by CADE... the brewer ran ads on radio and in newspapers to try to convince public opinion that the merger harms consumers' interests, since it would bring about market concentration and, consequently, higher prices" [@FernandesPrates2000]. Critics also argued that the operation amounted not to a merger but to Brahma absorbing Antarctica outright, creating a firm capable of eliminating competition and threatening the survival of medium and small rivals, with no significant efficiencies or logistical or technological innovations to benefit consumers, suppliers, or distributors. Kaiser further criticized Ambev for operating as a unified company even before CADE's final ruling вҖ” a fait accompli meant to make the operation practically irreversible regardless of the agency's eventual decision (CADE AC 08012.005846.1999-12, Vol. 2, p. 49).
109	
110	Despite these concerns, the executive branch strongly supported the merger, with the Ministry of Development affirming its alignment with national interests. Minister of Industrial Development ClГіvis Carvalho, for instance, stated that he wanted to see a wave of mergers across several of the country's largest industries вҖ” especially paper, steel, and petrochemicals вҖ” so that they could prosper in international markets, offering up to three billion reais (roughly US$1.7 billion) from the National Bank for Economic and Social Development (BNDES) to finance such deals [@TheEconomist1999]. Public statements by President Cardoso suggested a pragmatic acceptance of market concentration, encapsulated in the observation that "the world operates in oligopolies." In an interview with the Jornal do Brasil, Cardoso addressed the merger directly, arguing that Brazilian firms needed to restructure to compete in a more open global economy; asked about the possibility of a "monopoly" forming, he replied: "But the world today operates in terms of oligopoly. ... What is Coca-Cola? How will they compete, later on, with Coca-Cola, if they are not strong here? Isn't that so? That is the concept. You can no longer think in terms of the domestic market. The market is internationalized. So you have to look at whether there are competitors in the sector or not [...] I don't even want to anticipate whether this will be legal or not [referring to CADE]. Let's discuss it. I am telling you what the global trend is: mega-mergers. That is, we will need Brazilian multinationals" [@Abdallah2020, p. 170]. Cardoso would later write in his diaries that "companies are merging like crazy all over the world, and this could hurt Brazil, which, given our legislation, has no way of having competitive jobs" [@Abdallah2020, p. 170]. This position signaled a shift away from strict adherence to competition principles and toward a more flexible approach that accommodated the realities of global markets.
111	
112	Press, congressional, and government pressure on CADE was intense throughout the proceeding, as former counselors later recounted in detail. Ruy Lima, the only counselor to vote against the merger, recalled that CADE was pressured by the government to expedite the case toward a favorable outcome: "The Ambev case was one of CADE's biggest cases, mostly because of the media attention generated by the applicants themselves... There was a political dimension to it; the government applied a great deal of pressure вҖ” calls from ministers, from the leader of the government's party in Congress вҖ” all of it landing on CADE's president, the most visible figure" [@Dutra2009, pp. 101-103].
113	
114	In March 2000, CADE approved the merger subject to a set of conditions formalized in a Performance Commitment Term. These conditions included divestments, measures to ensure access to distribution networks, and commitments to maintain employment levels. While these remedies were intended to address competition concerns, they also reflected a broader acceptance of the merger's underlying rationale.
115	
116	One option debated within CADE was to require the divestment of one of the resulting firm's major beer brands. Since Brahma also owned Skol вҖ” whose market share was comparable to the Brahma brand itself вҖ” selling it off was seen by some as a feasible restriction that would allow the merger to proceed without creating a monopoly. Counselor LГәcia Helena Salgado, who did not vote in the case because her husband represented Kaiser as counsel, considered the eventual decision a mistake: "I would have approved the deal, but it was obvious вҖ” known to everyone familiar with competition law, since one only had to read the prevailing case law and doctrine вҖ” that an unavoidable condition for approving that case, among others, was the sale of a beer brand to a competitor, along with the plants capable of producing it competitively and the means to distribute it. And that brand, as everyone who knows competition law knew and still knows, was Skol. ... As is well known, that is not what happened. CADE's approval imposed as a condition the sale of a handful of plants the companies were going to close anyway, since they were entirely obsolete, and the sale of a brand that, poor thing, besides being inexpressive, had been вҖ” over the course of the review вҖ” intentionally run down, since its market share had fallen sharply during the period. It was a truly regrettable decision" [@Dutra2009, pp. 61-62]. Ruy Lima likewise recalled that this possibility was discussed, though it was ultimately reduced to the sale of the BavГЎria brand вҖ” a brand still under construction, with roughly nine percent of the market at the start of the case, and considerably less by the time it was decided [@Dutra2009, pp. 101-103].
117	
118	The Ambev case thus illustrates the stabilization of the interpretive shifts that had emerged in the Gerdau case. The ambiguity surrounding market definition and efficiency was no longer a source of acute conflict but had become part of the routine operation of the competition regime. Antitrust policy, in this context, functioned as a mechanism for enabling consolidation while maintaining the formal language of competition and consumer welfare.
119	
120	For Ruy Lima, this outcome had lasting consequences for the antitrust authority's reputation, signaling that CADE would no longer stand as an obstacle to market concentration after the Ambev case: "I think that, by approving the Ambev case, CADE lost the great opportunity to show what competition defense in Brazil is, and what it should be. ... It seems to me that NestlГ© only felt emboldened to buy Garoto because of that signal CADE sent in the Ambev case... The idea took hold that CADE could always find a way to approve a large-scale concentration act, especially one accompanied by heavy media coverage and political pressure. CADE would always find a way to solve the problem without causing major disruption" [@Dutra2009, pp. 103-104]. Once authorized, the merged firm accelerated its international expansion across the Southern Cone. Although approved with conditions, institutional changes at CADE shortly after the ruling made it unlikely that a firm could be approved again with such a high a market share: "Ambev was born with 70 percent of domestic beer production in its hands. CADE's green light was only possible at that particular moment. CADE made a series of changes to its rules in the 2000s that would not allow the formation of a firm with that market share today" [@Boucas2018]. In 2004, Ambev merged with the Belgian brewer Interbrew, becoming a global company with a strong presence across several continents вҖ” a rapid foreignization of a firm whose creation, only four years earlier, had been justified by the need to preserve a Brazilian champion. In the end, the merged firm's headquarters moved to Belgium, and Brazilians held the same number of board seats as their Belgian counterparts, but from the year following the merger onward the company's CEO вҖ” with only brief interregnums вҖ” was consistently Brazilian, as was much of its senior management.
121	
122	[^kaiser-coke]: Kaiser had an equity stake held by Coca-Cola, which Ambev used to argue that the firm was only representing the interests of the foreign multinational that would gain a strong competitor with Ambev's formation [@Abdallah2020].
123	
124	## 6. Discussion: Institutional Conversion and the Reconfiguration of State Intervention
125	
126	The comparative analysis of the Gerdau and Ambev cases reveals a process of institutional conversion in which the meaning and application of antitrust policy were transformed without formal legislative change. Central to this process was the strategic use of ambiguity in key legal concepts, particularly the definition of the relevant market. By expanding the scope of the market to include international competition, actors were able to reconcile high levels of domestic concentration with the requirements of competition law.
127	
128	This reinterpretation was facilitated by political intervention and institutional change, including the reconfiguration of CADE's composition and the increasing prominence of economic expertise aligned with transaction-cost approaches. Over time, these changes contributed to the normalization of a more permissive stance toward mergers, in which concerns about market structure were balanced against considerations of efficiency and competitiveness.
129	
130	The result was a form of industrial policy that operated through passive regulatory means and was justified in covert terms. While the government continued to reject industrial policy in its discourse, it enabled the consolidation of domestic firms and their expansion into international markets. This suggests that liberalization does not necessarily entail a retreat of the state but may instead involve a reconfiguration of its modes of intervention.
131	
132	## 7. Conclusion
133	
134	This paper has argued that antitrust policy in Brazil during the 1990s functioned as a form of covert industrial policy implemented through passive regulatory means. By enabling firm consolidation through the reinterpretation of legal concepts and the selective enforcement of competition law, the state fostered the emergence of nationally rooted multinational firms without resorting to traditional industrial policy tools.
135	
136	The Brazilian case of the mid-1990s and early 2000s offers a counterpoint to this history. Unlike the United States in the early twentieth century, during its Progressive Era of economic expansion, Brazil in the 1990s faced severe fiscal constraints and economic opening, with foreign capital entering numerous industrial sectors. Under these conditions, government actors вҖ” including the Presidency вҖ” engaged in a loosening of antitrust rules to permit mergers and acquisitions that produced firms with substantial market power, on the grounds of supporting domestic firms and averting the denationalization of entire sectors of the economy.
137	
138	These findings have important implications for the study of industrial policy and competition in international political economy. They highlight the need to move beyond dichotomous frameworks that oppose state intervention and market discipline and to consider the ways in which regulatory regimes can be repurposed to achieve developmental objectives. They also suggest that contemporary debates about the relationship between antitrust and industrial policy, particularly in advanced economies, may benefit from greater attention to the covert and passive forms that such policies can take.
139	
140	By disaggregating discourse, instruments, and effects, the paper provides a framework for analyzing these dynamics and contributes to a more nuanced understanding of the role of the state in shaping market outcomes under conditions of liberalization.
141	
142	## Declaration of Generative AI Use {.unnumbered}
143	
144	The authors report that generative AI tools were used in two limited and clearly bounded ways. In the research process, the authors used NotebookLM to assist with the organization of a large volume of primary-source material (CADE administrative rulings, technical opinions, corporate statements, and media coverage) вҖ” specifically to reconstruct procedural timelines, identify recurring argumentative patterns, and compare the positions of different actors across documents. This use was strictly supportive; all analytical interpretation and conclusions are the authors' own. In the manuscript preparation process, the authors used Claude (Anthropic) for mechanical and organizational tasks under direct author supervision: converting the original manuscript from Word to Quarto Markdown format, merging author-drafted Portuguese-language text into the existing English manuscript on a section-by-section basis, and verifying bibliographic citation keys against the authors' Zotero reference library. No AI-generated analytical content was incorporated without direct authorial review.
145

36	
37	Estudos de organizaГ§ГЈo industrial e especialistas em antitruste

39	meio do antitruste e polГӯticas industriais (White, 2008; Sokol, 2015).

41	viam as polГӯticas industriais como prejudiciais Г  concorrГӘncia justa, ao
42	favorecer setores especГӯficos, particularmente grandes indГәstrias,
43	levando ao poder corporativo concentrado em detrimento das pequenas
44	empresas locais. Posteriormente, os estudos \"novos\" de antitruste,
45	baseados na economia dos custos de transaГ§ГЈo, opuseram-se Г s polГӯticas
46	industriais destinadas a proteger a concorrГӘncia local, justificando a
47	concentraГ§ГЈo de mercado com base em eficiГӘncias econГҙmicas e no
48	bem-estar do consumidor (Williamson, 1974). HГЎ ainda, esforГ§os para
49	conciliar a aparente contradiГ§ГЈo entre a desregulamentaГ§ГЈo da polГӯtica
50	antitruste que teria ocorrido com a ascensГЈo da perspectiva de
51	Williamson sobre a de Brandeis, a defesa da concorrГӘncia e as polГӯticas
52	industriais, limitando seu escopo de intervenГ§ГЈo e definindo limites

54	(Farina, 1994).
55	
56	AlГ©m do discurso acadГӘmico, as polГӯticas econГҙmicas monetaristas tambГ©m
57	apoiaram a aplicaГ§ГЈo rigorosa das leis antitruste para garantir a

59	lado polГӯticas industriais protetoras que poderiam distorcer os
60	mercados. Recentemente, os esforГ§os governamentais para reviver
61	polГӯticas industriais protetoras, como as do governo Biden nos EUA,
62	enfrentaram crГӯticas significativas de estudiosos de antitruste. Nesse
63	contexto, o uso pelo governo brasileiro de fusГөes de empresas nos anos
64	1990 para aumentar a competitividade internacional de suas indГәstrias
65	surge como um caso particularmente interessante.
66	
67	O caso torna-se ainda mais intrigante devido Г  negaГ§ГЈo explГӯcita do
68	governo de estar perseguindo uma polГӯtica industrial. Os governos
69	Cardoso (1995-2002) sГЈo frequentemente descritos como um perГӯodo
70	transformador na economia polГӯtica do Brasil, marcado pela adoГ§ГЈo de
71	polГӯticas focadas na estabilidade monetГЎria. Essa mudanГ§a envolveu
72	liberalizaГ§ГЈo econГҙmica, reduГ§ГЈo da interaГ§ГЈo governo-empresas e o
73	estabelecimento de um novo Sistema Nacional de Defesa da ConcorrГӘncia,
74	destinado a garantir eficiГӘncia e estabilizar preГ§os relativos
75	(Bresser-Pereira e Diniz, 2009; Schneider, 2015). A concorrГӘncia
76	internacional foi um pivГҙ da polГӯtica de estabilizaГ§ГЈo de preГ§os do
77	Plano Real. Simultaneamente, sinalizou o abandono explГӯcito de polГӯticas
78	industriais e medidas protetoras para as empresas domГ©sticas.
79	
80	Pedro Malan, Ministro da Fazenda de FHC entre 1995 e 2002, famosamente
81	proferiu que \"a melhor polГӯtica industrial Г© nГЈo ter polГӯtica
82	industrial\" (Folha, 2004). O SecretГЎrio de Acompanhamento EconГҙmico Г 
83	Г©poca reafirmou que a "concorrГӘncia Г© uma polГӯtica de desenvolvimento
84	econГҙmico" e que decisГөes em matГ©ria concorrencial nГЈo deveriam levar em
85	conta o fortalecimento de empresas nacionais ou o emprego (AC n.
86	08012.005846.1999.12, Vol. 11, p. 27).
87	
88	No entanto, surgiram controvГ©rsias acaloradas entre atores polГӯticos
89	sobre fusГөes e aquisiГ§Гөes por grandes grupos empresariais brasileiros,
90	que foram finalmente aprovadas pela autoridade de concorrГӘncia com apoio
91	explГӯcito do governo. Este artigo examina dois dos casos mais
92	proeminentes durante o governo FHC para explorar seu significado para a
93	polГӯtica econГҙmica do Brasil durante o perГӯodo de liberalizaГ§ГЈo: a fusГЈo
94	entre a Brahma e Antarctica que levou a criaГ§ГЈo da Ambev e as aquisiГ§Гөes
95	da Gerdau realizadas na dГ©cada de 1990. Os processos tornaram as
96	empresas resultantes oligopolistas no mercado brasileiro.
97	
98	Antes do experimento neodesenvolvimentista do Brasil ou da promoГ§ГЈo
99	ativa de campeГөes nacionais por meio de polГӯticas de investimento
100	centradas no Banco Nacional de Desenvolvimento EconГҙmico e Social (SicsГә
101	et al., 2007), uma coalizГЈo de lГӯderes empresariais e atores polГӯticos
102	aproveitou a ambiguidade da nova legislaГ§ГЈo antitruste
103	recГ©m-implementada. Uma resposta distinta de um governo fiscalmente
104	conservador Г s pressГөes da globalizaГ§ГЈo, essa polГӯtica, argumentamos,
105	funcionou como uma polГӯtica industrial passiva, permitindo que grandes
106	grupos empresariais brasileiros consolidassem suas posiГ§Гөes de mercado
107	por meio de fusГөes e aquisiГ§Гөes. Eles justificaram sua dominГўncia de
108	mercado ampliada---oligopolГӯstica---no Brasil como essencial para
109	garantir competitividade contra entrantes estrangeiros ou como uma
110	estratГ©gia para facilitar sua prГіpria internacionalizaГ§ГЈo.
111	
112	Ao fazer isso, ao fazer isso, as empresas enquadraram sua expansГЈo de
113	poder de mercado como um meio de gerar lucros e promover o
114	desenvolvimento nacional e o bem-estar---objetivos tradicionalmente
115	associados Г  polГӯtica industrial, contra uma legislaГ§ГЈo recГ©m-promulgada
116	que associava uma maior concorrГӘncia ao bem-estar e desenvolvimento. As
117	fusГөes permitiriam a criaГ§ГЈo de "multinacionais verde-amarelas" -
118	expressГЈo utilizada pela defesa da Ambev - que competiriam com suas
119	concorrentes internacionais, evitando a aquisiГ§ГЈo de todo o setor pelas
120	empresas estrangeiras caso elas continuassem fragmentadas.
121	
122	A seguir, resgatamos algumas das discussГөes da literatura sobre a
123	relaГ§ГЈo entre a polГӯtica antitruste e a polГӯtica industrial, sobretudo a
124	partir do caso estadunidense. O artigo segue analisando as longas
125	disputas sobre os sentidos do desenvolvimento nacional e sua relaГ§ГЈo com
126	a concorrГӘncia nos casos escolhidos. Ambos mobilizaram os trГӘs poderes e
127	opuseram em diferentes argumentos sustentados como estritamente
128	tГ©cnicos, visГөes sobre a eficiГӘncia de indГәstria e mercados. A
129	aprovaГ§ГЈo, em ambos os casos, foi parcial, e envolveu vaivГ©m de votos de
130	conselheiros, compromissos de performance em medidas compensatГіrias,
131	pedidos de reconsideraГ§ГЈo, questionamento por parte de membros do
132	Legislativo e acusaГ§Гөes de suborno. Demonstramos, em seguida, como essas
133	operaГ§Гөes de fusГЈo e aquisiГ§ГЈo serviram de plataforma para a expansГЈo
134	internacional das empresas. O artigo encerra com uma reflexГЈo sobre o
135	significado dos casos para a compreensГЈo da relaГ§ГЈo entre antitruste e
136	polГӯtica industrial.
137	
138	### As duas concepГ§Гөes de Antitruste e sua relaГ§ГЈo com a polГӯtica industrial
139	
140	A literatura, conforme ressaltam White (2008) e Sokol (2015), geralmente
141	opГөe a polГӯtica antitruste e a polГӯtica industrial. Tais polГӯticas sГЈo
142	colocadas como contraditГіrias e opostas. Normalmente, a polГӯtica
143	antitruste Г© lida como uma polГӯtica voltada ao combate a grandes
144	corporaГ§Гөes, desmantelar cartГ©is e bloquear fusГөes e a criaГ§ГЈo de
145	grandes empresas, tendo como meta explГӯcita promover o livre mercado e a
146	eficiГӘncia, enquanto a polГӯtica industrial Г© vista como um conjunto de
147	aГ§Гөes estatais de intervenГ§ГЈo mais direta, visando a proteГ§ГЈo a setores
148	estratГ©gicos (como agricultura, energia, ou outros setores considerados
149	estratГ©gicos pelo Estado) ou ainda promover e proteger campeГөes
150	nacionais (ibid.)[^1].
```

## DiferenГ§a entre os dois arquivos

**`3-texts/Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.qmd`** вҖ” Г© o **texto de autoria primГЎria atual**, em inglГӘs, formato Quarto (149 linhas, 7 seГ§Гөes numeradas + declaraГ§ГЈo de uso de IA + referГӘncias). CitaГ§Гөes usam citekeys Better BibTeX (`[@Onto2017]`, `[@Mahoney-Thelen2010]`) que resolvem contra o `.bib` gerenciado pelo Zotero. JГЎ tem uma estrutura de argumento madura e estabilizada (conversГЈo institucional, os dois casos Gerdau-Pains e Ambev, seГ§ГЈo de discussГЈo e conclusГЈo).

**`9-vers/previous-versions/2026 Antitrust as industrial policy - Nahoum & ManГ§ano.docx`** вҖ” Г© um **rascunho anterior em portuguГӘs**, explicitamente marcado no topo como "**Early draft. Do not cite.**". DiferenГ§as-chave:

1. **Idioma**: majoritariamente em portuguГӘs (o `.qmd` Г© 100% inglГӘs).
2. **CitaГ§Гөes**: estilo APA solto no corpo do texto (`Folha, 2004`, `Mahoney & Thelen, 2009`) em vez de citekeys вҖ” nГЈo usa o `.bib` do Zotero.
3. **Marcadores de trabalho pendente**: contГ©m trechos envoltos em `[...]{.mark}` (ex. o parГЎgrafo inteiro da introduГ§ГЈo, linhas 25вҖ“35) вҖ” sinal de texto ainda nГЈo finalizado pelos autores, igual Г  convenГ§ГЈo usada no `.qmd`.
4. **Estrutura de seГ§Гөes diferente**: sГі 2 seГ§Гөes com heading `###` visГӯveis (`As duas concepГ§Гөes de Antitruste...` e `Criando as multinacionais verde-amarelas...`), mais uma seГ§ГЈo de `ConclusГөes` e `ReferГӘncias` вҖ” nГЈo bate com a divisГЈo em 7 seГ§Гөes numeradas do `.qmd` atual.
5. **Lista de referГӘncias mais ampla e menos curada**: inclui itens (ex. `Bolle et al. 2025`, `Clemente 2024`, `Foster & Thelen 2024/2025`) que nГЈo aparecem citados no `.qmd` atual вҖ” sugere material de leitura/apoio que nГЈo foi (ou ainda nГЈo foi) incorporado ao texto final.

Ou seja: este `.docx` parece ser um **rascunho intermediГЎrio em portuguГӘs** вҖ” provavelmente um insumo anterior Г  conversГЈo/fusГЈo para o `.qmd` em inglГӘs mencionada no `CLAUDE.md` (que registra que "o rascunho em portuguГӘs... foi incorporado seГ§ГЈo por seГ§ГЈo" ao artigo atual). 150	nacionais (ibid.)[^1].
151	
152	No entanto o antitruste, em sua concepГ§ГЈo no fim do sГ©culo XIX nos EUA
153	estava mais relacionada a proteger pequenos negГіcios \"for its own
154	sake\" (White, 2008, p. 2), ou seja, atuando como uma polГӯtica que, em
155	sua prГЎtica inicial, servia como uma forma de protecionismo para
156	pequenas empresas em vez de ter como principal objetivo a eficiГӘncia
157	econГҙmica. Essa oposiГ§ГЈo nos ajuda a tratar de como, na realidade, as
158	concepГ§Гөes sobre os objetivos da polГӯtica antitruste se transformaram
159	amplamente ao longo do sГ©culo XX.
160	
161	A visГЈo predominante nas primeiras dГ©cadas do antitruste (especialmente
162	da chamada "era progressista" dos EUA, entre 1900 e 1920, atГ© os anos
163	1960/70), era baseada na estrutura de mercado. A alta concentraГ§ГЈo de
164	mercado era considerada um risco intrГӯnseco para a livre concorrГӘncia. A
165	simples existГӘncia de empresas com alto *market-share* jГЎ era vista como
166	um problema, independentemente de seus efeitos imediatos sobre os
167	preГ§os. A presunГ§ГЈo era que tal poder de mercado inevitavelmente levaria
168	a abusos (Khan, 2016). Essa abordagem visava evitar o que era percebido
169	como poder excessivo nas mГЈos de poucas corporaГ§Гөes, o que era visto
170	como uma ameaГ§a nГЈo apenas econГҙmica, mas tambГ©m democrГЎtica, ecoando o
171	pensamento de figuras como Louis Brandeis, que defendia a dispersГЈo do
172	poder e o combate Г  \'maldiГ§ГЈo do gigantismo\' (Brandeis, 1914; Wu,
173	2018) promovendo (ou construindo) o mercado de competiГ§ГЈo regulada
174	(Berk, 2009).
175	
176	A partir dos anos 1960 e 1970, impulsionada pela ascensГЈo da Escola de
177	Chicago (Bork, 1974) e por economistas como Oliver Williamson (1968,
178	1974), o foco explГӯcito no \"bem-estar do consumidor\" (consumer
179	welfare), frequentemente definido em termos de preГ§os baixos, tornou-se
180	a meta dominante da polГӯtica antitruste nos EUA (Khan, 2016). Farina
181	(1994) caracteriza essa reorientaГ§ГЈo de "desregulamentaГ§ГЈo", e diferente
182	do que alguns discursos correntes fazem parecer, como ressalta Thelen
183	(2025), esse processo nГЈo foi o ponto de partida, mas sim o culminar de
184	uma histГіria mais longa em que a estrutura legal e polГӯtica antitruste
185	estadunidense, caracterizada pela hostilidade judicial Г  coordenaГ§ГЈo
186	coletiva, se transformou.
187	
188	Essa mudanГ§a consolidou a ideia de que a principal funГ§ГЈo do antitruste
189	era garantir que os consumidores tivessem acesso a bens e serviГ§os aos
190	preГ§os mais baixos possГӯveis. Os economistas citados argumentam que ela
191	era excessivamente intervencionista e nГЈo se baseava em uma compreensГЈo
192	econГҙmica (entendida por eles como uma abordagem centrada na eficiГӘncia
193	e no bem-estar do consumidor, medida primariamente pelos preГ§os). Seria
194	necessГЎrio considerar os benefГӯcios potenciais de eficiГӘncia dos casos
195	de fusГөes e aquisiГ§Гөes de empresas (como economias de escala, escopo ou
196	reduГ§ГЈo de custos de transaГ§ГЈo) contra seus provГЎveis impactos
197	anticompetitivos. Pelos efeitos protecionistas da perspectiva anterior
198	do antitruste, adeptos dessa perspectiva chegaram a rotular, de forma
199	depreciativa, a primeira perspectiva do antitruste como de polГӯtica
200	industrial (White, 2008), tendo os efeitos opostos do que eles concebiam
201	que o antitruste deveria ter (de promover a eficiГӘncia econГҙmica) e por
202	ser excessivamente intervencionista.
203	
204	As anГЎlises de Williamson (1968, 1978) apoiavam a ideia de que a
205	desagregaГ§ГЈo excessiva de uma empresa de rede poderia ser
206	contraproducente, defendendo uma forma de integraГ§ГЈo que promovesse a
207	eficiГӘncia via reduГ§ГЈo dos custos de transaГ§ГЈo, o que poderia ocorrer em
208	grandes empresas hierГЎrquicas, ao invГ©s da organizaГ§ГЈo do mercado.
209	Assim, os argumentos posteriores prГі-fusГөes de empresas mobilizam
210	especialmente a perspectiva centrada nas provГЎveis sinergias e
211	eficiГӘncias dos grandes negГіcios e as das fusГөes, enquanto os crГӯticos
212	da concentraГ§ГЈo de mercado enfatizam o carГЎter potencialmente
213	anticompetitivo e a oportunidade de abusos econГҙmicos quando as empresas
214	concentram a maior parte da capacidade produtiva de determinado produto
215	em determinados mercados relevantes.
216	
217	Em parte importante do debate, o antitruste Г© colocado em oposiГ§ГЈo e
218	contradiГ§ГЈo com a polГӯtica industrial. A influГӘncia da teoria dos custos
219	de transaГ§ГЈo de Williamson e da Escola de Chicago e a proposta de que o
220	antitruste deveria, como principal missГЈo, promover a eficiГӘncia
221	econГҙmica, enquanto a polГӯtica industrial era vista como uma polГӯtica
222	que promovia ineficiГӘncias e levava a distorГ§Гөes de mercado, organizou
223	parte importante do debate.
224	
225	No entanto, o resgate histГіrico mais longo complexifica essa relaГ§ГЈo,
226	evidenciando a necessidade de especificar de que antitruste e de que
227	polГӯtica industrial se esta falando. Certas concepГ§Гөes de antitruste e
228	polГӯtica industrial podem ser conciliadas, argumenta Farina (1994),
229	quando tГӘm objetivos e limites delimitados. Outras anГЎlises,
230	especialmente por autores do neoinstitucionalismo sociolГіgico e
231	histГіrico, como Frank Dobbin (1997) e Monica Prasad (2006, 2015), mudam
232	o olhar, tratando de como a polГӯtica antitruste pode fazer parte de uma
233	polГӯtica industrial em si.
234	
235	Гү verdade que instituiГ§Гөes, normas e prГЎticas de regulaГ§ГЈo do poder de
236	mercado foram empregadas no passado, em particular nos Estados Unidos,
237	como uma forma de polГӯtica industrial. Dobbin (1994) faz da regulaГ§ГЈo de
238	mercado, primeiro por meio de uma comissГЈo interestadual de comГ©rcio, e
239	depois pela legislaГ§ГЈo de defesa da concorrГӘncia, o traГ§o definidor de
240	uma polГӯtica industrial em um paГӯs avesso Г  intervenГ§ГЈo e ao
241	planejamento estatais que caracterizam a polГӯtica industrial de outros
242	paГӯses. A regulaГ§ГЈo de mercados seria a forma residual com que, nos
243	Estados Unidos, governos promoveriam o ideal de mercados competitivos
244	com a proteГ§ГЈo da autonomia local, a proteГ§ГЈo dos consumidores contra
245	carteis e dos empresГЎrios locais contra grandes empresas com poder de
246	mercado exacerbado (Dobbin e Down 2000). Dobbin nomeia essas polГӯticas
247	adotadas nos Estados Unidos de repressГЈo a condutas anticoncorrenciais
248	de polГӯtica industrial de implementaГ§ГЈo de mercados.
249	
250	Prasad (2012) tambГ©m articula como polГӯtica industrial as medidas
251	antitruste adotadas nos Estados Unidos no inГӯcio do sГ©culo XX. Isso
252	porque, para ela, o antitruste nГЈo foi uma medida de promoГ§ГЈo da
253	eficiГӘncia nos mercados, mas efetivamente uma polГӯtica resultante da
254	alianГ§a do populismo agrГЎrio com reformadores progressistas visando a
255	administrar o poder corporativo e refletindo a desconfianГ§a em um
256	governo ampliado e em corporaГ§Гөes grandes. NГЈo se tratava da promoГ§ГЈo de
257	preГ§os menores, bem estar do consumidor, e competiГ§ГЈo e livre-mercado,
258	mas uma proteГ§ГЈo contra ele.
259	
260	Se compreendemos a polГӯtica industrial como "um esforГ§o concentrado do
261	governo para promover um setor especГӯfico com ferramentas como
262	subsГӯdios, proteГ§Гөes comerciais, fusГөes forГ§adas e proteГ§ГЈo contra
263	aquisiГ§Гөes estrangeiras."[^2] (White, 2008, p.8), o antitruste pode ser
264	lido, como fazem Dobbin (1994) e Prasad (2012) como parte da polГӯtica
265	industrial. NГЈo Г  toa os crГӯticos da polГӯtica de proteГ§ГЈo Г s pequenas
266	empresas nos Estados Unidos do inГӯcio do sГ©culo XX foi frequentemente
267	descrita como "polГӯtica industrial" por seus detratores que apontavam as
268	ineficiГӘncias geradas (Thelen 2025).
269	
270	O caso estadunidense indica, portanto, que o antitruste pode servir como
271	instrumento de uma polГӯtica industrial. Nele, porГ©m, ele esteve a
272	serviГ§o da repressГЈo a prГЎticas de abuso de poder econГҙmico e da
273	proteГ§ГЈo da pequena empresa. No caso brasileiro, ao contrГЎrio, um novo
274	aparato de defesa da concorrГӘncia serГЎ utilizado para constituir grandes
275	empresas com significativo poder (oligopolista) no mercado nacional sob
276	a justificativa de que elas poderiam, assim, ser players no mercado
277	internacional e proteger-se de aquisiГ§Гөes estrangeiras.
278	
279	A comparaГ§ГЈo entre os casos pode contribuir para a compreensГЈo de como
280	instituiГ§Гөes podem ter efeitos surpreendentes e atores podem manipular
281	criativamente as ambiguidades das normas em face a diferentes condiГ§Гөes
282	que demandam adaptaГ§ГЈo para atingirem seus objetivos, como a proteГ§ГЈo de
283	certos atores de mercado de seus competidores maiores, sejam eles
284	pequenos negГіcios rurais do interior dos EUA demandando proteГ§ГЈo de
285	grandes empresas e trusts ou grandes empresas em formaГ§ГЈo no Brasil em
286	um cenГЎrio em que vislumbram competiГ§ГЈo acirrada com competidores
287	internacionais ainda maiores e mais capitalizados, como veremos na seГ§ГЈo
288	seguinte. Em ambos os casos, o Estado Г© pressionado para promover uma
289	regulaГ§ГЈo que efetivamente proteja os demandantes e adapte a regulaГ§ГЈo
290	para alcanГ§ar os objetivos da polГӯtica industrial passiva via
291	antitruste, adaptaГ§ГЈo essa que pode significar regras bastante
292	distintas.
293	
294	### Criando as multinacionais verde-amarelas: quando o novo aparato de antitruste brasileiro foi utilizado criativamente para produzir grandes firmas internacionalizadas
295	
296	A regulaГ§ГЈo relacionada Г  defesa da concorrГӘncia e contra o abuso do
297	poder econГҙmico no Brasil tem suas origens no perГӯodo Vargas, com o
298	Decreto-lei de "proteГ§ГЈo Г  economia popular", e posteriormente a "Lei
299	Malaia", posteriormente incorporada na constituiГ§ГЈo que sucedeu o Estado
```
