See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
```
305	C. Foster and K. Thelen                                                                                        BRANDEIS IN BRUSSELS?
306	
307	
308	drawn from foreign jurisdictions. We argue that over time, this dynamic learning process has led EU competition
309	law to increasingly approximate BrandeisвҖҷ ideal: to вҖңsupplement the prohibition of monopoly by the regulation of
310	competitionвҖқ (Brandeis, 1913 reprinted in Fraenkel, 1934, p. 124).
311	     This ideal has been more difп¬Ғcult to realize in the judicialized American antitrust system, despite good-faith
312	efforts of many practitioners. Federal judges have consistently limited agenciesвҖҷ procedural discretion, most nota-
313	bly by discouraging the FTC from using its rulemaking authority to develop clear rules regulating вҖңunfair compe-
314	titionвҖқ (Chopra & Khan, 2020; Rybnicek & Wright, 2013). The judiciary has played an equally consequential role
315	limiting agenciesвҖҷ substantive discretion in enforcement actions, often substituting its own factual or analytical
316	determinations for that of agencies. Judges operating on the assumption that speciп¬Ғc economic theories are supe-
317	rior to administrative expertise have made it more difп¬Ғcult to make the nuanced distinctions between вҖңbeneп¬ҒcialвҖқ
318	and вҖңharmfulвҖқ types of interп¬Ғrm coordination as envisioned by Brandeis. This institutional constellation has also
319	disincentivized and sometimes even prevented regulators from updating policy and practice in response to past
320	experience, new information, and lessons from other countries.
321	
322	
323	3. Industrialization, competition regimes, and the Brandeisian ideal
324	The United States and Europe charted distinctive paths of competition policy over a century ago. In the turbulent
325	closing years of the 19th century, political economies on both sides of the Atlantic were upended by п¬Ғnancial cri-
326	ses and deep economic depressions. Overcapacity across key markets caused wages and proп¬Ғts to plummet, set-
327	ting in motion vicious cutthroat competition among п¬Ғrms and provoking considerable industrial strife. American
328	and European п¬Ғrms across a wide range of industries sought to stabilize markets by banding together into
329	arrangements to defend against destructive competition and to restore industrial peace.
330	     The fate of these arrangements turned centrally on the response of the state and the courts. In the
331	United States, where the development of a strong administrative state followed rather than preceded the rise of
332	large corporations, the judiciary played an outsized role in the early development of federal economic regulation
333	(Skowronek, 1982). On the question of economic coordination between п¬Ғrms, the US Supreme Court was
334	unusuallyвҖ”indeed, in comparative perspective, uniquelyвҖ”hostile to emerging forms of horizontal organization
335	while simultaneously taking a permissive approach to corporate consolidation and economic dominance that fell
336	short of a complete monopoly.2 The passage of the Sherman Act, or more precisely the CourtвҖҷs interpretation of
337	that law, created strong prohibitions on horizontal coordination, providing tools for employers to attack nascent
338	worker organizations and encouraging large producers to stabilize markets through alternative strategies such as
339	corporate consolidation or the use of vertical restraints to restrict competition from above. The ensuing вҖңgreat
340	merger movementвҖқ in the United States at the turn of the century led to corporate hierarchy and arms-length
341	exchange becoming the dominant modes of coordination (Lamoreaux, 1988; Sklar, 1988). The strong binary
342	between вҖңpureвҖқ competition and corporate hierarchy that the American judiciary enforced and policed left a deep
343	imprint on the American political economy (Berk, 1996, p. 108; Paul, 2020; Phillips Sawyer, 2018).
344	     By contrast, competition policy in European countries during this period was characterized by a laissez-faire
345	approach that largely permitted the emergence of private governance structures to stabilize market volatility
346	(Djelic, 2001, pp. 42вҖ“64). Where courts did weigh in on private law disputes they showed forbearance toward
347	(if not active support for) these emerging forms of coordination.3 Such forbearance of course gave rise to cartels,
348	but it also provided a more hospitable context for the growth of centralized labor unions and strong trade associ-
349	ations that would come to deп¬Ғne EuropeвҖҷs more вҖңcoordinatedвҖқ variety of capitalism (Hall & Soskice, 2001;
350	Thelen, 2020). So, while cartels were legal and widespread, national courts and governments also took a more
351	neutral stance toward organizations of workers, farmers, and small producers, all of which were consistently
352	undercut by judge-made law in the United States.4
353	     When European countries did eventually enact public statutes and create regulatory ofп¬Ғces to address compe-
354	tition concerns, they adopted administrative models that maintained a forbearing approach to many forms of
355	horizontal agreements. The п¬Ғrst German Cartel Law, enacted in 1923, became the blueprint for other early
356	European competition regulations, including in Sweden (1925), Holland (1935), and Denmark (1937)
357	(Harding & Joshua, 2010, pp. 80вҖ“81). Unlike in the United States, these laws were not designed to suppress car-
358	tels, but were instead meant to root out cartel вҖңabusesвҖқ through public monitoring and sanctions
359	1088                                             В© 2023 The Authors. Regulation & Governance published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd.
360	
361	
362	--- PAGE BREAK ---
363	
364	                                                                                                                            17485991, 2024, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/rego.12570 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [30/05/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
365	BRANDEIS IN BRUSSELS?                                                                             C. Foster and K. Thelen
366	
367	
368	(Feldenkirchen, 1992; Harding & Joshua, 2010, pp. 74вҖ“81; Lapidus, 2013). Crucially, all European systems
369	depended on administrativeвҖ”rather than judicialвҖ”models of cartel regulation. Under these systems, economics
370	ministries were given wide legal discretion to directly intervene in cartel activity if it was deemed to endanger
371	public welfare, while leaving it largely up to expert administrators how to deп¬Ғne these speciп¬Ғc conditions
372	(Fellman & Shanahan, 2015; Gerber, 1998, p. 120).
373	    In the interwar period, these regulatory systems came to be rightly criticized as overly-permissive, particularly
374	in Germany, where cartels grew powerful and reactionary. But virtually all European countriesвҖ”including
375	Austria, Belgium, Finland, the Netherlands, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland, Spain, Italy, and the United
376	KingdomвҖ”took a comparatively permissive stance toward horizontal coordination (SchrГ¶ter, 1996).5 Moreover,
377	these arrangements were not captured by powerful private interests everywhere, nor was administrative regulation
378	inevitably ineffective. In Norway, for example, all cartel agreements were required to be published and explicitly
379	approved by a politically independent regulator that was granted broad authority to demand information from
380	companies and to investigate the conduct of cartels and dominant п¬Ғrms. During the interwar period, this Norwe-
381	gian Cartel Control ofп¬Ғce decided more than 800 cases, placing signiп¬Ғcant constraints on вҖңprice discrimination,
382	exclusive dealings and certain boycottsвҖқ if they were found to be harmful to the public interest (Gerber, 1998,
383	pp. 156вҖ“159). Large п¬Ғrms were subject to special scrutiny, and enforcement centered, among other things, on
384	ensuring fair terms and conditions for weaker cartel members (Espeli, 2015). The competition authority possessed
385	considerable discretion, including the ability to alter cartel terms, block cartel enforcement actions and even to
386	dissolve cartel organizations, although the most extreme of these measures were rarely invoked (Espeli, 2015,
387	pp. 145вҖ“148; Gerber, 1998). While the Norwegian experience was in some ways exceptional, that countryвҖҷs com-
388	petition laws shared with a far wider range of European countries an orientation that focused on policing abuse
389	rather than enforcing strict prohibitions, and that was therefore distinct from the hard-lined prohibitions devel-
390	oped by courts in the United States (Gerber, 1998, pp. 158вҖ“164).
391	    EuropeвҖҷs distinct approach to competition policy was a choice not an oversight. Lawmakers in the late 19th
392	and early 20th centuries had often drawn вҖңnegative lessonsвҖқ from US antitrust policy, explicitly consideringвҖ”and
393	rejectingвҖ”the American approach. They saw the US Supreme CourtвҖҷs interpretation of the Sherman Antitrust
394	Act and the strict prohibition against horizontal combinations as detrimental for the way it encouraged monop-
395	oly power while allowing cutthroat competition among п¬Ғrms to go unchecked. Indeed, coordination among п¬Ғrms
396	was viewed as highly desirable to prevent the growth of large US-type monopoliesвҖ”both to forestall вҖңdestructiveвҖқ
397	competition and to protect against the вҖңravages of hyper-individualismвҖқ (NГ¶rr, 1995, p. 7).
398	    In the same way that Europeans were looking to the United States in this period (albeit in the case of anti-
399	trust, as a negative model), American observers in the early 20th century had looked across the ocean from the
400	other side, and some of them found much to recommend in EuropeвҖҷs alternative approach to social and eco-
401	nomic institutions (Rodgers, 1998). In the area of competition policy speciп¬Ғcally, the Progressive Era lawyer and
402	anti-monopolist Louis Brandeis admired some elements of EuropeвҖҷs alternative model of вҖңorganizedвҖқ or вҖңman-
403	agedвҖқ capitalism, especially its cooperative associations and strong trade organizations (Berk, 2009;
404	Brandeis, 1912; Strum, 1993, p. 41; Winerman, 2003, p. 36). Brandeis had observed how unrestricted competition
405	in the United States often led to monopoly by fueling cycles of overproduction and destructive competition, what
406	he called вҖңcompetition that killsвҖқ (Brandeis, 1913). He was struck by the fact that in many European countries
407	industrialization had not resulted in the same degree of corporate consolidation, and вҖңruinous competitionвҖқ had
408	been more limited, in large part because of the role these cooperative and trade associations played in
409	regulatingвҖ”and in important ways limitingвҖ”competition. He expressed particular admiration for EuropeвҖҷs
410	vibrant cooperative movements, which combined efп¬Ғciencies of scale with local democratic control. He praised
411	Danish cooperatives for allowing small producers to hold their own against dominant actors, and held up
412	BritainвҖҷs Cooperative Wholesale Society as a вҖңbeneп¬ҒcentвҖқ alternative to concentrated power in American industry
413	(Brandeis, 1914).
414	    Like many American progressives of the period, Brandeis was familiar with the German Historical School of
415	Economics, which opposed the laissez-faire system defended by classical economists and contended that state reg-
416	ulation and private associations could foster economic and social progress by, among other things, raising the
417	plane of competition (Dorfman, 1955; NГ¶rr, 1995). Similarly, BrandeisвҖҷ approach to economics was based more
418	on inductive reasoning through empirical historical analysis of how the economy actually worked than the
419	В© 2023 The Authors. Regulation & Governance published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd.                         1089
420	
421	
422	--- PAGE BREAK ---
423	
424	                                                                                                                                               17485991, 2024, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/rego.12570 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [30/05/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
425	C. Foster and K. Thelen                                                                                         BRANDEIS IN BRUSSELS?
426	
427	
428	deductive and formalistic reasoning preferred by classical and marginalist economists (Adelstein, 1989, pp. 632вҖ“
429	634). Applying this inductive approach to German and Danish trade associations, Brandeis observed that
430	industry-led coordination on price or output could upgrade competition in ways that allowed smaller п¬Ғrms (often
431	catering to niche markets) to survive, without sacriп¬Ғcing productivity. Indeed, such agreements could be
432	productivity-enhancing because they channeled competition away from price and toward innovation and quality
433	(Berk, 2009, pp. 60вҖ“62; Brandeis, 1912). In BrandeisвҖҷ words, вҖңthe proper role of the government is to encourage
434	not combination, but co-operationвҖқ (Winerman 2003, p. 1). Practices such as information sharing among non-
435	dominant competitors in an industry, joint standard setting, and transparency in pricing could facilitate scale
436	economies through cooperation rather than consolidation, while also preventing the cycles of over-production
437	and cutthroat price competition that foster economic centralization in capitalism (Phillips Sawyer, 2011).
438	    Brandeis was well aware of the potential abuses that could occur within private regulatory structures, particu-
439	larly when dominant companies were permitted to coerce smaller п¬Ғrms. Without a neutral state arbiter that could
440	direct competition and coordination toward beneп¬Ғcial ends, while also policing the abuses of dominant players,
441	trade associations and cartels brought with them the risk of private exploitation (Berk, 2009, p. 57). The solution
442	for Brandeis, however, was not to develop strict rules that enforced competition in every case. He called instead
443	for a system of вҖңregulated competitionвҖқ that empowered state ofп¬Ғcials to permit certain forms of inter-п¬Ғrm coop-
444	eration especially among non-dominant players that encouraged meritorious competition or upgraded production
445	into higher value-added products while also policing restraints of trade that suppressed competition or exploited
446	private economic power (Berk, 2009; Phillips Sawyer, 2011, 2018). In short, Brandeis envisioned a system of com-
447	petition regulation characterized by signiп¬Ғcant bureaucratic discretion to write and enforce rules directly, and to
448	update these policies and practices on an ongoing basis as they acquired new experience and knowledge about
449	how competition worked in practice.
450	    Thus, in debates over the Clayton Act, Brandeis articulated a vision for the FTC designed to re-center
451	decision-making away from the federal courts and toward a more responsive and proactive expert administrative
452	agency. Noting that the decades since the Sherman Act had laid bare the failures of the previous regime, he joined
453	the call for judicial remedies to be вҖңsupplemented by other adequate machinery to be administered by a federal
454	board or a commissionвҖқ (Brandeis, 1913 reprinted in Fraenkel, 1934, p. 124 and p. 131). Administrative expertise
455	would be brought to bear not just to prohibit monopoly but to regulate competition: вҖңWe need the inspector and
456	the policeman, even more than we need the prosecuting attorney; and we need for the enforcement of the Sher-
457	man law and regulation of competition, an administrative board with broad powersвҖҰ.вҖқ (Brandeis, 1913 in
458	Fraenkel, 1934, p. 134). In fact, he envisioned a board whose authority would grow over time: вҖңit is probable that
459	whatever powers are conferred upon the board at the outset will be increased from time to time as we learn from
460	experience what powers may be safely conferred upon the boardвҖқ (Brandeis, 1913 in Fraenkel, 1934, p. 134).6
461	    In short, Brandeis foresaw regulators using their expertise and discretion to distinguish вҖңgoodвҖқ from вҖңbadвҖқ
462	coordinationвҖ”i.e., combining rules that permit some forms of cooperation between competitors, while also
463	protecting coordination among п¬Ғrms by embedding competition within a body of rules that limits aggressive
464	forms of competition that would jeopardize the economic freedom of smaller producer groups and workers. In
465	BrandeisвҖҷ view, such a distinction would be difп¬Ғcult to achieve through judicial processes that emphasized
466	abstract, universally applied legal principles over the technical knowledge and practical experience relevant to the
467	operation of competition in practice and where the prevailing interpretation of the law often varied from judge to
468	judge (McCraw, 1986, p. 116). Rather, he envisioned expert administrators, ideally with previous regulatory expe-
469	rience working closely with industry, developing stable and predictable rules through deliberative processes char-
470	acterized by extensive fact-п¬Ғnding and research and ongoing consultation with stakeholders.
471	    The FTC was initially granted signiп¬Ғcant bureaucratic discretion, including the authority to regulate unfair
472	competition, to determine the meaning of these provisions, and to enforce them directly through вҖңcease-and-
473	desistвҖқ orders (Averitt, 1979). For a time, the early FTC used this authority to facilitate вҖңcooperative
474	competition,вҖқ developing programs to foster industrial standard setting, uniform accounting methods, informa-
475	tion sharing regarding inventory and shipments, and the adoption of best practices through trade associations
476	(Hawley, 1979; Peritz, 1996, pp. 76вҖ“77). Through its Trade Practices Conference Division, the agency п¬Ғnalized
477	more than 100 standardization and conservation agreements and more than 50 trade practice codes across a wide
478	range of industries (Hawley, 1979, pp. 102вҖ“104). As Phillips Sawyer (2018) has shown in a detailed study, the
479	1090                                              В© 2023 The Authors. Regulation & Governance published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd.
480	
481	
482	--- PAGE BREAK ---
483	
484	                                                                                                                            17485991, 2024, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/rego.12570 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [30/05/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
485	BRANDEIS IN BRUSSELS?                                                                             C. Foster and K. Thelen
486	
487	
488	experiment with associationalism during this period was important for American political development, generat-
489	ing вҖңlasting effects on the development of state police powers, bureaucratic capacity, and antitrust policy, not to
490	mention private trade associationsвҖқ (3).
491	    However, the fact that antitrust had been institutionalized within an adversarial legal system that severely cir-
492	cumscribes bureaucratic discretion made it difп¬Ғcult to stabilize a durable system of regulated competition along
493	the lines Brandeis envisioned. The FTCвҖҷs efforts were consistently frustrated or blocked by federal courts, which
494	prevented the agency from developing an independent, directive role over the economy (Kovacic, 1982, p. 616).
495	Courts largely prevented the FTC from using its delegated rulemaking authority to establish clear and predictable
496	rules to regulate both competition and monopoly as envisioned by Brandeis and intended by Congress
497	(McCraw, 1986).7 When the agency sought to approve вҖңbeneп¬ҒcialвҖқ agreements that it believed upgraded competi-
498	tion, it found itself stymied by the decentralized judicial enforcement system, which allowed public and private
499	actors to challenge approved trade practices, limiting their enforceability (Hawley, 1979; Himmelberg, 1976). The
500	DOJ proved to be a particular problem, prosecuting trade associations for antitrust violations and challenging
501	many of the FTCвҖҷs agreements in court (Berk, 2009, pp. 228вҖ“232).
502	    Agency efforts to curtail вҖңunfair competitionвҖқ also ran into problems. When the agency sought to investigate
503	or limit corporate abuses, its orders were often dismissed by judges or overturned on appeal (Winerman &
504	Kovacic, 2010, pp. 176вҖ“192). In its п¬Ғrst ruling on the FTCвҖҷs authority, the Supreme Court overturned an FTC
505	order forbidding a tying arrangement by a dominant steel company on the grounds that this practice had not
506	previously been forbidden in case law. As Justice McReynolds explained in the majority opinion: вҖңit is for the
507	courts, not the commission, ultimately to determine as a matter of law what they include.вҖқ8 FTC challenges to
508	other unfair competition practices, including exclusive dealing in the publishing industry, exclusive contracts
509	between dominant oil companies and retailers, price discrimination against cooperatives and individual retailers,
510	and base point pricing in the oil and steel industries were all struck down by judges (2010, 177-187). FTC
511	monopolization cases against Eastman Kodak, General Electric, and the Aluminum Company of America were
512	similarly blocked by courts (2010, 188-190). All in all, 82% of the restraint-of-trade orders issued during its п¬Ғrst
513	decade were either dismissed by judges outright or eventually overturned during judicial review.9 The one area
514	where the FTC did п¬Ғnd some courtroom success was in prosecuting horizontal conspiraciesвҖ”a category of agree-
515	ments that were already prohibited in Sherman Act case law, and where Brandeis had long argued for a more
516	permissive approach (2010, pp. 192вҖ“193).
517	
518	
519	4. The development of regulated competition in Europe
520	The upheaval of World War IIвҖ”and the monumental political and economic changes it inspired in the
521	postwarвҖ”created a rare opportunity to completely overhaul European economic policy, including in the competi-
522	tion п¬Ғeld. During the occupation of Germany, Americans presided over the dismantling of that countryвҖҷs most
523	notorious cartels, forcing a break with the industrial production model that predominated at the beginning of the
524	20th century (Djelic, 2001). The war and its aftermath had also profoundly shaken European elitesвҖҷ belief in
525	the economic effectiveness and political legitimacy of cartels, which now had strong associations with fascism
526	(Djelic, 2001; Wells, 2001).
527	     Yet even as outright cartels were delegitimized, most national competition laws in the postwar maintained
528	core aspects of the administrative system of cartel control developed during the interwar period. At the core of
529	the model was the idea that вҖңlaw should protect the process of competition by administratively controlling the
530	harmful conduct of economically powerful п¬Ғrms rather than by prohibiting particular types of conductвҖқ
531	(Gerber, 1998, pp. 163вҖ“164). Toward that end, most European countries established variants of вҖңcartel registriesвҖқ
532	that empowered regulators to approve вҖңbeneп¬ҒcialвҖқ forms of coordination while proscribing discrete forms of car-
533	tel abuse (Fellman & Shanahan, 2015).
534	     At the supranational level, US government ofп¬Ғcials were again heavily involved in designing the competition
535	provisions of the European Coal and Steel Community and the US experience with the Sherman Act provided a
536	primary reference point for the competition regime established a few years later in the European Economic Com-
537	munity (EEC) (Djelic, 2001; Wells, 2001). In particular, the stricter approach to price agreements and output
538	restrictions would have a lasting effect on European Community law, contributing to the decartelization of
539	В© 2023 The Authors. Regulation & Governance published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd.                         1091
540	
541	
542	--- PAGE BREAK ---
543	
544	                                                                                                                                               17485991, 2024, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/rego.12570 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [30/05/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
545	C. Foster and K. Thelen                                                                                         BRANDEIS IN BRUSSELS?
546	
547	
548	European industry and the instantiation of market competition as a core principle of the European Union
549	(Djelic, 2001; Jabko, 2006). Nonetheless, in several important respects, the EU competition system preserved sig-
550	niп¬Ғcant features of the historically evolved understandings and practices reviewed in the previous
551	section (Freyer, 2006, pp. 278вҖ“314; Gerber, 1998). The European system would essentially blend these orienta-
552	tions inherited from the past (including, centrally, administrative enforcement and forbearance toward coordina-
553	tion among non-dominant actors) with elements of the so-called вҖңordoliberalвҖқ school of competition that
554	crystallized in Germany in the post war period and that called for a вҖңconstitutionalвҖқ framework to limit the
555	power of dominant actors (Gerber, 1994).
556	    The supranational European system established under the Treaties of Paris and Rome stemmed from a series
557	of political compromises. The enactment of substantive rules that prohibited cartels while providing broad
558	exemptions for вҖңbeneп¬ҒcialвҖқ horizontal agreements reп¬Ӯected a compromise between вҖңcross-national liberal mod-
559	ernizersвҖқ supportive of the American cartel prohibition model and European business associations and unions,
560	which saw horizontal cooperation as essential for rationalization, standardization, and specialization in European
561	industry (Djelic, 2001, pp. 232вҖ“235). The instantiation of an administrative enforcement system whose decisions
562	could be reviewed by independent courts reп¬Ӯected a compromise between Germany, which preferred a strong
563	juridical element, and France, which wanted to maintain a more discretionary administrative control approach
564	(Gerber, 1998, pp. 343вҖ“344).
565	    The вҖңhybridвҖқ combination of a French-style administration with a German-inspired ordoliberal framework
566	developed into a set of institutions that are arguably closer to BrandeisвҖҷ vision of вҖңfair tradeвҖқ and вҖңregulated com-
567	petitionвҖқ than most observers recognize, and certainly closer to that vision than ever achieved in the
568	United States. In particular, the current system embodies key features that Brandeis had identiп¬Ғed as important
569	to managing markets and preserving competition. Some of these are proceduralвҖ”in particular, the central role
570	assigned to agencies staffed with experts to preside over an administrative system aimed at preserving a competi-
571	tive market structure while also encouraging forms of cooperation seen as improving economic performance.
572	Others are substantiveвҖ”in particular, recognizing the beneп¬Ғts of providing weaker parties with coordination
573	rights as a form of countervailing power, while also aggressively policing large companies to combat their efforts
574	to exploit their economic clout. In what follows, we discuss the emergence and evolution of EU competition law,
575	highlighting parallels to AmericaвҖҷs own вҖңlost historical alternativeвҖқ of the Brandeisian vision on each of these
576	dimensions (Schneiberg, 2007).
577	
578	
579	4.1. Proactive administrative enforcement
580	Central to BrandeisвҖҷ model of вҖңregulated competitionвҖқ was a proactive system of administrative governance.
581	Toward this end, he supported the establishment of an expert administrative agency, empowered to devise tai-
582	lored rules that were based on economic studies and consultation with industry. As he explained in a 1913 article
583	outlining his preferred approach, вҖңIn the complicated questions involved in dealing with вҖҳBig BusinessвҖҷ, the п¬Ғrst
584	requisite is knowledgeвҖ”comprehensive, accurate, and up-to-dateвҖ”of the details of business operationsвҖқ
585	(reprinted in Fraenkel, 1934: 134). While Brandeis preferred a cooperative relationship between business and gov-
586	ernment, he also recognized that for such a system to be effective, administrators would need to be delegated an
587	authoritative say over the rules of competition as well as вҖңcoerciveвҖқ powers to police abuses, particularly by domi-
588	nant players (Berk, 2009, p. 101). As he noted: вҖңThe time has come to utilize that experience and to embody its
589	dictates into rules of positive law, which will instruct the many business men who desire to obey the statute, what
590	they should avoidвҖ”and admonish those less conscientious what they must avoidвҖқ (Brandeis, 1913 in
591	Fraenkel, 1934: 131).
592	    From the start, the European CommunityвҖҷs system of competition law hewed to older European traditions in
593	opting for an administrative rather than judicial system of enforcement that empowered an expert agency to write
594	rules that permitted as well as prohibited certain categories of restrictive agreements. During the negotiations over
595	the Treaties of Paris and Rome, the six founding member states (all steeped in a civil law rather than a common
596	law tradition) explicitly rejected proposals modeled on the US antitrust system that would criminalize all horizon-
597	tal intercompany agreements and be enforced by private and public litigants in courts. Across all six of the
598	founding countries of the EEC, industry (organized into strong trade associations) joined with labor unions to
599	1092                                              В© 2023 The Authors. Regulation & Governance published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd.
600	
601	
602	--- PAGE BREAK ---
603	
604	                                                                                                                          17485991, 2024, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/rego.12570 by Capes, Wiley Online Library on [30/05/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License
605	BRANDEIS IN BRUSSELS?                                                                           C. Foster and K. Thelen
606	
607	
608	oppose, to varying degrees, the establishment of a вҖңSherman ActвҖқ in Europe, which they saw as undermining
609	their ability to pursue cooperative arrangements that were needed for economic recovery and reconstruction
610	(Gillingham, 2004; Milward, 1984).
611	    Member states chose instead to grant the Commission signiп¬Ғcant procedural and substantive discretion over
612	the development and enforcement of competition policy (Majone, 2001; Pollack, 2003). Reп¬Ӯecting continental tra-
613	ditions of bureaucratic legalism, the system was both hierarchical and formalistic. Decision-making was domi-
614	nated by the European Commission, which was empowered to develop authoritative norms and standards
615	regarding both the structure of its own implementation authority as well as the speciп¬Ғc rules that companies
616	should follow. At the same time, individual bureaucratic decisions conformed to written legal guidelines and pro-
617	cedures that set out in great detail the kinds of restrictive practices that were permitted and prohibited and the
618	speciп¬Ғc processes involved in enforcement.
619	    The foundational procedural rules enacted by the new EEC gave the Commission the exclusive authority both
620	to enforce violations of competition rules and to provide forbearance to registered business agreements deter-
621	mined to be economically or socially beneп¬Ғcial (Kelleher, 1967). A few years later, this authority was expanded to
622	include block exemptions, providing the Commission with a quasi-legislative power over inter-п¬Ғrm agreements
623	(Gerber, 1998, pp. 350вҖ“351). The CommissionвҖҷs broad administrative authority was made subject to judicial
624	review. But unlike US courts, which have a long history of overruling antitrust regulators, the Court of Justice of
625	the European Union (CJEU) has generally supported the CommissionвҖҷs substantive determinations, establishing
626	the precedent of deferring to the CommissionвҖҷs expertise in questions involving вҖңcomplex evaluations of eco-
627	nomic mattersвҖқ even as it also demanded a strong evidentiary basis for decisions and strict compliance with pro-
628	cedural and rule of law norms (Kalintri, 2016). In practice, this provided the Commission with вҖңsufп¬Ғcient
629	discretion to resolve difп¬Ғcult competition issues, including the granting of exemptionsвҖқ (Freyer, 2006, p. 285).
630	    During the 1960s and 1970s, the European Commission used its bureaucratic discretion to develop rules and
631	procedures that sought to distinguish between вҖңhealthyвҖқ and вҖңunhealthyвҖқ forms of cooperation that bear a strong
632	resemblance to the role that Brandeis had envisioned for the FTC in the 1910s. Whereas the US DOJ has long
633	been skeptical of the beneп¬Ғts of business cooperation, the European Commission developed rules that were more
634	permissive and sometimes even supportive of cooperation agreements that were viewed as serving the purpose of
635	either breaking down national barriers or increasing productive efп¬Ғciency. As Hans von der Groeben, the п¬Ғrst
636	competition commissioner explained in an early policy document, the enforcement of European competition law
637	should not be based on overly broad legal presumptions such as the per se ban against horizontal cartels devel-
638	oped by American courts (von der Groeben, 1961). Rather, the Commission would develop tailored rules that
639	were informed by the economic analysis of agreements in practice. Whether considering a horizontal arrange-
640	ment between a group of small manufacturers, the marketing practices of a large company, or a member state
641	governmentвҖҷs industrial policy, the evaluation could not be determined by legal content alone, but instead
642	required an вҖңeconomic interpretation in each caseвҖқ (13).
643	    Despite signiп¬Ғcant changes over time, this model of proactive administrative management has generally
644	proved resilient. Following a political impasse in the 1960s and 1970s, the CommissionвҖҷs administrative pow-
645	ers have steadily grown, with the Commission increasingly using legislation and guidelines to establish com-
646	petition law norms, substituting вҖңan administrative institution for a judicial institution as the driving force
647	within the systemвҖқ (Gerber, 1998, p. 381). This administrative rulemaking authority allows the Commission
648	to вҖңprovide lists of speciп¬Ғc clauses that are considered, respectively, acceptable, unacceptable, and possibly
649	acceptable,вҖқ and then вҖңinduce п¬Ғrms to structure their agreements to conform to these checklistsвҖқ
650	(Gerber, 1998, pp. 378вҖ“379). In effect, this makes it possible for the Commission to distinguish between
651	вҖңgoodвҖқ and вҖңbadвҖқ restrictive agreements (Georgieva, 2015). Even after substantial вҖңmodernizationвҖқ reforms
652	enacted in 2004 that decentralized enforcement by empowering national regulators and courts to also enforce
653	the law, the Commission still retains broad substantive discretion in this arena (European Commission,
654	2009). The Commission not only maintained its authority to issue block exemptions and directly enforce the
655	law through administrative processes, but also received new remedial and investigative powers, including the
656	ability to impose structural remedies, to provide leniency to cooperative companies, and to enter settlements
657	with parties under investigation (Wilks, 2005).

668	4.2. Recognizing the beneп¬Ғts of horizontal coordination among weaker parties
669	As indicated earlier, Brandeis strongly supported arrangements that endowed weaker partiesвҖ”smaller competi-
670	tors, workers, farmersвҖ”with what Paul (2020) terms вҖңcoordination rightsвҖқ as a way of countervailing the power
671	of large corporations. Far from demanding the suppression of all forms of coordination, therefore, Brandeis called
672	for nurturing precisely those forms of coordination that safeguard competition by channeling it away from price
673	(and labor sweating) into quality and innovation. Beyond providing certain weaker parties such as farmers and
```

<details>
<summary>рҹ§  RaciocГӯnio interno</summary>

I'm getting a clearer picture of the article's focus on institutional design differences between the EU and USвҖ”administrative discretion versus judicial review in mature economiesвҖ”which means it's less directly relevant to industrial policy and developing-country antitrust concerns. Let me quickly scan the remaining sections and then check the Foster-Thelen coordination rights paper to see what else might be applicable.

The Foster-Thelen piece looks more theoretically promising since it connects competition law to varieties of capitalism, which could help me think through "coordination rights" as an analytical framework. I'm noticing an interesting inversion: while Brandeis-style coordination rights protect weaker actors like workers and small firms against dominant firms, Brazil's approach might have extended coordination rights to large domestic firms as a way to level the playing field against foreign multinationalsвҖ”that's a useful parallel to explore. Let me read through the 2025 paper to evaluate whether its framework actually strengthens the argument I'm building.

```
19	Abstract
20	Competition law is a constitutive institution in capitalist markets, establishing
21	the rules for when interп¬Ғrm coordination is allowed and where competition is
22	required (Paul, 2020). Yet comparativists have spent decades debating the
23	varieties of capitalism frameworkвҖ”which places the issue of coordination at
24	the center of the distinction between capitalist typesвҖ”while paying virtually
25	no attention to cross-national variation in antitrust rules. This article develops
26	an original theoretical framework to conceptualize the relationship between
27	competition law and the organization of capitalism. We go beyond the usual
28	binaries (coordinated vs. liberal market economies, вҖңrestrictiveвҖқ vs. вҖңper-
29	missiveвҖқ antitrust regimes) to disentangle two dimensions of the law that
30	fundamentally shape patterns of coordination and competition both across
31	regulatory jurisdictions and over time. Applying our framework to analyze the
32	evolution of American and European competition law, we show how a
33	comparative coordination rights framework can be used to conceptualize key
34	institutional changes within contemporary capitalist systems.
```
