61	Introduction
62	Nearly a century and a half after the Sherman Antitrust Act of 1890, the ascent
63	of mega-companies such as Amazon and Apple has put вҖңmonopoly powerвҖқ
64	and antitrust back in the headlines. Legal scholars and economists have begun
65	to analyze recent developments in competition policy, but with the notable
66	exception of a few EU scholars, students of the comparative political economy
67	of the rich democracies have not had much at all to say about antitrust law and
68	enforcement.1 This is peculiar, because competition law is a key constitutive
69	institution in capitalist markets, establishing the rules for where interп¬Ғrm
70	coordination is allowed and where competition is required.2 Yet even the
71	dominant varieties of capitalism literature вҖ“ which famously distinguishes
72	political economies according to the institutional features that support two
73	distinct models of coordination вҖ“ is strikingly silent when it comes to the role
74	of competition law and policy in structuring markets.
75	    This gap is consequential because both the American and European po-
76	litical economies have undergone massive structural changes over the past
77	four decades that are linked to competition law changes, but have п¬Ӯown under
78	the radar of dominant theories of CPE. Speciп¬Ғcally, the US has diverged from
79	fellow liberal market economies, migrating from a classically liberal model to
80	become highly oligopolistic in a trend that tracks signiп¬Ғcant developments in
81	American antitrust theory and jurisprudence that the CPE literature has largely
82	ignored (Philippon, 2019). Meanwhile, EuropeвҖҷs coordinated market econ-
83	omies have experienced a mix of changes that are also connected to com-
84	petition law developments. Some of these, such as the liberalization of
85	regulated industries and the greenlighting of transnational mergers, have
86	brought European economies closer to the neoliberal model of capitalism
87	(Buch-Hansen & Wigger, 2010). Other developments, however, such as legal
88	protections for interп¬Ғrm and worker coordination, and an increasingly pro-
89	active approach to economic dominance, are more consonant with coordi-
90	nated market economies (Foster & Thelen, 2023).
91	    In this article, we propose a general framework for analyzing changes in
92	competition law and their impact, both across countries and within countries
93	over time, that helps explain these developments. Building on the legal scholar
94	Sanjukta PaulвҖҷs (2020) seminal reconceptualization of US antitrust law, we
95	move beyond the usual binaries (liberal vs. coordinated market economies,
96	вҖңrestrictiveвҖқ vs. вҖңpermissiveвҖқ antitrust regimes) to disentangle change along
97	two distinct dimensions: in the rules governing horizontal coordination
98	
99	
100	--- PAGE BREAK ---
101	
102	Foster and Thelen                                                        1201
103	
104	(between non-dominant economic actors) and those governing hierarchical
105	coordination (controls imposed by dominant players). Based on these dis-
106	tinctions, we identify four ideal-typical competition models that reп¬Ӯect dif-
107	ferent combinations of coordination rights: cartelistic competition,
108	oligopolistic competition, armвҖҷs length competition, and cooperative com-
109	petition. Furthermore, we show how this framework can be used to understand
110	important recent and ongoing trajectories of change in the organization of
111	capitalism.
112	    We apply our framework to developments over the postwar period in the
113	US and Europe to show how it yields insights of interest both to VOC scholars
114	and to EU scholarsвҖ”highlighting important blind spots in the VOC literature
115	while also resolving longstanding debates in the EU literature. Speciп¬Ғcally, we
116	show how changes in the American competition regime since the 1970s and
117	1980s have moved the US away from the armвҖҷs length relationships and п¬Ӯuid
118	labor markets traditionally associated with the вҖңliberalвҖқ model and toward
119	highly concentrated oligopolistic product markets and signiп¬Ғcant labor market
120	rigidities. Turning to Europe, we revisit вҖ“ and revise вҖ“ dominant accounts that
121	emphasize convergence on the US by drawing attention to key dimensions
122	along which competition rules have diverged sharply from American
123	developments.
124	    The paper is organized as follows. We begin our analysis by noting the
125	striking absence in the comparative political economy literature of substantial
126	discussion of competition law and its role in shaping the organization of
127	capitalism cross nationally and over time. We contrast this with the attention
128	paid to these issues by economists and legal scholars who attribute growing
129	oligopoly power in American capitalism to changes in antitrust policy and
130	enforcement since the 1970s. We turn then to unresolved debates among EU
131	scholars about the extent to which European competition rules have embraced
132	American practices in ways that undermine the cooperative arrangements that
133	have long anchored EuropeвҖҷs alternative model of CMEs.
134	    We argue that contemporary debates relating both to the US and to Europe
135	fail to distinguish two distinct dimensions of competition rules: those gov-
136	erning horizontal coordination between small and medium sized companies
137	(SMEs) that individually lack market power, and those governing hierarchical
138	controls by dominant players. We show that different combinations of co-
139	ordination rights produce four ideal-typical models of competition, and we
140	apply our framework to conceptualize the trajectories of competition law
141	change in Europe and the United States. Disentangling the two distinct di-
142	mensions of coordination allows us to document important continuities in the
143	US, alongside some convergence by the EU with respect to horizontal co-
144	ordination, but also вҖ“ more importantly вҖ“ to highlight the ways in which the
145	EU and US have diverged in their approaches to hierarchical coordination. A
146	п¬Ғnal section highlights the contribution of the framework we propose to the
147	
148	
149	--- PAGE BREAK ---
150	
151	1202                                         Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
152	
153	literature on the comparative political economy of the rich democracies and
154	beyond.
155	
156	Theories: Varieties of Competition Law in Political
157	Economy and Legal Scholarship
158	The dominant вҖңvarieties of capitalismвҖқ framework famously distinguishes
159	political economies according to the institutional features that support two
160	distinct models of coordination. Liberal market economies, or LMEs, are
161	those in which вҖңп¬Ғrms coordinate their activities primarily via hierarchies and
162	competitive market arrangements,вҖқ and in which coordination outside of the
163	п¬Ғrm primarily occurs via вҖңarmвҖҷs-length exchange of goods or services in a
164	context of competition and formal contractingвҖқ (Hall & Soskice, 2001, p. 8).
165	Coordinated market economies, or CMEs, by contrast, are characterized by
166	heavier reliance on non-market institutions, and where п¬Ғrms are often em-
167	bedded in arrangements that involve вҖңmore extensive relational or incomplete
168	contracting, network monitoring based on the exchange of private information
169	inside networks, and more reliance on collaborative, as opposed to com-
170	petitive, relationships to build the competencies of the п¬ҒrmвҖқ (Ibid).
171	    Given the centrality within the VOC framework of different modes of
172	coordinationвҖ”whether through corporate hierarchy and competitive market
173	arrangements as in LMEs or through various nonmarket institutions as in
174	CMESвҖ”it is surprising that competition policy has not been a major focus of
175	comparative capitalism scholarship. Historical treatments of capitalist de-
176	velopment underscored the central importance of competition law in con-
177	ditioning national market structures and corporate organization in the late 19th
178	and early 20th centuries (Berk, 2009; Chandler & Hikino, 2009; Djelic, 2001;
179	Fligstein, 1993; Keller, 1981; Lamoreaux, 1988; Thelen, 2020, 2024). Yet the
180	relationship between contemporary capitalist organization and competition
181	law has not been a major theoretical or empirical focus within the VOC
182	literature. The introduction to Hall and SoskiceвҖҷs seminal volume mentions
183	US antitrust policy in passing (2001: 31), and other chapters address the role
184	of the law in structuring production regimes (Casper, 2001; Teubner, 2001),
185	but competition law is not central to the framework and has been neglected by
186	subsequent VOC scholars.
187	    Critics of varieties of capitalism have paid somewhat more attention to the
188	role of competition law. For example, studies emphasizing the common
189	neoliberal trajectory of capitalist systems have sometimes portrayed com-
190	petition law as a homogenizing force that reinforces processes of economic
191	liberalization, п¬Ғnancialization, and marketization that undermine producer
192	group coordination and organized capitalism. In particular, the intensiп¬Ғcation
193	of competition law at the EU level and its enforcement by the European
194	Commission is often seen as contributing to the disorganization of capitalism
195	
196	
197	--- PAGE BREAK ---
198	
199	Foster and Thelen                                                         1203
200	
201	and a gradual convergence on a common liberal model (Buch-Hansen &
202	Wigger, 2010; HoМҲpner & SchaМҲfer, 2012; Wigger & NoМҲlke, 2007). Such
203	research has been important for drawing our attention to the ways American
204	inп¬Ӯuence in the post WWII period shaped early developments in Europe, and
205	how the more recent adoption of some elements of вҖҳlaw and economicsвҖҷ
206	theories (including elements of the so-called вҖңChicago schoolвҖқ of law and
207	economics) has affected some areas of policy and enforcement (Bartalevich,
208	2016; Buch-Hansen & Wigger, 2010). However, in emphasizing liberaliza-
209	tion, critics have often over simpliп¬Ғed the effects of competition law on
210	EuropeвҖҷs coordinated market economies, missing important elements of
211	continuity in European competition policy as well as new divergences that
212	have intensiп¬Ғed the enforcement of rules regulating the exploitation of
213	economic power. Moreover, studies that conп¬Ӯate вҖңliberalizationвҖқ with
214	вҖңAmericanizationвҖқ often miss the extent to which the US is no longer itself
215	particularly вҖңliberal.вҖқ
216	    In short, the current state of the debate вҖ“ mostly absent from VOC
217	scholarship and viewed primarily as a tool of liberalization among VOCвҖҷs
218	critics вҖ“ has led CPE scholars to overlook a number of important develop-
219	ments in the political economies of the advanced industrial countries on both
220	sides of the Atlantic вҖ“ developments that have been more central to dis-
221	cussions within the disciplines of economics and legal studies. In the United
222	States there is growing evidence that the collapse of antitrust enforcement
223	following the adoption of вҖҳChicago SchoolвҖҷ ideas in court jurisprudence and
224	agency policy (R. A. Posner, 1979) has contributed to growing oligopolistic
225	power and caused the American economy to drift away from the classic LME
226	model. The U.S. economy is increasingly characterized not by competitive
227	and contestable markets and armвҖҷs-length contracting, but rather by oli-
228	gopolistic market structures, where a handful of entrenched п¬Ғrms predominate
229	(Azar et al., 2022; Grullon et al., 2019; Philippon, 2019). Proп¬Ғt rates have
230	soared to historic highs, particularly in the most concentrated sectors and
231	among п¬Ғrms with high numbers of patents, suggesting the presence of market
232	power (Grullon et al., 2019; GutieМҒrrez & Philippon, 2018). As pure proп¬Ғts
233	have increased, labor income shares have fallen, particularly in the most
234	concentrated industries (Barkai, 2020).
235	    In addition, labor markets have long since ceased to be characterized by the
236	kind of п¬Ӯuidity that is a core deп¬Ғning feature of the classic liberal model;
237	indeed, labor market mobility in the US is at the lowest rate since the Census
238	Bureau began keeping track.3 Increasing industrial concentration across a
239	wide range of sectors means that declining regions are often dominated by a
240	few monopsonistic employers (Azar et al., 2022; Naidu et al., 2018; E. A.
241	Posner, 2021; Yeh et al., 2022). Workers are increasingly stuck in place by the
242	lack of opportunity in the countryвҖҷs most dynamic regions (Autor et al., 2020;
243	Haп¬Ғz, 2022), declining employer-based beneп¬Ғts (Madrian, 1994), rising
244	
245	
246	--- PAGE BREAK ---
247	
248	1204                                         Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
249	
250	housing costs (Ansell & Cansunar, 2021; Coate & Mangum, 2021), and
251	employersвҖҷ growing use of non-compete and no-poach agreements in em-
252	ployment contracts (Krueger & Ashenfelter, 2022; E. A. Posner, 2020;
253	Staszak, 2020).
254	   Meanwhile, European political economies have developed along a dif-
255	ferent trajectory. The trend toward greater employer market power observed in
256	the United States is not observed in the United Kingdom, where labor market
257	concentration has slightly declined over the last quarter century (CMA, 2024).
258	A growing body of evidence also suggests that the secular trend toward
259	increased concentration in product markets is less pronounced in many
260	European countries. A number of close studies have concluded that economic
261	concentration levels and markups in Europe have been either stable or
262	characterized by modest decreases following the integration of the European
263	single market (Cavalleri et al., 2019; DoМҲttling et al., 2017; GutieМҒrrez &
264	Philippon, 2018; Kalemli-Ozcan et al., 2024).4 Other studies, using differ-
265	ent data, have identiп¬Ғed some secular trends similar to the US, toward lower
266	labor shares (Autor et al., 2020; Piketty, 2014), increases in industrial con-
267	centration levels (Bajgar et al., 2023; Koltay et al., 2023), and a rise in
268	consumer markups (De Loecker & Eeckhout, 2018) in many European
269	countries. However, even in these studies, the observed changes are usually
270	more modest in magnitude and starting from lower baselines than in the
271	United States (De Loecker & Eeckhout, 2018; Koltay et al., 2023).
272	   While EuropeвҖҷs coordinated market economies have no doubt faced new
273	pressures stemming from globalization and technological change, they
274	nevertheless also continue to be characterized by much higher levels of
275	nonmarket coordination. European trade unions have weakened but they still
276	retain higher coverage rates than in the United States, particularly in the
277	private sector (Darvas et al., 2023). European employer and trade associations
278	have proven even more resilient, with membership levels remaining stable in
279	most countries since the 1970вҖҷs even as their role in collective goods provision
280	and advocacy has continually evolved (Brandl & Lehr, 2019). Compared to
281	the United States, EU business associations are denser and less fragmented
282	(Spillman, 2012; Traxler, 2004) and more actively involved in coordinating
283	research, standard setting, skills development, and collective bargaining
284	(Gooberman & Hauptmeier, 2022).5 Comparative business demography
285	studies clearly indicate that, across most major sectors of the economy, small
286	and medium sized enterprises continue to play a more prominent role in
287	European economies (Del Sorbo et al., 2018).
288	   Existing frameworks have a difп¬Ғcult time accounting for both the shift
289	toward oligopoly in the US and the mix of continuity and change observed in
290	Europe. The broad distinction between вҖңliberalвҖқ and вҖңcoordinatedвҖқ capitalism
291	used in VOC theory is not supple enough to capture these developments and
292	assess their implications. Economic liberalization and convergence
293	
294	
295	--- PAGE BREAK ---
296	
297	Foster and Thelen                                                              1205
298	
299	frameworks proposed by VOC critics better account for some elements of
300	change in the EU, particularly the weakening of labor unions and the
301	transnational consolidation of industry. However, they are less effective at
302	explaining how and why the EU has continued to diverge from the US on
303	other dimensions. And they have very little to say about the oligopolistic
304	tendencies found in the American economy, or indeed about the dramatic
305	differences in the treatment of dominant п¬Ғrms on the two sides of the Atlantic.
306	   One of the core reasons for this oversight is the tendency of nearly all
307	observers to view competition law in a unidimensional way. Both VOC
308	theorists and VOCвҖҷs critics conп¬Ӯate the different targets of competition lawвҖ”
309	whether aimed at horizontal coordination or hierarchical restraints and
310	mergers. So, any move by a jurisdiction on one dimension (whether pro-
311	cedural or substantive; whether relating to horizontal or hierarchical coor-
312	dination; and whether the rules relate to cartels or abuse of dominance) is
313	coded as signaling more or less stringent competition rules. As closely
314	connected as these different dimensions are on some issues and at some times,
315	nothing in the broader historical record suggests that they necessarily go
316	together. In fact, variation on both dimensions is important for tracking key
317	changes in the evolution of capitalist organizations over time.
318	
319	
320	A Comparative Coordination Rights Framework
321	Building on the work of Sanjukta Paul (2020) we propose a new framework
322	for analyzing such changes. Among legal scholars of antitrust, Paul is dis-
323	tinctive in that she conceives of competition regimes not in terms of com-
324	petition law stringency, but rather in terms of coordination rights. In doing so,
325	she effectively п¬Ӯips the script of antitrust as it is typically presented: instead of
326	thinking about competition law as concerned primarily with the regulation of
327	anti-competitive practices (strict or permissive), she pushes us instead to
328	consider how competition law allows, permits or even encourages certain
329	kinds of coordination between п¬Ғrms and other producer groups, while pre-
330	venting other kinds. As she puts it: вҖңantitrust lawвҖҷs core function is to allocate
331	coordination rights to some economic actors and deny them to othersвҖқ (382).
332	Her key insight is that both coordination and competition are required in
333	capitalism, but that the structure of coordination and competitionвҖ”and in
334	particular who gets to coordinate and who is forced to competeвҖ”will differ
335	depending on the competition regime.
336	    Although Paul did not conceive hers as a framework for comparative
337	analysis, it can be fruitfully deployed to re-conceptualize cross-national dif-
338	ferences as well as trajectories of change over time in competition regimes and
339	in the organization of capitalism writ large. Adapting her framework, we
340	distinguish between two main dimensions of economic coordination in capi-
341	talist economies, and use these to conceptualize four competition models that
342	
343	
344	--- PAGE BREAK ---
345	
346	1206                                          Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
347	
348	reп¬Ӯect different combinations of coordination rights.6 The п¬Ғrst dimension is
349	horizontal coordination beyond the boundaries of the п¬Ғrm. This refers to re-
350	strictive agreements, practices, or mergers between п¬Ғrms that lack market power
351	and that are operating in either the same market or adjacent markets. Horizontal
352	coordination can thus include restrictive practices between direct competitors
353	that sell the same or similar products or services. It can also include agreements
354	between suppliers and distributors as long as none of the involved п¬Ғrms are
355	economically dominant. In both cases, competition regimes vary in the extent to
356	which they permit or forbid such arrangements.
357	    This is distinct from a second dimension, which we term hierarchical
358	coordination beyond the boundaries of the п¬Ғrm. This encompasses a range of
359	unilateral practices, restrictive agreements or mergers involving large com-
360	panies that have signiп¬Ғcant market shares, with the most extreme example
361	being the establishment of a monopoly or the erection of a permanent barrier
362	to entry. Here again, competition regimes vary from more permissive
363	(вҖңforbearingвҖқ) to more restrictive (limiting or restricting such coordination
364	through state regulation).7
365	    The emphasis on horizontal or hierarchical coordination rights can and
366	does differ both across regulatory jurisdictions and historically over time. We
367	propose that these differences help shape the forms of producer group co-
368	ordination that have long been central to theories of advanced capitalism.
369	Based on these distinctions, we identify four ideal-typical competition models
370	that reп¬Ӯect different combinations of horizontal and hierarchical coordination
371	rights: cartelistic competition, oligopolistic competition, armвҖҷs length com-
372	petition and cooperative competition. The horizontal dimension of Table 1
373	refers to the extent to which non-dominant п¬Ғrms have the right to use
374	nonmarket forms of coordination in their relationships with competitors,
375	i.e., п¬Ғrms selling the same or similar products or services, as well as their
376	suppliers and distributors. On the left side of the table, horizontal coordination
377	rights are extensive, while on the right side, they are narrow, and companies
378	must interact with other п¬Ғrms primarily through armвҖҷs-length, market-based
379	contracts. The hierarchical dimension refers to the extent to which dominant
380	companies have the right to use exclusionary agreements or unilateral
381	practices to structure the marketplace in their interests. On the permissive
382	(вҖңforbearingвҖқ) side of the continuum, competition regimes enable hierarchical
383	control by dominant п¬Ғrms, while on the other end, hierarchical coordination is
384	subject to state regulation that limits or redirects it.
385	   These distinctions yield the following four ideal types:
386	
387	Cartelistic competition model. The regulatory regime in the lower left quadrant
388	of Table 1 permits п¬Ғrms to coordinate on both the horizontal and hierarchical
389	dimensions without being subject to state interference. Since п¬Ғrms are
390	
391	
392	--- PAGE BREAK ---
393	
394	Foster and Thelen                                                         1207
395	
396	Table 1. Coordination Rules and Competition Models.
397	
398	
399	
400	
401	interested in stabilizing proп¬Ғt expectations by creating вҖҳmarket structuresвҖҷ
402	(Beckert, 2009), this regulatory regime tends to produce cartelized markets,
403	where competition is partially restricted through private agreements between
404	producers in the same industry (SchroМҲter, 2013). Cartelistic market structures
405	have historically facilitated economic stability and growth in some countries
406	(Tilly, 1982; Webb, 1980), while simultaneously enabling horizontal non-
407	market coordination institutions to develop (Thelen, 2020). But in the absence
408	of regulation, the largest cartel members are free to leverage their economic
409	power over other actors within the economy. This ideal-type is closest to
410	Japan, Germany, and many other European countries prior to World II, when
411	states not only permitted but often encouraged interп¬Ғrm coordination and most
412	industries were highly cartelized.
413	
414	Oligopolistic Competition Model. The lower right quadrant of Table 1 indicates a
415	regulatory regime where horizontal coordination between smaller companies
416	is strictly prohibited, but dominant companies are given wide leeway to create
417	
418	
419	--- PAGE BREAK ---
420	
421	1208                                        Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
422	
423	barriers to entry and impose hierarchical controls on their suppliers and
424	distributors. In contrast to the cartelized competition model, where the market
425	is stabilized through horizontal agreements while maintaining п¬Ғrm inde-
426	pendence, hierarchical coordination systems rely primarily on corporate hi-
427	erarchy and economic concentration to stabilize market volatility (Lamoreaux,
428	1988; Sklar, 1988). This encourages the development of oligopolistic markets,
429	characterized by a small number of large producers or sellers. Like cartelized
430	competition, oligopolistic competition can reduce transaction costs and fa-
431	cilitate economic stability, scale economies, and long-term investment
432	(Chandler & Hikino, 2009; Williamson, 1985). However, oligopolistic
433	structures also enable economic exploitation, rent-seeking, and the en-
434	trenchment of incumbent players. This ideal-type is closest to the United
435	States at the beginning of the 20th century when horizontal coordination
436	between smaller п¬Ғrms and workers was limited by antitrust law while ex-
437	clusionary practices by large companies were largely permitted as вҖҳreason-
438	ableвҖҷ restraints of trade.
439	
440	Cooperative Competition Model. The regulatory regime in the top left quadrant
441	allows competing п¬Ғrms to extensively cooperate while limiting the power of
442	dominant companies to impose hierarchical restraints on other п¬Ғrms. Like
443	cartelistic competition, п¬Ғrms and workers are permitted to establish robust
444	trade associations and unions, which can be used to upgrade competition, and
445	push the coordinates of competition away from price and toward quality. But
446	unlike cartelistic competition, the state regulates both exclusionary private
447	market structures as well as hierarchical controls by dominant companies
448	(Ergen & Kohl, 2019). This ideal-type can facilitate the development of
449	coordinated market structures, where competing п¬Ғrms engage in substantial
450	nonmarket coordination to facilitate collective goods, reduce problems of free
451	riding, and pool resources so that smaller п¬Ғrms can better compete against
452	bigger ones (Crouch, 1993). Cooperative competition is best exempliп¬Ғed by
453	many northern European countries during the second half of the 20th century,
454	when companies were permitted to pursue extensive forms of horizontal
455	coordination, but where the exploitation of economic power by large com-
456	panies was strictly regulated by the state or held in check by countervailing
457	corporatist institutions.
458	
459	ArmвҖҷs-Length Competition Model. A regulatory regime characterized by strict
460	rules limiting both horizontal and hierarchical coordination beyond the
461	boundary of the п¬Ғrm will instantiate an armвҖҷs length market regime (upper
462	right quadrant). Chandlerian hierarchies are found within individual п¬Ғrms, but
463	relationships beyond the boundaries of the п¬Ғrm, including with competitors,
464	suppliers, distributors, creditors, and employees, are organized through formal
465	contracts and competitive markets (Hall & Soskice, 2001, pp. 33вҖ“36).
466	
467	
468	--- PAGE BREAK ---
469	
470	Foster and Thelen                                                         1209
471	
472	Crucially, the state not only enforces price competition between equally
473	matched competitors, but also aggressively polices the exploitation of eco-
474	nomic power by large corporations. Companies with market power not only
475	are prevented from establishing barriers to entry, but are sometimes also
476	required to actively facilitate entry. This ideal-type is best exempliп¬Ғed by the
477	United States from the 1940вҖҷs until the 1970вҖҷs, when both horizontal co-
478	operation and the exclusionary practices of dominant companies were limited
479	by regulators.
480	
481	Mapping Trajectories of Change
482	Opening up the analytic space to disentangle the complex (multi- not uni-
483	dimensional) evolution of competition rules governing different types of
484	market coordination allows us to move beyond the current terms of debate in
485	both the VOC and EU literature. It forces us to think harder about how
486	different competition regimes affect who exactly is allowed to coordinate with
487	whom, and to do what, and about how differences in the answers to these
488	questions drive variation in the trajectories of change in competition law on
489	both sides of the Atlantic. By distinguishing movement related to horizontal
490	versus hierarchical coordination we can identify with more precision the
491	arenas in which American antitrust law continues to enforce armвҖҷs-length
492	relations, and those where the law permits (or even encourages) hierarchical
493	coordination. Similarly, we can also better evaluate where EU competition law
494	and practice have converged with the United States, where they remain
495	distinct (in degree if not in kind), and where they have diverged in new ways.
496	   Figure 1 captures in a stylized way the overall trajectory of change in the
497	rules governing market competition in the European Union and the United
498	States since the 1970вҖҷs.8 On the horizontal dimension, the US has maintained
499	and even sharpened its traditionally strict policies toward almost all forms of
500	interп¬Ғrm (horizontal) coordination. Meanwhile, the EU has indeed moved in
501	the direction of an armвҖҷs length competition model, by developing stricter
502	rules for both cartels and many other forms of horizontal coordination
503	(Billows et al., 2021; Buch-Hansen & Wigger, 2010; Philippon, 2019).
504	Nonetheless, especially compared to the US, EU competition law continues to
505	allow substantial horizontal nonmarket coordination in certain areas, par-
506	ticularly for SMEs, workers, and interп¬Ғrm cooperation agreements aimed at
507	generating productive efп¬Ғciencies вҖ“ thus retaining signiп¬Ғcant elements that are
508	historically characteristic of CMEs (Foster & Thelen, 2023).
509	   On the hierarchical dimension, however, the US and EU have sharply
510	diverged, as the US has largely abandoned anti-monopoly, vertical restraint,
511	and non-horizontal merger enforcement and the EU has intensiп¬Ғed en-
512	forcement in each of these areas, particularly for dominant companies that
513	control key infrastructures such as online platforms. In the EU, strengthening
514	
515	
516	--- PAGE BREAK ---
517	
518	1210                                        Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
519	
520	
521	
522	
523	Figure 1. Trajectories of Change in Postwar US and EU Competition Regimes.
524	Source: AuthorsвҖҷ characterization of competition law change, 1970вҖ“2020.
525	
526	
527	enforcement against abuse of dominance has helped protect nonmarket co-
528	ordination by smaller п¬Ғrms from the exploitative and exclusionary practices of
529	larger companies. Meanwhile, the move in the US toward hierarchical for-
530	bearance has pushed antitrust policy away from an armвҖҷs-length competition
531	model characterized by extensive anti-dominance and horizontal enforcement
532	to maintain market contestability, and toward an oligopolistic model where
533	horizontal coordination continues to be aggressively policed, but large
534	companies are given wide leeway to structure the marketplace through hi-
535	erarchical coordination.
536	   In the next two sections, we analyze how and why the trajectories of change
537	have proceeded in this way, examining developments in policy and law, as
538	well as patterns of enforcement in order to draw a connection between
539	competition rules and the political economy. Our analysis builds on previous
540	work by economists and legal scholars who have focused closely on changes
541	to hierarchical rules in the US, alongside that by EU scholars who have
542	tracked changes to horizontal rules in Europe and the effect on CMEs (e.g.
543	Billows et al., 2021; Wigger & NoМҲlke, 2007). However, we expand and reп¬Ғne
544	both streams of analysis by distinguishing changes across both the horizontal
545	and hierarchical dimensions, and by pointing out how the changes we
546	document track the divergent trajectories of the American and European
547	
548	
549	--- PAGE BREAK ---
550	
551	Foster and Thelen                                                          1211
552	
553	economies discussed above. While space precludes a full empirical test of the
554	framework, these two case studies serve to illustrate the utility of our
555	comparative coordination rights framework for better conceptualizing the co-
556	evolution of regulatory institutions, producer group organization, and market
557	structures within contemporary capitalist systems.9
558	
559	
560	
561	Coordination Rights and the Evolution of the American
562	Political Economy
563	Historically, the US embraced what in comparative perspective was in fact a
564	wholly unique approach to competition policy and jurisprudence (or what in
565	American parlance is called antitrust). In the closing years of the 19th century,
566	the political economies in both the US and Europe were upended by п¬Ғnancial
567	crises and deep economic depressions. Overcapacity across key markets set in
568	motion vicious cutthroat competition among п¬Ғrms, provoking considerable
569	market turmoil and industrial strife. American and European п¬Ғrms alike
570	sought to stabilize markets by banding together into arrangements to defend
571	against destructive competition and to restore industrial peace. European
572	governments encouraged and often actively reinforced coordination among
573	п¬Ғrms within industries, and judicial forbearance toward these forms of co-
574	operation supported the п¬Ӯourishing of the dense associational landscape of
575	producer group politics (trade associations, employer organizations, and labor
576	unions) that we now associate with coordinated capitalism.
577	    In the United States, by contrast, the passage of the Sherman Act, and the
578	Supreme CourtвҖҷs interpretation of that law, created strong prohibitions against
579	what Paul calls вҖңhorizontalвҖқ coordination вҖ“ i.e., coordination among non-
580	dominant actors beyond the boundaries of the п¬Ғrm, including but not limited
581	to labor (Paul, 2020, p. 383).10 Large producers turned to alternative strategies
582	to stabilize markets through corporate consolidation or the use of vertical
583	restraints to restrict competition from above. These п¬Ғrms avoided antitrust
584	prosecution by internalizing coordination functions вҖ“ merging with their
585	former rivals and swallowing up smaller competitors. This was the context
586	that inspired the вҖҳgreat merger movementвҖҷ in the US at the turn of the century
587	that resulted in a dramatic increase in industrial concentration and led to
588	corporate hierarchy and arms-length exchange becoming the dominant modes
589	of coordination (Lamoreaux, 1988; Sklar, 1988).11 The strong binary between
590	вҖңpureвҖқ competition and corporate hierarchy that the American judiciary
591	enforced and policed left a deep imprint on the American political economy
592	(Berk, 1996). It also helped instantiate an oligopolistic competition model,
593	where many industries were dominated by a small number of п¬Ғrms and large
594	corporations had broad economic power to structure the marketplace in their
595	interests (Chandler & Hikino, 2009; Sklar, 1988).
596	
597	
598	--- PAGE BREAK ---
599	
600	1212                                        Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
601	
602	    American competition policy continued to combine strict enforcement
603	against horizontal combinations with more lenient (вҖңrule of reasonвҖқ) treatment
604	of hierarchical coordination through the interwar period.12 But the Great
605	Depression and the accompanying dramatic increase in industrial concen-
606	tration inspired a deep rethinking of American competition policy. A series of
607	government studies assessing the impact of economic concentration on the
608	economy in the late 1930вҖҷs delivered a scathing indictment of the behavior of
609	the countryвҖҷs dominant producers, contributing to an abrupt reversal on the
610	American approach to hierarchical coordination (Peinert, 2023). Allying with
611	populist Democrats in Congress, President Roosevelt expanded the Depart-
612	ment of JusticeвҖҷs (DOJ) Division of Antitrust budget п¬Ғve-fold, growing its
613	staff from 58 lawyers to over 300 (Brinkley, 1996, p. 111). The new head of
614	the Antitrust Division, Thurman Arnold, launched a period of reinvigorated
615	enforcement, taking on some of the biggest names in American industry,
616	including Paramount Studios, the Pullman Company, the Big Three car
617	companies, and major steel and aluminum producers, during what in retro-
618	spect is now considered the вҖңGolden AgeвҖқ of American antitrust enforcement.
619	    During his п¬Ғve-year tenure, Arnold pursued many major anti-monopoly
620	cases, several of which resulted in the structural reorganization of dominant
621	companies.13 At the same time, he intensiп¬Ғed horizontal enforcement,
622	launching major cases against dairy producers, construction companies, oil
623	reп¬Ғneries, and newspapers, among other industries. In many of these cases, the
624	targets were large companies, but in others they were small п¬Ғrms or labor
625	unions (Waller, 2004, pp. 600вҖ“603). Altogether, Arnold prosecuted more
626	cases during his short (1938вҖ“43) tenure as head of the DOJвҖҷs Antitrust Di-
627	vision than the previous п¬Ғfty years of antitrust combined (Waller, 2004,
628	p. 583). The combination of a strict approach to both hierarchical and hor-
629	izontal coordination pushed the American antitrust system squarely toward
630	the top right вҖҳarmвҖҷs length competition modelвҖҷ in Table 1.
631	    Following World War II, the armвҖҷs length model was further institution-
632	alized. The Celler-Kefauver Act in 1950 made it much easier for regulators to
633	block mergers and acquisitions. From 1955вҖ“1969, the government challenged
634	167 combinations through litigation (R. A. Posner, 1970, p. 407). Although
635	these enforcement actions did not always prevent aggregate consolidation via
636	conglomerate mergers, they did slow down and occasionally reverse con-
637	centration trends within many industries. Careful economic studies from the
638	period suggest that agency merger rules and litigation deterred or blocked
639	numerous horizontal and vertical mergers, leading to a decline in the con-
640	centration rates of many sectors.14
641	    In addition to placing a new democratic check on mergers, the government
642	also continued to limit hierarchical coordination beyond the boundaries of the
643	п¬Ғrm through the active enforcement of anti-monopoly and vertical restraint
644	rules. During the 1950вҖҷs and 1960вҖҷs, the DOJ and FTC successfully
645	
646	
647	--- PAGE BREAK ---
648	
649	Foster and Thelen                                                         1213
650	
651	prosecuted dominant companies for monopoly violations in a variety of areas,
652	from the exclusionary use of intellectual property to localized price cuts
653	(Kovacic & Shapiro, 2000, pp. 9вҖ“12). Many of these prosecutions constrained
654	the autonomy of the corporate monoliths of the era including Eastman Kodak,
655	General Electric, Westinghouse, Procter & Gamble, and Bell Laboratories
656	(Kovaleff, 1980; Watkins, 1961). Sometimes the mere threat of prosecution
657	was sufп¬Ғcient to force powerful companies to show pricing restraint, as ar-
658	guably occurred in the petroleum sector in response to ongoing DOJ in-
659	vestigations of the seven largest oil and gas companies (McFarland & Colgan,
660	2023). To be sure, regulators also continued to place limits on horizontal
661	coordination by prosecuting Sherman Act cases against small п¬Ғrms. However,
662	to a greater degree than in prior or future eras, the enforcement emphasis was
663	on large companies.15 Furthermore, courts and regulators largely avoided
664	using antitrust to limit the coordination rights of workers, at least when
665	coordination occurred in the context of labor unions. The result was a political
666	economy that, while retaining many oligopolistic features, was also char-
667	acterized by contestable markets and countervailing power in most industries
668	(Galbraith, 1952).
669	
670	
671	
672	Hierarchical Forbearance and the Move toward Oligopoly
673	The era, however, was short-lived. Already in the 1960s, American antitrust
674	policy and practice had come under attack from both the left and right.
675	Liberals such as Ralph Nader complained of corporate capture, while con-
676	servatives blasted an overweening bureaucracy. By the late 1960s, economists
677	and legal scholars attached to the so-called вҖңChicago SchoolвҖқ of law and
678	economics had become vocally skeptical about the wisdom of the entire
679	postwar antitrust regime. Robert BorkвҖҷs inп¬Ӯuential book, The Antitrust
680	Paradox (published in 1978, but much longer in the works), had steadily
681	gained inп¬Ӯuence in legal circles throughout the late 1960s and 1970s. Federal
682	courts and regulators began to incrementally adjust existing precedents and
683	policies in ways that led to more permissive hierarchical coordination rules. In
684	several key cases at the end of the 1970вҖҷs, the Supreme Court reversed its own
685	precedents by establishing a new вҖҳconsumer welfareвҖҷ test as a requirement for
686	prosecuting hierarchical restrictions (Orbach, 2011). In the aftermath of these
687	decisions, most of the non-horizontal вҖҳper seвҖҷ rules that had been established
688	during the post-war period were reversed and replaced with the more per-
689	missive вҖҳrule of reasonвҖҷ (Khan, 2016; Kovacic & Shapiro, 2000). The change
690	in jurisprudence made it much more difп¬Ғcult for US regulators to enforce
691	hierarchical coordination rules, leading to a series of government losses in
692	major merger and anti-monopoly cases (Kovacic & Shapiro, 2000, p. 54).16
693	These changes gave large п¬Ғrms more autonomy to impose vertical restraints
694	
695	
696	--- PAGE BREAK ---
697	
698	1214                                          Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
699	
700	on their suppliers and distributors, erect barriers to entry, and pursue mergers
701	that consolidated their market positions.
702	    In 1981 Reagan elevated Chicago School thinking to government policy
703	with his appointments of Borkian acolyte William F. Baxter to head the DOJвҖҷs
704	Antitrust Division and fellow Chicago schooler James Miller III as FTC chair.
705	The new appointees presided over a sharp decline in all forms of antitrust
706	enforcement against dominant market actors, while simultaneously intensi-
707	fying horizontal enforcement, especially against smaller п¬Ғrms. Henceforth,
708	antitrust policy and enforcement would fully embrace the Chicago school
709	formula; the core criterion against which anti-competitive actions would be
710	judged was whether they explicitly harmed consumer welfare, deп¬Ғned largely
711	in terms of price (Khan, 2016). The shift toward a permissive approach to
712	hierarchical coordination has only intensiп¬Ғed since the early 2000вҖҷs, as the
713	Supreme Court has ruled in favor of hierarchical controls by dominant п¬Ғrms вҖ“
714	and against antitrust interventions вҖ“ in almost all of its major cases (Wu,
715	2018).
716	    Combined with the empowerment of economists relative to lawyers within
717	US regulatory agencies, these court-led changes in coordination rights have
718	contributed to a systematic collapse in anti-monopoly and hierarchical re-
719	straint enforcement (Berman, 2022; Ergen & Kohl, 2019). Figure 2 reports the
720	pattern of DOJ enforcement before and after the adoption of the consumer
721	welfare standard by courts in 1979. It shows that monopoly, vertical restraints,
722	and non-horizontal merger cases made up only 2% of enforcement actions in
723	the 1980вҖҷs and 1990вҖҷs, representing a dramatic decrease from the 1960вҖҷs and
724	1970вҖҷs when such actions represented one in п¬Ғve DOJ prosecutions. A similar
725	pattern can be seen in merger enforcement. While in the 1960вҖҷs and 1970вҖҷs, the
726	
727	
728	
729	
730	Figure 2. DOJ enforcement before and after the adoption of вҖҳChicago SchoolвҖҷ ideas.
731	Source: Gallo et al. 2000. Calculations by authors. Checkered (blue) areas indicate
732	enforcement actions limiting hierarchical coordination; striped pattern (red) areas
733	indicate enforcement actions limiting horizontal coordination.
734	
735	
736	--- PAGE BREAK ---
737	
738	Foster and Thelen                                                       1215
739	
740	DOJ blocked a steady mix of horizontal and vertical measures, after 1980,
741	merger challenges signiп¬Ғcantly declined. As can be seen in Figure 2, hori-
742	zontal merger challenges became rarer after 1980, shifting from a quarter of
743	total cases to just 8%. Meanwhile, non-horizontal merger enforcement almost
744	entirely disappeared.
745	    In response to this carte blanche, corporate America pursued an un-
746	precedented merger boom. According to one study, the number and value of
747	mergers and acquisitions dramatically increased in the years following the
748	adoption of new merger guidelines in 1984 (Pryor, 2001). From 1985вҖ“2000,
749	the annual volume of mergers and acquisitions tripled, with three-quarters of
750	combinations occurring between п¬Ғrms operating in the same industry (Ibid,
751	pp. 305вҖ“308). Over the same period, the average degree of industrial con-
752	centration in individual industries signiп¬Ғcantly increasedвҖ”the reverse of the
753	trend observed from 1960вҖ“1980, when hierarchical coordination rights were
754	more restricted by US antitrust law (Ibid).
755	    Even as U.S. regulators largely abandoned anti-monopoly and merger
756	enforcement, they intensiп¬Ғed enforcement of cartels and other forms of
757	horizontal coordination, successfully prosecuting hundreds of cases (Gallo
758	et al., 2000; Kovacic, 2003, pp. 415вҖ“420). Unlike the Golden Era of antitrust
759	when large corporations were the main focus, the vast majority of these
760	prosecutions were against small and medium-sized companies.17 Within many
761	industries, these prosecutions undermined the ability of domestic п¬Ғrms to
762	effectively compete against foreign п¬Ғrms in Japan and Europe that enjoyed
763	extensive horizontal coordination rights (Arslan, 2023). Workers and inde-
764	pendent contractors seeking to pool their bargaining power were also more
765	frequently targeted; these included, among others, groups of low-paid at-
766	torneys, piano teachers, ice skating instructors and church organists (Paul,
767	2020, pp. 391вҖ“392). Regulators not only prosecuted smaller п¬Ғrms, inde-
768	pendent contractors and workers more frequently, but they did so with
769	criminal sanctions. From 1980вҖ“1997, horizontal criminal cases constituted
770	85% of DOJ enforcement actions, compared to just 36% prior to 1980.
771	    In the United States, the combination of weak enforcement against hier-
772	archical coordination by dominant п¬Ғrms and intensiп¬Ғed prosecution of
773	horizontal collusion by smaller п¬Ғrms has allowed the US to drift toward the
774	oligopolistic competition model in the bottom right quadrant of Figure 1.
775	Scholars have identiп¬Ғed clear links between speciп¬Ғc changes to antitrust rules
776	regulating mergers, hierarchical restraints, and independent contractors and
777	the decline of worker power and increase in labor market rigidity described
778	earlier (Azar et al., 2022; Callaci, 2021a, 2021b; Haп¬Ғz, 2022; Naidu et al.,
779	2018). A growing number of economic studies have also provided evidence
780	that the decline in anti-monopoly enforcement likely contributed to the trend
781	toward oligopoly (GutieМҒrrez & Philippon, 2017, 2018) and that the largest
782	American п¬Ғrms have supported this shift through the political process
783	
784	
785	--- PAGE BREAK ---
786	
787	1216                                         Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
788	
789	(Lancieri et al., 2022). Indeed, a competition regime that combines strict rules
790	on horizontal coordination with permissive rules on hierarchical control
791	beyond the boundaries of the п¬Ғrm allows dominant п¬Ғrms to effectively have
792	their cake and eat it too. On the one hand, they are provided signiп¬Ғcant
793	autonomy to structure the marketplace in their own interests, through ex-
794	tensive vertical contracting and exclusionary behavior. At the same, they are
795	largely free from horizontally organized forms of countervailing power such
796	as organized SMEs or workers that might limit this autonomy or provide a
797	signiп¬Ғcant competitive challenge. Put differently, the largest п¬Ғrms in the
798	United States are able to exploit the asymmetry in who has coordination rights
799	in ways that lead to the predominance of hierarchical over horizontal forms of
800	coordination (Paul, 2020).
801	
802	Coordination Rights and the Evolution of the European
803	Political Economy
804	In the formative period of industrial capitalism, many European industries
805	were highly organized through cartels and other forms of business associa-
806	tions that relied heavily on both horizontal and hierarchical coordination
807	beyond the boundaries of the п¬Ғrm. This placed European market structures in
808	many countries clearly in the cartelistic competition model found in the
809	bottom left of Table 1. Virtually all western and northern European countries вҖ“
810	including not only Germany but also Austria, Belgium, Finland, the Neth-
811	erlands, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland, Spain, Italy, and even the United
812	Kingdom вҖ“ took a much more permissive stance toward horizontal coordi-
813	nation than the United States (SchroМҲter, 1996). Well into the postwar period,
814	cartel organizations remained a prominent feature of many capitalist econ-
815	omies on the continent (Gerber, 1998; Shanahan & Fellman, 2022).
816	   The creation of a supranational European competition law through the
817	Treaties of Paris and Rome presented a direct challenge to the cartelistic
818	competition model. American ofп¬Ғcials were heavily involved in designing the
819	competition provisions of the European Coal and Steel Community and the
820	U.S. experience with the Sherman Act provided a primary reference point for
821	legal developments within the European Economic Community (EEC). The
822	prohibition against anti-competitive agreements, enshrined in the Treaty of
823	Rome, would have a lasting effect on the European economy, contributing to
824	the decartelization of European industry and instantiating a more вҖҳarmвҖҷs
825	lengthвҖҷ competition model within many sectors (Djelic, 2001; Harding &
826	Joshua, 2010).
827	   Yet across all six of the founding EEC countries, trade associations and
828	labor unions had also expressed hesitation toward (in many cases outright
829	opposition to) the establishment of a вҖҳSherman ActвҖҷ in Europe, which they
830	saw as undermining their ability to pursue the cooperative arrangements and
831	
832	
833	--- PAGE BREAK ---
834	
835	Foster and Thelen                                                          1217
836	
837	long-term investments needed to rebuild the economy (Buch-Hansen &
838	Wigger, 2011; Gillingham, 2004; Milward, 1984). The supranational Euro-
839	pean system established under the Treaties of Paris and Rome accommodated
840	these concerns. The enactment of substantive rules that prohibited cartels
841	while providing broad exemptions for вҖҳbeneп¬ҒcialвҖҷ horizontal agreements
842	reп¬Ӯected a compromise between вҖңcross-national liberal modernizersвҖқ sup-
843	portive of the American cartel prohibition model and European business
844	associations and unions, which saw horizontal cooperation as essential for
845	rationalization, standardization, and specialization in European industry
846	(Djelic, 2001, pp. 232вҖ“235).
847	    So, while US inп¬Ӯuence clearly made a mark on EuropeвҖҷs postwar com-
848	petition regime, Europe would never go so far as the US in prohibiting all
849	forms of horizontal non-market coordination through rules that could be
850	enforced by both private and public parties in the courts.18 Indeed, EU
851	competition law preserved a signiп¬Ғcant space for coordination beyond the
852	boundaries of the п¬Ғrm. Thus, for example, the Treaty of RomeвҖҷs prohibition of
853	restrictive practices included a broad exception for any agreement вҖңwhich
854	contributes to improving the production or distribution of goods or to pro-
855	moting technical or economic progressвҖқ and did not run afoul of other general
856	principles (Article 101[85]). One of the early laws enacted by the new EEC
857	was Regulation 17/1962, which provided the Commission with the authority
858	to permit restrictive agreements that fell within certain categories. A few years
859	later, this power was expanded to include block exemptions, providing the
860	Commission with a quasi-legislative role to determine what kinds of
861	agreements were acceptable or unacceptable in the economy (Gerber, 1998,
862	p. 351).
863	    Over time, and in ongoing consultation with industry, these exemptions
864	came to include a wide range of horizontal inter-п¬Ғrm collaborations and joint
865	ventures seen as economically beneп¬Ғcial. This included cooperation in
866	research and development and standard setting, joint licensing, and restrictive
867	selling and purchasing contracts that involved two companies in different
868	countries (Hawk, 1972). The Commission also encouraged coordination and
869	technology transfer between small- and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) by
870	exempting companies with a level of economic activity below a certain
871	threshold, covering an estimated 90% of all companies (Buch-Hansen &
872	Wigger, 2011, p. 67). Indeed, the EEC competition authority actively sought
873	to promote cooperation between SMEs as a way to allow them to more
874	effectively compete against larger companies. By 1980, several
875	thousand cooperation agreements between SMEs had been approved by the
876	Commission (European Commission, 1980, p. 16). In the face of economic
877	shocks or industrial difп¬Ғculties, the EEC competition directorate even per-
878	mitted the establishment of вҖҳcrisis cartelsвҖҷ designed to address excess capacity
879	in an orderly manner (Fiebig, 1999).
880	
881	
882	--- PAGE BREAK ---
883	
884	1218                                         Comparative Political Studies 58(6)
885	
886	    At the same time, the EU also gradually strengthened hierarchical coor-
887	dination rules that placed limits on the power of large companies to eco-
888	nomically coerce less powerful ones: whether through trade associations,
889	restrictive agreements, unilateral practices, or mergers (BГјthe, 2007). In its
890	п¬Ғrst abuse of dominance investigation under Article 86 of the Treaty of Rome,
891	which involved the American Continental Can Company, at the time the
892	worldвҖҷs largest producer of metal cans, the Commission charged the company
893	with distorting the competitive process by creating barriers to entry and
894	restricting the liberty of its smaller European distributors. In developing the
895	case, the Commission drew directly from the вҖҳordoliberalвҖҷ school of com-
896	petition that had crystallized in Germany in the post war period and which
897	called for a strong regulatory framework that limited the exploitation of
898	private economic power (Gerber, 1994). The CJEU upheld the CommissionвҖҷs
899	decision, establishing the precedent that abuse of dominance covered not only
900	exclusionary practices that directly harmed consumers but also those that
901	caused indirect harm through вҖңtheir impact on an effective competition
902	structureвҖқ (Schweitzer, 2008). Subsequent cases and jurisprudence reinforced
903	the ordoliberal paradigm, establishing a вҖҳspecial obligationвҖҷ on large com-
904	panies not to exploit their economic power, and prohibiting a range of вҖҳunfairвҖҷ
905	methods of competition. From 1971вҖ“1992, the Commission п¬Ғnalized
906	25 abuse of dominance decisions, with many decisions involving large
907	penalties and behavioral remedies (Carree et al., 2010, p. 110).
908	    The combination of moderate rules for horizontal coordination with in-
909	creasingly strict rules on hierarchical coordination placed the EU framework
910	squarely in the вҖңcooperative competition modelвҖқ found in the top left box of
911	our typology. Signiп¬Ғcant exemptions for horizontal coordination created
912	important space for п¬Ғrms to continue to pursue a range of cooperative
913	strategies that allowed them to more effectively compete against bigger п¬Ғrms.
914	As long as п¬Ғrms, workers and the associations that represented them did not
915	clearly discriminate against economic actors located in other EEC member
916	states or violate a limited number of hard-core prohibitions, they rarely faced a
917	challenge from the Commission. Indeed, many forms of coordination were
918	explicitly endorsed by the Commission, especially if they were seen as in-
919	creasing economic productivity, encouraging economic cooperation across
920	member states or facilitating industrial transformation. This legal structure
921	facilitated long-term relationships and specialization in high value-added
922	products long seen as at the core of CMEs (Wigger & NoМҲlke, 2007, p. 490).
923	    At the same time, the EECвҖҷs abuse of dominance rules placed some
924	constraints on dominant companiesвҖҷ ability to exert economic power over
925	other п¬Ғrms. While abuse of dominance enforcement remained modest during
926	this period, it did contribute to an emerging legal framework aimed at
927	constraining economic coercion by more powerful п¬Ғrms. By providing
928	smaller companies with some protection from exploitative practices, EU
929	
930	
931	--- PAGE BREAK ---
932	
933	Foster and Thelen                                                          1219
934	
935	competition law may have therefore helped to вҖңstabilize ownership and
936	control structuresвҖқ in ways that reduced pressures for вҖҳhostile takeoversвҖҷ as
937	well as other means of п¬Ғrm consolidation (Wigger & NoМҲlke, 2007, p. 490).
938	Thus, at least indirectly, the EUвҖҷs restrictions on hierarchical coordination
939	helped sustain horizontal and coordinative non-market relations.
940	
941	Liberalization and the Intensiп¬Ғcation of Hierarchical Enforcement
942	During the 1980вҖҷs and 1990вҖҷs, the European Community underwent major
943	institutional and policy reforms that profoundly shaped the European coor-
944	dination rights regime. As part of the broader economic liberalization program
945	pursued under the Single European Act, the competition directorate initiated a
946	number of high-proп¬Ғle enforcement actions against state-owned companies in
947	the telecommunications, energy, and transportation sectorsвҖ”sectors that
948	previously had not been subject to a strict competition principle (Buch-Hansen
949	& Wigger, 2010; Quack & Djelic, 2005). At the same time, the Commission
950	received new authority to approve transnational mergers, providing a channel
951	to facilitate corporate consolidation (and hierarchical coordination rights) over
952	member state objections (Billows et al., 2021; Thatcher, 2014). Finally, the
953	European Commission adopted institutional reforms that expanded the role of
954	economic analysis in competition decisions вҖ“ a move seen by many observers
955	as aligning EU practices more closely with the consumer welfare standard in
956	the United States (Bartalevich, 2016; Wigger & NoМҲlke, 2007).
957	    But what is equally striking (albeit less commented upon) is the extent to
958	which policymakers also maintained and even reinforced core aspects of
959	earlier arrangements conducive to CMEs. On the horizontal dimension, rules
960	that facilitate and protect long-standing non-market forms of coordination
961	were never directly supplanted and sometimes even expanded. On the hi-
962	erarchical dimension, the EU not only continues to place limits on exclu-
963	sionary practices by dominant companies, but actually has intensiп¬Ғed
964	enforcement. Even EU merger policy вҖ“ which we agree bears some re-
965	semblance to the contemporary U.S. approach вҖ“ is still comparatively stricter
966	when it comes to dominant companies. Thus, even as the Commission ar-
967	ticulated a вҖңcompetition only visionвҖқ (Buch-Hansen & Wigger, 2010, p. 35) it
968	maintained a number of policies and practices that are complementary to
969	horizontal nonmarket coordination. As illustrated in Figure 1, elements of
970	continuity вҖ“ and continued divergence from the US вҖ“ can be seen in the
971	resilience of horizontal exemptions, while new elements of divergence can be
972	seen in the EU approach to vertical restraints, abuse of dominance and
973	mergers.
974	    Examining п¬Ғrst the horizontal dimension of coordination, we can see a
975	mixed pattern of continuity and change. On the one hand, the Commission,
976	along with national regulators, has tightened horizontal cartel rules and

<details>
<summary>рҹ§  RaciocГӯnio interno</summary>

Both the 2025 and 2024 papers are narrowly focused on the US and EU, with no engagement with the Global South or developing country cases like Brazil. What's striking is that neither paper explicitly invokes the institutional conversion framework from Mahoney and Thelen's earlier work, even though I'd expect Thelen to reference her own foundational pieceвҖ”instead, these newer papers adopt Paul's "coordination rights" framework as their analytical lens.

<details>
<summary>рҹ§  RaciocГӯnio interno</summary>

I'm noticing that Foster-Thelen 2024 and 2025 actually don't use "institutional conversion" as their frameworkвҖ”they're working with "coordination rights" and drift/social learning instead. So while it's still Thelen's work, it's a different theoretical vocabulary applied to a different lens than what I initially suggested.

That said, Ergen-Kohl 2019 and 2022 are already in the references and do cite Mahoney-Thelen 2010 directly, discussing "conversion" explicitlyвҖ”so those papers are more aligned with the institutional conversion framework the article currently uses. I should check whether they're already cited in the document or if they're part of the pending literature review expansion.

For the user's question about how Foster-Thelen 2024 contributes to the Brazil argument: it reinforces that competition law isn't a neutral technical domain, and that administrative discretionвҖ”not just legislative changeвҖ”drives institutional evolution. This parallels CADE's transformation when economists replaced lawyers in its composition, reshaping enforcement without formal legislative reform. It's a useful comparative reference point for showing how bureaucratic change operates as a mechanism of institutional change.

However, it doesn't directly address the Brazil paper's core concerns: it focuses on developed-country variation (US vs EU), ignores industrial policy, doesn't engage with efficiency-justified concentration for competitiveness against foreign competitors, and has no Global South perspective. So while topically adjacent to competition law history, it's more tangential to the substance of the argument than I initially worriedвҖ”not a glaring omission if left uncited, since it's not really making the same "conversion" claim, just adjacent institutional development literature.

Now looking at Foster-Thelen's coordination rights frameworkвҖ”this one actually maps onto the Brazil case quite well. Their two-dimensional model (horizontal coordination among non-dominant firms versus hierarchical coordination by dominant firms) producing four ideal types is conceptually useful for what happened with Gerdau and Ambev: Brazil essentially shifted from a nominally arm's-length regime toward deliberate oligopolistic competition, expanding hierarchical coordination rights for big domestic firms while being flexible on market definition.

The key insight is reframing this as a strategic move along Foster-Thelen's hierarchical coordination axisвҖ”Brazil granted domestic dominant firms expanded rights through permissive merger review specifically to counter the hierarchical coordination power already held by larger foreign multinationals. This gives the argument much sharper analytical vocabulary than just calling it "passive industrial policy."

What's particularly valuable here is the comparative angle: both the US and Brazil ended up in the oligopolistic quadrant of the typology, but through different causal pathways. The US moved there via ideological Chicago-School capture and judicial permissiveness, while Brazil moved there defensively and developmentally under executive pressure. Same structural outcome, different underlying logicвҖ”that's a genuine analytical contribution showing how oligopolistic competition can emerge from multiple distinct mechanisms.

But there's a real limitation: the Foster-Thelen framework is built entirely on US and EU cases with no theoretical engagement with Global South catch-up dynamics or developmental state literature. Applying it to Brazil risks the "adapting a US/EU box to a fundamentally different institutional context" problem, and the authors would still have to do the heavy lifting themselves to properly extend the framework rather than just slot Brazil into an existing category.

I need to decide whether to keep institutional conversion as the primary lens and treat coordination rights as the substantive mechanism being converted, or add coordination rights as a separate analytic layer вҖ” either way, it's additional conceptual work beyond just citing Foster-Thelen. That said, I should correct my earlier overstatement: having actually read both papers in full, the novelty threat to the Brazil work is smaller than I initially flagged. The overlap is real but manageable.

For the answer itself, I'll confirm the extraction is complete, then give an honest reassessment of what each paper contributes вҖ” Foster-Thelen 2025 offers precise vocabulary for describing hierarchical coordination rights that sharpens the Gerdau and Ambev analysis, while 2024 is more peripheral, useful mainly for one point about administrative discretion as a vehicle of institutional change. The key move is positioning Brazil as an unstudied case within their typology while highlighting the different causal logic at work: developmental and defensive motivations rather than ideological capture.

I should also decide whether to update the TODO.md file now that I've read the full texts and can moderate the earlier severity assessment about editorial rejection risk вҖ” the papers are less alarming than the initial citekey suggested, so leaving that overstated claim would be inconsistent with this more thorough evaluation. I'll append an amendment to the TODO item noting this refinement, following the same pattern used elsewhere in the file for updates, then write out the full answer in Portuguese with specifics and honesty. Let me also double-check that the reference files are accessible.

</details>

- `Foster-Thelen-2024-Brandeis-in-Brussels.txt`
- `Foster-Thelen-2025-Coordination-Rights-Competition-Law.txt`

Li os dois por completo (nГЈo sГі abstract/metadados). Isso me faz **corrigir para baixo** a severidade que dei a esse ponto na avaliaГ§ГЈo anterior вҖ” a leitura real Г© mais tranquilizadora do que a citaГ§ГЈo bibliogrГЎfica sugeria.

## Foster & Thelen (2024) вҖ” "Brandeis in Brussels?"
**O que Г©**: histГіria institucional comparada EUAГ—UE sobre por que a UE (nГЈo os EUA) acabou chegando mais perto da visГЈo de "concorrГӘncia regulada" de Brandeis. O mecanismo central Г© *discriГ§ГЈo administrativa/burocrГЎtica* (a ComissГЈo Europeia decide e executa direto, sem precisar de chancela judicial prГ©via) vs. *legalismo adversarial* nos EUA (agГӘncias dependem de tribunais, que historicamente boicotaram o FTC).

**Contribui**: dГЎ um paralelo estrutural interessante e citГЎvel вҖ” a ideia de que *mudanГ§a na composiГ§ГЈo/expertise do ГіrgГЈo regulador* Г©, por si sГі, o mecanismo de transformaГ§ГЈo da polГӯtica de concorrГӘncia (nГЈo Г© preciso mudar a lei). Isso ecoa exatamente o que aconteceu no CADE pГіs-Gerdau (substituiГ§ГЈo de conselheiros por economistas por decisГЈo de Cardoso) вҖ” vocГӘs jГЎ narram esse episГіdio, mas citar este texto dГЎ um antecedente comparativo de que "captura da composiГ§ГЈo do ГіrgГЈo via nomeaГ§ГЈo" Г© um canal reconhecido de conversГЈo institucional em direito da concorrГӘncia, nГЈo uma peculiaridade brasileira.

**NГЈo contribui**: Г© inteiramente EUA/UE, sem uma palavra sobre industrializaГ§ГЈo tardia, polГӯtica industrial, ou o Sul Global. NГЈo ameaГ§a a novidade do argumento de vocГӘs вҖ” Г© mais um "pano de fundo comparativo" citГЎvel em uma frase da SeГ§ГЈo 6 (DiscussГЈo) do que uma lacuna crГӯtica.

## Foster & Thelen (2025) вҖ” "Coordination Rights, Competition Law and Varieties of Capitalism"
**O que Г©**: um framework teГіrico novo (baseado em Sanjukta Paul 2020) que separa direito da concorrГӘncia em duas dimensГөes вҖ” **coordenaГ§ГЈo horizontal** (entre firmas nГЈo-dominantes) e **coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica** (poder de firmas dominantes) вҖ” gerando 4 tipos ideais: cartelista, oligopolista, cooperativo, e arm's-length. Aplicado empiricamente sГі a EUA e UE, 1970-2020.

**Contribui вҖ” este Г© o achado mais interessante da leitura**: o framework Г© quase feito Г  mГЈo para o caso Gerdau/Ambev, mesmo sem os autores terem pensado nisso. O que Brasil fez nos dois casos Г© exatamente conceder **direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica** a firmas dominantes domГ©sticas (permitir que Gerdau e Ambev consolidassem poder de mercado) вҖ” sГі que por uma lГіgica *defensiva/desenvolvimentista* (competir com multinacionais estrangeiras que jГЎ tinham esse mesmo poder hierГЎrquico em escala global), e nГЈo pela lГіgica *ideolГіgica* de captura pela Chicago School que eles descrevem para os EUA pГіs-1980. Isso dГЎ aos autores um movimento teГіrico real e vendГЎvel: **o Brasil chegou ao mesmo "quadrante" (modelo oligopolista) que os EUA, mas por uma via institucional completamente diferente** вҖ” pressГЈo executiva direta e justificativa developmentalista, nГЈo captura judicial/ideolГіgica de longo prazo. Гү uma extensГЈo legГӯtima do framework para um caso (economia emergente/catch-up) que os prГіprios autores nГЈo cobrem, e Г© exatamente o tipo de "conversation with the literature" que reviewers de RIPE recompensam.

**NГЈo contribui diretamente**: os autores nГЈo usam o vocabulГЎrio de "conversГЈo institucional" (Mahoney-Thelen 2010) вҖ” usam "coordination rights"/"drift". Ou seja, nГЈo Г© um substituto do framework que vocГӘs jГЎ usam, Г© complementar: dГЎ conteГәdo mais preciso (quais direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo, entre quem) ao que "conversГЈo institucional" descreve de forma mais abstrata (como as regras mudam de sentido). IntegrГЎ-lo exige trabalho conceitual вҖ” decidir se vira uma camada analГӯtica adicional ou sГі uma nota comparativa вҖ” nГЈo Г© um citation fix de uma linha.
