1	---
2	title: "Antitrust as industrial policy: Government-Sponsored Mergers as Passive Industrial Policy in Brazil, 1995-2002"
3	author:
4	  - name: AndrГ© Vereta-Nahoum
5	  - name: Tales ManГ§ano
6	abstract: |
7	  This article analyzes how antitrust policy can function as passive industrial policy, promoting competitiveness and internationalization in economies under fiscal constraint. Drawing on government-supported mergers in Brazil during the 1990s, the study examines how flexibility in the enforcement of competition law was justified by the need to strengthen national sectors in the face of economic liberalization. Antitrust and industrial policy are traditionally regarded as antagonistic. At the turn of the century, however, Brazil's antitrust authority allowed the consolidation of major corporate groups, such as Gerdau and Ambev, on the grounds of efficiency gains and global competitiveness вҖ” even as the Cardoso government prioritized monetary stability and denied adopting sectoral industrial policies. The Brazilian case illustrates how fiscally conservative governments can strategically use antitrust policy to drive economic development and the internationalization of firms, aligning indirectly and passively with objectives traditionally associated with classic, strengthening-oriented industrial policy.
8	keywords:
9	  - antitrust policy
10	  - industrial policy
11	  - institutional conversion
12	  - Brazil
13	  - mergers and acquisitions
14	format:
15	  pdf: default
16	  html: default
17	  docx: default
18	---
19	
20	## Introduction
21	
22	Can governments pursue industrial policy while explicitly denying doing so? This article examines how Brazil's antitrust enforcement in the 1990s operated as a form of industrial policy under conditions of fiscal constraint and liberalizing reform, despite the official rejection of such policy by leading policymakers. During the administrations of Fernando Henrique Cardoso (FHC), economic policy was publicly organized around macroeconomic stabilization, market liberalization, and a commitment to limiting state intervention in markets. As the then Finance Minister Pedro Malan put it, "the best industrial policy is to have no industrial policy" [@2004-07-03_news_fsp_autores-apontam-caminhos-para-a-politica-industrial]. Yet, at the same time, major mergers involving leading Brazilian firms were approved with explicit support from the executive branch and justified in terms of the need to compete internationally. This apparent contradiction constitutes the central puzzle of the paper. In the absence and even explicital denial of specific industrial policy mechanisms, antitrust can serve as an indirect regulatory means with similar effects of benefitting specific firms and industries with firm-level competitiveness or sectoral concentration. In this sense, industrial policy may operate not through visible interventions such as subsidies or protection, but through the reinterpretation and selective enforcement of existing legal frameworks. This case also emphasizes the importance of analytically disentangling state discourses, policy instruments, and policy effects.
23	
24	Passive industrial policy, as defined in this article, refers to the promotion of sectoral or firm-level competitiveness through regulatory practices that do not involve fiscal transfers or direct state coordination, but instead operate by enabling market restructuring. When such policies are pursued under explicit discursive denial, they take the form of covert industrial policy. This passivity should not be mistaken for state absence. Rather, it reflects a form of indirect and strategically mediated intervention, in which the state shapes market outcomes by exercising discretion over enforcement, interpretation, and institutional design. In the Brazilian case, this occurred through the lenient application of antitrust law, which allowed firms to consolidate their domestic market positions and subsequently expand internationally.
25	
26	The mechanism through which this process unfolds can be understood as a form of institutional conversion, in the sense developed by @Mahoney-Thelen2010. Existing rules are not formally changed but are reinterpreted in ways that produce outcomes that diverge from their original intent. In the context of antitrust policy, two legal concepts are particularly central to this process: efficiency and the definition of the relevant market. While efficiency was formally tied to consumer welfare in the new competition regime, it could be expanded in practice to include considerations of firm competitiveness and technological upgrading. More importantly, the concept of the relevant market proved highly malleable. By defining markets as global rather than national, actors were able to dilute measures of concentration and justify mergers as necessary responses to international competition. This ambiguity created room for interpretation that enabled the conversion of antitrust policy into an instrument of industrial policy.
27	
28	The empirical analysis is based on a comparative study of two emblematic merger cases in Brazil during the 1990s: the acquisition of Pains by the Gerdau Group in the steel sector and the merger of Antarctica and Brahma into Ambev in the beverage sector. These cases are particularly revealing because they were highly contested, involved extensive documentation, and generated significant political debate. The study employs process tracing based on administrative rulings by Administrative Council for Economic Defense (CADE), technical reports produced by government agencies, corporate statements, and media coverage. In order to manage and analyze a large volume of textual material, the research also relied on computational assistance using large language models, namely NotebookLM. These tools were used to reconstruct procedural timelines, identify recurring argumentative patterns, and compare the positions of different actors across documents. Their use was strictly supportive, facilitating the organization and interrogation of the material, while all analytical interpretations were carried out by the authors.
29	
30	The Brazilian case is especially illuminating because it unfolds in a context where industrial policy was not only absent but explicitly disavowed. Following the enactment of the 1994 competition law, the CADE was strengthened as an autonomous authority tasked with promoting competition and supporting macroeconomic stabilization. The broader policy framework emphasized the reduction of state intervention and the disciplining of markets, in part as a strategy to control inflation. At the same time, trade liberalization exposed domestic firms to increased foreign competition, raising concerns about their capacity to survive and expand in a more open economy. It is within this context that antitrust policy became a site of strategic reinterpretation.
31	
32	Taken together, the two cases reveal the process of institutional conversion that allowed the manipulation of antitrust regulatory principles to serve as a passive industrial policy. In the Gerdau case, the reinterpretation of antitrust principles required direct political intervention and generated visible conflict within the regulatory framework. By the time of the Ambev merger, similar interpretive moves were more readily accommodated within the logic of the competition regime itself. The ambiguity surrounding the definition of the relevant market played a central role in this transformation, providing the legal and analytical flexibility necessary to reconcile high levels of domestic concentration with the formal requirements of competition law. Through this process, antitrust policy came to function as a mechanism for enabling firm consolidation and promoting international competitiveness, even as industrial policy continued to be publicly disavowed.
33	
34	These findings have broader implications for debates on industrial policy and competition in international political economy. They suggest that industrial policy need not take the form of explicit intervention or fiscal expenditure, but can be embedded in the operation of regulatory regimes typically associated with market discipline. They also challenge the view of antitrust as a neutral and purely technocratic instrument, highlighting its susceptibility to political reinterpretation and strategic use. Finally, the Brazilian case resonates with contemporary debates in the United States and the European Union, where policymakers have increasingly sought to reconcile antitrust enforcement with the need to foster globally competitive firms in sectors such as digital platforms, artificial intelligence, and green technologies. These debates are often framed as a choice between maintaining strict competition or relaxing enforcement to enable scale [@Khan2016; @Khan-Vaheesan2017; @Juhasz-etal2024].
35	
36	The Brazilian case suggests that this dichotomy may be misleading. Rather than formally suspending antitrust principles, states may pursue industrial objectives through the internal reinterpretation of competition law, allowing concentration while preserving the formal language of market efficiency and consumer welfare. In this sense, contemporary efforts to align antitrust with industrial strategy may already involve forms of covert and passive industrial policy, even where such policies are not explicitly acknowledged.
37	
38	The article proceeds as follows. The next section situates the argument within existing debates on antitrust and industrial policy. The subsequent sections analyze the Gerdau and Ambev cases in detail, followed by a comparative discussion of the mechanisms at work and their broader implications.
39	
40	## Antitrust and Industrial Policy: Reframing the Relationship
41	
42	The relationship between antitrust and industrial policy has long been framed as one of tension, if not outright incompatibility. Early antitrust traditions in the United States, associated with Louis Brandeis and progressive-era reformers, viewed concentration as both an economic and political threat, opposing large-scale corporate organization on the grounds that it undermined democracy and fair competition [@Berk2009; @Hovenkamp2005]. Within this perspective, industrial policy вҖ” understood as state support for specific sectors or firms вҖ” appeared as a dangerous extension of concentrated power, further entrenching dominant actors at the expense of smaller competitors.
43	
44	This skepticism persisted, albeit in different form, within the Chicago School and transaction-cost approaches that came to dominate antitrust thinking in the late twentieth century. While these approaches rejected Brandeisian hostility to large firms, they nonetheless opposed industrial policy interventions on the grounds that they distorted market signals and reduced efficiency [@Posner1979; @Bork1978; @Williamson1975]. Antitrust, in this view, should focus narrowly on consumer welfare, allowing concentration where it produces efficiency gains and intervening only where clear harm can be demonstrated.
45	
46	In recent years, the resurgence of industrial policy in advanced economies has reopened these debates. Scholars and policymakers have increasingly questioned whether strict adherence to competition principles is compatible with the need to foster innovation, build strategic industries, and compete in global markets [@Khan2016; @Khan-Vaheesan2017; @Juhasz-etal2024]. Yet even in this renewed context, the relationship between antitrust and industrial policy is often framed as a trade-off, with governments facing a choice between maintaining competitive markets and enabling the emergence of large, globally competitive firms.
47	
48	This binary framing obscures the possibility that antitrust itself may serve as a vehicle for industrial policy. Historical institutionalist accounts have pointed in this direction, showing that regulatory regimes can shape market structures in ways that resemble industrial policy outcomes, even in the absence of explicit state planning [@Dobbin1994; @Prasad2012]. However, these accounts have focused primarily on earlier periods and have not systematically examined how such processes unfold in contemporary liberalizing contexts.
49	
50	The Brazilian case suggests that the opposition between antitrust and industrial policy is not only overstated but analytically misleading. Rather than choosing between competition and intervention, governments may pursue industrial objectives through the internal reinterpretation of competition law. This requires shifting attention away from formal policy categories and toward the ways in which legal concepts are mobilized and redefined in practice. In particular, it calls for examining how ambiguities embedded in antitrust frameworks---such as those surrounding efficiency and market definition---create opportunities for strategic reinterpretation.
51	
52	By foregrounding these dynamics, the paper shows that antitrust policy, far from being a neutral or purely technical domain, is deeply embedded in broader struggles over economic strategy and state intervention. In doing so, it opens up a new perspective on the relationship between liberalization and industrial policy, suggesting that the former may not displace the latter but rather transform its modalities.
53	
54	## Context: Liberalization, Institutional Reform, and the Emergence of a New Antitrust Regime
55	
56	The transformation of Brazil's political economy in the 1990s provides a critical backdrop for understanding the cases analyzed in this paper. Following decades of state-led development characterized by import substitution, state-owned enterprises, and corporatist arrangements, the country embarked on a process of economic liberalization that reshaped the relationship between state and market. Trade barriers were reduced, privatization programs were implemented, and macroeconomic stabilization became the central objective of economic policy.
57	
58	<!--
59	
60	Brazil's competition-defense framework did not emerge from this liberalization all at once. Regulation against the abuse of economic power has its origins in the Vargas era, with the decree-law on the "protection of the popular economy" and, later, the so-called Lei Malaia, subsequently incorporated into the constitution that succeeded the Estado Novo [@Onto2017]. CADE itself was created in 1962, under the government of JoГЈo Goulart, inspired by the U.S. antitrust authority (ibid.). Although the 1962 Competition Law established the combating of "abuse of economic power" as its aim, CADE operated with limited effectiveness between 1962 and 1994, owing to judicialization and, notably, an intentional hollowing-out of the agency during the military dictatorship, when it was instrumentalized mainly to oversee the administration and finances of state-owned enterprises [@Onto2017].
61	
62	After the promulgation of the 1988 Constitution, debate over the contours of state regulation of economic activity gained momentum in Brazil. The period was marked by trade opening and domestic market liberalization, alongside a transition from a developmentalist outlook to a liberal economic order [@Bresser-Pereira-Diniz2009]. The state was seen as abandoning "its role as a protective shell against external competition and as a lever for industrial development and domestic private enterprise" [@SallumJr1999, p. 28]. The privatization of state-owned enterprises, measures to increase the autonomy of the Central Bank, and the creation of sectoral regulatory agencies were all expressions of this shift.
63	-->
64	
65	Within this broader transformation, the reform of the competition regime played a key role. The enactment of the 1994 antitrust law strengthened the institutional capacity and autonomy of CADE, positioning it as the central authority responsible for enforcing competition policy. The new framework aimed not only to promote efficiency and consumer welfare but also to support macroeconomic stabilization by preventing price distortions and anti-competitive practices that could undermine inflation control.
66	
67	<!--
68	
69	The 1994 law, in particular, made CADE a federal autarchy and created the Brazilian System for the Defense of Competition (SBDC), understanding the defense of competition as a fundamental instrument for the "efficient and fair functioning of the market economy" [@Brasil1993]. Drafted by a commission of jurists and economists, the law required "notorious legal or economic expertise" [@Onto2017, p. 68] of CADE's members, which drove the entry of economists onto the council and the growing use of economic theories and concepts вҖ” such as "relevant market" and efficiency analysis вҖ” to inform the agency's decisions. As an autarchy, CADE was able to expand its technical staff and legal counsel, and its remit was considerably broadened: the law instituted mandatory review of corporate concentration acts (mergers, acquisitions, associations) meeting certain revenue thresholds, and gave CADE the resources to investigate and repress anticompetitive conduct, causing the number of cases before the agency to grow substantially from 1994 onward [@Onto2017]. The law also specified that there would be no appeal to the Executive branch, and that CADE's decisions were to be judicially enforced without delay. It defined as violations of the economic order any acts that had as their object, or could produce effects such as, limiting free competition or free enterprise, dominating a relevant market for goods or services, arbitrarily increasing profits, or abusively exercising a dominant position [@Brasil1994; @Carvalho-Ragazzo2012]. President Itamar Franco, for his part, was especially keen to mobilize the legislation against what he considered abusive pricing вҖ” particularly in the pharmaceutical sector вҖ” and against a broader culture of inflation, at a moment when competition was seen as helping to prevent abuses following economic opening [@Onto2017].
70	-->
71	
72	At the same time, the ideological orientation of the government emphasized a retreat from direct state intervention in the economy. Policymakers sought to distance themselves from the developmentalist strategies of previous decades, framing liberalization as both a technical necessity and a normative commitment to market principles. This orientation was reflected in public statements that rejected industrial policy and in efforts to limit the role of the state in coordinating economic activity.
73	
74	Yet this apparent withdrawal of the state coincided with new challenges. The opening of the economy exposed domestic firms to increased competition from foreign producers, raising concerns about their ability to survive and expand in a more integrated global market. In this context, the consolidation of domestic firms emerged as a potential strategy for enhancing competitiveness, even as the formal policy framework discouraged direct intervention.
75	
76	
77	It is within this tension between ideological commitment and economic necessity that the reinterpretation of antitrust policy took place. The new competition regime, while designed to promote market discipline, also contained ambiguities that could be mobilized to accommodate consolidation. The following sections examine how these ambiguities were exploited in practice through the Gerdau and Ambev cases.
78	
79	## The Gerdau--Pains Case: Conflict, Intervention, and the Reinterpretation of Antitrust
80	
81	<!--
82	Gerdau was founded in Porto Alegre in 1901 as a nail manufacturer and expanded significantly after the Second World War amid strong demand for its products and import restrictions. Instability in steel supply led the firm to integrate vertically, acquiring SiderГәrgica Riograndense in 1948 to secure its raw materials; it then expanded its operations in Rio Grande do Sul and the Southeast with the support of the military regime, which facilitated the acquisition of a mill in Pernambuco in 1969. During the so-called Brazilian Economic Miracle, Gerdau continued to expand: in 1973, in a joint venture with the German group Thyssen ATH and bank financing, it acquired the state-owned Cosigua mill in Rio de Janeiro, making Sepetiba Bay a hub of its operations. The company also purchased SiderГәrgica GuaГӯra (1972) and Cia. SiderГәrgica de Alagoas (1974), built SiderГәrgica Cearense (1981) with support from the CearГЎ state government, and acquired Usina SiderГәrgica Paraense (1986); its first operation in Minas Gerais, Usina BarГЈo de Cocais, was purchased in 1988 following its privatization. In short, Gerdau had received substantial federal support for its ventures since the dictatorship, and grew considerably through the privatization of state-owned steel mills and the acquisition and construction of mills across the country, much of it financed with public loans and tax incentives. In the steel sector, the 1990s economic opening saw state-owned and even foreign mills being sold off as the state's role in certain economic sectors was redefined [@Nassif1995; @Teixeira-etal2001, p. 186], with the developmentalist tradition giving way to a liberal vision of the state's role [@Schneider2015; @Onto2017]. Non-tariff barriers to imports were suspended, and import tariffs were gradually reduced.
83	-->
84	
85	The Gerdau-Pains case provides an early and revealing instance of the tensions inherent in the new competition regime. The Pains acquisition was among the first post-1994 CADE cases to generate widespread controversy [@Onto2017, p. 59]. In the mid-1990s, Gerdau's acquisition of assets associated with the German group Korf, including the Pains steel mill, raised concerns within CADE about excessive concentration in the domestic market for long steel products, particularly rebar.[^gerdau-marketshare] Central to the dispute were the definition of the relevant market and the assessment of potential efficiency gains, which could justify, in the terms of the antitrust law of the period, increased market concentration. Efficiency, in this context, significa que a empresa poderia ter "sinergias", ou seja, pelos ganhos de centralizaГ§ГЈo logГӯstica e de produГ§ГЈo ela poderia reduzir os custos de produГ§ГЈo o produto no mercado mais barato e/ou aumentar suas margens. The case unfolded over a protracted period marked by legal disputes, political intervention, and institutional transformation.
86	
87	The firmsвҖҷ legal teams defined the relevant market as international in nature, asserting steel was a global commodity. They also claimed the merger would produce efficiency gains, including access to proprietary technologies and the rescue of financially distressed operations [@CADE1995-AC16-1994].
88	
89	In its initial assessment, CADE adopted a relatively strict interpretation of competition principles. The authority emphasized that the merger would substantially increase concentration in the domestic market and questioned the validity of the efficiency claims advanced by the firms.CADE's technical analysis suggested that, despite trade liberalization, domestic prices for steel products remained largely decoupled from international benchmarks, indicating that the market retained a predominantly national character. This finding implied that the merger would create significant market power within Brazil, raising concerns about potential anti-competitive effects [@CADE1995-AC16-1994].
90	
91	The authority initially issued a partial approval, requiring the deconstitution of part of the operation. Gerdau and its allies contested this interpretation, arguing that steel should be understood as a global commodity subject to international competition. By framing the relevant market in global terms, they sought to demonstrate that domestic concentration was less problematic and that the merger was necessary to achieve the scale required to compete internationally [@Seidl1996].
92	
93	Rather than pursue the matter through the courts, Gerdau pressured FHC's Justice Minister, Nelson Jobim вҖ” who immediately took the firm's side against the regulator вҖ” despite the recently enacted Law 8,884/94, whose Article 50 established that CADE's decisions "are not subject to review within the Executive Branch" [@Brasil1994]. In a departmental order, Jobim nonetheless granted the appeal Gerdau had filed with the ministry against CADE's October decision ordering the dissolution of its purchase of Pains, suspending that decision on the grounds that "the Brazilian steel market is open to international products, and the concentration act does not eliminate competition in a substantial part of the market" [@Ribeiro1995a]. In the minister's view, since steel products were a commodity, it made no sense to treat the roughly 46-percent concentration in common long steel at stake as grounds for a veto, since the product could easily be imported. The order reached further than the case at hand: Jobim held that CADE had the final word only on infractions against the economic order вҖ” price-fixing agreements, for instance вҖ” but not on the acquisition and merger of firms [@Ribeiro1995a]. Without any legislative change, the minister thus redrew the boundary of the agency's jurisdiction at precisely the point where the new competition regime bore on industrial structure, subordinating merger review to the Executive while leaving the letter of Law 8,884/94 intact. Gerdau's public response was forceful: according to the firm, CADE's opposition to the Pains purchase "does not follow the government's political strategy, nor does it track the evolution of the international scenario" [@1995-10-14_news_fsp_contra-a-mare], suggesting that the firm counted on active government support for the acquisition. Gerdau widened its attacks on CADE, arguing that the decision was anticompetitive and undermined the country's economic opening, and that the agency's definition of the relevant market was inappropriate вҖ” a rhetorical move resembling that of Chicago School economists against the earlier antitrust tradition. As chairman Jorge Gerdau Johannpeter put it, CADE was "yet another chapter in the delay of Brazil's economic opening," [@Seidl1996] arguing that antitrust law was anachronistic because it was tied to a closed-market vision, and that the country should either adopt structural solutions for competitiveness or watch businesses turn, out of desperation, to protectionist ones. The chairman argued that mergers and acquisitions were the path to competitiveness and efficiency in an era of economic globalization, and that CADE interfered with this process through legislation far more restrictive than that of First World and Asian countries and that the steel sector was inherently oligopolistic вҖ” profitable only at large production scales вҖ” pointing to a single steel group operating in France and only two in England, and to a merger trend already under way among U.S. steelmakers; the note added that the sector was open to international competition, since import tariffs stood at around 10 percent [@Seidl1996].
94	
95	<!--
96	The firms challenged the decision, refused to comply, and appealed to the Minister of Justice, arguing that the legal framework granting CADE final authority was unconstitutional. The Minister accepted the appeal and suspended enforcement, effectively intervening in the process. The argument raised by Gerdau's legal team was supported by broader claims about the need for consolidation in the context of globalization, with political actors assuming steel is a global commodity and emphasizing that mergers and acquisitions among Brazilian firms in the industry were essential for competitiveness in an open economy. -->
97	
98	The case was then reconsidered by a reconfigured CADE, which ultimately approved the acquisition with conditions, including divestments and commitments regarding supply and designed to mitigate anti-competitive effects. However, the outcome of the case extended beyond the specific operation. It had significant institutional consequences and contributed to the redefinition of antitrust policy in Brazil. In response to the tensions generated by the decision, President Cardoso altered the composition of CADE, replacing members with legal backgrounds with economists trained in transaction-cost approaches to antitrust [@Onto2017]. President Cardoso reportedly warned the newly appointed counselors: "Don't cause me any problems!" [@Onto2017, p. 70]. 
99	
100	<!-- According to LГәcia Helena Salgado, one of the economists who joined CADE shortly after the Gerdau-Pains case, this period marked the beginning of a search for the "rationalization," of the agency's activity вҖ” a search that, for the Presidency, was meant to avoid the political problems CADE could generate. As sociologist Carlos Bello recounts it, Finance Minister Pedro Malan and Justice Minister Nelson Jobim publicly stated that the new counselors should be technocrats; shortly before the appointments were announced, the two ministers met with Minister JosГ© Serra and President Cardoso and agreed to nominate specialized technical experts with legal or economic training. For Bello, the president was "very concerned that political balkanization could generate new conflicts with CADE ... the economists would serve to block CADE," rather than to make it more effective [@Onto2017, p. 70]. -->
101	
102	This shift in expertise contributed to a reorientation of the authority toward a more permissive stance regarding mergers and acquisitions, emphasizing efficiency and conduct remedies over structural constraints. The case established a precedent for the acceptance of mergers justified in terms of international competitiveness. This transformation can be understood as part of a broader process of institutional conversion [@Mahoney-Thelen2010], in which the meaning and application of competition law were reshaped without formal legislative change.
103	
104	<!-- The reconsideration process stretched over several years. Gerdau was ultimately allowed to keep the Pains mill after complying with ten conditions set by the council to prevent market concentration in the long-steel sector, including reconstituting and selling Pains's rolling unit in Contagem (MG), selling its transport subsidiary (Transpains), and rehiring previously dismissed workers [@1998-03-19_news_fsp_gerdau-fica-com-a-pains-cade-aprova]. The group also redirected more than half of Pains's output toward special long steel вҖ” reducing its share of the common long-steel market while helping to deconcentrate the special long-steel segment, then dominated by three other firms вҖ” and committed to additional investments of US$78 million, along with further measures on production reorientation, distribution, technology, and periodic reporting to CADE. The settlement marked the saturation of the firm's domestic growth and the acceleration of its international expansion across the Americas вҖ” Uruguay, Chile, Canada, and Argentina вҖ” and, from the late 1990s onward, particularly the United States: in 1999 Gerdau acquired 75 percent of AmeriSteel Corporation, entering the U.S. market with a production capacity of two million tonnes of steel per year [@Teixeira-etal2001, p. 204]. -->
105	
106	[^gerdau-marketshare]: The acquisition would have raised Gerdau's share of the long-steel market from 39.6 to 46.2 percent, and its overall share of the national steel market to roughly 43 percent [@1995-03-23_news_fsp_cade-adia-decisao-sobre-fusao]. When it ordered the deal undone in October 1995, CADE put the resulting share of the market for common long steel at 46.5 percent [@Ribeiro1995a]; under Law 8,884/94 the agency could bar acquisitions and mergers resulting in control of more than 20 percent of a market.
107	
108	## The Ambev Case: Consolidation, Legitimization, and the Stabilization of Conversion
109	
110	The Ambev case, decided at the end of the decade, reflects the consolidation of this new orientation. The merger between Antarctica and Brahma, announced in 1999, brought together the two largest beer producers in the country, creating a firm that controlled approximately 70 percent of the beer market and a substantial share of distribution networks.
111	
112	Throughout the 1990s economic opening, Brazil's brewers had been courted by foreign multinationals for acquisitions or distribution partnerships, seen by many as a prelude to the entry of the sector's leading global firms into the domestic market [@Abdallah2020]. In any eventual competition with these foreign entrants, domestic firms would be at a serious disadvantage: with access only to the costlier domestic credit market, they would have to pay higher interest rates, raising the cost of investment and limiting what they could offer to acquire other firms in Brazil or abroad, while foreign firms вҖ” able to borrow at significantly lower rates, as in the U.S. credit market вҖ” could offer more for the same targets. As Antarctica's chairman put it: "If an American company wanted to acquire a Brazilian brewery, it could pay twice what a domestic competitor offered, since over eight years the rate of return would be identical in both cases" [@Abdallah2020, p. 136]. Since the 1980s, both Brahma and Antarctica had been approached by multinationals such as Anheuser-Busch, Miller, Kirin, Coca-Cola, and Pepsi interested in partnering with them; distribution agreements and joint ventures were repeatedly attempted, but these either failed to materialize or were curtailed by CADE, which at times demanded additional investment commitments that discouraged the foreign firms [@Abdallah2020, p. 140]. Facing the prospect of eventual acquisition by one of these larger, better-capitalized foreign rivals, Brahma's controllers вҖ” Jorge Paulo Lemann and Marcel Telles вҖ” instead pursued a different ambition: to become the world's largest brewer, including through the acquisition of Anheuser-Busch itself [@Abdallah2020, p. 148]. The alternative that emerged from informal conversations between Brahma's and Antarctica's leadership was to merge the country's two largest brewers instead, in order to build a firm strong enough to expand abroad. As Antarctica's Victorio De Marchi recalled of the conversations preceding the merger: "should we leave the foreigners out, and think about building a company strong enough to secure ourselves domestically and allow us to grow abroad?" [@Abdallah2020].
113	
114	The operation was announced by its proponents as essential for the creation of a "green-and-yellow multinational" capable of competing with global industry leaders and warned that failure to achieve sufficient scale would result in the denationalization of the sector. This framing resonated with political actors, who publicly endorsed the merger as part of a broader strategy to strengthen domestic firms in the face of international competition. The merger was in fact announced to the press from the PalГЎcio da Alvorada, with President Cardoso present, following a meeting between the businessmen and the president [@LoPrete1999]; the new firm's international ambitions were championed by Cardoso from the very start of the negotiations.
115	
116	Following the announcement of the merger in July 1999, CADE issued a precautionary measure suspending its implementation pending review. Technical bodies within the government, including the Secretary for Economic Monitoring, challenged the efficiency claims advanced by the firms, arguing that projected cost reductions were overstated and unlikely to benefit consumers. At the same time, debates over the definition of the relevant market reemerged, with the firms advocating for a broad definition encompassing all beverages, while critics insisted on focusing on the beer market, where concentration was extreme and exports remained minimal.
117	
118	Opposition to the merger was led by Kaiser, one of Ambev's rival brewers,[^kaiser-coke] which ran a series of media campaigns arguing that concentrating roughly two-thirds of the country's beer production and sales in the new firm could lead to anticompetitive conduct, including price increases contrary to the national interest: "Kaiser, enemy number one of the union, put out yet another campaign yesterday вҖ” the third вҖ” to prevent the association, which is under review by CADE... the brewer ran ads on radio and in newspapers to try to convince public opinion that the merger harms consumers' interests, since it would bring about market concentration and, consequently, higher prices" [@FernandesPrates2000]. Critics also argued that the operation amounted not to a merger but to Brahma absorbing Antarctica outright, creating a firm capable of eliminating competition and threatening the survival of medium and small rivals, with no significant efficiencies or logistical or technological innovations to benefit consumers, suppliers, or distributors. Kaiser further criticized Ambev for operating as a unified company even before CADE's final ruling вҖ” a fait accompli meant to make the operation practically irreversible regardless of the agency's eventual decision (CADE AC 08012.005846.1999-12, Vol. 2, p. 49).
119	
120	Despite these concerns, the executive branch strongly supported the merger, with the Ministry of Development affirming its alignment with national interests [CitaГ§ГЈo do Processo?]. Minister of Industrial Development ClГіvis Carvalho, for instance, stated that he wanted to see a wave of mergers across several of the country's largest industries вҖ” especially paper, steel, and petrochemicals вҖ” so that they could prosper in international markets, offering up to three billion reais (roughly US$1.7 billion) from the National Bank for Economic and Social Development (BNDES) to finance such deals [@TheEconomist1999]. Public statements by President Cardoso suggested a pragmatic acceptance of market concentration. In an interview with the Jornal do Brasil, Cardoso addressed the merger directly, arguing that Brazilian firms needed to restructure to compete in a more open global economy; asked about the possibility of a "monopoly" forming with the merger, he replied: "But the world today operates in terms of oligopoly. ... What is Coca-Cola? How will they compete, later on, with Coca-Cola, if they are not strong here? Isn't that so? That is the concept. You can no longer think in terms of the domestic market. The market is internationalized. So you have to look at whether there are competitors in the sector or not [...] I don't even want to anticipate whether this will be legal or not [referring to CADE]. Let's discuss it. I am telling you what the global trend is: mega-mergers. That is, we will need Brazilian multinationals" [@Abdallah2020, p. 170]. 
121	
122	Press, congressional, and government pressure on CADE was intense throughout the proceeding, as former counselors later recounted in detail. Ruy Lima, the only counselor to vote against the merger, recalled that CADE was pressured by the government to expedite the case toward a favorable outcome: "The Ambev case was one of CADE's biggest cases, mostly because of the media attention generated by the applicants themselves... There was a political dimension to it; the government applied a great deal of pressure вҖ” calls from ministers, from the leader of the government's party in Congress вҖ” all of it landing on CADE's president, the most visible figure" [@Dutra2009, pp. 101-103].
123	
124	In March 2000, CADE approved the merger subject to a set of conditions formalized in a Performance Commitment Term. These conditions included divestments, measures to ensure access to distribution networks, and commitments to maintain employment levels. While these remedies were intended to address competition concerns, they also reflected a broader acceptance of the merger's underlying rationale.
125	
126	<!-- One option debated within CADE was to require the divestment of one of the resulting firm's major beer brands. Since Brahma also owned Skol вҖ” whose market share was comparable to the Brahma brand itself вҖ” selling it off was seen by some as a feasible restriction that would allow the merger to proceed without creating a monopoly. Counselor LГәcia Helena Salgado, who did not vote in the case because her husband represented Kaiser as counsel, considered the eventual decision a mistake: "I would have approved the deal, but it was obvious вҖ” known to everyone familiar with competition law, since one only had to read the prevailing case law and doctrine вҖ” that an unavoidable condition for approving that case, among others, was the sale of a beer brand to a competitor, along with the plants capable of producing it competitively and the means to distribute it. And that brand, as everyone who knows competition law knew and still knows, was Skol. ... As is well known, that is not what happened. CADE's approval imposed as a condition the sale of a handful of plants the companies were going to close anyway, since they were entirely obsolete, and the sale of a brand that, poor thing, besides being inexpressive, had been вҖ” over the course of the review вҖ” intentionally run down, since its market share had fallen sharply during the period. It was a truly regrettable decision" [@Dutra2009, pp. 61-62]. Ruy Lima likewise recalled that this possibility was discussed, though it was ultimately reduced to the sale of the BavГЎria brand вҖ” a brand still under construction, with roughly nine percent of the market at the start of the case, and considerably less by the time it was decided [@Dutra2009, pp. 101-103]. -->
127	
128	The Ambev case thus illustrates the stabilization of the interpretive shifts that had emerged in the Gerdau case. The ambiguity surrounding market definition and efficiency was no longer a source of acute conflict but had become part of the routine operation of the competition regime. Antitrust policy, in this context, functioned as a mechanism for enabling consolidation while maintaining the formal language of competition and consumer welfare.
129	
130	<!-- For Ruy Lima, this outcome had lasting consequences for the antitrust authority's reputation, signaling that CADE would no longer stand as an obstacle to market concentration after the Ambev case: "I think that, by approving the Ambev case, CADE lost the great opportunity to show what competition defense in Brazil is, and what it should be. ... It seems to me that NestlГ© only felt emboldened to buy Garoto because of that signal CADE sent in the Ambev case... The idea took hold that CADE could always find a way to approve a large-scale concentration act, especially one accompanied by heavy media coverage and political pressure. CADE would always find a way to solve the problem without causing major disruption" [@Dutra2009, pp. 103-104]. Once authorized, the merged firm accelerated its international expansion across the Southern Cone. Although approved with conditions, institutional changes at CADE shortly after the ruling made it unlikely that a firm could be approved again with such a high a market share: "Ambev was born with 70 percent of domestic beer production in its hands. CADE's green light was only possible at that particular moment. CADE made a series of changes to its rules in the 2000s that would not allow the formation of a firm with that market share today" [@Boucas2018]. In 2004, Ambev merged with the Belgian brewer Interbrew, becoming a global company with a strong presence across several continents вҖ” a rapid foreignization of a firm whose creation, only four years earlier, had been justified by the need to preserve a Brazilian champion. In the end, the merged firm's headquarters moved to Belgium, and Brazilians held the same number of board seats as their Belgian counterparts, but from the year following the merger onward the company's CEO вҖ” with only brief interregnums вҖ” was consistently Brazilian, as was much of its senior management. 
131	
132	As Ambev co-president Marcel Telles put it: "If you have a huge German shepherd that barks at your visitors, you have to keep it under control, not kill it. Plausible restrictions need to be created for the merger of two large companies, but the chance for the country to have a global competitor вҖ” a green-and-yellow multinational that will already be born as the world's third-largest brewery вҖ” should not be eliminated" [@Seidl2000]. 
133	 -->
134	
135	[^kaiser-coke]: Kaiser had an equity stake held by Coca-Cola, which Ambev used to argue that the firm was only representing the interests of the foreign multinational that would gain a strong competitor with Ambev's formation [@Abdallah2020].
136	
137	## Discussion: Institutional Conversion and the Reconfiguration of State Intervention
138	
139	The comparative analysis of the Gerdau and Ambev cases reveals a process of institutional conversion in which the meaning and application of antitrust policy were transformed without formal legislative change. Central to this process was the strategic use of ambiguity in key legal concepts, particularly the definition of the relevant market. By expanding the scope of the market to include international competition, actors were able to reconcile high levels of domestic concentration with the requirements of competition law.
140	
141	This reinterpretation was facilitated by political intervention and institutional change, including the reconfiguration of CADE's composition and the increasing prominence of economic expertise aligned with transaction-cost approaches. Over time, these changes contributed to the normalization of a more permissive stance toward mergers, in which concerns about market structure were balanced against considerations of efficiency and competitiveness.
142	
143	The result was a form of industrial policy that operated through passive regulatory means and was justified in covert terms. While the government continued to reject industrial policy in its discourse, it enabled the consolidation of domestic firms and their expansion into international markets. This suggests that liberalization does not necessarily entail a retreat of the state but may instead involve a reconfiguration of its modes of intervention.
144	
145	## Conclusion
146	
147	This paper has argued that antitrust policy in Brazil during the 1990s functioned as a form of covert industrial policy implemented through passive regulatory means. By enabling firm consolidation through the reinterpretation of legal concepts and the selective enforcement of competition law, the state fostered the emergence of nationally rooted multinational firms without resorting to traditional industrial policy tools.
148	
149	Facing severe fiscal constraints and economic opening, with foreign capital entering numerous industrial sectors, government actors вҖ” including the Presidency вҖ” engaged in a loosening of antitrust rules to permit mergers and acquisitions that produced firms with substantial market power, on the grounds of supporting domestic firms and averting the denationalization of entire sectors of the economy.
150	
151	These findings have important implications for the study of industrial policy and competition in international political economy. They highlight the need to move beyond dichotomous frameworks that oppose state intervention and market discipline and to consider the ways in which regulatory regimes can be repurposed to achieve developmental objectives. They also suggest that contemporary debates about the relationship between antitrust and industrial policy, particularly in advanced economies, may benefit from greater attention to the covert and passive forms that such policies can take.
152	
153	By disaggregating discourse, instruments, and effects, the paper provides a framework for analyzing these dynamics and contributes to a more nuanced understanding of the role of the state in shaping market outcomes under conditions of liberalization.
154	
155	## Declaration of Generative AI Use {.unnumbered}
156	
157	The authors report that generative AI tools were used in two limited and clearly bounded ways. In the research process, the authors used NotebookLM to assist with the organization of a large volume of primary-source material (CADE administrative rulings, technical opinions, corporate statements, and media coverage) вҖ” specifically to reconstruct procedural timelines, identify recurring argumentative patterns, and compare the positions of different actors across documents. This use was strictly supportive; all analytical interpretation and conclusions are the authors' own. In the manuscript preparation process, the authors used Claude (Anthropic) for mechanical and organizational tasks under direct author supervision: converting the original manuscript from Word to Quarto Markdown format, merging author-drafted Portuguese-language text into the existing English manuscript on a section-by-section basis, and verifying bibliographic citation keys against the authors' Zotero reference library. No AI-generated analytical content was incorporated without direct authorial review.
158	
159	## References {.unnumbered}

162	:::

```

22	
23	# Plano вҖ” RevisГЈo do artigo com o coautor AndrГ© Vereta-Nahoum
24	
25	> **Status**: ATIVO
26	> **O que Г© este documento**: roteiro para a prГіxima rodada de trabalho no artigo em `3-texts/Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.qmd`, motivada por mensagens do coautor AndrГ© Vereta-Nahoum repassadas por Tales ManГ§ano em 2026-07-14. Cobre quatro frentes: (1) fusГЈo de trechos em portuguГӘs ainda nГЈo fornecidos, (2) resoluГ§ГЈo dos gaps de material de caso jГЎ sinalizados pelos prГіprios autores no texto (`[...]{.mark}`), (3) aprofundamento da revisГЈo de literatura citando Timur Ergen, e (4) criaГ§ГЈo da infraestrutura de bibliografia (`.bib` gerenciado externamente via Zotero/Better BibTeX).
27	> **Elaborado por**: Claude Sonnet 5 (Claude Code, VS Code), a pedido do autor.
28	> **Por quГӘ**: AndrГ© avisou que nГЈo conseguiu, antes de enviar o material, mesclar os trechos em portuguГӘs nem adensar as seГ§Гөes de caso, e sГі volta a mexer no artigo em agosto (envio esperado inГӯcio de setembro). Este plano organiza o que pode ser adiantado agora (infraestrutura de citaГ§ГЈo, mapeamento dos gaps) e o que depende de material que ainda vai chegar (trechos em PT, fontes reais do processo CADE).
29	> **Como usar**: as tarefas 1-2 dependem de material que Tales/AndrГ© ainda vГЈo fornecer вҖ” nГЈo iniciar a reescrita sem esse insumo. As tarefas 4-5 (infraestrutura do `.bib`) podem ser adiantadas de forma independente. A tarefa 3 (literatura) pode comeГ§ar jГЎ com as duas referГӘncias do Ergen abaixo, mas idealmente aguarda uma lista mais completa se AndrГ© tiver outras sugestГөes.
30

32	
33	## 0. Contexto (mensagens do AndrГ©, 2026-07-08)
34	
35	> "Achei que ia conseguir encaixar os trechos da versГЈo em portuguГӘs na versГЈo que tenho antes de te enviar e pedir para dar mais densidade ao material dos casos. Precisamos tambГ©m de mais literatura sobre industrial policy e antitrust. Vale citar o Timur Ergen. [...] Agora sГі consigo voltar a mexer em agosto. EsperanГ§osamente, mandarГӯamos no inГӯcio de setembro."
36	>
37	> "Uma coisa que vocГӘ pode ir fazendo Г© jogar os trechos do artigo em portuguГӘs neste arquivo, mesmo que seja em portuguГӘs para reescrita."
38	>
39	> "Outra coisa Г© ver os trechos em amarelo, que precisam de atenГ§ГЈo (alguma informaГ§ГЈo ou material dos casos)"
40	
41	Artigos do Timur Ergen indicados:
42	- Ergen, Timur & Kohl, Sebastian (2019). "Varieties of Economization in Competition Policy: Institutional Change in German and American Antitrust, 1960вҖ“2000." *Review of International Political Economy*, 26(2), 256вҖ“286. DOI: 10.1080/09692290.2018.1563557
43	- Ergen, Timur & Kohl, Sebastian (2022). "Rival views of economic competition." *Socio-Economic Review*, 20(3), 937вҖ“965. DOI: 10.1093/ser/mwaa041
44

52	    *   Inserir trechos em portuguГӘs fornecidos por Tales (formato a definir: bloco de comentГЎrio Quarto `<!-- PT: ... -->` ao lado do trecho em inglГӘs correspondente, ou seГ§ГЈo separada no fim do arquivo вҖ” decidir com Tales quando o material chegar).
53	    *   Resolver os marcadores `[...]{.mark}` listados na SeГ§ГЈo 2 abaixo, um a um, conforme material real for chegando (do AndrГ© ou de pesquisa em processos do CADE).
54	    *   Expandir a SeГ§ГЈo 2 (e possivelmente a Discussion) com a literatura do Ergen & Kohl e outras referГӘncias indicadas.
55	    *   Adicionar `bibliography: Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.bib` ao YAML do front matter, e (depois que o .bib existir) converter citaГ§Гөes em texto plano para `@citekey`.

Tales configura a coleГ§ГЈo no Zotero e aponta o auto-export para este caminho.

I see the ATIVO plan is addressing the literature expansion for Section 2, but it's outdatedвҖ”it references old file paths and mentions resolved issues that are no longer relevant. I need to refresh this plan with current file locations and remove the stale markers.

Rather than creating a new plan, I'll update the existing one to reflect what's actually been completed (bibliography infrastructure, marker resolution, citation key conversion) and add new tasks for incorporating Foster-Thelen (2024/2025) with specific proposed text and locations. I'll also add the language-review findings as a task section. This approach maintains continuity and avoids duplicating planning efforts. I'm identifying the specific insertion points in the manuscript where Foster-Thelen's framework fits best: first in Section 2 to extend the historical institutionalist discussion of administrative discretion as a driver of institutional change, then more substantially in the Discussion section where I can introduce the coordination-rights typology and reframe the Gerdau/Ambev cases as a domestically-specific path to oligopolistic competition through executive intervention rather than judicial capture.

I should also consider whether to integrate Ergen & Kohl's work since it's already been extracted and AndrГ© originally requested itвҖ”though the current focus is on Foster-Thelen, I may need to clarify how both literatures complement the revision. I haven't actually read the Ergen-Kohl texts in this conversation, so I should keep the plan focused on Foster-Thelen for now and note Ergen-Kohl as related work that's already extracted but would need separate verification before I integrate it.

I'm also catching some language issues in the document: a typo ("explicital" should be "explicit"), missing articles before "CADE," inconsistent dash formatting, and what looks like an untranslated Portuguese phrase that needs fixing. I'm also noting a placeholder that needs author input, and there's a minor terminology question about whether to standardize "competition law" versus "antitrust law" throughout. For the straightforward fixes like typos and spacing issues, I can apply those directly, but I should flag the more complex onesвҖ”the dangling modifiers, run-on sentences, and the Portuguese noteвҖ”for the author to review since they involve judgment calls about phrasing and content. ent gap), I'll present the substantive rewrites as suggestions in my response and ask for author approval since those involve structural changes to co-authored text. My plan is to apply the straightforward mechanical fixes directly, then document the full language reviewвҖ”including the suggested rewritesвҖ”in an updated plan document with pending tasks for author confirmation.

I'm realizing the distinction between what needs approval: the article text itself in `3-texts/` requires explicit sign-off before committing, but the plan file updates can go through with a NEWS.md entry following the synchronized commit policy. So I can commit the plan changes immediately, but the mechanical fixes inside the article file need to wait for approval. respects the user's authority over commits involving primary-authorship changes. I'll apply the mechanical fixes to the qmd file, update the plan with new tasks and literature notes, then present everything and ask for explicit approval before committing anything.

</details>

IncorporaГ§ГЈo de Foster & Thelen (2024, 2025)\n\n> **Contexto**: durante uma avaliaГ§ГЈo crГӯtica de prontidГЈo do artigo para a RIPE (2026-07-16), veio Г  luz que o `.bib` jГЎ continha `Foster-Thelen2024` e `Foster-Thelen2025` (ambos de Kathleen Thelen, coautora de `Mahoney-Thelen2010`, o framework central do artigo) sem que nenhum dos dois fosse citado no `.qmd`. Os dois textos foram extraГӯdos integralmente para `9-vers/references/Foster-Thelen-2024-Brandeis-in-Brussels.txt` e `9-vers/references/Foster-Thelen-2025-Coordination-Rights-Competition-Law.txt` e lidos por completo (nГЈo sГі abstract) вҖ” ver avaliaГ§ГЈo na conversa de 2026-07-22. ConclusГЈo da leitura: nenhum dos dois ameaГ§a a novidade do argumento (nenhum usa o vocabulГЎrio de \"conversГЈo institucional\", nenhum discute o Sul Global), mas o framework de 2025 Г© uma ferramenta analГӯtica genuinamente Гәtil para precisar os casos Gerdau/Ambev.\n\n### 5.1. O que cada texto oferece\n\n- **Foster & Thelen (2024), \"Brandeis in Brussels?\"**: mecanismo central Г© a *discriГ§ГЈo administrativa/burocrГЎtica* como motor de mudanГ§a institucional em direito da concorrГӘncia (a ComissГЈo Europeia decide e executa direto, sem chancela judicial prГ©via; nos EUA, o FTC dependeu de tribunais e foi repetidamente bloqueado). Uso proposto: **citaГ§ГЈo de apoio, uma frase**, nГЈo um novo parГЎgrafo вҖ” reforГ§a o ponto jГЎ feito no artigo sobre a recomposiГ§ГЈo do CADE (substituiГ§ГЈo de conselheiros por economistas apГіs o caso Gerdau) como mecanismo de conversГЈo institucional sem mudanГ§a legislativa.\n- **Foster & Thelen (2025), \"Coordination Rights, Competition Law and Varieties of Capitalism\"**: framework com duas dimensГөes вҖ” **direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo horizontal** (entre firmas nГЈo-dominantes) e **direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica** (poder de firmas dominantes) вҖ” gerando 4 tipos ideais (cartelista, oligopolista, cooperativo, arm's-length). Uso proposto: **1-2 parГЎgrafos novos na seГ§ГЈo \"Discussion\"**, recastando a trajetГіria Gerdau/Ambev como uma concessГЈo deliberada de direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica a firmas dominantes domГ©sticas вҖ” chegando ao mesmo \"quadrante oligopolista\" que os EUA (Foster & Thelen descrevem isso para o caso americano pГіs-1980), mas por uma via institucional distinta: intervenГ§ГЈo executiva direta e justificativa developmentalista/defensiva (competir com hierarquias corporativas estrangeiras jГЎ existentes), nГЈo captura ideolГіgica de longo prazo pela Chicago School via tribunais. Isso Г© uma extensГЈo do framework para um caso (economia emergente/catch-up sob liberalizaГ§ГЈo) que os prГіprios autores nГЈo cobrem вҖ” o tipo de \"conversa com a literatura\" que reviewers de RIPE valorizam.\n\n### 5.2. Pontos de inserГ§ГЈo propostos no `.qmd`\n\n1. **SeГ§ГЈo \"Antitrust and Industrial Policy: Reframing the Relationship\"**, no parГЎgrafo que cita `@Dobbin1994; @Prasad2012` (linha ~48, \"Historical institutionalist accounts have pointed in this direction...\"): adicionar `@Foster-Thelen2024` Г  lista de exemplos de como a discriГ§ГЈo administrativa (nГЈo a mudanГ§a formal da lei) Г© o motor reconhecido de conversГЈo institucional em direito da concorrГӘncia вҖ” uma frase, sem reescrever o parГЎgrafo.\n2. **SeГ§ГЈo \"Discussion: Institutional Conversion and the Reconfiguration of State Intervention\"** (linhas 137-143): inserir 1-2 novos parГЎgrafos logo apГіs o parГЎgrafo que jГЎ cita `@Mahoney-Thelen2010` implicitamente (linha 139), introduzindo o vocabulГЎrio de \"coordination rights\" de `@Foster-Thelen2025` e posicionando explicitamente os casos Gerdau/Ambev como concessГЈo de direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica por via developmentalista вҖ” distinta, mas paralela em resultado, Г  via americana de captura ideolГіgica que os prГіprios Foster & Thelen descrevem. Rascunho de abertura sugerido para o primeiro parГЎgrafo novo (para revisГЈo do AndrГ©/Tales, nГЈo para inserir literalmente sem revisГЈo):\n\n   > *\"This trajectory can be further specified using Foster and Thelen's (2025) distinction between horizontal coordination rights (among non-dominant firms) and hierarchical coordination rights (exercised by dominant firms). In these terms, the Brazilian antitrust regime moved from a nominally arm's-length model toward what Foster and Thelen call an oligopolistic competition model вҖ” one in which dominant firms are granted wide latitude to consolidate market power. Notably, Brazil arrived at the same structural outcome that Foster and Thelen document for the United States after the 1980s, but through a distinct institutional pathway: not the gradual, court-mediated ideological capture of antitrust doctrine by Chicago School economics, but direct executive intervention justified in developmentalist terms вҖ” the perceived need to arm domestic firms against the hierarchical coordination rights already exercised by larger foreign multinationals.\"*\n\n3. Avaliar se cabe tambГ©m uma frase na IntroduГ§ГЈo (linha ~34, onde o artigo jГЎ conecta o caso brasileiro aos debates contemporГўneos de antitruste nos EUA/UE) citando `@Foster-Thelen2025` como o framework comparativo mais recente sobre essa mesma tensГЈo hierГЎrquica вҖ” decisГЈo do AndrГ©/Tales, pode ser redundante com o ponto 2.\n\n### 5.3. PendГӘncias antes de executar\n\n- Confirmar com AndrГ© se o vocabulГЎrio de \"coordination rights\" deve ser adotado como camada analГӯtica adicional (ao lado de \"conversГЈo institucional\") ou sГі citado como paralelo comparativo вҖ” a leitura integral mostrou que sГЈo frameworks complementares, nГЈo substitutos, e a escolha afeta quanto texto novo Г© necessГЎrio.\n- Ergen & Kohl (2019/2022) seguem pendentes de leitura integral (sГі extraГӯdos) вҖ” tarefa separada, mesma seГ§ГЈo de literatura, mas fora do escopo desta rodada atГ© serem lidos.\n\n---\n\n## 6. RevisГЈo de LГӯngua do `.qmd` (2026-07-22)\n\nReleitura integral do artigo em 2026-07-22 encontrou o texto em estado bem mais avanГ§ado do que na versГЈo de 2026-07-14 (marcadores `[...]{.mark}` majoritariamente resolvidos, citaГ§Гөes reais do processo CADE incorporadas, seГ§Гөes sem numeraГ§ГЈo manual). TambГ©m encontrou trechos comentados (`<!-- ... -->`) que parecem cortes deliberados de conteГәdo para controle de word count вҖ” nГЈo tratados como erro, apenas registrados aqui para conhecimento.\n\n**JГЎ corrigido nesta rodada** (mecГўnico, baixo risco, aplicado direto):\n- \"explicital denial\" вҶ’ \"explicit denial\" (IntroduГ§ГЈo)\n- \"administrative rulings by Administrative Council\" вҶ’ \"by the Administrative Council\" (artigo faltando)\n- \"the CADE was strengthened\" вҶ’ \"CADE was strengthened\" (consistГӘncia вҖ” resto do texto nГЈo usa artigo antes de CADE)\n- \"market definition---create\" вҶ’ \"market definition вҖ” create\" (travessГЈo triplo isolado, inconsistente com o resto do texto que usa \"вҖ”\")\n- \"advanced by the firms.CADE's technical analysis\" вҶ’ \"advanced by the firms. CADE's technical analysis\" (espaГ§o faltando entre frases)\n\n**Pendente de decisГЈo do autor/coautor** (mudam frase/estrutura, nГЈo sГЈo sГі typo вҖ” nГЈo aplicados sem confirmaГ§ГЈo):\n1. **Frase em portuguГӘs nГЈo traduzida**, seГ§ГЈo Gerdau-Pains: *\"Efficiency, in this context, significa que a empresa poderia ter 'sinergias', ou seja, pelos ganhos de centralizaГ§ГЈo logГӯstica e de produГ§ГЈo ela poderia reduzir os custos de produГ§ГЈo o produto no mercado mais barato e/ou aumentar suas margens.\"* вҖ” nota de trabalho do autor ainda em portuguГӘs (a frase em portuguГӘs tambГ©m tem uma lacuna gramatical: \"reduzir os custos de produГ§ГЈo o produto\" parece faltar uma palavra, ex. \"e vender o produto\"). Proposta de traduГ§ГЈo para revisГЈo: *\"Efficiency, in this context, meant that the firm could achieve 'synergies' вҖ” that is, through gains from logistical and productive centralization, it could reduce production costs, sell more cheaply in the market, and/or increase its margins.\"*\n2. **Modificador solto**, caso Gerdau: *\"As chairman Jorge Gerdau Johannpeter put it, CADE was 'yet another chapter in the delay of Brazil's economic opening,' [@Seidl1996] arguing that antitrust law was anachronistic...\"* вҖ” gramaticalmente, \"arguing\" fica pendurado em \"CADE\" (sujeito mais prГіximo), mas quem argumenta Г© o prГіprio Johannpeter. SugestГЈo: fechar a citaГ§ГЈo com ponto-e-vГӯrgula e reiniciar com \"he argued that...\".\n3. **Referente perdido**, mesmo parГЎgrafo: \"...the note added that the sector was open to international competition...\" вҖ” \"the note\" aparece sem ter sido introduzida (a versГЈo anterior do texto tinha \"in an official note\" antes desse trecho; foi cortada na ediГ§ГЈo, deixando a referГӘncia solta). Precisa reintroduzir \"in an official statement\" (ou similar) antes, ou remover \"the note added\".\n4. **Sujeito incoerente**, caso Ambev: *\"The operation was announced by its proponents as essential for the creation of a 'green-and-yellow multinational'... and warned that failure to achieve sufficient scale would result in...\"* вҖ” sujeito gramatical de \"warned\" Г© \"the operation\" (por causa da voz passiva anterior), mas uma operaГ§ГЈo nГЈo pode \"avisar\". SugestГЈo: \"...global industry leaders; its proponents warned that failure to achieve...\"\n5. **ParГӘnteses/paralelismo confuso**, caso Gerdau: \"...approved the acquisition with conditions, including divestments and commitments regarding supply and designed to mitigate anti-competitive effects.\" вҖ” \"and designed to mitigate\" flutua sem sujeito claro. SugestГЈo: \"...with conditions вҖ” including divestments and supply-related commitments вҖ” designed to mitigate anti-competitive effects.\"\n6. **Marcador ainda aberto** (nГЈo Г© erro de lГӯngua, Г© pendГӘncia de fonte): `[CitaГ§ГЈo do Processo?]` no caso Ambev, parГЎgrafo do MinistГ©rio do Desenvolvimento вҖ” mantido intocado, conforme proibiГ§ГЈo de preencher marcadores de citaГ§ГЈo pendente.\n7. **ConsistГӘncia terminolГіgica (menor, opcional)**: o artigo alterna \"1994 competition law\" e \"1994 antitrust law\" para se referir Г  mesma lei вҖ” considerar padronizar em um termo sГі, se os autores concordarem que nГЈo hГЎ distinГ§ГЈo proposital.\n\n---\n\n## 4. 100	## 5. IncorporaГ§ГЈo de Foster & Thelen (2024, 2025)
101	
102	> **Contexto**: durante uma avaliaГ§ГЈo crГӯtica de prontidГЈo do artigo para a RIPE (2026-07-16), veio Г  luz que o `.bib` jГЎ continha `Foster-Thelen2024` e `Foster-Thelen2025` (ambos de Kathleen Thelen, coautora de `Mahoney-Thelen2010`, o framework central do artigo) sem que nenhum dos dois fosse citado no `.qmd`. Os dois textos foram extraГӯdos integralmente para `9-vers/references/Foster-Thelen-2024-Brandeis-in-Brussels.txt` e `9-vers/references/Foster-Thelen-2025-Coordination-Rights-Competition-Law.txt` e lidos por completo (nГЈo sГі abstract) вҖ” ver avaliaГ§ГЈo na conversa de 2026-07-22. ConclusГЈo da leitura: nenhum dos dois ameaГ§a a novidade do argumento (nenhum usa o vocabulГЎrio de "conversГЈo institucional", nenhum discute o Sul Global), mas o framework de 2025 Г© uma ferramenta analГӯtica genuinamente Гәtil para precisar os casos Gerdau/Ambev.
103	
104	### 5.1. O que cada texto oferece
105	
106	- **Foster & Thelen (2024), "Brandeis in Brussels?"**: mecanismo central Г© a *discriГ§ГЈo administrativa/burocrГЎtica* como motor de mudanГ§a institucional em direito da concorrГӘncia (a ComissГЈo Europeia decide e executa direto, sem chancela judicial prГ©via; nos EUA, o FTC dependeu de tribunais e foi repetidamente bloqueado). Uso proposto: **citaГ§ГЈo de apoio, uma frase**, nГЈo um novo parГЎgrafo вҖ” reforГ§a o ponto jГЎ feito no artigo sobre a recomposiГ§ГЈo do CADE (substituiГ§ГЈo de conselheiros por economistas apГіs o caso Gerdau) como mecanismo de conversГЈo institucional sem mudanГ§a legislativa.
107	- **Foster & Thelen (2025), "Coordination Rights, Competition Law and Varieties of Capitalism"**: framework com duas dimensГөes вҖ” **direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo horizontal** (entre firmas nГЈo-dominantes) e **direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica** (poder de firmas dominantes) вҖ” gerando 4 tipos ideais (cartelista, oligopolista, cooperativo, arm's-length). Uso proposto: **1-2 parГЎgrafos novos na seГ§ГЈo "Discussion"**, recastando a trajetГіria Gerdau/Ambev como uma concessГЈo deliberada de direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica a firmas dominantes domГ©sticas вҖ” chegando ao mesmo "quadrante oligopolista" que os EUA (Foster & Thelen descrevem isso para o caso americano pГіs-1980), mas por uma via institucional distinta: intervenГ§ГЈo executiva direta e justificativa developmentalista/defensiva (competir com hierarquias corporativas estrangeiras jГЎ existentes), nГЈo captura ideolГіgica de longo prazo pela Chicago School via tribunais. Isso Г© uma extensГЈo do framework para um caso (economia emergente/catch-up sob liberalizaГ§ГЈo) que os prГіprios autores nГЈo cobrem вҖ” o tipo de "conversa com a literatura" que reviewers de RIPE valorizam.
108	
109	### 5.2. Pontos de inserГ§ГЈo propostos no `.qmd`
110	
111	1. **SeГ§ГЈo "Antitrust and Industrial Policy: Reframing the Relationship"**, no parГЎgrafo que cita `@Dobbin1994; @Prasad2012` (linha ~48, "Historical institutionalist accounts have pointed in this direction..."): adicionar `@Foster-Thelen2024` Г  lista de exemplos de como a discriГ§ГЈo administrativa (nГЈo a mudanГ§a formal da lei) Г© o motor reconhecido de conversГЈo institucional em direito da concorrГӘncia вҖ” uma frase, sem reescrever o parГЎgrafo.
112	2. **SeГ§ГЈo "Discussion: Institutional Conversion and the Reconfiguration of State Intervention"** (linhas 137-143): inserir 1-2 novos parГЎgrafos logo apГіs o parГЎgrafo que jГЎ cita `@Mahoney-Thelen2010` implicitamente (linha 139), introduzindo o vocabulГЎrio de "coordination rights" de `@Foster-Thelen2025` e posicionando explicitamente os casos Gerdau/Ambev como concessГЈo de direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica por via developmentalista вҖ” distinta, mas paralela em resultado, Г  via americana de captura ideolГіgica que os prГіprios Foster & Thelen descrevem. Rascunho de abertura sugerido para o primeiro parГЎgrafo novo (para revisГЈo do AndrГ©/Tales, nГЈo para inserir literalmente sem revisГЈo):
113	
114	   > *"This trajectory can be further specified using Foster and Thelen's (2025) distinction between horizontal coordination rights (among non-dominant firms) and hierarchical coordination rights (exercised by dominant firms). In these terms, the Brazilian antitrust regime moved from a nominally arm's-length model toward what Foster and Thelen call an oligopolistic competition model вҖ” one in which dominant firms are granted wide latitude to consolidate market power. Notably, Brazil arrived at the same structural outcome that Foster and Thelen document for the United States after the 1980s, but through a distinct institutional pathway: not the gradual, court-mediated ideological capture of antitrust doctrine by Chicago School economics, but direct executive intervention justified in developmentalist terms вҖ” the perceived need to arm domestic firms against the hierarchical coordination rights already exercised by larger foreign multinationals."*

## O que eu fiz

**Reli o `.qmd` inteiro** вҖ” e percebi que ele mudou bastante desde a Гәltima vez que li nesta conversa (provavelmente ediГ§ГЈo sua ou do AndrГ© direto na IDE, em paralelo): as seГ§Гөes perderam a numeraГ§ГЈo manual, os marcadores `[...]{.mark}` foram quase todos resolvidos com fontes reais do processo CADE, e o texto agora tem vГЎrios trechos comentados (`<!-- -->`) que parecem cortes deliberados de conteГәdo.

**Corrigi direto 5 problemas mecГўnicos** (typos/espaГ§amento/consistГӘncia, baixo risco, sem mudar sentido):
- "explicital denial" вҶ’ "explicit denial"
- artigo faltando antes de "Administrative Council for Economic Defense"
- "the CADE" вҶ’ "CADE" (consistГӘncia com o resto do texto)
- travessГЈo triplo isolado вҶ’ padronizado para "вҖ”"
- espaГ§o faltando entre duas frases coladas ("firms.CADE's" вҶ’ "firms. CADE's")

**Escrevi o plano de incorporaГ§ГЈo** вҖ” atualizei o plano jГЎ `ATIVO` `9-vers/plan/2026-07-14_Plano_Revisao_Artigo_Coautor.md` (em vez de criar um novo: ele jГЎ cobria exatamente esta frente de "revisГЈo de literatura com o coautor"). Marquei como concluГӯdas as tarefas que jГЎ foram feitas (infraestrutura de citaГ§ГЈo, resoluГ§ГЈo de marcadores) e adicionei duas seГ§Гөes novas:

- **SeГ§ГЈo 5** вҖ” como incorporar Foster & Thelen: 2024 como citaГ§ГЈo de apoio de uma frase (SeГ§ГЈo 2 do artigo); 2025 como 1-2 parГЎgrafos novos na Discussion, com um rascunho de abertura proposto recastando Gerdau/Ambev em termos de "direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica" вҖ” Brasil chegando ao mesmo resultado estrutural que os EUA, por uma via institucional diferente (intervenГ§ГЈo executiva developmentalista, nГЈo captura ideolГіgica via tribunais).
- **SeГ§ГЈo 6** вҖ” os 5 achados substantivos da revisГЈo de lГӯngua que **nГЈo** aplicei sozinho porque mudam frase/estrutura (nГЈo sГЈo sГі typo): uma frase em portuguГӘs nГЈo traduzida no meio do texto em inglГӘs (com erro gramatical na prГіpria frase em portuguГӘs), um modificador solto no caso Gerdau, um "the note" sem referente (perdido numa ediГ§ГЈo anterior), um sujeito incoerente no caso Ambev ("the operation... warned"), e um paralelismo confuso nas condiГ§Гөes impostas Г  Gerdau. Cada um tem uma sugestГЈo de reescrita para vocГӘs avaliarem.

## O que falta decidir
NГЈo toquei nos 5 itens da SeГ§ГЈo 6 nem redigi a incorporaГ§ГЈo de Ergen & Kohl (2019/2022) вҖ” esses textos estГЈo extraГӯdos em `9-vers/references/` mas eu ainda nГЈo os li integralmente nesta conversa. TambГ©m nГЈo commitei nada ainda (nem o plano, nem os 5 typos corrigidos no `.qmd`) вҖ” quer que eu aplique as 5 reescritas sugeridas da SeГ§ГЈo 6 e jГЎ prepare um commit Гәnico (artigo + plano + `NEWS.md`), ou prefere revisar o plano primeiro?

### [2026-07-22 19:06] UsuГЎrio

<ide_selection>The user selected the lines 85 to 86 from c:\Users\Mancano\Documents\MancanoSync\Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust\3-texts\Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.qmd:
The Gerdau-Pains case provides an early and revealing instance of the tensions inherent in the new competition regime. The Pains acquisition was among the first post-1994 CADE cases to generate widespread controversy [@Onto2017, p. 59]. In the mid-1990s, Gerdau's acquisition of assets associated with the German group Korf, including the Pains steel mill, raised concerns within CADE about excessive concentration in the domestic market for long steel products, particularly rebar.[^gerdau-marketshare] Central to the dispute were the definition of the relevant market and the assessment of potential efficiency gains, which could justify, in the terms of the antitrust law of the period, increased market concentration. Efficiency, in this context, significa que a empresa poderia ter "sinergias", ou seja, pelos ganhos de centralizaГ§ГЈo logГӯstica e de produГ§ГЈo ela poderia reduzir os custos de produГ§ГЈo o produto no mercado mais barato e/ou aumentar suas margens. The case unfolded over a protracted period marked by legal disputes, political intervention, and institutional transformation.


This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_selection>

Mas vocГӘ nГЈo perrcebeu o trecho em portugues ao ler?

Proposta de traduГ§ГЈo: *"Efficiency, in this context, meant that the firm could achieve 'synergies' вҖ” that is, through gains from logistical and productive centralization, it could reduce production costs, sell more cheaply in the market, and/or increase its margins."*
