79	## The Gerdau--Pains Case: Conflict, Intervention, and the Reinterpretation of Antitrust
80	
81	<!--
82	Gerdau was founded in Porto Alegre in 1901 as a nail manufacturer and expanded significantly after the Second World War amid strong demand for its products and import restrictions. Instability in steel supply led the firm to integrate vertically, acquiring SiderГәrgica Riograndense in 1948 to secure its raw materials; it then expanded its operations in Rio Grande do Sul and the Southeast with the support of the military regime, which facilitated the acquisition of a mill in Pernambuco in 1969. During the so-called Brazilian Economic Miracle, Gerdau continued to expand: in 1973, in a joint venture with the German group Thyssen ATH and bank financing, it acquired the state-owned Cosigua mill in Rio de Janeiro, making Sepetiba Bay a hub of its operations. The company also purchased SiderГәrgica GuaГӯra (1972) and Cia. SiderГәrgica de Alagoas (1974), built SiderГәrgica Cearense (1981) with support from the CearГЎ state government, and acquired Usina SiderГәrgica Paraense (1986); its first operation in Minas Gerais, Usina BarГЈo de Cocais, was purchased in 1988 following its privatization. In short, Gerdau had received substantial federal support for its ventures since the dictatorship, and grew considerably through the privatization of state-owned steel mills and the acquisition and construction of mills across the country, much of it financed with public loans and tax incentives. In the steel sector, the 1990s economic opening saw state-owned and even foreign mills being sold off as the state's role in certain economic sectors was redefined [@Nassif1995; @Teixeira-etal2001, p. 186], with the developmentalist tradition giving way to a liberal vision of the state's role [@Schneider2015; @Onto2017]. Non-tariff barriers to imports were suspended, and import tariffs were gradually reduced.
83	-->
84	
85	The Gerdau-Pains case provides an early and revealing instance of the tensions inherent in the new competition regime. The Pains acquisition was among the first post-1994 CADE cases to generate widespread controversy [@Onto2017, p. 59]. In the mid-1990s, Gerdau's acquisition of assets associated with the German group Korf, including the Pains steel mill, raised concerns within CADE about excessive concentration in the domestic market for long steel products, particularly rebar.[^gerdau-marketshare] Central to the dispute were the definition of the relevant market and the assessment of potential efficiency gains, which could justify, in the terms of the antitrust law of the period, increased market concentration. Efficiency, in this context, significa que a empresa poderia ter "sinergias", ou seja, pelos ganhos de centralizaГ§ГЈo logГӯstica e de produГ§ГЈo ela poderia reduzir os custos de produГ§ГЈo o produto no mercado mais barato e/ou aumentar suas margens. The case unfolded over a protracted period marked by legal disputes, political intervention, and institutional transformation.
86	
87	The firmsвҖҷ legal teams defined the relevant market as international in nature, asserting steel was a global commodity. They also claimed the merger would produce efficiency gains, including access to proprietary technologies and the rescue of financially distressed operations [@CADE1995-AC16-1994].
88	
89	In its initial assessment, CADE adopted a relatively strict interpretation of competition principles. The authority emphasized that the merger would substantially increase concentration in the domestic market and questioned the validity of the efficiency claims advanced by the firms. CADE's technical analysis suggested that, despite trade liberalization, domestic prices for steel products remained largely decoupled from international benchmarks, indicating that the market retained a predominantly national character. This finding implied that the merger would create significant market power within Brazil, raising concerns about potential anti-competitive effects [@CADE1995-AC16-1994].
90	
91	The authority initially issued a partial approval, requiring the deconstitution of part of the operation. Gerdau and its allies contested this interpretation, arguing that steel should be understood as a global commodity subject to international competition. By framing the relevant market in global terms, they sought to demonstrate that domestic concentration was less problematic and that the merger was necessary to achieve the scale required to compete internationally [@Seidl1996].
92	
93	Rather than pursue the matter through the courts, Gerdau pressured FHC's Justice Minister, Nelson Jobim вҖ” who immediately took the firm's side against the regulator вҖ” despite the recently enacted Law 8,884/94, whose Article 50 established that CADE's decisions "are not subject to review within the Executive Branch" [@Brasil1994]. In a departmental order, Jobim nonetheless granted the appeal Gerdau had filed with the ministry against CADE's October decision ordering the dissolution of its purchase of Pains, suspending that decision on the grounds that "the Brazilian steel market is open to international products, and the concentration act does not eliminate competition in a substantial part of the market" [@Ribeiro1995a]. In the minister's view, since steel products were a commodity, it made no sense to treat the roughly 46-percent concentration in common long steel at stake as grounds for a veto, since the product could easily be imported. The order reached further than the case at hand: Jobim held that CADE had the final word only on infractions against the economic order вҖ” price-fixing agreements, for instance вҖ” but not on the acquisition and merger of firms [@Ribeiro1995a]. Without any legislative change, the minister thus redrew the boundary of the agency's jurisdiction at precisely the point where the new competition regime bore on industrial structure, subordinating merger review to the Executive while leaving the letter of Law 8,884/94 intact. Gerdau's public response was forceful: according to the firm, CADE's opposition to the Pains purchase "does not follow the government's political strategy, nor does it track the evolution of the international scenario" [@1995-10-14_news_fsp_contra-a-mare], suggesting that the firm counted on active government support for the acquisition. Gerdau widened its attacks on CADE, arguing that the decision was anticompetitive and undermined the country's economic opening, and that the agency's definition of the relevant market was inappropriate вҖ” a rhetorical move resembling that of Chicago School economists against the earlier antitrust tradition. As chairman Jorge Gerdau Johannpeter put it, CADE was "yet another chapter in the delay of Brazil's economic opening," [@Seidl1996] arguing that antitrust law was anachronistic because it was tied to a closed-market vision, and that the country should either adopt structural solutions for competitiveness or watch businesses turn, out of desperation, to protectionist ones. The chairman argued that mergers and acquisitions were the path to competitiveness and efficiency in an era of economic globalization, and that CADE interfered with this process through legislation far more restrictive than that of First World and Asian countries and that the steel sector was inherently oligopolistic вҖ” profitable only at large production scales вҖ” pointing to a single steel group operating in France and only two in England, and to a merger trend already under way among U.S. steelmakers; the note added that the sector was open to international competition, since import tariffs stood at around 10 percent [@Seidl1996].
94	
95	<!--
96	The firms challenged the decision, refused to comply, and appealed to the Minister of Justice, arguing that the legal framework granting CADE final authority was unconstitutional. The Minister accepted the appeal and suspended enforcement, effectively intervening in the process. The argument raised by Gerdau's legal team was supported by broader claims about the need for consolidation in the context of globalization, with political actors assuming steel is a global commodity and emphasizing that mergers and acquisitions among Brazilian firms in the industry were essential for competitiveness in an open economy. -->
97	
98	The case was then reconsidered by a reconfigured CADE, which ultimately approved the acquisition with conditions, including divestments and commitments regarding supply and designed to mitigate anti-competitive effects. However, the outcome of the case extended beyond the specific operation. It had significant institutional consequences and contributed to the redefinition of antitrust policy in Brazil. In response to the tensions generated by the decision, President Cardoso altered the composition of CADE, replacing members with legal backgrounds with economists trained in transaction-cost approaches to antitrust [@Onto2017]. President Cardoso reportedly warned the newly appointed counselors: "Don't cause me any problems!" [@Onto2017, p. 70]. 
99	
100	<!-- According to LГәcia Helena Salgado, one of the economists who joined CADE shortly after the Gerdau-Pains case, this period marked the beginning of a search for the "rationalization," of the agency's activity вҖ” a search that, for the Presidency, was meant to avoid the political problems CADE could generate. As sociologist Carlos Bello recounts it, Finance Minister Pedro Malan and Justice Minister Nelson Jobim publicly stated that the new counselors should be technocrats; shortly before the appointments were announced, the two ministers met with Minister JosГ© Serra and President Cardoso and agreed to nominate specialized technical experts with legal or economic training. For Bello, the president was "very concerned that political balkanization could generate new conflicts with CADE ... the economists would serve to block CADE," rather than to make it more effective [@Onto2017, p. 70]. -->
101	
102	This shift in expertise contributed to a reorientation of the authority toward a more permissive stance regarding mergers and acquisitions, emphasizing efficiency and conduct remedies over structural constraints. The case established a precedent for the acceptance of mergers justified in terms of international competitiveness. This transformation can be understood as part of a broader process of institutional conversion [@Mahoney-Thelen2010], in which the meaning and application of competition law were reshaped without formal legislative change.
103	
104	<!-- The reconsideration process stretched over several years. Gerdau was ultimately allowed to keep the Pains mill after complying with ten conditions set by the council to prevent market concentration in the long-steel sector, including reconstituting and selling Pains's rolling unit in Contagem (MG), selling its transport subsidiary (Transpains), and rehiring previously dismissed workers [@1998-03-19_news_fsp_gerdau-fica-com-a-pains-cade-aprova]. The group also redirected more than half of Pains's output toward special long steel вҖ” reducing its share of the common long-steel market while helping to deconcentrate the special long-steel segment, then dominated by three other firms вҖ” and committed to additional investments of US$78 million, along with further measures on production reorientation, distribution, technology, and periodic reporting to CADE. The settlement marked the saturation of the firm's domestic growth and the acceleration of its international expansion across the Americas вҖ” Uruguay, Chile, Canada, and Argentina вҖ” and, from the late 1990s onward, particularly the United States: in 1999 Gerdau acquired 75 percent of AmeriSteel Corporation, entering the U.S. market with a production capacity of two million tonnes of steel per year [@Teixeira-etal2001, p. 204]. -->
105	
106	[^gerdau-marketshare]: The acquisition would have raised Gerdau's share of the long-steel market from 39.6 to 46.2 percent, and its overall share of the national steel market to roughly 43 percent [@1995-03-23_news_fsp_cade-adia-decisao-sobre-fusao]. When it ordered the deal undone in October 1995, CADE put the resulting share of the market for common long steel at 46.5 percent [@Ribeiro1995a]; under Law 8,884/94 the agency could bar acquisitions and mergers resulting in control of more than 20 percent of a market.
107	
108	## The Ambev Case: Consolidation, Legitimization, and the Stabilization of Conversion
109	
110	The Ambev case, decided at the end of the decade, reflects the consolidation of this new orientation. The merger between Antarctica and Brahma, announced in 1999, brought together the two largest beer producers in the country, creating a firm that controlled approximately 70 percent of the beer market and a substantial share of distribution networks.
111	
112	Throughout the 1990s economic opening, Brazil's brewers had been courted by foreign multinationals for acquisitions or distribution partnerships, seen by many as a prelude to the entry of the sector's leading global firms into the domestic market [@Abdallah2020]. In any eventual competition with these foreign entrants, domestic firms would be at a serious disadvantage: with access only to the costlier domestic credit market, they would have to pay higher interest rates, raising the cost of investment and limiting what they could offer to acquire other firms in Brazil or abroad, while foreign firms вҖ” able to borrow at significantly lower rates, as in the U.S. credit market вҖ” could offer more for the same targets. As Antarctica's chairman put it: "If an American company wanted to acquire a Brazilian brewery, it could pay twice what a domestic competitor offered, since over eight years the rate of return would be identical in both cases" [@Abdallah2020, p. 136]. Since the 1980s, both Brahma and Antarctica had been approached by multinationals such as Anheuser-Busch, Miller, Kirin, Coca-Cola, and Pepsi interested in partnering with them; distribution agreements and joint ventures were repeatedly attempted, but these either failed to materialize or were curtailed by CADE, which at times demanded additional investment commitments that discouraged the foreign firms [@Abdallah2020, p. 140]. Facing the prospect of eventual acquisition by one of these larger, better-capitalized foreign rivals, Brahma's controllers вҖ” Jorge Paulo Lemann and Marcel Telles вҖ” instead pursued a different ambition: to become the world's largest brewer, including through the acquisition of Anheuser-Busch itself [@Abdallah2020, p. 148]. The alternative that emerged from informal conversations between Brahma's and Antarctica's leadership was to merge the country's two largest brewers instead, in order to build a firm strong enough to expand abroad. As Antarctica's Victorio De Marchi recalled of the conversations preceding the merger: "should we leave the foreigners out, and think about building a company strong enough to secure ourselves domestically and allow us to grow abroad?" [@Abdallah2020].
113	
114	The operation was announced by its proponents as essential for the creation of a "green-and-yellow multinational" capable of competing with global industry leaders and warned that failure to achieve sufficient scale would result in the denationalization of the sector. This framing resonated with political actors, who publicly endorsed the merger as part of a broader strategy to strengthen domestic firms in the face of international competition. The merger was in fact announced to the press from the PalГЎcio da Alvorada, with President Cardoso present, following a meeting between the businessmen and the president [@LoPrete1999]; the new firm's international ambitions were championed by Cardoso from the very start of the negotiations.
115	
116	Following the announcement of the merger in July 1999, CADE issued a precautionary measure suspending its implementation pending review. Technical bodies within the government, including the Secretary for Economic Monitoring, challenged the efficiency claims advanced by the firms, arguing that projected cost reductions were overstated and unlikely to benefit consumers. At the same time, debates over the definition of the relevant market reemerged, with the firms advocating for a broad definition encompassing all beverages, while critics insisted on focusing on the beer market, where concentration was extreme and exports remained minimal.
117	
118	Opposition to the merger was led by Kaiser, one of Ambev's rival brewers,[^kaiser-coke] which ran a series of media campaigns arguing that concentrating roughly two-thirds of the country's beer production and sales in the new firm could lead to anticompetitive conduct, including price increases contrary to the national interest: "Kaiser, enemy number one of the union, put out yet another campaign yesterday вҖ” the third вҖ” to prevent the association, which is under review by CADE... the brewer ran ads on radio and in newspapers to try to convince public opinion that the merger harms consumers' interests, since it would bring about market concentration and, consequently, higher prices" [@FernandesPrates2000]. Critics also argued that the operation amounted not to a merger but to Brahma absorbing Antarctica outright, creating a firm capable of eliminating competition and threatening the survival of medium and small rivals, with no significant efficiencies or logistical or technological innovations to benefit consumers, suppliers, or distributors. Kaiser further criticized Ambev for operating as a unified company even before CADE's final ruling вҖ” a fait accompli meant to make the operation practically irreversible regardless of the agency's eventual decision (CADE AC 08012.005846.1999-12, Vol. 2, p. 49).
119	
120	Despite these concerns, the executive branch strongly supported the merger, with the Ministry of Development affirming its alignment with national interests [CitaГ§ГЈo do Processo?]. Minister of Industrial Development ClГіvis Carvalho, for instance, stated that he wanted to see a wave of mergers across several of the country's largest industries вҖ” especially paper, steel, and petrochemicals вҖ” so that they could prosper in international markets, offering up to three billion reais (roughly US$1.7 billion) from the National Bank for Economic and Social Development (BNDES) to finance such deals [@TheEconomist1999]. Public statements by President Cardoso suggested a pragmatic acceptance of market concentration. In an interview with the Jornal do Brasil, Cardoso addressed the merger directly, arguing that Brazilian firms needed to restructure to compete in a more open global economy; asked about the possibility of a "monopoly" forming with the merger, he replied: "But the world today operates in terms of oligopoly. ... What is Coca-Cola? How will they compete, later on, with Coca-Cola, if they are not strong here? Isn't that so? That is the concept. You can no longer think in terms of the domestic market. The market is internationalized. So you have to look at whether there are competitors in the sector or not [...] I don't even want to anticipate whether this will be legal or not [referring to CADE]. Let's discuss it. I am telling you what the global trend is: mega-mergers. That is, we will need Brazilian multinationals" [@Abdallah2020, p. 170]. 
121	
122	Press, congressional, and government pressure on CADE was intense throughout the proceeding, as former counselors later recounted in detail. Ruy Lima, the only counselor to vote against the merger, recalled that CADE was pressured by the government to expedite the case toward a favorable outcome: "The Ambev case was one of CADE's biggest cases, mostly because of the media attention generated by the applicants themselves... There was a political dimension to it; the government applied a great deal of pressure вҖ” calls from ministers, from the leader of the government's party in Congress вҖ” all of it landing on CADE's president, the most visible figure" [@Dutra2009, pp. 101-103].
123	
124	In March 2000, CADE approved the merger subject to a set of conditions formalized in a Performance Commitment Term. These conditions included divestments, measures to ensure access to distribution networks, and commitments to maintain employment levels. While these remedies were intended to address competition concerns, they also reflected a broader acceptance of the merger's underlying rationale.
125	
126	<!-- One option debated within CADE was to require the divestment of one of the resulting firm's major beer brands. Since Brahma also owned Skol вҖ” whose market share was comparable to the Brahma brand itself вҖ” selling it off was seen by some as a feasible restriction that would allow the merger to proceed without creating a monopoly. Counselor LГәcia Helena Salgado, who did not vote in the case because her husband represented Kaiser as counsel, considered the eventual decision a mistake: "I would have approved the deal, but it was obvious вҖ” known to everyone familiar with competition law, since one only had to read the prevailing case law and doctrine вҖ” that an unavoidable condition for approving that case, among others, was the sale of a beer brand to a competitor, along with the plants capable of producing it competitively and the means to distribute it. And that brand, as everyone who knows competition law knew and still knows, was Skol. ... As is well known, that is not what happened. CADE's approval imposed as a condition the sale of a handful of plants the companies were going to close anyway, since they were entirely obsolete, and the sale of a brand that, poor thing, besides being inexpressive, had been вҖ” over the course of the review вҖ” intentionally run down, since its market share had fallen sharply during the period. It was a truly regrettable decision" [@Dutra2009, pp. 61-62]. Ruy Lima likewise recalled that this possibility was discussed, though it was ultimately reduced to the sale of the BavГЎria brand вҖ” a brand still under construction, with roughly nine percent of the market at the start of the case, and considerably less by the time it was decided [@Dutra2009, pp. 101-103]. -->
127	
128	The Ambev case thus illustrates the stabilization of the interpretive shifts that had emerged in the Gerdau case. The ambiguity surrounding market definition and efficiency was no longer a source of acute conflict but had become part of the routine operation of the competition regime. Antitrust policy, in this context, functioned as a mechanism for enabling consolidation while maintaining the formal language of competition and consumer welfare.
129	
130	<!-- For Ruy Lima, this outcome had lasting consequences for the antitrust authority's reputation, signaling that CADE would no longer stand as an obstacle to market concentration after the Ambev case: "I think that, by approving the Ambev case, CADE lost the great opportunity to show what competition defense in Brazil is, and what it should be. ... It seems to me that NestlГ© only felt emboldened to buy Garoto because of that signal CADE sent in the Ambev case... The idea took hold that CADE could always find a way to approve a large-scale concentration act, especially one accompanied by heavy media coverage and political pressure. CADE would always find a way to solve the problem without causing major disruption" [@Dutra2009, pp. 103-104]. Once authorized, the merged firm accelerated its international expansion across the Southern Cone. Although approved with conditions, institutional changes at CADE shortly after the ruling made it unlikely that a firm could be approved again with such a high a market share: "Ambev was born with 70 percent of domestic beer production in its hands. CADE's green light was only possible at that particular moment. CADE made a series of changes to its rules in the 2000s that would not allow the formation of a firm with that market share today" [@Boucas2018]. In 2004, Ambev merged with the Belgian brewer Interbrew, becoming a global company with a strong presence across several continents вҖ” a rapid foreignization of a firm whose creation, only four years earlier, had been justified by the need to preserve a Brazilian champion. In the end, the merged firm's headquarters moved to Belgium, and Brazilians held the same number of board seats as their Belgian counterparts, but from the year following the merger onward the company's CEO вҖ” with only brief interregnums вҖ” was consistently Brazilian, as was much of its senior management. 
131	
132	As Ambev co-president Marcel Telles put it: "If you have a huge German shepherd that barks at your visitors, you have to keep it under control, not kill it. Plausible restrictions need to be created for the merger of two large companies, but the chance for the country to have a global competitor вҖ” a green-and-yellow multinational that will already be born as the world's third-largest brewery вҖ” should not be eliminated" [@Seidl2000]. 
133	 -->
134	
135	[^kaiser-coke]: Kaiser had an equity stake held by Coca-Cola, which Ambev used to argue that the firm was only representing the interests of the foreign multinational that would gain a strong competitor with Ambev's formation [@Abdallah2020].

137	## Discussion: Institutional Conversion and the Reconfiguration of State Intervention

Now I'm working through the fixes for the document. Starting with the Portuguese sentence on line 85 that needs translationвҖ”it's about how efficiency in this context means the firm could achieve synergies through centralized logistics and production, allowing them to reduce costs and offer cheaper products or increase margins.

For fix 2, I'm restructuring the sentence about Jorge Gerdau Johannpeter's quote on line 93. The issue is the dangling modifier where the citation and argument get tangled together. I need to close the quote after "economic opening," add the citation, then use a semicolon and rephrase it as "he argued that" to clarify who's making the point about antitrust law being anachronistic and tied to a closed-market vision.

For fix 3, I'm identifying a massive run-on sentence that combines multiple claims from the chairman about mergers, competitiveness, CADE's restrictive legislation, the oligopolistic nature of steel production, and international competition. The problem is that "the note added" appears without a clear referent вҖ” I need to reintroduce earlier that this information comes from an official note or statement so the reader understands where this claim originates. The fix is to split the sentence into two parts: first, establish the chairman's argument about M&A and CADE's restrictiveness, then introduce a separate official note where Gerdau elaborates on the steel sector's oligopolistic nature with specific examples and the tariff detail, so "the note added" has a clear antecedent.

Now I'm looking at another issue with Ambev вҖ” the sentence has a subject-verb mismatch where "the operation" is announced but then "warned" appears without a proper subject, so I need to clarify that it's the proponents doing both the announcing and the warning.

I'm considering converting this to active voice instead: "Its proponents announced the operation as essential for creating a 'green-and-yellow multinational' capable of competing globally, warning that insufficient scale would lead to sector denationalization." This avoids the awkward repetition and flows more naturally.

Now I'm moving to Fix 5, which has a parallelism problem in how the conditions are listed вҖ” the phrasing breaks down partway through with "designed to mitigate" not properly parallel to the earlier elements. I need to insert a Foster-Thelen2024 citation after the existing historical institutionalist reference to show that administrative discretion in competition law is a recognized mechanism of institutional change, then continue with the point about contemporary liberalizing contexts. The strategic reinterpretation of market definitionsвҖ”particularly by broadening them to encompass international competitionвҖ”allowed policymakers to reconcile high domestic concentration with competition law requirements. This shift was enabled by political intervention and CADE's institutional restructuring, which elevated economic expertise aligned with transaction-cost frameworks and gradually normalized a more permissive approach to mergers that weighed efficiency gains against structural concerns. The outcome was an industrial policy operating through regulatory passivity, cloaked in competition law language. I'm using Foster and Thelen's framework to distinguish between horizontal coordination rights for non-dominant firms versus hierarchical coordination rights exercised by dominant firms, showing how Brazilian antitrust policy shifted toward an oligopolistic competition model that granted dominant firms latitude to consolidate power. This produces a structural outcome similar to the post-1980s U.S. trajectory, but through a different institutional mechanism rather than court-driven change.

40	## Antitrust and Industrial Policy: Reframing the Relationship
41	
42	The relationship between antitrust and industrial policy has long been framed as one of tension, if not outright incompatibility. Early antitrust traditions in the United States, associated with Louis Brandeis and progressive-era reformers, viewed concentration as both an economic and political threat, opposing large-scale corporate organization on the grounds that it undermined democracy and fair competition [@Berk2009; @Hovenkamp2005]. Within this perspective, industrial policy вҖ” understood as state support for specific sectors or firms вҖ” appeared as a dangerous extension of concentrated power, further entrenching dominant actors at the expense of smaller competitors.
43	
44	This skepticism persisted, albeit in different form, within the Chicago School and transaction-cost approaches that came to dominate antitrust thinking in the late twentieth century. While these approaches rejected Brandeisian hostility to large firms, they nonetheless opposed industrial policy interventions on the grounds that they distorted market signals and reduced efficiency [@Posner1979; @Bork1978; @Williamson1975]. Antitrust, in this view, should focus narrowly on consumer welfare, allowing concentration where it produces efficiency gains and intervening only where clear harm can be demonstrated.
45	
46	In recent years, the resurgence of industrial policy in advanced economies has reopened these debates. Scholars and policymakers have increasingly questioned whether strict adherence to competition principles is compatible with the need to foster innovation, build strategic industries, and compete in global markets [@Khan2016; @Khan-Vaheesan2017; @Juhasz-etal2024]. Yet even in this renewed context, the relationship between antitrust and industrial policy is often framed as a trade-off, with governments facing a choice between maintaining competitive markets and enabling the emergence of large, globally competitive firms.
47	
48	This binary framing obscures the possibility that antitrust itself may serve as a vehicle for industrial policy. Historical institutionalist accounts have pointed in this direction, showing that regulatory regimes can shape market structures in ways that resemble industrial policy outcomes, even in the absence of explicit state planning [@Dobbin1994; @Prasad2012]. However, these accounts have focused primarily on earlier periods and have not systematically examined how such processes unfold in contemporary liberalizing contexts.
49	
50	The Brazilian case suggests that the opposition between antitrust and industrial policy is not only overstated but analytically misleading. Rather than choosing between competition and intervention, governments may pursue industrial objectives through the internal reinterpretation of competition law. This requires shifting attention away from formal policy categories and toward the ways in which legal concepts are mobilized and redefined in practice. In particular, it calls for examining how ambiguities embedded in antitrust frameworks вҖ” such as those surrounding efficiency and market definitioncreate opportunities for strategic reinterpretation.
51	
52	By foregrounding these dynamics, the paper shows that antitrust policy, far from being a neutral or purely technical domain, is deeply embedded in broader struggles over economic strategy and state intervention. In doing so, it opens up a new perspective on the relationship between liberalization and industrial policy, suggesting that the former may not displace the latter but rather transform its modalities.
53	
```

</details>

128:The Ambev case thus illustrates the stabilization of the interpretive shifts that had emerged in the Gerdau case. Historical institutionalist accounts have pointed in this direction, showing that regulatory regimes can shape market structures in ways that resemble industrial policy outcomes, even in the absence of explicit state planning [@Dobbin1994; @Prasad2012]. More recent comparative work reinforces this point: even among advanced economies, the degree of bureaucratic discretion afforded to competition authorities вҖ” rather than formal changes to statute вҖ” has been shown to condition how competition regimes evolve over time [@Foster-Thelen2024]. However, these accounts have focused primarily on earlier periods and have not systematically examined how such processes unfold in contemporary liberalizing contexts."

137	## Discussion: Institutional Conversion and the Reconfiguration of State Intervention
138	
139	The comparative analysis of the Gerdau and Ambev cases reveals a process of institutional conversion in which the meaning and application of antitrust policy were transformed without formal legislative change. Central to this process was the strategic use of ambiguity in key legal concepts, particularly the definition of the relevant market. By expanding the scope of the market to include international competition, actors were able to reconcile high levels of domestic concentration with the requirements of competition law.
140	
141	This reinterpretation was facilitated by political intervention and institutional change, including the reconfiguration of CADE's composition and the increasing prominence of economic expertise aligned with transaction-cost approaches. Over time, these changes contributed to the normalization of a more permissive stance toward mergers, in which concerns about market structure were balanced against considerations of efficiency and competitiveness.
142	
143	The result was a form of industrial policy that operated through passive regulatory means and was justified in covert terms. While the government continued to reject industrial policy in its discourse, it enabled the consolidation of domestic firms and their expansion into international markets. This suggests that liberalization does not necessarily entail a retreat of the state but may instead involve a reconfiguration of its modes of intervention.
144	
145	## Conclusion
146	
```

</details>

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "c:\\Users\\Mancano\\Documents\\MancanoSync\\Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust\\3-texts\\Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.qmd",
  "old_string": "This reinterpretation was facilitated by political intervention and institutional change, including the reconfiguration of CADE's composition and the increasing prominence of economic expertise aligned with transaction-cost approaches. Over time, these changes contributed to the normalization of a more permissive stance toward mergers, in which concerns about market structure were balanced against considerations of efficiency and competitiveness.\n\nThe result was a form of industrial policy that operated through passive regulatory means and was justified in covert terms.",
  "new_string": "This reinterpretation was facilitated by political intervention and institutional change, including the reconfiguration of CADE's composition and the increasing prominence of economic expertise aligned with transaction-cost approaches. Over time, these changes contributed to the normalization of a more permissive stance toward mergers, in which concerns about market structure were balanced against considerations of efficiency and competitiveness.\n\nThis trajectory can be further specified using the distinction proposed by @Foster-Thelen2025 between horizontal coordination rights вҖ” extended to non-dominant firms вҖ” and hierarchical coordination rights, exercised by dominant firms over suppliers, distributors, and competitors. In these terms, Brazilian antitrust policy moved from a nominally arm's-length model toward what Foster and Thelen call an oligopolistic competition model, in which dominant firms are granted wide latitude to consolidate market power. Notably, Brazil arrived at a structural outcome similar to the one Foster and Thelen document for the United States after the 1980s, but through a distinct institutional pathway: not the gradual, court-mediated capture of antitrust doctrine by Chicago School economics, but direct executive intervention justified in developmentalist terms вҖ” the perceived need to arm domestic firms against the hierarchical coordination rights already exercised by larger foreign multinationals. Extending a framework developed with reference to advanced economies to a liberalizing middle-income economy under fiscal constraint suggests that convergence on an oligopolistic competition model can follow more than one institutional route.\n\nThe result was a form of industrial policy that operated through passive regulatory means and was justified in covert terms."
}
```

  theme: cosmo
  title: 'Antitrust as industrial policy: Government-Sponsored Mergers as Passive Industrial Policy in Brazil, 1995-2002'
  author:
    - name: AndrГ© Vereta-Nahoum
    - name: Tales ManГ§ano
  abstract: |
    This article analyzes how antitrust policy can function as passive industrial policy, promoting competitiveness and internationalization in economies under fiscal constraint. Drawing on government-supported mergers in Brazil during the 1990s, the study examines how flexibility in the enforcement of competition law was justified by the need to strengthen national sectors in the face of economic liberalization. Antitrust and industrial policy are traditionally regarded as antagonistic. At the turn of the century, however, Brazil's antitrust authority allowed the consolidation of major corporate groups, such as Gerdau and Ambev, on the grounds of efficiency gains and global competitiveness вҖ” even as the Cardoso government prioritized monetary stability and denied adopting sectoral industrial policies. The Brazilian case illustrates how fiscally conservative governments can strategically use antitrust policy to drive economic development and the internationalization of firms, aligning indirectly and passively with objectives traditionally associated with classic, strengthening-oriented industrial policy.
  keywords:
    - antitrust policy
    - industrial policy
    - institutional conversion
    - Brazil
    - mergers and acquisitions
  
Output created: Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.html
```

I can work around this by searching for ASCII-safe substrings or patterns that don't rely on accented charactersвҖ”like searching for "Minist" instead of "MinistГ©rio", or using keywords that are naturally unaccented like "MDIC", "Desenvolvimento", or "interesse nacional". Let me test some grep patterns that can tolerate these encoding quirks and find the relevant content.

I'm looking at a Folha de S.Paulo article from February 2000 about the Brahma-Antarctica merger, where a government advisor formally questioned the Ministry of Development about whether the merger was truly necessary for the national economy.

The article reveals that the Ministry's spokesperson for Industrial Policy declined to comment on the case at that time, which directly contradicts what the current document claims about the executive branch strongly supporting the merger. The CADE rapporteur was still inquiring about the government's position, and there was uncertainty about whether any authority would actually endorse it.

I need to search for a later official response or "parecer" from the Ministry that might have been issued after this initial inquiry вҖ” perhaps the claim refers to a subsequent position that was eventually provided, rather than this early stage where they were still non-committal.   title: 'Antitrust as industrial policy: Government-Sponsored Mergers as Passive Industrial Policy in Brazil, 1995-2002'
  author:
    - name: AndrГ© Vereta-Nahoum
    - name: Tales ManГ§ano
  abstract: |
    This article analyzes how antitrust policy can function as passive industrial policy, promoting competitiveness and internationalization in economies under fiscal constraint. Drawing on government-supported mergers in Brazil during the 1990s, the study examines how flexibility in the enforcement of competition law was justified by the need to strengthen national sectors in the face of economic liberalization. Antitrust and industrial policy are traditionally regarded as antagonistic. At the turn of the century, however, Brazil's antitrust authority allowed the consolidation of major corporate groups, such as Gerdau and Ambev, on the grounds of efficiency gains and global competitiveness вҖ” even as the Cardoso government prioritized monetary stability and denied adopting sectoral industrial policies. The Brazilian case illustrates how fiscally conservative governments can strategically use antitrust policy to drive economic development and the internationalization of firms, aligning indirectly and passively with objectives traditionally associated with classic, strengthening-oriented industrial policy.
  keywords:
    - antitrust policy
    - industrial policy
    - institutional conversion
    - Brazil
    - mergers and acquisitions
  
Output created: Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.html
```

118	Opposition to the merger was led by Kaiser, one of Ambev's rival brewers,[^kaiser-coke] which ran a series of media campaigns arguing that concentrating roughly two-thirds of the country's beer production and sales in the new firm could lead to anticompetitive conduct, including price increases contrary to the national interest: "Kaiser, enemy number one of the union, put out yet another campaign yesterday вҖ” the third вҖ” to prevent the association, which is under review by CADE... the brewer ran ads on radio and in newspapers to try to convince public opinion that the merger harms consumers' interests, since it would bring about market concentration and, consequently, higher prices" [@FernandesPrates2000]. Critics also argued that the operation amounted not to a merger but to Brahma absorbing Antarctica outright, creating a firm capable of eliminating competition and threatening the survival of medium and small rivals, with no significant efficiencies or logistical or technological innovations to benefit consumers, suppliers, or distributors. Kaiser further criticized Ambev for operating as a unified company even before CADE's final ruling вҖ” a fait accompli meant to make the operation practically irreversible regardless of the agency's eventual decision (CADE AC 08012.005846.1999-12, Vol. 2, p. 49).
119	
120	Despite these concerns, the executive branch strongly supported the merger. When CADE consulted the Ministry of Development on the merger's relevance to the national economy, its Secretary of Industrial Policy, HГ©lio Mattar, submitted an opinion affirming that it was вҖ” a conclusion diametrically opposed to the one reached by CADE's own Office of the Attorney (Procuradoria), which maintained that the operation did not meet this standard and noted that the ministry's opinion, however informative, was not binding on the agency (CADE AC 08012.005846.1999-12, Vol. 17, pp. 160-161). Minister of Industrial Development ClГіvis Carvalho, for instance, stated that he wanted to see a wave of mergers across several of the country's largest industries вҖ” especially paper, steel, and petrochemicals вҖ” so that they could prosper in international markets, offering up to three billion reais (roughly US$1.7 billion) from the National Bank for Economic and Social Development (BNDES) to finance such deals [@TheEconomist1999]. Public statements by President Cardoso suggested a pragmatic acceptance of market concentration. In an interview with the Jornal do Brasil, Cardoso addressed the merger directly, arguing that Brazilian firms needed to restructure to compete in a more open global economy; asked about the possibility of a "monopoly" forming with the merger, he replied: "But the world today operates in terms of oligopoly. ... What is Coca-Cola? How will they compete, later on, with Coca-Cola, if they are not strong here? Isn't that so? That is the concept. You can no longer think in terms of the domestic market. The market is internationalized. So you have to look at whether there are competitors in the sector or not [...] I don't even want to anticipate whether this will be legal or not [referring to CADE]. Let's discuss it. I am telling you what the global trend is: mega-mergers. That is, we will need Brazilian multinationals" [@Abdallah2020, p. 170]. 
```

Preliminares

                                     Nos termos do art. 4 da Lei 8.884/94, todos os atos, sob
qualquer forma manifestados, que possam li tar ou de qualquer forma prejudicar a
livre concorrпҝҪncia, ou resultar na dominaпҝҪ de mercados relevantes de bens ou
serviпҝҪos, deverпҝҪo ser submetidos пҝҪ apreciaпҝҪ do CADE.

                                     Indubitavelmente, ,Ia anпҝҪlise dos fatos abaixo elencados,
ora preliminarmente expostos pela Kaiser, coi tatar-se-пҝҪ que a presente operaпҝҪпҝҪo de

expressivo aumento de poder de mercado por conseguinte, em dominaпҝҪпҝҪo de
mercado, em total prejuпҝҪzo da Coletividade titular do bem jurпҝҪdico tutelado pela
legislaпҝҪпҝҪo concorrencial, e, em particular, dos umidores.

HEBE ROMANO, datado de 14 do corrente aprovado пҝҪ unanimidade pelo PlenпҝҪrio
do E. CADE - jпҝҪ teriam causado irreversпҝҪv 1 e negativo impacto no mercado, nпҝҪo

I found the exact passage on page 49 discussing the "fait accompli" doctrineвҖ”it confirms that while formal acts may preexist the Council's decision, they cannot produce effects or have validity after the preventive determination is made.

This is Kaiser's formal "ImpugnaГ§ГЈo" (legal objection) filed by their attorneys Franceschini e Miranda, addressed to Conselheira-Relatora Hebe Romano, dated shortly after July 16, 1999. So I've now verified both key citations: Volume 2, page 49 contains Kaiser's objection petition with the fait accompli argument, and Volume 17, pages 160-161 contain CADE's Procuradoria opinion on the MinistГ©rio do Desenvolvimento matter.

The user also wants me to save the extracted text files to an organized folder in the repo to help the next agent. Since the file/ directory is gitignored for raw materials, I should place the extracted plain text versions of these process volumes alongside the PDFs in file/cade-proceedings/ вҖ” this way they'll be locally available as searchable text for whoever continues this work. I should add a README to document that this is a partial extraction covering only the Ambev case, so future work knows where to extend it. I'm also checking the project's own CLAUDE.md rules about structural changes to the file/ directory to make sure I'm following the right constraints. so I'll organize the text extracts in a dedicated subfolder, check the total file size to make sure it's manageable, then update the README to document this new addition вҖ” all low-risk changes that align with the existing conventions. After that, I'll circle back to finishing the bibtex entries.

Now I'm looking at the existing Gerdau entry to match its formatting exactly, then I'll construct the two new entries for Ambev and Brahma.

</details>

I'm noticing the template uses placeholder markers like "[VERIFICAR]" for volume and page fields that weren't fully specified, which explains why the citation in the .qmd file references this entry without a page locator.

Since I have precise volume and page information extracted directly from the PDFs, I can fill in exact values instead of placeholders. For Kaiser's ImpugnaГ§ГЈo, I need to pinpoint which page contains the "fait accompli" quote вҖ” the extraction covered pages 47-51, but I need to verify the exact page number where that specific text appears. The full title is "Ato de ConcentraГ§ГЈo NВә 08012.005846/1999-12 вҖ” ImpugnaГ§ГЈo da Cervejarias Kaiser Brasil Ltda. ao Ato de ConcentraГ§ГЈo (FusГЈo Brahma-Antarctica)". For the author field, I should list Cervejarias Kaiser Brasil Ltda. directly since this is a petition filed by Kaiser themselves, not a CADE ruling or decision document, which makes it different from the Gerdau precedent where CADE was the issuing body.

For the Procuradoria entry in Vol. 17, the author would be CADE's Procuradoria, following the same institutional-author pattern. On dating the Kaiser impugnaГ§ГЈo, the content references a press note from July 16, 1999 as already published, and since legal responses typically come a few days after the triggering event, this filing was likely submitted around July 19, 1999, though I haven't located the exact date in the available excerpt.

I should search further into the document for a signature line or date stamp, as Brazilian legal petitions typically conclude with a location and date. Let me look ahead in the extraction to find that closing section.

{
  "file_path": "c:\\Users\\Mancano\\Documents\\MancanoSync\\Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust\\file\\cade-proceedings\\ambev-antarctica-brahma-1999_AC-08012.005846-1999-12\\text-extracts\\README.md",
  "content": "# text-extracts/ вҖ” OCR text dos volumes do processo Ambev, para busca rГЎpida\n\nTexto plano extraГӯdo dos PDFs escaneados na pasta-mГЈe (`pdftotext -layout`, poppler 26.01.0), gerado em 2026-07-22 para permitir `grep`/busca em vez de abrir cada PDF de ~10-50MB manualmente. Um `.txt` por volume, mesmo nome-base (`vol-NN.txt` вҶ” `vol-NN.pdf`). `vol-07` estГЎ ausente porque o PDF original tambГ©m estГЎ (ver `file/README.md`). Os 2 \"via-cade\" e o anexo de processo relacionado **nГЈo foram extraГӯdos** вҖ” sГі os 30 volumes principais (`vol-01` a `vol-31`, exceto `vol-07`).\n\n**LimitaГ§Гөes conhecidas вҖ” leia antes de confiar em uma citaГ§ГЈo encontrada aqui:**\n- OCR de qualidade variГЎvel: muitos caracteres acentuados (Г§, ГЈ, Гө, Г©, ГЎ, Гӯ, Гә) saem como `пҝҪ` (mojibake). Busque por trechos sem acento ou tolerantes a esse ruГӯdo (ex. `interesse nacional` funciona, `Minist.rio` com `.` no lugar do `Г©` funciona).\n- **A numeraГ§ГЈo de linha do `.txt` nГЈo corresponde Г  paginaГ§ГЈo fГӯsica do processo** (fls./p.). Para confirmar a pГЎgina exata de uma citaГ§ГЈo, extraia a pГЎgina especГӯfica do PDF original com `pdftotext -f N -l N vol-NN.pdf -` (mais confiГЎvel que caГ§ar marcador de pГЎgina no dump concatenado) вҖ” foi assim que as duas citaГ§Гөes abaixo foram confirmadas.\n- Existem recortes de jornal digitalizados dentro do dossiГӘ (nГЈo sГі peГ§as processuais) вҖ” confirme o tipo de documento antes de citar como \"autos do processo\".\n\n**CitaГ§Гөes jГЎ localizadas e confirmadas** (Гәtil para nГЈo repetir a busca):\n- Kaiser, \"ImpugnaГ§ГЈo\" ao ato de concentraГ§ГЈo (fait accompli) вҖ” `vol-02.txt`, PDF p. 49 (confirmado via extraГ§ГЈo de pГЎgina especГӯfica).\n- CADE, Procuradoria, parecer sobre a consulta ao MinistГ©rio do Desenvolvimento (HГ©lio Mattar) вҖ” `vol-17.txt`, PDF pp. 160-161 (itens 619-624; a numeraГ§ГЈo \"160\"/\"161\" Г© a paginaГ§ГЈo interna do prГіprio parecer, impressa no documento).\n\nGerado a pedido de Tales ManГ§ano para acelerar a resoluГ§ГЈo de citaГ§Гөes pendentes do caso Ambev вҖ” ver `9-vers/plan/2026-07-14_Plano_Revisao_Artigo_Coautor.md` (SeГ§ГЈo 6, item 6) e a conversa de 2026-07-22.\n"
}

```
    This article analyzes how antitrust policy can function as passive industrial policy, promoting competitiveness and internationalization in economies under fiscal constraint. Drawing on government-supported mergers in Brazil during the 1990s, the study examines how flexibility in the enforcement of competition law was justified by the need to strengthen national sectors in the face of economic liberalization. Antitrust and industrial policy are traditionally regarded as antagonistic. At the turn of the century, however, Brazil's antitrust authority allowed the consolidation of major corporate groups, such as Gerdau and Ambev, on the grounds of efficiency gains and global competitiveness вҖ” even as the Cardoso government prioritized monetary stability and denied adopting sectoral industrial policies. The Brazilian case illustrates how fiscally conservative governments can strategically use antitrust policy to drive economic development and the internationalization of firms, aligning indirectly and passively with objectives traditionally associated with classic, strengthening-oriented industrial policy.
  keywords:
    - antitrust policy
    - industrial policy
    - institutional conversion
    - Brazil
    - mergers and acquisitions
  
Output created: Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust-Article.html

Kaiser, one of AmbevвҖҷs rival brewers,<a href="#fn2" class="footnote-ref" id="fnref2" role="doc-noteref"><sup>2</sup></a> which ran a series of media campaigns arguing that concentrating roughly two-thirds of the countryвҖҷs beer production and sales in the new firm could lead to anticompetitive conduct, including price increases contrary to the national interest: вҖңKaiser, enemy number one of the union, put out yet another campaign yesterday вҖ” the third вҖ” to prevent the association, which is under review by CADEвҖҰ the brewer ran ads on radio and in newspapers to try to convince public opinion that the merger harms consumersвҖҷ interests, since it would bring about market concentration and, consequently, higher pricesвҖқ <span class="citation" data-cites="FernandesPrates2000">(<a href="#ref-FernandesPrates2000" role="doc-biblioref">Fernandes and Prates 2000</a>)</span>. Critics also argued that the operation amounted not to a merger but to Brahma absorbing Antarctica outright, creating a firm capable of eliminating competition and threatening the survival of medium and small rivals, with no significant efficiencies or logistical or technological innovations to benefit consumers, suppliers, or distributors. Kaiser further criticized Ambev for operating as a unified company even before CADEвҖҷs final ruling вҖ” a fait accompli meant to make the operation practically irreversible regardless of the agencyвҖҷs eventual decision <span class="citation" data-cites="CADE1999-Kaiser-Impugnacao">(<a href="#ref-CADE1999-Kaiser-Impugnacao
Procuradoria), which maintained that the operation did not meet this standard and noted that the ministryвҖҷs opinion, however informative, was not binding on the agency <span class="citation" data-cites="CADE2000-Procuradoria-Parecer">(<a href="#ref-CADE2000-Procuradoria-Parecer
Procuradoria-Parecer
Procuradoria. 2000. <em>Ato de <span>ConcentraГ§ГЈo NВә</span> 08012.005846/1999-12 вҖ” <span>Parecer
Kaiser-Impugnacao

33	- [ ] AvaliaГ§ГЈo crГӯtica de prontidГЈo do artigo para submissГЈo Г  RIPE вҖ” pontos levantados em revisГЈo honesta feita a pedido de Tales (comparando o argumento/estrutura atuais do `.qmd` com o padrГЈo esperado de um Research Article na revista, ver critГ©rios jГЎ confirmados no item de estratГ©gia de publicaГ§ГЈo em "ConcluГӯdo"). Veredito da revisГЈo: **o argumento e o material empГӯrico tГӘm potencial real, mas o manuscrito ainda estГЎ em estГЎgio de working paper, nГЈo de artigo pronto para submissГЈo** вҖ” nГЈo Г© um problema de mГ©rito, Г© de estГЎgio de desenvolvimento. Depende de decisГЈo/discussГЈo com AndrГ© antes de virar tarefas executГЎveis, por isso fica em Prospectivo. Pontos especГӯficos, em ordem de severidade:
34	  1. **Lacuna bibliogrГЎfica вҖ” severidade revista para baixo apГіs leitura integral dos dois textos em 2026-07-16** (extraГӯdos para `9-vers/references/Foster-Thelen-2024-Brandeis-in-Brussels.txt` e `9-vers/references/Foster-Thelen-2025-Coordination-Rights-Competition-Law.txt` via skill `pdf-text-extractor`): o `Nahoum-Mancano-2026-Antitrust.bib` jГЎ contГ©m as entradas `Foster-Thelen2024` ("Brandeis in Brussels", *Regulation & Governance*) e `Foster-Thelen2025` ("Coordination Rights, Competition Law and Varieties of Capitalism", *Comparative Political Studies*), ambas de Kathleen Thelen (coautora de `Mahoney-Thelen2010`), mas **nenhuma Г© citada no `.qmd` atual**. A leitura integral (nГЈo sГі metadados) mostra que isso Г© uma oportunidade de enriquecimento, nГЈo uma ameaГ§a de desk rejection como a entrada original desta tarefa supunha: nenhum dos dois textos usa o vocabulГЎrio de "conversГЈo institucional" (usam "coordination rights"/"drift", de Sanjukta Paul 2020) nem discute economias emergentes/Sul Global вҖ” sГЈo inteiramente sobre trajetГіria EUAГ—UE. `Foster-Thelen2024` Г© tangencial (sГі reforГ§a, via um caso comparativo distinto, que mudanГ§a na composiГ§ГЈo/expertise do ГіrgГЈo regulador Г© um canal reconhecido de conversГЈo institucional em direito da concorrГӘncia вҖ” ecoa a substituiГ§ГЈo de conselheiros do CADE pГіs-Gerdau, citГЎvel em uma frase da SeГ§ГЈo 6). `Foster-Thelen2025` Г© mais valioso: seu framework de "direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo horizontal/hierГЎrquica" (4 tipos ideais: cartelista, oligopolista, cooperativo, arm's-length) encaixa bem nos casos Gerdau/Ambev вҖ” o Brasil concedeu direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica a firmas dominantes domГ©sticas por lГіgica developmentalista/defensiva, chegando ao mesmo "quadrante oligopolista" que os EUA alcanГ§aram por captura ideolГіgica (Chicago School) вҖ” uma extensГЈo legГӯtima do framework para um caso que os autores nГЈo cobrem, mas que exige trabalho conceitual de integraГ§ГЈo (nГЈo Г© citation fix de uma linha).
35	  2. **DependГӘncia excessiva de uma Гәnica fonte secundГЎria**: quase toda a reconstruГ§ГЈo institucional do CADE (1962-1994, composiГ§ГЈo, autonomia) no artigo se apoia em Onto (2017), uma tese de doutorado. Falta um trecho explГӯcito diferenciando a contribuiГ§ГЈo do artigo em relaГ§ГЈo ao projeto de Onto вҖ” provavelmente existe (o process tracing dos dois casos com foco na variГЎvel "polГӯtica industrial passiva" Г© mais especГӯfico que o projeto dela), mas isso nГЈo estГЎ articulado no texto hoje.
36	  3. **ExtensГЈo/densidade abaixo do registro tГӯpico esperado**: corpo de prosa atual tem ~6.900-7.200 palavras (mediГ§Гөes de 2026-07-15), abaixo do que normalmente se espera de um Research Article de revista Q1 de IPE em termos de densidade analГӯtica (mesmo estando dentro do limite duro de 12.000 palavras da RIPE вҖ” isso nГЈo Г© sobre estourar o limite, Г© sobre o texto ainda nГЈo ter os componentes esperados). Faltam hoje: (a) seГ§ГЈo de metodologia explГӯcita justificando a seleГ§ГЈo dos dois casos e a lГіgica comparativa (o texto menciona process tracing e o uso do NotebookLM de passagem, mas nГЈo argumenta a escolha metodolГіgica); (b) engajamento com a literatura de developmental state/estado oculto (ex. Weiss, Block) que fortaleceria a reivindicaГ§ГЈo teГіrica; (c) discussГЈo de condiГ§Гөes de escopo вҖ” por que esse mecanismo apareceu no Brasil dos anos 1990 e nГЈo em outros paГӯses latino-americanos liberalizando na mesma Г©poca (MГ©xico, Argentina).
37	  4. **Marcadores de pendГӘncia ainda abertos no `.qmd`** (`[ADD EXACT SOURCE]`, `[citar processo]`, `[INSERT QUOTE FROM CARDOSO OR MINISTER]` e afins) impedem submissГЈo por si sГі вҖ” ver tambГ©m os itens jГЎ em "Pendente" sobre citaГ§Гөes placeholder (`LoPrete1999`, `Nassif1995`, `Rodrik2021`).
38	  5. **Enquadramento IPE (linha crГӯtica/britГўnica, afinidade da RIPE) vs. economia polГӯtica regulatГіria domГ©stica**: o artigo jГЎ faz o gesto certo ao tratar a definiГ§ГЈo de mercado como "global" para justificar concentraГ§ГЈo domГ©stica e a ameaГ§a de desnacionalizaГ§ГЈo/internacionalizaГ§ГЈo das firmas, mas esse fio poderia ser puxado com mais forГ§a para deixar explГӯcito que o argumento nГЈo Г© "sГі" sobre polГӯtica regulatГіria domГ©stica brasileira, e sim sobre como a integraГ§ГЈo ao mercado global reconfigura o Estado regulador nacional вҖ” um argumento genuinamente de economia polГӯtica internacional.
39	  - RecomendaГ§ГЈo de sequenciamento sugerida na prГіpria revisГЈo: (1) resolver a lacuna Foster-Thelen вҖ” ler os dois textos e decidir se reforГ§am, contrastam ou limitam o argumento atual; (2) escrever a seГ§ГЈo de metodologia/seleГ§ГЈo de casos que hoje nГЈo existe como tal; (3) explicitar a diferenciaГ§ГЈo em relaГ§ГЈo a Onto (2017); (4) sГі depois reavaliar se falta puxar mais o fio de economia polГӯtica internacional antes de submeter.

Textos extraГӯdos integralmente para `9-vers/references/` e lidos por completo antes de decidir a integraГ§ГЈo (nГЈo sГі abstract). `Foster-Thelen2024` entrou como citaГ§ГЈo de apoio na SeГ§ГЈo 2 (discriГ§ГЈo administrativa como motor de conversГЈo institucional); `Foster-Thelen2025` gerou um parГЎgrafo novo na Discussion, recastando os casos Gerdau/Ambev em termos do framework de \"coordination rights\" вҖ” o Brasil concedendo direitos de coordenaГ§ГЈo hierГЎrquica a firmas dominantes domГ©sticas por via developmentalista, chegando ao mesmo \"quadrante oligopolista\" que os EUA alcanГ§aram por captura ideolГіgica (Chicago School).\n2. **RevisГЈo ortogrГЎfica/gramatical/de lГӯngua**: releitura integral do `.qmd` (que havia mudado substancialmente desde a Гәltima leitura вҖ” marcadores `[...]{.mark}` majoritariamente resolvidos por Tales/AndrГ© em paralelo). Corrigidos typos mecГўnicos (espaГ§amento, consistГӘncia de artigo/travessГЈo) e 5 problemas substantivos de estrutura de frase (modificador solto, referente perdido, sujeito incoerente em voz passiva, paralelismo confuso, uma frase de trabalho em portuguГӘs ainda nГЈo traduzida вҖ” com erro gramatical no prГіprio portuguГӘs original).\n3. **ResoluГ§ГЈo do marcador `[CitaГ§ГЈo do Processo?]`** no caso Ambev: busca direta nos autos originais do processo AC 08012.005846/1999-12 (`file/cade-proceedings/`, gitignored mas disponГӯvel localmente) via `pdftotext`, localizando o parecer da Procuradoria do CADE (Vol. 17) que registra a consulta ao MinistГ©rio do Desenvolvimento e a divergГӘncia com o SecretГЎrio de PolГӯtica Industrial HГ©lio Mattar. A mesma busca confirmou (Vol. 2, p. 49) a fonte de uma citaГ§ГЈo prГ©-existente sobre a impugnaГ§ГЈo da Kaiser (\"fait accompli\"). Geradas 2 entradas BibTeX seguindo o template jГЎ usado para o caso Gerdau (`CADE1995-AC16-1994`); Tales importou no Zotero e o Better BibTeX gerou exatamente os citekeys sugeridos (`CADE1999-Kaiser-Impugnacao`, `CADE2000-Procuradoria-Parecer`). CitaГ§Гөes no `.qmd` trocadas de texto parentГ©tico solto para `[@citekey, locator]` вҖ” o locator (vol./p.) precisa ficar dentro dos colchetes para aparecer in-text via citeproc, nГЈo basta estar no `.bib`.\n4. **ExtraГ§ГЈo de texto organizada para reuso**: os 30 volumes principais do dossiГӘ Ambev foram convertidos para texto plano (`pdftotext -layout`) e salvos em `file/cade-proceedings/ambev-antarctica-brahma-1999_AC-08012.005846-1999-12/text-extracts/` (gitignorado como todo `file/`, exceto o prГіprio `README.md` da pasta-mГЈe, que foi atualizado para documentar a novidade) вҖ” evita reextrair na prГіxima vez que alguГ©m precisar caГ§ar uma citaГ§ГЈo do processo Ambev.\n\nTodas as ediГ§Гөes no `.qmd` foram validadas renderizando para HTML (`quarto render --to html`) a cada etapa, checando que as citaГ§Гөes novas resolvem e aparecem na lista de referГӘncias; artefatos de build (`.html`, `_files/`) descartados apГіs cada checagem.   title: 'Antitrust as industrial policy: Government-Sponsored Mergers as Passive Industrial Policy in Brazil, 1995-2002'
  author:
    - name: AndrГ© Vereta-Nahoum
    - name: Tales ManГ§ano
  abstract: |
    This article analyzes how antitrust policy can function as passive industrial policy, promoting competitiveness and internationalization in economies under fiscal constraint. Drawing on government-supported mergers in Brazil during the 1990s, the study examines how flexibility in the enforcement of competition law was justified by the need to strengthen national sectors in the face of economic liberalization. Antitrust and industrial policy are traditionally regarded as antagonistic. At the turn of the century, however, Brazil's antitrust authority allowed the consolidation of major corporate groups, such as Gerdau and Ambev, on the grounds of efficiency gains and global competitiveness вҖ” even as the Cardoso government prioritized monetary stability and denied adopting sectoral industrial policies. The Brazilian case illustrates how fiscally conservative governments can strategically use antitrust policy to drive economic development and the internationalization of firms, aligning indirectly and passively with objectives traditionally associated with classic, strengthening-oriented industrial policy.
  keywords:
    - antitrust policy
    - industrial policy
    - institutional conversion
    - Brazil
    - mergers and acquisitions

52	
53	REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY
54	2019, VOL. 26, NO. 2, 256вҖ“286
55	https://doi.org/10.1080/09692290.2018.1563557
