60	Varieties of economization in competition policy:
61	institutional change in German and American
62	antitrust, 1960вҖ“2000
63	Timur Ergena and Sebastian Kohla,b
64	a
65	 Max Planck Institute for the Study of Societies, Cologne, Germany; bUppsala University,
66	Sociology/IBF, Uppsala, Sweden
67	
68	    ABSTRACT
69	    This paper explains the different trajectories of German and American competition pol-
70	    icy and its permissiveness toward economic concentration in the last few decades.
71	    While the German political economy had moved to a stronger antitrust regime after
72	    1945 and stuck to it even after the economic governance shifts of the 1980s, the trad-
73	    itional antitrust champion, the United States, has shed considerable parts of its basic
74	    governance toolkit against anticompetitive conduct since the 1960s. Drawing on theo-
75	    ries of institutional change driven by bureaucratic and professional elites, the paper
76	    claims that different pathways of professional ideas in competition policy can account
77	    for the cross-country differences. In the 1960s and early 1970s, movements to
78	    strengthen competition policy enforcement emerged in both countries. While German
79	    as well as American professionals reacted to the seemingly increasing encroachment of
80	    societal concerns into antitrust with economized notions of the policiesвҖҷ goals, they did
81	    so in fundamentally different ways. Whereas US professionals proposed an effect-based
82	    approach in which consumer welfare and gains in efficiency may justify less competi-
83	    tion, the more strongly law-based profession in Germany to a degree strengthened a
84	    form-based approach aiming at the preservation of competitive market structures.
85	    Such extrapolitical pathways of ideas, we argue, provide important guidelines for the
86	    implementation of competition policy by administrations and courts, whose decisions
87	    can have a far-reaching impact on industries and political economies as a whole.
88	
89	
90	KEYWORDS Competition; institutional change; professions, ideas; governance; liberalization
91	
92	
93	
94	1. Introduction
95	When do concentrations of power in the economy become a problem for capital-
96	ism and democracy? When industrial capitalism matured during the late nineteenth
97	century, intellectuals, social movements and legislators intensely debated this basic
98	political question. While some countries, like the United States, settled on a skep-
99	tical position toward corporate power and on regulation of anticompetitive con-
100	duct, others, like Germany, followed a more permissive stance and appreciated the
101	
102	CONTACT Timur Ergen            te@mpifg.de         Max Planck Institute for the Study of Societies, Paulstr. 3, 50676
103	Cologne, Germany
104	Гҹ 2019 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.
105	This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (http://
106	creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided
107	the original work is properly cited.
108	
109	
110	--- PAGE BREAK ---
111	
112	                                       REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY       257
113	
114	
115	advantages of big corporations and cooperation between competitors. Since the
116	1960s, however, the cards in the antitrust debates have been reshuffled. While the
117	German political economy incrementally developed a stronger antitrust regime in
118	the control of concentration and anticompetitive conduct after 1945 вҖ“ and stuck to
119	it even through the 1980s and 1990s вҖ“ the traditional antitrust champion, the
120	United States, has moved toward a more lax position on corporate power since the
121	1970s (Gutierrez & Philippon, 2018).
122	    This paper tries to explain why these two countries have been diverging cases in
123	competition policy. Drawing on public debates, secondary literature and reports by
124	competition authorities in the US and Germany, we claim that the different develop-
125	ments on the two sides of the Atlantic cannot be understood through the three domin-
126	ant explanations, business power, varieties of capitalism (VoC) and legal systems.
127	Rather, we argue that ideological orientations that gained the upper hand in the
128	respective national regulatory and intellectual communities are an important and over-
129	looked factor in understanding the differences across countries and time. As we docu-
130	ment, there were similarly inspired intellectual movements to push back against
131	increasingly vocal progressive interpretations of competition policy in both countries
132	since the late 1960s. Yet, the intellectual moves by reformers in the US allowed for an
133	incremental weakening of the regime over time, while the reaction in Germany locked
134	the regime into a more robust path. While both regimes moved away from the explicit
135	consideration of overall societal goals and were hence changed in the spirit of neo-
136	liberal ideas (Buch-Hansen & Wigger, 2010), they diverged in their enforcement norms
137	concerning industrial concentration. Reacting to calls for a more effective US competi-
138	tion policy that would actively reduce concentration in American industry, institutional
139	entrepreneurs in the US pushed for economization, a reorientation of the regime from
140	broad societal considerations toward purely economic goals вҖ“ вҖҳconsumer welfareвҖҷ above
141	all. This effect-based approach to competition regulation, inspired by the Chicago
142	school, allows for business cooperation and concentration if it is in the interest of eco-
143	nomic goals, notably overall efficiency and consumer welfare. Compared to the 1950s
144	and 1960s, enforcement norms in virtually every field of antitrust except pure price-fix-
145	ing between competitors have been weakened. German antitrust elites, by contrast,
146	rejected early progressive calls for a more instrumental competition policy by question-
147	ing in principle the practical, goal-directed application of the law as such вҖ“ irrespective
148	of whether these goals were economic or noneconomic in nature. Instead, their form-
149	based approach to competition regulation, inspired by the Ordoliberal school, main-
150	tains that rule-based state intervention in favor of competitive pluralistic market struc-
151	tures is a safeguard against economic and political abuse as well as inefficiencies.
152	    The two countries under study are largely dissimilar in terms of the most fre-
153	quent typologies in political economy, not least with regard to the trajectory of
154	their competition policies. Starting in the late nineteenth century, when the concen-
155	tration of industry became subject to debate and regulation, the United StatesвҖҷ anti-
156	trust policy established a tradition of populist protest and public regulation against
157	monopoly power (Letwin, 1965). At the same time, significant parts of German
158	intellectual and political discourse considered the cartelization of the economy to
159	be a more efficient means of coordinating economic development and nurturing
160	domestic вҖ“ a tradition that, with few interruptions, persisted throughout the Third
161	Reich (Ambrosius, 1981). The power of big business and cartels was considered to
162	be one of the factors behind German military aggressiveness and fascism, and the
163	
164	
165	--- PAGE BREAK ---
166	
167	258     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
168	
169	
170	Allies transplanted American antitrust ideas and regulation after the war (Quack &
171	Djelic, 2005). These ideas fell on fertile grounds in Ordoliberal thought, which rose
172	to dominance in Germany during the postwar years and shaped such important
173	economic institutions as the Bundesbank, the German Council of Economic
174	Experts and, most relevant here, the antitrust authorities (Hien & Joerges, 2017).
175	These views, entrenched in regulatory authorities and reproduced through the legal
176	profession, remained an ideological bulwark against the diffusion of the вҖҳmore eco-
177	nomicвҖҷ or effect-based approach to antitrust.
178	   Our focus on ideational and professional changes intends to complement a
179	range of alternative explanations for institutional differences between the German
180	and American political economies. It contributes to the comparative study of insti-
181	tutional inertia and change across countries by highlighting the important role that
182	different professional ideas can play. It shows within the American case how the
183	zeal of charismatic movements, such as the anti-monopolist Populists, can become
184	disenchanted in bureaucratic bodies where original ideas can even be perverted. It
185	thus contributes to the growing literature on economic professions in the making
186	of modern capitalism (Seabrooke & Henriksen, 2017) and the literature on bureau-
187	cratic institutions (central banks, regulatory agencies, competition authorities in
188	our case), whose Eigenlogik, at times beyond democratic control, can have an
189	important impact on political economic outcomes. Overall, we break new ground
190	in the neglected comparative study of antitrust in comparative political economy
191	(CPE). Given the importance of market structures for consumers, workers, political
192	systems, and societies as a whole, this particular policy field has been largely over-
193	looked or all too often subsumed under countriesвҖҷ general type of capitalism.
194	   The remainder of the article is divided into three parts. We first situate our ana-
195	lysis in the literature on institutional change through professional ideas and bur-
196	eaucratic agencies and in the general political science literature on competition
197	policy. Secondly, we review changes in American antitrust since the 1960s. The
198	reorientation of US antitrust, we argue, resulted from an intellectual opposition
199	movement against activist tendencies in the 1960s that championed a narrowing of
200	the objectives of competition policy and a strictly economic consequentialist rea-
201	soning in antitrust enforcement. Opposition against similar activist tendencies took
202	a very different form in Germany. In a third step, we show how the German
203	opposition movement was led by legal rather than economic thinking, which ques-
204	tioned the legitimacy of effect-oriented reasoning in competition policy in general.
205	The paradoxical effect of this movement was that even though the reaction against
206	activist competition policy in Germany was intellectually more radical in the begin-
207	ning, German antitrust enforcement became partly immune to the incremental eco-
208	nomic appreciation of the potentially beneficial effects of anticompetitive behavior
209	that happened in the United States. Finally, we conclude by highlighting the rele-
210	vance of the study of bureaucratic agencies as forgotten drivers of large-scale insti-
211	tutional change that can have an explanatory say beyond institutional VoC.
212	
213	
214	2. Antitrust regimes and extrapolitical institutional change
215	To lay the ground for our comparative historical analysis, this section establishes
216	three points. First, we argue that prominent explanations of institutional change in
217	competition policy fail to comprehensively account for decisive historical turning
218	
219	
220	--- PAGE BREAK ---
221	
222	                                      REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY     259
223	
224	
225	points as well as international variation. Second, we connect conceptual arguments
226	from political economy, institutional theory, and political sociology to demonstrate
227	that analyses of bureaucracies and professional communities can explain path-
228	breaking change in situations of low politicization and issue salience. Third, we
229	argue that competition policy as a regulatory field is vulnerable for change on the
230	level of enforcement.
231	
232	
233	2.1. Weaknesses of alternative accounts of institutional change
234	in competition policy
235	Debates on the causes of institutional change in competition policy over the last 50
236	years have not been settled conclusively. While the literature in the antitrust field
237	itself is vast and of great historical depth, it rarely aims at systematic explanation
238	and theoretical generalization. By contrast, in-depth studies of antitrust regimes are
239	rare in the political economy literature, while there are plenty of passing generaliza-
240	tions about the drivers of institutional change in competition policy вҖ“ especially for
241	the American case. We review three types of explanations of changes in competi-
242	tion policy вҖ“ accounts highlighting business pressures, national institutions, and
243	legal systems. Our aim is not to outright reject these perspectives, but to demon-
244	strate that they have difficulties to make sense of the historical record and the
245	larger comparative picture, which can be remedied by an examination of the inter-
246	sections between professional communities and bureaucracies.
247	    Accounts of economic policy change since the 1960s in the United States often
248	locate the origin of path-breaking policy initiatives in the troubling situation of
249	American business in the 1970s and early 1980s. In this view, domestic and inter-
250	national pressures on economic activity and profits led to a concerted counter-
251	movement by business and its political allies to improve American firmsвҖҷ standing
252	at home and abroad. The most elaborate version of this argument applied to com-
253	petition policy is ChristophersвҖҷ recent study of twentieth century competition and
254	patent policy in Great Britain and the United States (Christophers, 2016). Drawing
255	heavily on economic historian Galambos (2004), Christophers argues that laxer
256	antimonopoly enforcement and more permissive merger and takeover policing
257	since the 1980s were essentially industrial policies, meant to hasten restructuring
258	and revitalize American industry in international competition. Attacks on rigorous
259	antitrust enforcement on revitalization grounds were indeed common in the 1970s
260	and 1980s вҖ“ as undertaken by both progressive as well as conservative thought
261	leaders (Graham, 1992), the business community (Driggs, 1985), parts of the
262	Congress (US Congress, 1985), and the Reagan administration (DPC/EPC, 1985).
263	    While the political significance of revitalization pressures cannot be denied, we
264	see two problems with this explanatory strategy. First, institutional change in com-
265	petition policy has not been implemented in a comprehensive manner, but through
266	piecemeal precedent and experiments. In consequence, political economic interests
267	with respect to individual cases and enforcement practices were much more hetero-
268	geneous than business pressure-accounts make them out to be. To give just a single
269	example, the landmark 1983 permission of the Federal Trade Commission to let
270	General Motors and Toyota jointly operate their NUMMI-plant in Fremont,
271	California, was fiercely attacked by their American competitors (New York Times,
272	1984). In more abstract terms, issue- and case-specific changes in competition
273	
274	
275	--- PAGE BREAK ---
276	
277	260     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
278	
279	
280	policies almost as a rule divide the business community internally into short-run
281	winners and losers, which arguably makes business an unreliable driver of institu-
282	tional change. Second, relaxing antitrust enforcement is not a natural reaction to
283	depressed business activity. To the contrary, as Galambos (2004, p. 155) recognizes,
284	just a few years earlier, the natural competition policy strategy to spur industrial
285	growth was stepping up enforcement to eliminate what New Deal antitrust cham-
286	pion Thurman Arnold called the Bottlenecks of Business. Furthermore, although
287	revitalization via more permissive competition policies was a prominent strategy in
288	the United States in that specific historical period, other countries with large-scale
289	industrial restructuring and revitalization needs lacked similarly vigorous move-
290	ments. Economic pressures have to be вҖҳchanneledвҖҷ into specific policy action or
291	inaction (Gourevitch, 1986). As we argue in this article, a critical channeling mech-
292	anism making sense of our comparative-historical puzzle consisted of the workings
293	of ideas, professional communities, and enforcement agencies.
294	    The fact that different states pursued different strategies in antitrust enforcement
295	despite similar economic challenges of course does not imply that ideational proc-
296	esses played a critical role. As antitrust regimes significantly impact the structure of
297	the supply side of economies, a second straightforward candidate for explaining
298	divergence is the institutional setup of different political economies. The CPE lit-
299	erature dedicated to antimonopoly policies is surprisingly limited, which makes it
300	difficult to derive clear-cut theoretical predictions from this line of research.
301	Building on the logic of institutional complementarities, the original 2001 volume
302	on VoC contains sparse remarks that the US political economy is characterized by
303	вҖҳrigorous antitrust regulationsвҖҷ, while coordinated models of capitalism lean toward
304	laxer enforcement (Hall & Soskice, 2001, p. 31; Tate, 2001). Especially for the
305	period between World War II and the early 1980s, this is a fitting observation.
306	Often discussed examples of German and Japanese business practices foreign to the
307	American competition policy regime were intensive intra-industry collaboration in
308	research and development, extensive cross-shareholding in certain industries and
309	business groups, interfirm cooperation in skill-formation, and the use of rational-
310	ization and crisis cartels (Dore, 1986; Streeck, 1991). Since the 1970s, however, the
311	institutional trajectories of the German and the American competition policy
312	regimes moved in the opposite direction. Orthodox readings of the original VoC-
313	formulation are a poor guide to understand key developments in competition pol-
314	icy. Good examples are the movement against resale price maintenance in
315	Germany, while US administrators stepped away from their traditionally strict
316	policing of such restrictions on wholesalers and retailers or the increasing appreci-
317	ation of consortia and growing friendliness toward large-scale mergers and acquisi-
318	tions in the US.
319	    There exists a more refined argument applying a comparative institutionalist
320	framework to explain international differences of antimonopoly policies. This argu-
321	ment does not primarily focus on substantive differences in antitrust, but on differ-
322	ences of enforcement procedures. In an article criticizing European Commission
323	efforts in the early 2000s to move European antitrust enforcement practices closer
324	to American conventions, Wigger and NвӮ¬           olke (2007) argue that Continental
325	European procedural codes and legal traditions historically supported вҖҳRhenishвҖҷ
326	models of capitalism. Two traditions in particular strike them as supportive of a
327	вҖҳlong-term orientationвҖҷ and of вҖҳlong-term investmentsвҖҷ by curbing legal
328	
329	
330	--- PAGE BREAK ---
331	
332	                                      REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY      261
333	
334	
335	uncertainties. The weakness of private competition policy enforcement in Europe
336	means that businesses have to deal with challenges from a limited set of relatively
337	transparent state agencies. The existence of advance notification systems, which
338	allow corporations to gain certainty about the legality of business practices before
339	committing to them, furthermore, may decrease legal uncertainties. While their
340	argument about the homologies between production and legal systems is illuminat-
341	ing, Wigger and NвӮ¬    olkeвҖҷs enforcement system characteristics leave a number of sig-
342	nificant historical developments unexplained. As documented in our comparative
343	analysis in Section 3, German attempts to reform antitrust enforcement in consid-
344	eration of broader societal values in the late 1960s proposed more, not less, case-
345	specific flexibility, while the ensuing Ordoliberal rejection of more flexible enforce-
346	ment practices tried to block, rather than preserve, the influence of wider societal
347	concerns on competition policy. Equally problematic, the Common law based US
348	competition policy regime produced a large number of structure-oriented per se
349	rules until the 1960s, which only later came under increasing fire. Similar to the
350	under-determination of policies through economic pressures, institutional regimes
351	leave considerable room for conflicts, deviating policies, and institutional
352	experiments.
353	    A third alternative explanation would see change in the US and resistance in
354	Germany as consequences of the respective legal regimes. The general resistance of
355	the German legal profession against the more recent economic arguments is also
356	an expression of its more general skepticism against вҖҳlaw and economicsвҖҷ вҖ“ i.e. the
357	application of economic theories and principles to the analysis of law (Kirchner,
358	1991). In Common law systems economic approaches were often seized by the
359	judiciary in its struggle for a balance of power. The German judiciary has historic-
360	ally been much more confined to interpreting the laws as set by the legislature or
361	to interpreting cases as defined by existing legal doctrines (Kirchner, 1991).
362	Emblematically, although the subject of law and economics is taught at universities
363	in Germany, it is not a required part of German lawyersвҖҷ training. Furthermore,
364	Continental European legal regimes are often characterized as inquisitorial, in con-
365	trast to Anglo-American adversarial regimes (Rueschemeyer, 1976). While the for-
366	mer rely more heavily on justices and state officials as neutral participants in trials,
367	the latter give more room to the contending parties to introduce evidence and
368	structure trials. Modern economic antitrust thinking, with its emphasis on complex
369	economic modeling and on questions of quantitative trade-offs, seems to be more
370	fitting for an adversarial system in which there are extensive possibilities for the
371	parties involved to present expert assessments of the given case. While both dimen-
372	sions of institutional difference have undoubtedly contributed to the trajectories of
373	antitrust thinking, we would caution against a monocausal institutional explan-
374	ation. Widespread beliefs in underlying systems of thought are not a necessary con-
375	dition for the adoption of certain doctrinal elements, as GermanyвҖҷs immediate
376	postwar legal history documents. In addition, state agencies and courts around the
377	world have tried to keep up with the increasing technical demands of modern anti-
378	trust prosecution, which shows that the skills necessary are in no way exclusive to
379	contending parties.
380	    While legal traditions certainly influence pathways of legal change, they as well
381	leave room for deviant actors and processes, which may culminate in institutional
382	
383	
384	--- PAGE BREAK ---
385	
386	262     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
387	
388	
389	change. The purpose of the following section is to suggest that changing ideas in
390	bureaucracies and associated professions can shape such deviations.
391	
392	
393	2.2. Bureaucracies and professional ideas as sources of institutional change
394	Our paper draws on two strands of literature to understand the phenomenon of
395	cross-country and over-time differences in antitrust enforcement practices: the pol-
396	itical sociology of bureaucracies and research on the role of ideas in shaping insti-
397	tutional change. At the latest since Evans, Rueschemeyer, and Skocpol (1985) call
398	to bring the state back in, CPE has come to pay attention to the structure of state
399	institutions to explain country-specific political economic pathways. The institu-
400	tions covered range from parliaments, governments, and regulatory agencies to
401	entire political systems. As pointed out by a number of recent disciplinary surveys,
402	however, the comparative institutionalist literature has less frequently analyzed the
403	intricacies of political economic change generated after major laws have been
404	implemented and major political battles have been settled (Hacker, Pierson, &
405	Thelen, 2015; Mahoney & Thelen, 2010, pp. 13вҖ“14; Patashnik & Zelizer, 2013). In
406	this implementation phase of policies and regulations, the semi-autonomous nature
407	of bureaucracies and related professional communities can become a major driver
408	of large-scale institutional change.
409	
410	
411	2.2.1. Study of state bureaucracies
412	The role of bureaucracies in institutional change has often been described as one
413	of incremental state-building. Almost as a rule, more encompassing regulations and
414	state functions emerge with low levels of specification and practical enactment.
415	Past research has traced how bureaucracies, in interaction with societal forces and
416	professional discourse, translated vague regulatory missions into full-blown regula-
417	tory regimes вҖ“ for example in Equal Opportunity regulation or in environmental
418	policy (Dobbin, 2009; UekвӮ¬    otter, 2014). The often-implicit model of institutional
419	change underlying accounts of incremental state-building locates political conflict
420	and actual change in policies and thereby belittles implementation as a process of
421	translating vague regulatory missions into practice. Policy implementation regularly
422	appears as a technical matter that, even if riddled with professional fads and
423	mission creep by concerned agencies, is less consequential for institutional develop-
424	ment than political processes. Related to this understanding of policy implementa-
425	tion are strong distinctions between вҖҳsettledвҖҷ and вҖҳunsettledвҖҷ social formations
426	(Swidler, 1986), the latter giving rise to fundamental institutional change.
427	   We suggest going beyond such an вҖ“ admittedly highly stylized вҖ“ understanding
428	of the role of bureaucracies in institutional change in two respects. First, we argue
429	that the technical implementation of policies has a politics of its own вҖ“ with pos-
430	sibly equally far-reaching institutional effects. Second, we argue that the sharp div-
431	ide between bureaucratized and politicized institutional realms is misleading. The
432	common idea that ideological conflicts, clashes of interest, and social movement-
433	like fads are largely confined to the sphere of the political system whereas bureau-
434	cratized fields grow through technical processes can leave important changes in
435	modern political economies unexplained. As our comparative study shows, antitrust
436	вҖҳas a techniqueвҖҷ was no less shaped by value-laden, вҖҳnon-rationalвҖҷ factors than
437	
438	
439	--- PAGE BREAK ---
440	
441	                                      REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY      263
442	
443	
444	antitrust as a political creed. The difference is one of the type, rather than the
445	intensity, of political challenge.
446	    Belittling the вҖҳimplementationвҖҷ of legal rules or social norms in favor of
447	emphases on their emergence or change is a longstanding deficiency in the social
448	sciences вҖ“ especially in macro-sociology and political economy. Great legislative
449	victories or defeats, battles of grand ideologies, and mass mobilization obviously
450	strike observers as much more relevant for societal development than changes in
451	the nitty-gritty of administrative procedures or in the day-to-day enforcement prac-
452	tices. There are, however, notable exceptions to the focus on legislative politics in
453	the investigation of institutional change вҖ“ especially in various more recent institu-
454	tionalist literatures (a useful overview of earlier studies is in Evans et al., 1985).
455	
456	
457	2.2.2. Institutional change through bureaucracies
458	Two main types of argument as to why bureaucracies can be at the origins of proc-
459	esses of institutional change are especially relevant for our comparative case study.
460	The first stresses the ambiguity of legal provisions and describes bureaucracies as
461	the agents, which bring certain interpretations into practice. The second builds on
462	the idea that bureaucracies are the institutional вҖҳbridgesвҖҷ between professional ideas
463	and the state, which enable policy change through ideational change without overt
464	politicization.
465	   The first perspective has been worked out in detail by Lauren Edelman. In a
466	number of studies, she has advanced a perspective called the endogeneity of legal
467	regulation. In what might in part be an outgrowth of her research focus on a
468	Common law system and a comparatively weak state,1 she demonstrates how
469	diverse organizations influence the interpretation of legal rules by establishing
470	dominant forms of compliance, by lobbying for favorable jurisdiction and enforce-
471	ment practices, and by contributing to professional networksвҖҷ activities (Edelman,
472	1991, 1992). For the American context, policy analyses in the 1970s and 1980s
473	stressed that governance evolves in issue-specific networks between specialized state
474	agencies, parts of the legislature and government, courts, and dominant interest
475	groups.2 In a similar vein, Streeck and Thelen made the interpretative flexibility of
476	rules a starting point to theorize institutional change in general: вҖҳthe meaning of a
477	rule is never self-evident and always subject to and in need of interpretation,вҖҷ they
478	assert (Streeck & Thelen, 2005, p. 14). Therefore, the вҖҳreal meaning of an institu-
479	tion вҖҰ is inevitably вҖҰ subject to evolution driven, if by nothing else, by its
480	necessarily imperfect enactment on the ground, in directions that are often unpre-
481	dictableвҖҷ (Streeck & Thelen, 2005, p. 16). The more recent institutionalist literature
482	in political science has identified bureaucracies and courts as the main arenas for
483	such forms of incremental change. Hacker et al. (2015, p. 189) argue that institu-
484	tions vary in their вҖҳprecision.вҖҷ Hence, one may expect different political processes
485	around policies laying out clear enforcement criteria and policies вҖҳwhose provisions
486	are ambiguous and whose effects depend on interpretation and discretionвҖҷ and
487	thereby вҖҳoffer fertile terrain for strategies of conversionвҖҷ (ibd.). The latter are sub-
488	ject to challenges through вҖҳbackroomвҖҷ strategies by state agents, organized interests,
489	and professional groups and generally involve a high degree of what Culpepper
490	(2011) has called вҖҳquiet politics.вҖҷ
491	
492	
493	--- PAGE BREAK ---
494	
495	264     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
496	
497	
498	    The second perspective on institutional change through bureaucracies builds on
499	the fact that bureaucratic agencies work at the intersection of professional com-
500	munities and the state, which strengthens the role of ideas in institutional change
501	and persistence (Evans et al., 1989). Bureaucratic agencies are staffed with profes-
502	sionally trained officials вҖ“ in most developed countries predominantly by lawyers,
503	social scientists, and engineers вҖ“ who can become the institutional carriers of ideas
504	in policy-making. This can happen both through ideological conversion and
505	through personnel replacement, and often takes place gradually. Hence, new ideas
506	are often only realized when the existing staff of regulatory bodies makes an ideo-
507	logical shift вҖ“ or, more likely, when it is replaced by a new generation of bureau-
508	crats trained within a new ideational current. Both processes can give rise to
509	country-specific pathways in policy and regulation as they are shaped by the struc-
510	tures of the respective agencies and communities. Comparing the diffusion of
511	Keynesian ideas into US and UK economic policy-making since the Great
512	Depression, Margaret Weir, for example has shown how, contingent on the struc-
513	ture of the respective bureaucracies, demand management diffused slowly but in a
514	resilient way into the hierarchical official apparatus in the UK, while it gained
515	quick support in the open and fragmented US administration without taking a lon-
516	ger-term hold (Weir, 1989). With a similar theoretical outlook, Christopher Allen
517	has claimed that ideational resilience at the German Bundesbank вҖ“ which since the
518	1970s maintains its own university вҖ“ helped to undermine the diffusion of
519	Keynesian ideas into GermanyвҖҷs economic policies (Allen, 1989). Pierre Bourdieu
520	analyzed the change from capital-heavy вҖҳstone-basedвҖҷ housing subsidies to individu-
521	ally tailored housing allowances in France in the 1970s as caused by the economet-
522	ric ideas brought in by a new generation of technocrats (Bourdieu, 2005). Similar
523	processes of ideational infusion at the level of bureaucracies are at play when new
524	state capacities are built up. Thus, the influence of interest groups like realtors in
525	the creation of the New Deal housing administration, made up in large part by for-
526	mer realtors, was key to creating an industry-friendly housing policy and keeping
527	the US from supporting public housing more systematically (Mason, 2014). In
528	France, by contrast, the inflow of former public works engineers from the Pont-de-
529	Chaussee school into the newly formed ministry of housing led to the infamous
530	state Grands Ensembles constructions (Thoenig, 1973).
531	
532	
533	2.3. Implementation of competition policies and incremental change
534	Competition policies are paradigmatic cases of the two openings for incremental
535	change outlined above. First, they are characterized by widely acknowledged ambi-
536	guity with regard to enforcement practices and enforcement goals. In the above-
537	mentioned terminology, they have a very low degree of institutional precision.
538	Second, they are significantly shaped by national and transnational professional
539	communities in legal studies and economics.
540	   The far-reaching susceptibility of competition policy regimes to processes of
541	reinterpretation has often been pointed out (Fligstein, 1990, p. 213; Gerber, 1998).
542	During the first years of its existence, the American antitrust regime, for example,
543	changed from being essentially a dead letter to being used to prosecute labor
544	unions вҖ“ and from there to the basis for the breaking up of Standard Oil. Whether
545	caused by cynicism or by the genuine substantial puzzlement of legislators
546	
547	
548	--- PAGE BREAK ---
549	
550	                                      REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY     265
551	
552	
553	(Hofstadter [1964] 1996, pp. 191вҖ“192, 198вҖ“200), early American antitrust statutes
554	consisted of an odd mix of potentially very far-reaching prohibitions and vague
555	specifications. In effect, it was up to administrators and the courts to translate gen-
556	eral regulatory principles into enforcement practices. What is more, far-reaching
557	ambiguity and ambivalence marked competition policy as a regulatory field.
558	Depending on business context and theoretical sensemaking, one and the same
559	business activity вҖ“ for example, undercutting oneвҖҷs rival вҖ“ can have radically differ-
560	ent meanings for antitrust objectives. There exists a long history of different meth-
561	ods, conventions, and theories to categorize competitive conduct with regard to
562	antitrust objectives. In addition, competition law, since its inception, has been
563	plagued by ambivalence regarding the exact nature and interrelationships of its
564	principles and goals. To give just one example, the Sherman Act was passed in a
565	time of intense popular critique of вҖҳbignessвҖҷ in economic life вҖ“ especially with
566	regard to the consequences of monopolies for the political arena вҖ“ while at the
567	same time the economics profession began to favorably reinterpret the causes and
568	consequences of economic concentration in a вҖҳnew economyвҖҷ characterized by the
569	giant corporation and oligopolistic competition.3 Over the last 130 years, competi-
570	tion policy was pursued for a number of вҖ“ sometimes complementary, sometimes
571	conflicting вҖ“ goals, like consumer sovereignty, consumer welfare, democracy, geo-
572	graphical decentralization, or national economic development. Again, it was regu-
573	larly up to the enforcement layer of antitrust, and only occasionally to that of its
574	design, to come up with specifications and trade-offs for competing objectives.
575	    The vulnerability of competition policy to institutional change through spill-
576	overs between professional communities and bureaucracies is an equally well-estab-
577	lished finding. Particularly Eisner has worked out a comprehensive account of
578	institutional change in American antitrust enforcement standards that advances the
579	claim that in antitrust вҖҳpolitics вҖҰ lagged behind policyвҖҷ (Eisner, 1991, p. 233). He
580	documents how the so-called Reagan Revolution in antitrust was вҖҳat most a coupвҖҷ
581	(Eisner, 1991, p. 189): the administration merely reaffirmed changed enforcement
582	practices in the antitrust agencies and at many courts which were caused by
583	changed thinking about competition policy. Similar assessments about the root
584	causes of institutional change in American antitrust are advanced by legal scholars
585	and industrial economists (Kovacic, 1990, 2003; Pitofsky, 2008). What distinguishes
586	these accounts from the traditional view of the role of bureaucrats in institutional
587	change cited above is that state agents and professional communities do not just
588	extend or implement policies in a path-dependent manner, but are themselves
589	responsible for incremental, but quite drastic, changes of policy course. Such
590	accounts hardly fit a standard model of bureaucratic activity in which bureaucracies
591	serve as agents taking care of the straightforward application of policies to practice.
592	Our cross-country comparison serves to illustrate exactly that point. State agents
593	and professional communities reacted to similar perceived challenges with funda-
594	mentally different intellectual currents, which led to diverging trajectories of incre-
595	mental change in competition policy.
596	
597	
598	3. Varieties of economization in competition policy
599	Our comparative historical analysis reconstructs the historical origins of what com-
600	mentators have called the вҖҳAtlantic divideвҖҷ in antitrust (Gifford & Kudrle, 2015;
601	
602	
603	--- PAGE BREAK ---
604	
605	266     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
606	
607	
608	Gutierrez & Philippon, 2018). Competition policy regimes are complex regulatory
609	structures, composed of a loosely coupled multitude of regulations and multiple
610	enforcement layers. Today, most competition policy regimes in advanced countries
611	are roughly composed of four main regulatory areas вҖ“ the prosecution of cartel
612	activities, the policing of attempts to monopolize markets, the control of merger
613	activity, and the enforcement of statutes against deceptive and unfair trade practi-
614	ces. The diagnosis of an вҖҳAtlantic divideвҖҷ is a widely shared professional generaliza-
615	tion based on observations of diverging enforcement practices, in particular with
616	respect to attempts to monopolize markets and mergers. Antitrust scholars speak
617	about these historically highly flexible parts of competition policy as вҖҳantitrust law
618	other than cartel law,вҖҷ as the prosecution of вҖҳhard-core cartels,вҖҷ fixing prices, out-
619	put, or structures of distribution, has rarely been contentious and in need of inter-
620	pretation and might even have been strengthened across the globe during the last
621	50 years (Fox, 2008, p. 98, Fn. 18).
622	    Despite being widely shared among antitrust scholars, the exact parameters of
623	divergence between Europe and the US вҖ“ of policy change in either region as well
624	as in comparison вҖ“ are notoriously difficult to pin down with precision. It is gener-
625	ally difficult to estimate the effects antitrust policies have on the overall structure
626	of industrial organization. Effective competition policies would, to a large degree,
627	prevent actions in restraint of competition before the fact. It is equally difficult to
628	quantify the effects of new doctrines on the activity of state agencies. Rising or fall-
629	ing case numbers, for example, say very little about the direction of enforcement
630	practice, both because cases vary widely in their scope and depth and because
631	numbers of cases are contingent on agency activity as well as economic activity.
632	Moreover, as complex regulatory structures as a rule involve decentral experiments,
633	there are numerous cases deviating from the more general pattern of regime devel-
634	opment. These measurement problems are at the core of fundamentally different
635	views of how American antitrust enforcement has changed since the 1960s. What
636	for some amounts to a вҖҳcorporate takeover of the marketвҖҷ (Crouch, 2011, Ch. 3) or
637	вҖҳa return to the period of neglect of the 1920sвҖҷ (Pitofsky, 2008, p. 5), represents a
638	healthy dose of self-questioning and analytical sharpening for others (Kovacic,
639	2003; Scherer, 2008).
640	    Scholars often resort to key cases and decisions in Europe and the US to make
641	the case for divergence. One example is the European blocking of the merger
642	between GE and Honeywell, which had passed US enforcement agencies without
643	problems, another one the European tendency to enforce rules against price dis-
644	crimination and bundling by dominant firms. Finally, there are repeated European
645	attempts to apply antitrust laws more aggressively to the IT and software sectors.4
646	Another apparent indication of divergence is the fact that the European
647	Commission, which until the 2000s took over leadership in significant parts of EU
648	Member StatesвҖҷ enforcement functions, faced outright hostility from professionals
649	and national antitrust authorities when it since the late 1990s tried to implement
650	conventions firmly established in American enforcement (Buxbaum, 2005; Gerber,
651	2007). What the extensive conflicts over the вҖҳmodernizationвҖҷ of European antitrust
652	law brought to the fore was that significant parts of European doctrine and
653	enforcement practice were incompatible with the way the antitrust enterprise had
654	changed across the Atlantic.
655	
656	
657	--- PAGE BREAK ---
658	
659	                                      REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY       267
660	
661	
662	   We argue that the major fault lines of the Atlantic divide can be made explicit
663	through a focus on the German antitrust doctrine and hence GermanвҖ“American
664	comparison. For once, the German antitrust authorities were originally as much an
665	institutional blueprint in the set-up of the European ones as was the Bundesbank
666	for the European Central Bank. Then, it was German professionals and agencies,
667	which were at the forefront of critique of the EUвҖҷs вҖҳmodernizationвҖҷ initiatives since
668	the 1990s. Among European nations, Germany sticks out, due to the size of its
669	economy and its importance for antitrust in Europe. More than 50% of all
670	European merger and acquisition cases since 1991, for instance, included a German
671	company (own calculation). Finally, from a structural-economic perspective,
672	Germany could arguably have been a likely nation to follow Chicago School doc-
673	trines given its strong restraint of trade-friendly stance in the past.
674	   In fact, our analysis shows that core parts of the incompatibilities between
675	European and American antitrust enforcement can be traced back to the specific
676	way in which the German antitrust profession reacted to progressive reform pro-
677	posals in the 1960s and 1970s. The German profession incrementally developed
678	beliefs about the proper goals and procedures of competition policy enforcement
679	that вҖ“ like its American counterpart вҖ“ sought to push back against the influence of
680	broader societal concerns. But, it did so in a way that shielded its enforcement sys-
681	tem from the lowering of enforcement standards seen across the Atlantic.
682	   As growing incompatibilities between doctrines are difficult to describe compre-
683	hensively, we resort to an analysis of the language used by competition policy
684	authorities to describe their activities and goals. An indication of the diverging tra-
685	jectories of German and American enforcement can be found in the annual activity
686	reports antitrust authorities produce for their supervisory bodies. In contrast to
687	content analyses of more explicit political documents, like manifestos and speeches,
688	the more procedural nature of competition policy reports makes an analysis of the
689	implicitly used categories more suitable to uncover changes over time than a clas-
690	sical discourse analysis.
691	   To this purpose, we constructed a dictionary for the typical vocabulary associ-
692	ated with the more traditional form-based approach (and hence not the more
693	recent effect-based approach). We expect documents to be more likely of the form-
694	based orientation if they refer to economic concentration as a problem, if economic
695	freedom is an ideal, and if small- and medium-sized enterprises facing monopoly
696	power are a concern.5 The over-time trend of the terms (and their variants) mak-
697	ing up this dictionary is depicted in Figure 1 for the American case. The relative
698	frequency of all terms tends to decline over time, which we take as supportive evi-
699	dence for a decreasing importance of the form-based approach in the US. The con-
700	cern for free competition and small enterprises or the fear of monopoly power was
701	very present from the 1940s to 1960s, as when a report stated in 1947 that the
702	вҖҳdemocratic political structure upon which this country is based requires a system
703	of free competitive enterprise to sustain it industrially and to realize its fullest eco-
704	nomic potential.вҖҷ By 1994, in turn, the language had shifted as when the respective
705	report claimed that вҖҳcompetitive markets serve consumers by fostering innovation
706	and efficient resource allocation.вҖҷ
707	   Quite a different picture over time is found in the reports of the German anti-
708	trust authorities, as shown in Figure 2. There the occurrence of references to con-
709	centration and freedom remain important through time, while the concern about
710	
711

715	
716	
717

722	
723	
724

726	Figure 2. Relative frequency of form-based dictionary terms in German antitrust reports. Source:

729	
730	monopolies and a proper market structure even increases. Only references to small
731	businesses and вҖ“ very recently вҖ“ monopolies decrease, although later and less
732	severe than in the US. An explanation for this finding might be that, compared to
733	the US, European antitrust institutions were more heavily involved in the de novo
734	creation of Union-wide markets. The focus on nation statesвҖҷ established restrictions
735	of trade often referred to formal and quasi-monopolies or monopoly like struc-
736	tures, which have been reduced significantly in the construction of the European
737	Common Market. Despite this, the general picture of diverging antitrust vocabula-
738	ries reveals sufficient variation to justify our comparative focus on the evolution of
739	professional doctrines.
740	
741	
742	--- PAGE BREAK ---
743	
744	                                         REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY             269
745	
746	
747	3.1. The administrative economization of American antitrust
748	Compared to the German case, the historical literature on institutional change in
749	American competition policy since the 1960s is large and well developed. We pre-
750	sent a highly stylized historical account of that process, highlighting the main pro-
751	grammatic cleavages at the core of debates about the direction of US competition
752	policy in the formative period between the late 1960s and the mid-1980s. The goals
753	of our analysis are twofold: We document the emergence and structure of enforce-
754	ment doctrines that enabled substantial institutional changes and lay the ground
755	for understanding the alternative path of German doctrines.
756	
757	
758	3.1.1. Strengthening antitrust
759	The vulnerability of the American antitrust system for processes of incremental
760	change has important institutional determinants. It formally operates largely inde-
761	pendent from the political system. American antitrust is jointly enforced by the
762	Department of Justice Antitrust Division and the Federal Trade Commission, sub-
763	ject to extensive judicial review, and relies heavily on litigation by private parties.
764	Estimates for the share of private initiative in American antitrust suggest that
765	вҖҳprivate cases filed annually in US District Courts ranged from 83 to 96 percent of
766	the total from 1975 to 2003вҖҷ (Gifford & Kudrle, 2015, p. 18). The DOJ and the
767	FTC share many authorities, cooperate in investigations, and traditionally specialize
768	by sectors or industries. While both bureacraciesвҖҷ heads are appointed by
769	Administrations, commentators usually credit them with substantial independence
770	and discretion (Kovacic, 2011).
771	   This institutional embedding was at the core of the dramatic incremental expan-
772	sion of enforcement activity between the 1920s and 1970s, largely in the absence of
773	momentous legislative changes (Hofstadter, [1964] 1996). With regard to enforce-
774	ment practices, the American antitrust bureaucracy since the New Deal has usually
775	been characterized as an entrepreneurial driver of expansion in the direction of
776	early populist understandings of the antitrust laws. Without further qualifications,
777	Richard Hofstadter was able to claim that antitrust in his time was вҖҳessentially a
778	political rather than an economic enterpriseвҖҷ (Hofstadter, [1964] 1996, p. 200).
779	Even though the history of governmental attempts to use the antitrust laws as a
780	tool to lower the concentration of American industry after the fact is, with few
781	exceptions, a history of costly failures and misses (Kovacic, 1989), deconcentration
782	for economic, political, and cultural reasons has been a centerpiece of the antitrust
783	agenda since the 1930s. In many high-profile cases before the 1970s, it was offi-
784	cially recognized by American courts (Gifford & Kudrle, 2015, pp. 12вҖ“13). Court
785	opinions and politiciansвҖҷ speeches before the 1970s contained references to small
786	businesses as вҖҳworthy menвҖҷ, warned against the political ramifications of corporate
787	concentration, and quoted the вҖҳsocial and moral effectsвҖҷ of economic independence
788	(Pitofsky, 1979). The вҖҳpolitical content of antitrustвҖҷ (ibid.) pervaded large parts of
789	post-New Deal enforcement and jurisdiction, often with a good amount of enthusi-
790	astic вҖҳovershootingвҖҷ:
791	   вҖҰ tiny mergers that could not seriously be viewed as challenges to a competitive market
792	   were consistently blocked, abbreviated (so-called per se) rules were introduced to outlaw
793	   behavior that rarely produced anticompetitive or anticonsumer effects, and licensing
794	   practices were challenged, which were little more than efforts to engage in aggressive
795	
796	
797	--- PAGE BREAK ---
798	
799	270         T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
800	
801	
802	      innovation. All of this was accompanied by an almost total disregard for business claims of
803	      efficiency. (Pitofsky, 2008, p. 4)
804	
805	   The intellectual bases of much of this activist style of enforcement stemmed
806	from what came to be called the Harvard School of industrial economics вҖ“ a school
807	of microeconomic analysis with a longstanding interest in systematizing the struc-
808	tural preconditions of anticompetitive behavior.6 In effect, many of the activist
809	enforcement policies of the postwar decades were legitimized by the belief that the
810	preservation of certain industry structures would render future anticompetitive
811	behavior more unlikely. Both the professionвҖҷs focus on structure and its pursuit of
812	the deconcentration agenda in many ways culminated in the report of Lyndon
813	JohnsonвҖҷs Task Force on Antitrust Policy, the so-called Neal Report. The experts
814	suggested the passage of what they called the Concentrated Industries Act, a meas-
815	ure that would allow administrators to force firms in concentrated industries to
816	divest structures to limit their market share to 12% (Neal, 1968).7
817	
818	
819	3.1.2. The effect-based revolution
820	While the administrative movement against such activist tendencies came to for-
821	mally dominate the field during the presidency of Ronald Reagan, conclusive evi-
822	dence exists that the so-called Reagan Revolution in competition policy was merely
823	part of an official acknowledgment of changes already firmly anchored among pro-
824	fessionals, bureaucrats, and in the judiciary (Eisner, 1991; Eisner & Meier, 1990).
825	The original doctrinal challenge carrying that movement is captured in Robert
826	BorkвҖҷs dissenting statement to the Neal Report. Bork, who in many ways led the
827	intellectual assault on structure-focused activist antitrust, was a member of the
828	Neal Commission and criticized the proposed deconcentration initiative on the
829	basis of a then comparatively extreme faith in market processes that widely diffused
830	into US antitrust thinking during the following decades,
831	      When firms grow to sizes that create concentration or when such a structure is created by
832	      merger and persists for many years, there is a very strong prima facie case that the firmsвҖҷ
833	      sizes are related to efficiency вҖҰ If the leading firms in a concentrated industry are
834	      restricting their output in order to obtain prices above the competitive level, their
835	      efficiencies must be sufficiently superior to that of all actual and potential rivals to offset
836	      that behavior. Were this not so, rivals would be enabled to expand their market shares
837	      because of the abnormally high prices and would thus deconcentrate the industry. Market
838	      rivalry thus automatically weighs the respective influences of efficiency and output
839	      restriction and arrives at the firm sizes and industry structures that serve consumers best
840	      (Bork, 1969, p. 54).
841	
842	   There was both an analytical and a normative side to critiques like BorkвҖҷs.
843	Analytically, they challenged established views of what certain market structures
844	and processes вҖҳactually meantвҖҷ in economic terms. Many theoretical attacks
845	revolved around the question of whether certain business activities formerly catego-
846	rized as attempts to restrict competition вҖ“ like extensive conglomerate mergers,
847	price wars, or tie-ins, for example вҖ“ could really be understood as directed against
848	competition on the basis of neoclassical microeconomics. In rebutting theories of
849	вҖҳpredatory pricingвҖҷ (firmsвҖҷ attempts to undercut rivals at a loss, in order to profit
850	from increased market power after the rivalsвҖҷ exit), for example conservative
851	thinkers made reference to turn-of-century arguments about the disciplining role
852	of вҖҳpotential competitionвҖҷ and to the long-run rationality of businesses to discredit
853	
854	
855	--- PAGE BREAK ---
856	
857	                                            REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY                 271
858	
859	
860	the view that aggressive underselling could at any time be interpreted as an attempt
861	to achieve market power:
862	   Selling below cost in order to drive out a competitor is unprofitable even in the long run,
863	   except in the unlikely case in which the intended victim lacks equal access to capital to
864	   finance a price war. The predator loses money during the period of predation and, if he
865	   tries to recoup it later by raising his price, new entrants will be attracted, the price will be
866	   bid down to the competitive level, and the attempt at recoupment will fail. Most alleged
867	   instances of below-cost pricing must, therefore, be attributable to factors other than a
868	   desire to eliminate competition (Posner, 1979, p. 927).8
869	
870	   Such analytical rebuttals of earlier thought about anticompetitive conduct
871	appeared for almost every target of postwar antitrust enforcement during the 1970s
872	and 1980s вҖ“ notably for vertical integration, many kinds of vertical restraints, and
873	persistent high concentration (Bork, 1954; Burns, 1993; Hovenkamp, 2009).
874	   The accompanying normative assault on antitrust enforcement practices targeted
875	the question of the legitimate aims of competition policy. Conservative thinkers
876	and practitioners in the 1960s and 1970s directly reacted to the multitude of values
877	and political objectives in antitrust enforcement:
878	   That amalgam of muddled thinking, social mythology, and sentimental rhetoric known to
879	   its intimates as вҖңthe social purposes of antitrust,вҖқ however sonorously it may ring upon
880	   ritual occasions for mock-Jeffersonian oratory, must be excluded from judicial and
881	   prosecutorial decisions about actual cases (Bork, 1970, p. 666).
882	
883	    In favor of antitrust as a political common carrier (Pierson, 2004, pp. 109вҖ“110)
884	that reacted to a multitude of political objectives, efficiency in the service of con-
885	sumer welfare came to be the dominant criterion to judge whether business activ-
886	ities fell into the purview of antitrust agencies. In practice this meant a decline of
887	per se reasoning by the bureaucracy and by courts, a higher technical threshold for
888	charging businesses with anticompetitive conduct, and the extension of thinking in
889	terms of вҖҳwelfare trade-offsвҖҷ between the negative effects of concentration and anti-
890	competitive conduct and the efficiency advantages of certain restraining activities
891	and structures (Williamson, 1968). Most important, perhaps, Chicago antitrust
892	thinkers openly stated that their intellectual attacks had a political economic object-
893	ive to push back against what they perceived to be increasingly interventionist ten-
894	dencies in the deconcentration movement:
895	   A further aspect of the ChicagoвҖ“Harvard difference on deconcentration arises from the
896	   difference between the deep distrust of government intervention that is associated with the
897	   Chicago School of Economics вҖҰ and the (rapidly diminishing) complacency toward such
898	   intervention associated with traditional Harvard-M.I.T. economic thinking.
899	   Deconcentration is a more ambitious form of public control than is usually involved in
900	   antitrust enforcement, so oneвҖҷs attitudes toward the capabilities of regulatory-type
901	   governmental interventions naturally come into play (Posner, 1979, p. 948, Fn. 67).
902	
903	   It is difficult to unbundle the historical causes of the spectacular success of effi-
904	ciency criteria in American antitrust. In the last three decades it was, it seems,
905	overdetermined. There certainly has been executive sanctioning of the new doctrine
906	by the Nixon, Carter, and Reagan administrations, as visible, for example, in
907	NixonвҖҷs installation of his own task force on the state of antitrust headed by
908	Chicago economist George Stigler; in the Carter administrationвҖҷs experimentation
909	with supply side economic revitalization policies; in several nominations, guide-
910	lines, and statements by the Reagan administration; or in the extension of
911	
912	
913	--- PAGE BREAK ---
914	
915	272     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
916	
917	
918	intellectual property protection since the 1970s. In the academy, the more rigorous
919	approach to industrial economics revived academic interest in thinking about com-
920	petition, while the earlier, more empirical Harvard School of microeconomics was
921	a relatively marginalized field. Within the rising Chicago School itself, there had
922	also been a shift from a previous anti-monopoly stance to a much more permissive
923	position toward concentration in business (Van Horn, 2011). Furthermore, as is
924	visible in the success of the law and economics movement, the legal profession in
925	the United States was remarkably open to economic reasoning and an instrumen-
926	talist logic in enforcement. To give just one example of how far thinking about
927	monopoly in the American political economy changed with the prominence of wel-
928	fare economics, Robert Crandall, a consultant to Microsoft during the failed gov-
929	ernmental attempts to break up the corporation, after reviewing the welfare effects
930	of structural remedies in American history, concluded that
931	    вҖҳвҖңa number of empirical studies suggest that the total cost of monopoly is very
932	small indeed. [One study, TE/SK] found that the social cost of monopoly is only
933	0.1% of gross national product вҖҰ If monopoly is not much of a problem in the
934	first place, it is understandable that Section 2 cases are rare and Section 2 remedies
935	are not very effectiveвҖҷ (Crandall, 2001, pp. 196вҖ“197) (Section 2 of the Sherman Act
936	covers attempts to monopolize a market).
