```
937	    The change in enforcement doctrines was not so much about a switch from
938	unfocused or habitual types of enforcement toward a more reasoned and goal-ori-
939	ented approach, as Chicago school representatives often had it, but about the incre-
940	mental replacement of the mission of the regime вҖ“ with quite drastic changes for
941	the character of the institution of antimonopoly policy. To reiterate, measuring the
942	precise impact of these changes on enforcement activity and industrial organization
943	is inherently difficult. There is, however, scattered evidence that antitrust enforce-
944	ment became more permissive since the 1960s вҖ“ that it changed course from deca-
945	des of activist expansion. It is uncontested among both proponents and critics of
946	the Chicago revolution in antitrust thinking that the regulation of both horizontal
947	and vertical mergers has been more lax since the early 1980s, when the William
948	Baxter-led Antitrust Division codified many of the new efficiency-focused ideas in
949	revised merger guidelines in reaction to what it described as вҖҳchanges in economic
950	analysis and judicial precedentвҖҷ (Department of Justice, 1982, p. 135). To give
951	another example of the entrepreneurial activities of the new generation of bureau-
952	crats to disseminate the new ideational current in merger control, the Antitrust
953	Division helped prepare legislative proposals for an overhaul of the Clayton Act
954	regulating mergers in 1986, promising вҖҳto distinguish more clearly between pro-
955	competitive mergers and mergers that create a significant probability of increasing
956	prices to consumersвҖҷ (Department of Justice, 1986, p. 112). During the 1980s anti-
957	trust division repeatedly pushed for legislative reform, for example in intellectual
958	property right protection, as these would вҖҳpromote consumer welfare, enhance the
959	ability of U.S. firms to compete in worldwide marketplaces, and stimulate product-
960	ivity and efficiencyвҖҷ (ibd.). The prosecution of predatory competition has, after a
961	brief revival during the 1980s, virtually been abandoned in the early 1990s. In fact,
962	the 1986 Antitrust Division of the Department of Justice (DOJ) voluntarily filed a
963	Supreme Court amicus brief to push back against a lower courtвҖҷs decision that
964	might have allowed competitors to challenge mergers based on the possibility that
965	the post-merger firm would engage in predatory pricing, citing вҖҳthe strong
966	
967	
968	--- PAGE BREAK ---
969	
970	                                     REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY     273
971	
972	
973	incentive of competitors to block procompetitive transactions вҖҰ , the rarity of
974	actual predation and the ease with which intense competition may be characterized
975	as predationвҖҷ (Department of Justice, 1986, p. 113). Several high-profile cases
976	against dominant firms were settled during the 1980s, notably those against IBM
977	and AT&T. In many fields of enforcement, courts have denied the applicability of
978	per se rules. And the вҖҳefficiency-defenseвҖҷ for anticompetitive conduct has been
979	firmly established in both the bureaucracy and the judiciary.9
980	
981	
982	3.2. Impaired вҖҳmodernizationвҖҷ in Germany
983	In the larger historical picture, the main puzzling fact about German competition
984	policy might be its similarity to American antitrust in the postwar era, rather than
985	the remaining institutional differences which concern us here (see Djelic, 2002).
986	After all, until the late 1950s when the German antitrust law was passed, the
987	German political economy was the prime example of an advanced political econ-
988	omy organized by doctrines revolving around sectoral organization, coordinated
989	industrial upgrading, and horizontal agreements, rather than by American ideals of
990	oligopolistic competition.10
991	    A few decades after the end of the Second World War, however, the German
992	and other European nations had full-blown antitrust regimes in place, considerable
993	parts of which were either directly transferred from or modeled after the American
994	doctrinal and institutional system. In terms of codified legal rules, European and
995	American antitrust regimes are remarkably similar вҖ“ in both content and structure.
996	Still, there always have been significant remaining institutional differences. Even
997	though the European Court of Justice, for example, has shown a strong tendency
998	to extend its own reach and thereby вҖҳdriveвҖҷ rather than вҖҳinterpretвҖҷ the law (Scharpf,
999	2016), the US and many European nations still have different legal regimes with
1000	respect to case law or the adversarial design of trials.
1001	    With regard to its institutional structure, German competition policy is
1002	embedded in the multilevel EU governance regime. The original German antitrust
1003	authority, the Bundeskartellamt founded in 1957, from the beginning enjoyed far-
1004	reaching independence from the political system. Like in the US, the German cartel
1005	office independently investigates cases and is subject to extensive judicial review by
1006	both district and national courts and since the 1960s by the European Court of
1007	Justice. German states (LвӮ¬ander) maintain additional cartel offices, which are less
1008	independent, but usually perceived to work in tandem with the federal agency
1009	(Fiebig, 1993). Different from the United States, antitrust enforcement responsibil-
1010	ities in the executive are not located in the German Ministry of Justice, but in the
1011	Ministry of the Economy, which has the (in practice limited) ability to block
1012	enforcement in cases of overarching public concern. The relationship between
1013	national competition authorities and the European Union since the Treaty of Rome
1014	of 1957 is contentious, complex, and constantly evolving. For a long time, specific
1015	sectors, like steel and coal, were handled at the EU-level. European governance in
1016	principle is based on subsidiarity, so that national authorities are in charge of
1017	national cases and prosecution. From certain relevance thresholds of European
1018	activities and revenues onwards, however, the European Commission usually takes
1019	over investigations.
1020	
1021	
1022	--- PAGE BREAK ---
1023	
1024	274     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
1025	
1026	
1027	   With regard to substance, European enforcement for a long time seemed to be
1028	more lenient toward restraints on trade than that of the United States during its
1029	activist enforcement era. For a long time, European and German competition pol-
1030	icy was seen to be an вҖҳincompleteвҖҷ institutional transfer (good overviews are Hesse,
1031	2016a; Quack & Djelic, 2005). Until recently, German and European competition
1032	law contained quite far-reaching explicit exceptions with respect to various eco-
1033	nomic sectors and various types of horizontal cooperation (Hardach, 2016, pp.
1034	223вҖ“226), such as the 1957 field exemption of banking and insurance that was
1035	repealed only in 1998. Formal merger control was only added to German antitrust
1036	in 1973. Private litigation is still underdeveloped in Europe and is bound to public
1037	authoritiesвҖҷ discovery of wrongdoing (Gifford & Kudrle, 2015, p. 20). And
1038	Europeans displayed a general hesitance when it came to activist antitrust and
1039	deconcentration in the private sector вҖ“ notwithstanding the bureaucracyвҖҷs later
1040	enthusiasm for using competition law to go against state interference in the econ-
1041	omy and limited support for American decartelization initiatives in the immediate
1042	postwar time, which were organized by temporary special laws.
1043	   During the last two decades, institutional divergence in the opposite direction
1044	has occurred. European antitrust agencies still prosecute predatory competition,
1045	they go after numerous types of vertical restraints, they are still more open to per
1046	se reasoning and, for a long time at least, they stuck to more form-based stances in
1047	merger control (Gifford & Kudrle, 2015). In the following section, we argue that
1048	more hesitant enforcement during the American activist era and more strict
1049	enforcement during the era of American leniency have a common and underappre-
1050	ciated cause вҖ“ professional identities and doctrines in German legal thinking about
1051	competition and the role of the state that to a large degree solidified in reaction to
1052	an activist challenge similar to the one in the United States. 11
1053	
1054	
1055	3.2.1. Resisting activist antitrust
1056	The German and European antitrust agencies have a strong non-economic legal
1057	tradition, and even though they have hired increasing numbers of economics pro-
1058	fessionals since the early 1970s, legal professionals still make up about half of their
1059	bureaucrats today (Buch-Hansen & Wigger, 2011; Ortwein, 1998).12
1060	   The resistance to American enforcement standards is not the first case in which
1061	German Ordoliberal legal elites resisted economic definitions of the goals of com-
1062	petition policy and in which they had a hard time bringing a policy in line with
1063	liberal convictions that made deep state interventions into the economy the norm.
1064	Ordoliberalism, a loose network of intellectuals that emerged in the 1920s, is a typ-
1065	ically German variety of liberal thought that came to dominate postwar debates
1066	about the economy (Hien & Joerges, 2017). Ideologically situated between a rejec-
1067	tion of state planning and the idea that competitive markets need sustaining state
1068	institutions, this school came to shape many postwar economic institutions in
1069	Germany and Europe.
1070	   While Ordoliberal elites certainly were the driving domestic forces for the adop-
1071	tion of both the European and German antitrust regimes (Quack & Djelic, 2005),
1072	they had difficulties coming up with a conception of the new law that was compat-
1073	ible with their political guiding principle of process-neutral вҖҳframework policiesвҖҷ
1074	from the very beginning. The enforcement of вҖҳperfect competitionвҖҷ вҖ“ or the
1075	
1076	
1077	--- PAGE BREAK ---
1078	
1079	                                     REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY     275
1080	
1081	
1082	enforcement of behavior of powerful firms as if they were in вҖҳperfect competitionвҖҷ
1083	вҖ“ would mean that the state had to push the economy into an arbitrary and artifi-
1084	cial state of organization. At the latest during the 1960s, Ordoliberals converged on
1085	an ideal conception of the new law that limited it to вҖҳnegativeвҖҷ state interventions,
1086	meaning that it was only to вҖҳpreventвҖҷ but not to вҖҳprescribeвҖҷ economic activities in
1087	the service of the maintenance of market structures that would limit market power.
1088	The core purpose of such a system was not the creation of a specific form of com-
1089	petition, but something Ordoliberals early on called вҖҳcompetitive freedom.вҖҷ German
1090	cartel law was intended to protect the freedom to engage in competition and the
1091	freedom of sellers and buyers to choose among competing offers. Thus, the first
1092	annual report of the antitrust agency emphasized that
1093	    вҖҳ the law is not supposed to punish but to create order. It leaves every possible
1094	freedom to the entrepreneur, as long as he does not attempt to arbitrarily alter the
1095	economic conditions through distortions of and obstacles to competition. Thus, the
1096	law not only serves an economic, but a societal purposeвҖҷ(Bundeskartellamt, 1959, p.
1097	11; our translation).
1098	    What sounded like an odd legalistic formalization of an institutional transfer led
1099	into heated debates about enforcement practices in the 1960s and 1970s that came
1100	to be known as the Hoppmann-Kantzenbach controversy. This intellectual battle
1101	was triggered by a series of publications in which Erhard Kantzenbach, a microeco-
1102	nomist whose ideas were closely related to the American Harvard School and who
1103	became chairman of the German Monopoly Commission between 1979 and 1986,
1104	tried to develop a system of desirable functions of economic competition as a
1105	guideline for antitrust enforcement (Kantzenbach, 1968). As such, these functions
1106	were largely uncontentious вҖ“ improving factor allocation, stimulating technological
1107	change, and enhancing the adaptability of the industrial system, for example. What
1108	triggered the fierce attack by Erich Hoppmann, the successor to Friedrich Hayek at
1109	the Ordoliberal bastion, the Walter Eucken Institute at the University of Freiburg,
1110	were policy prescriptions formulated by Kantzenbach to allow certain вҖҳlimitedвҖҷ
1111	restraints on trade in antitrust enforcement if these served his system of the desired
1112	effects of competition вҖ“ temporary cartels and the stimulation of mergers, for
1113	example вҖ“ and his openness toward a more active deconcentration agenda.
1114	    In a series of publications, Hoppmann violently attacked KantzenbachвҖҷs plea for
1115	a more instrumental approach to antitrust in its infancy. First, Ordoliberals seemed
1116	to be concerned about the political economic consequences of KantzenbachвҖҷs vision
1117	for antitrust. They feared a slippery slope which would turn competition policy
1118	into another tool of state intervention and planning. After all, the late 1960s and
1119	1970s were the high time of experimentation in industrial policy in rich Western
1120	nations. Moves away from per se restrictions toward increasing consideration of
1121	individual cases could have further opened the regime up to the increasing influ-
1122	ence of interest groups on cartel policy, undermining the central bank-like inde-
1123	pendent status of the cartel office. Second, Hoppmann fundamentally doubted the
1124	claim that there were conflicts between KantzenbachвҖҷs functions of competition
1125	and a formalistically enforced вҖҳfreedom to competeвҖҷ which could be known to
1126	bureaucrats before the competitive process unfolded. Most distinctive, perhaps,
1127	Hoppmann took up the early Ordoliberal notion of вҖҳcompetitive freedomвҖҷ and
1128	emphasized that it should have a certain non-teleological character вҖ“ it
1129	
1130	
1131	--- PAGE BREAK ---
1132	
1133	276         T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
1134	
1135	
1136	      is about competition as an end in itself, as certain forms of economic freedom are
1137	      manifested in it. Freedom of competition means: freedom of initiative, freedom to advance
1138	      into new technical, organizational, and economic territories, to create new goods, new
1139	      processes, new markets, freedom of economic progress. On the other side of the market
1140	      there is a corresponding freedom to choose among alternatives вҖҰ Restraints of the
1141	      freedom of competition are identical to the artificial creation of market power and vice
1142	      versa. The norm of competition policy must be that kind of competition that results if the
1143	      freedom of competition is secured against restraining business practices (Hoppmann, 1966,
1144	      p. 19; our translation).
1145	
1146	   The doctrine of competitive freedom developed in two important strands of
1147	thought. On the one hand, the epistemological argument against instrumentalist
1148	antitrust enforcement became one of the main arguments for a form-based and
1149	rule-oriented approach. If the results of competition were known in advance, an
1150	often-cited 1969 quip by Friedrich Hayek went, capitalist societies would not have
1151	to rely on it to organize their economies in the first place:
1152	      Competition is thus, like experimentation in science, first and foremost a discovery
1153	      procedure. No theory can do justice to it which starts from the assumption that the facts
1154	      to be discovered are already known. There is no predetermined range of known or вҖңgivenвҖқ
1155	      facts which will ever all be taken into account. All we can hope to secure is a procedure
1156	      that is on the whole likely to bring about a situation where more of the potentially useful
1157	      objective facts will be taken into account than would be done in any other procedure
1158	      which we know. It is the circumstances which makes so irrelevant for the choice of a
1159	      desirable policy all evaluation of the results of competition that starts from the assumption
1160	      that all the relevant facts are known to some single mind (Hayek, [1979] 1998, p. 68).
1161	
1162	
1163	
1164	3.2.2. From resisting activism to blocking economization
1165	In such a view, early German deconcentration debates, proposed merger control
1166	(on which Hoppmann later revised his views), and KantzenbachвҖҷs instrumental
1167	vision for antitrust enforcement became just another hopeless exercise in central
1168	planning, in which state agents tried to come up with optimal firm sizes, proper
1169	market shares and desirable rates of industrial adjustment. While radical arguments
1170	like HayekвҖҷs never came to dominate the German and European antitrust profes-
1171	sion, they made generations of practitioners more hesitant when it came to more
1172	вҖҳrationalвҖҷ, вҖҳmodernвҖҷ, and вҖҳgoal-orientedвҖҷ enforcement standards.
1173	    Subsequently, the idea that competition policy protects competitive freedom вҖҳin
1174	itselfвҖҷ proved to be widely influential in legal thought. In part, the allergic reaction
1175	of legal scholars to welfare goals in antitrust was a symptom of a more general dis-
1176	trust of postwar legal thought with positivist, instrumental interpretations of the
1177	rule of law. In the hands of one of the most influential legal scholars in the
1178	German antitrust profession, Ernst-Joachim MestmвӮ¬acker, the вҖҳvalue-rationalвҖҷ pro-
1179	tection of competitive freedom came to symbolize the autonomy of the law. In an
1180	attack on the law and economics movement, he lamented,
1181	      Cost-benefit analysis is end-neutral. It can be applied to any given purpose. Constitutions,
1182	      statutes and precedents, however, are as a rule not end-neutral. The question then is how
1183	      to accommodate the normative implications of economic analysis with diverse non-
1184	      economic legal purposes. In law, the relation of ends to means is more than a pragmatic
1185	      methodological operation вҖҰ Wealth maximization is no substitute for the purpose of law
1186	      in general (MestmвӮ¬acker, 2007, p. 13).
1187	
1188	
1189	--- PAGE BREAK ---
1190	
1191	                                     REVIEW OF INTERNATIONAL POLITICAL ECONOMY    277
1192	
1193	
1194	    Such debates were not exclusive to academia. As Hesse (2016b, pp. 443вҖ“446)
1195	documents, they were at the core of conflicts between key actors in economic pol-
1196	icy during the first Grand Coalition in Germany (1966вҖ“1969). Eberhard GвӮ¬     unter, a
1197	key bureaucrat behind the original 1957 German antitrust law and until 1976 the
1198	first President of the German antitrust authority, publicly subscribed to
1199	KantzenbachвҖҷs general plea in 1967, what led ordoliberal intellectuals to send pro-
1200	test-notes to the economics ministry. Karl Schiller, progressive economics minister
1201	from 1966 to 1972, had his bureaucracy work toward a far-reaching amendment of
1202	German antitrust law вҖҳto stimulate structural change in industry and productivity
1203	growthвҖҷ since 1968 and tried to steer the scientific advisory board of the economics
1204	ministry toward the development of an accompanying new mission statement for
1205	German antitrust (Hesse, 2016b, p. 445). These plans were blocked when key legal
1206	members of the board, such as Franz BвӮ¬     ohm, diverted discussions from different
1207	theories of competition toward the problem of вҖҳinstrumentality,вҖҷ as discussed by
1208	Hoppmann and MestmвӮ¬acker. In general, personnel and ideational spill-overs
1209	between ordoliberal intellectuals and the political sphere can be observed over the
1210	entire history of German antitrust. MestmвӮ¬acker, for example became the first presi-
1211	dent of the antitrust- commission Monopolkommission an expert council, which
1212	advises the government on antitrust matters and annually publishes general and
1213	special reports on the state of competition in Germany that was instituted to
1214	accompany German merger control in 1973.
1215	    As a whole, the professional doctrines around competitive freedom, as refined
1216	in the 1960sвҖҷ and 1970sвҖҷ debates, worked as a strong barrier to the transatlantic
1217	вҖҳharmonizationвҖҷ of antitrust enforcement. Since the early 2000s, the EU
1218	CommissionвҖҷs Directorate-General for Competition launched a campaign to bring
1219	EU member statesвҖҷ enforcement practices into line with modern American stand-
1220	ards (Gerber, 2007). Through a series of discussion papers, conferences, court deci-
1221	sions, restructuring moves, and guidelines worked out since the late 1990s, the
1222	Commission tried to institutionalize new, more welfare-focused tests of abusive
1223	behavior, the efficiency defense in merger control and abuse cases, and a focus on
1224	consumer welfare in the multi-level EU antitrust regime. While many of the pro-
1225	posed changes have affected antitrust practices across the EU in one way or
1226	another, almost all of them met with staunch resistance from significant legal intel-
1227	lectuals and antitrust practitioners and ended up in hybrid practical manifestations
1228	(Buxbaum, 2005; Gerber, 2007). Compared to the spectacularly far-reaching econo-
1229	mization of US competition law since the 1960s, вҖҳmodernвҖҷ economic thinking in
1230	European antitrust enforcement has been markedly impaired by systems of ideas
1231	and doctrines.
1232	    These doctrines were not simply detached from the enforcing agencies, but are
1233	referred to in their commentary, mission statements, and advocacy output. Thus,
1234	the aforementioned Hayek is often cited in general commitments to competition as
1235	a method of discovery (Bundeskartellamt, 1982, p. 6), and legal professionals like
1236	MestmвӮ¬acker and Ulrich Immenga led influential antitrust commissions in the
1237	1970s and 1980s. One of the main representatives of the legal doctrine of competi-
1238	tive freedom, Immenga, resigned as head of the German Monopolkommission in
1239	1989 in a public battle about the merger of Daimler and MBB as part of the Airbus
1240	project, which was blocked by antitrust authorities but subsequently allowed by the
1241	German economics ministry on industrial policy grounds (for a richer account of
1242	
1243

1245	
1246	278         T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
1247	
1248	
1249	the battle of German Ordoliberals against the вҖҳindustrial policy relativizationвҖҷ of
1250	competition policy, see Monopolkommission, 1992). In the process, Immenga
1251	declared that the passage of the merger would вҖҳgive insights into conflicts between
1252	industrial policy and competition policy, especially if one understands competition
1253	not just as an economic phenomenon, but recognizes its function in society to safe-
1254	guard freedomвҖҷ (quoted in Ortwein, 1998, p. 231; our translation).
1255	    A closer look at the annual reports of the German antitrust agency reveals that
1256	the discourse in favor of continuing form-oriented interventions promoting compe-
1257	tition remained prominent in the 1980s (cf. Figure 2), even though the general per-
1258	ception of increasing international competition made this a contested policy issue.
1259	When German corporations seemed to maintain international competitiveness in
1260	the 1980s, the antitrust agency even claimed credit, arguing that it enhanced com-
1261	paniesвҖҷ international competitiveness by cultivating competition in the home mar-
1262	ket (Bundeskartellamt, 1984, p. 4) вҖ“ a striking difference from US reformersвҖҷ
1263	catering to the 1980sвҖҷ industrial policy debates in praise of more lenient enforce-
1264	ment standards (see, e.g. Baxter, 1985). Another frequent argument mentioned in
1265	administratorsвҖҷ self-descriptions is support of small and medium-sized enterprises
1266	for whom, unlike for big ones, certain forms of horizontal cooperation were a trad-
1267	itionally permitted means to survive in competition with big corporations
1268	(Bundeskartellamt, 1987). Along similar lines, the institutionalization of merger
1269	control in Germany and the establishment of an independent agency monitoring
1270	concentration in German industry, the Monopolkommission, were accompanied by
1271	pleas to simultaneously ease cooperation between small and medium enterprises
1272	(Brandt, 1969). The high inflation period of the 1970s raised the specter of less
1273	competition driving prices even further (Bundeskartellamt, 1978, p. 6). Finally, in
1274	the pro-market atmosphere of the 1980s, the antitrust agency successfully sold its
1275	activity to safeguard the competitive process вҖ“ not distributional results вҖ“ as fur-
1276	thering a common cause.
1277	    By the end of the 1990s, the antitrust agency more frequently discussed the
1278	вҖҳmore economic approach,вҖҷ which by then had been adopted more broadly in the
1279	US. These discussions were triggered by initiatives by the European CommissionвҖҷs
1280	DG for Competition under Mario Monti (1999вҖ“2004) and successive European
1281	Court of Justice case law, which were favoring the American effects-based approach
1282	to competition law. Significant parts of the German antitrust profession reacted
1283	adversely to reform proposals. In a 2000 discussion paper, for example, a softening
1284	of basic enforcement principles in cases of horizontal cooperation between compa-
1285	nies was basically rejected by the German antitrust agency (Bundeskartellamt,
1286	2000), a position shared by the German government in its commentary on the
1287	annual antitrust report:
1288	      From the point of view of the federal government, the consideration of economic insights
1289	      may not counteract the basic principles of competition policy. The core and agreed-upon
1290	      goal of competition law, to work toward the long-term interests of consumers by
1291	      structurally safeguarding dynamic competition, should not be called into question in the
1292	      process of adapting to analytical methods in industrial economics. Hence, the
1293	      вҖңeconomization of competition lawвҖқ should not lead to a replacement of the practice of
1294	      antitrust enforcement in Germany that has been developed and proven for decades
1295	      (Bundeskartellamt, 2001).

1315	
1316	4. Conclusions
1317	In the 1960s, Hofstadter ([1964] 1996) observed a bureaucratic routinization of
1318	antitrust enforcement in postwar embedded liberalism. While his diagnosis that
1319	competition policy had been transformed from an issue relevant for mass mobiliza-
1320	tion and contentious politics to a professional enterprise turned out to be highly
1321	accurate, the amount of policy change through processes within that routinized sys-
1322	tem was beyond his grasp. All four regimes of antitrust enforcement described
1323	above вҖ“ the activist and economized US regimes and the postwar German regimes
1324	вҖ“ did not just exist in states of rule-bound вҖҳimplementation.вҖҷ They were driven by
1325	ideational fads and systems of professionally negotiated beliefs and values. In our
1326	discussion of alternative approaches to explain change in competition policy, we
1327	highlighted the fact that structural determinants of economic regulation under-
1328	determine policy trajectories. This is what the trajectories of American and
1329	German enforcement doctrines reveal. Switches from form-based to effect-based
1330	approaches within legal regimes, from deconcentrating agendas to efficiency
1331	enhancing ones and vice versa within established Varieties of Capitalism, and
1332	diverging trajectories despite similar economic pressures are examples of how
1333	changing ideas can alter policy directions in вҖҳimpreciseвҖҷ institutional regimes.
1334	   A note on the comparative dimension of our study: as it should not per se be
1335	considered surprising that different national bureaucracies maintain indigenous
1336	practices over time, we want to highlight that the two national pathways we
1337	described are, at their core, not stories of mere cultural persistence or institutional
1338	path dependence. It was the decade-long struggle on the level of enforcement to
1339	enact the antitrust laws вҖ“ to act on the challenge to deal with concentrations of pri-
1340	vate economic power вҖ“ that made the American regime vulnerable to a change in
1341	enforcement doctrines that would eventually counteract some of the core ideas of
1342	the laws themselves. In the German context, the ideas that impaired American-style
1343	economization since the 1990s emerged in conflicts about how to implement com-
1344	petition policy вҖ“ they were to a certain degree endogenous to the regime and a
1345	contingent outcome of ideological battles between professionals. The goal of look-
1346	ing at two вҖ“ historically intimately related вҖ“ cases was to demonstrate that alterna-
1347	tive politics on the enforcement level of competition policy led to alternative
1348	regime characteristics, not directly to highlight national characteristics as decisive
1349	factors in policy design. Our study suggests that rule-enforcement practices are not
1350	
1351	
1352	--- PAGE BREAK ---
1353	
1354	280     T. ERGEN AND S. KOHL
1355	
1356	
1357	time-invariant structures but must be constantly upheld by the respective profes-
1358	sional groups in order to retain stable characters (Stinchcombe, 1997, p. 9).
1359	    Our findings have broader implications for debates on neoliberal ideas and on
1360	technocratic liberalization. The discussion of the antitrust case reveals that competi-
1361	tion policies cut across simple classifications into left and right, into neoliberal and
1362	non-neoliberal ideas. Both the Ordoliberal and the Chicago-school ideas on compe-
1363	tition are part of the economics profession and both embrace free-market ideas to
1364	the fullest, albeit with different views on what a free market actually is (Wigger,
1365	2017). Ordoliberals have thus been far from letting broader societal goals intrude
1366	into the shaping of market structures and cannot simply be equated with a non-
1367	market liberal or left-wing movement favoring a вҖҳsocial market economyвҖҷ. The
1368	paper thus highlights the need to distinguish between sweeping claims about neo-
1369	liberalism from more fine-grained accounts of economization through economic
1370	professionals which, as we have seen, can differ across time and countries. It thus
1371	speaks to the rather underdeveloped comparative empirical study of the economic
1372	profession (Fourcade, 2009), not only in its academic ramifications but also among
1373	practicing professionals in their often вҖҳperformativeвҖҷ institutional work (Eberle &
1374	Lauter, 2011; MacKenzie, Muniesa, & Siu, 2007; Seabrooke & Henriksen, 2017).
1375	While there exist excellent case studies of individual antitrust authorities, doctrines,
1376	and cases, truly comparative antitrust is still largely absent from the IPE or
1377	CPE literature.
1378	    Finally, our analysis points to important country differences in regulatory bodies
1379	that are not directly and democratically elected, whose decisions can still have last-
1380	ing impacts on consumer welfare and economic structure. The neglect of these

1383	few вҖ“ results from the idea that their role is restricted to implementation and fol-
1384	lowing of the rules of a delegating principal. With parliamentary capacity being
1385	restricted by supranational powers, ideological stalemates, or global pressures, these
1386	agencies are becoming more powerful actors than was previously thought (Quinn,
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>рҹ§  RaciocГӯnio interno</summary>

I'm getting the full scope of the paper now. Ergen & Kohl argue that the Atlantic divide in antitrust enforcementвҖ”with Germany taking a stricter, form-based approach while the US adopted a more lenient, effects-based framework from the 1960s onwardвҖ”stems not from differences in business power, varieties of capitalism, or legal systems, but rather from ideological and professional conflicts within regulatory bureaucracies. Since competition law relies on vague statutory language with low institutional precision, it becomes particularly susceptible to being shaped by whichever professional group gains influence within these agencies.

The mechanism they identify is personnel turnover and ideological conversion within enforcement bureaucraciesвҖ”exactly the institutional-conversion framework that Nahoum-ManГ§ano's own paper uses as its theoretical foundation, making this a much more directly relevant precedent than Foster-Thelen. In the US case, this played out as the Harvard School's structuralist, deconcentration-focused approach gradually gave way to Chicago School thinking centered on effects-based analysis and consumer welfare. Nahoum-ManГ§ano's work is directly relevant here вҖ” it's a RIPE publication using the same theoretical toolkit (institutional conversion, bureaucratic imprecision as strategic terrain) applied to antitrust, so any reviewer will likely know it or the editor may flag it as essential. Not engaging with it would be a significant oversight.

The mechanism they identify вҖ” ideological conversion through personnel replacement in regulatory bodies вҖ” maps precisely onto what happened in the antitrust cases I'm examining, where new cohorts of bureaucrats trained in different intellectual traditions shifted how institutions operated. grounds, but the Ministry of Economics overruled it on industrial-policy grounds вҖ” mirrors the Gerdau-Pains dynamic almost exactly (CADE blocks, the Executive overrides for competitiveness reasons). This German precedent is powerful because it demonstrates the Brazilian pattern isn't unique вҖ” even the strictest antitrust regime has documented cases where industrial policy trumps competition law, which directly addresses that scope-conditions gap the assessment identified.

Ergen & Kohl's argument about competition policy's inherent ambiguity and institutional imprecision вҖ” what makes it vulnerable to conversion in the first place вҖ” is actually the theoretical foundation the paper needs but mostly just asserts through Mahoney-Thelen without independent support. Using them as a citation anchors that claim concretely: they explain *why* competition law specifically (rather than other policy domains) is susceptible to this kind of institutional reinterpretation, which is a crucial load-bearing piece of the argument.

But there's a real tension here: Ergen & Kohl's mechanism operates through quiet professional capture вҖ” lawyers and economists battling over ideas within insulated bureaucratic communities вҖ” whereas the Nahoum-ManГ§ano cases are the opposite: loud, high-salience political interventions where executives like Jobim directly override CADE, Cardoso personally threatens new counselors, and Congress and the press are actively involved. These look like fundamentally different causal pathways, not just variations on the same institutional conversion story.

The key move is to use Ergen & Kohl strategically: cite them to establish that antitrust is indeed unusually malleable, but then sharply distinguish the mechanism at work in Brazil вҖ” this is actually a theoretical contribution worth making explicit. Rather than treating them as describing the same process, I can argue that institutional conversion in antitrust operates through at least two distinct channels: the quiet bureaucratic-ideational drift they document, and the high-salience executive capture visible in the Brazilian cases.

That said, I need to be careful not to overload the Discussion section with too many theoretical frameworks. I've already added the Foster-Thelen coordination-rights extension, and piling on another citation-heavy paragraph about Ergen & Kohl risks diluting the paper's focus.

The real issue is that Ergen & Kohl, like Foster-Thelen, doesn't actually engage with the Global South, industrial policy, or the passive/covert industrial policy framing that's central to my argument. So while it's a useful comparative lens for differentiating bureaucratic professional drift from political intervention, it doesn't directly strengthen the paper's core claims about developing economies.

I should slot it into Section 2 alongside the other historical institutionalist accounts, where it naturally fits with the discussion of institutional mechanisms and path dependence. The Daimler-MBB case offers a powerful comparative anchorвҖ”showing that even Germany's strict, rule-bound antitrust regime succumbed to executive industrial-policy overrideвҖ”which lets me differentiate Brazil's mechanism (personalistic, developmentalist capture) from the quieter, bureaucratic ideational drift Ergen & Kohl describe, positioning this as exactly the kind of literature conversation RIPE reviewers expect to see.

## O que o texto argumenta

Ergen & Kohl (2019, publicado na prГіpria RIPE) explicam a "divergГӘncia atlГўntica" em antitruste вҖ” Alemanha manteve um regime rГӯgido/form-based, EUA relaxou para um regime laxo/effect-based desde os anos 1960-70 вҖ” **nГЈo** por poder empresarial, nem por *varieties of capitalism*, nem por sistemas jurГӯdicos (as trГӘs explicaГ§Гөes dominantes, que eles atacam uma a uma), mas por **disputas ideacionais dentro das prГіprias burocracias regulatГіrias**. O argumento central:

- PolГӯtica antitruste tem "baixa precisГЈo institucional" вҖ” a lei Г© ambГӯgua por natureza, sobrando margem enorme para quem controla a *implementaГ§ГЈo* (nГЈo a legislaГ§ГЈo) definir o que ela significa na prГЎtica.
- O mecanismo de mudanГ§a Г© a substituiГ§ГЈo de quadros dentro do ГіrgГЈo regulador ("personnel replacement") por uma nova geraГ§ГЈo formada em outra corrente ideolГіgica вҖ” nos EUA, juristas/economistas estruturalistas (Harvard School) cederam espaГ§o a economistas da Chicago School (Bork, Posner) via nomeaГ§Гөes e nas cortes, sem grande reforma legislativa. Na Alemanha, o mesmo tipo de pressГЈo apareceu (controvГ©rsia Hoppmann-Kantzenbach, anos 1960-70) mas os juristas ordoliberais (MestmГӨcker, Hoppmann) venceram a disputa interna e blindaram o regime.
- Eles citam explicitamente Mahoney & Thelen (2010) e Hacker/Pierson/Thelen (2015) вҖ” a mesma famГӯlia teГіrica que vocГӘs jГЎ usam como framework central вҖ” para dizer que instituiГ§Гөes ambГӯguas "offer fertile terrain for strategies of conversion".
- Achado lateral Гіtimo: o caso Daimler-MBB/Airbus na Alemanha (1989) вҖ” o ГіrgГЈo antitruste **vetou** a fusГЈo, mas o MinistГ©rio da Economia **derrubou o veto por razГөes de polГӯtica industrial**. Estruturalmente, Г© quase um espelho do episГіdio Gerdau-Jobim.

## Como isso dialoga com o argumento de vocГӘs

Isso Г© **mais prГіximo e mais relevante** do que o Foster-Thelen que resolvemos antes вҖ” por trГӘs motivos concretos:

1. **Гү publicado na prГіpria RIPE**, sobre o mesmo framework teГіrico (conversГЈo institucional via ambiguidade), sobre o mesmo domГӯnio (antitruste comparado). Um revisor da RIPE tem chance real de conhecer esse artigo especificamente вҖ” nГЈo citГЎ-lo aqui pesa mais do que nГЈo citar Foster-Thelen.
2. **O mecanismo de "substituiГ§ГЈo de quadros" que eles teorizam Г© literalmente o que aconteceu no caso Gerdau** (Cardoso troca conselheiros juristas por economistas de custos de transaГ§ГЈo). VocГӘs jГЎ narram o episГіdio вҖ” Ergen & Kohl dГЈo a ele um nome e uma teoria geral, em vez de deixГЎ-lo como um fato isolado do caso brasileiro.
3. **O caso Daimler-MBB/Airbus dГЎ exatamente o que faltava**: uma condiГ§ГЈo de escopo/comparaГ§ГЈo alГ©m do Brasil вҖ” mostra que atГ© um regime antitruste canonicamente rГӯgido (Alemanha) tem um episГіdio de veto derrubado por razГөes de polГӯtica industrial pelo Executivo. Isso ataca direto o ponto #3 da minha avaliaГ§ГЈo anterior sobre a RIPE (falta de condiГ§Гөes de escopo/comparaГ§ГЈo).

**Mas hГЎ uma ressalva importante, que Г© onde estГЎ o valor real de se engajar**: o mecanismo de Ergen & Kohl Г© *quiet politics* вҖ” disputa ideacional de baixa saliГӘncia dentro da burocracia, sem grande alarde pГәblico. O mecanismo de vocГӘs Г© o oposto вҖ” intervenГ§ГЈo presidencial pessoal, pressГЈo de imprensa, "Jorge Gerdau foi ao presidente Fernando Henrique Cardoso". Citar Ergen & Kohl como se fosse "a mesma coisa" seria impreciso e um revisor atento notaria. O movimento certo Г© **diferenciar**: usar o artigo deles para estabelecer a premissa (antitruste Г© um domГӯnio de baixa precisГЈo institucional, logo vulnerГЎvel Г  conversГЈo) e o caso alemГЈo como comparaГ§ГЈo, mas marcar explicitamente que o Brasil segue uma **via distinta** вҖ” captura polГӯtica de alta saliГӘncia via intervenГ§ГЈo executiva direta, nГЈo deriva ideacional silenciosa de burocratas. Isso Г© exatamente o tipo de "extensГЈo do framework" que teoricamente fortalece o paper, em vez de sГі emprestar autoridade.

## Vale a pena se engajar para a RIPE?

**Sim, mais do que valeu para Foster-Thelen вҖ” e eu erraria se recomendasse nГЈo citar.** DiferenГ§a de posicionamento: com Foster-Thelen o risco era baixo mas a oportunidade era moderada; aqui a oportunidade Г© alta (mesma revista, mesmo framework, mecanismo-espelho, condiГ§ГЈo de escopo de graГ§a) e o risco de *nГЈo* citar tambГ©m Г© mais alto.

**Onde eu colocaria**: um parГЎgrafo (nГЈo sГі uma frase) logo onde `@Mahoney-Thelen2010` Г© invocado pela primeira vez ou na seГ§ГЈo 2, junto a `@Dobbin1994; @Prasad2012; @Foster-Thelen2024` вҖ” para (a) justificar teoricamente por que antitruste especificamente Г© um domГӯnio propenso Г  conversГЈo institucional (hoje isso Г© sГі afirmado, nГЈo sustentado por literatura prГіpria do campo), e (b) diferenciar explicitamente o mecanismo brasileiro (captura executiva de alta saliГӘncia) do mecanismo americano/alemГЈo (deriva profissional de baixa saliГӘncia) вҖ” com o caso Daimler-MBB como o comparador que mostra que a lГіgica "antitruste cede Г  polГӯtica industrial sob pressГЈo" nГЈo Г© exclusividade brasileira, mas a via institucional pela qual isso acontece varia.

Quer que eu jГЎ redija esse parГЎgrafo (nos moldes do que fiz para o Foster-Thelen) para vocГӘ revisar antes de aplicar?

### [2026-07-22 20:44] UsuГЎrio

Comente o seguinte diagnГіstico e reflita se faz sentido ou nГЈo: O que Ergen & Kohl 2019 Г©
Uma explicaГ§ГЈo da divergГӘncia transatlГўntica no antitruste (1960вҖ“2000): por que os EUA вҖ” campeГЈo histГіrico do antitruste вҖ” afrouxaram o controle de concentraГ§ГЈo desde os anos 1970, enquanto a Alemanha endureceu e resistiu. A resposta deles rejeita as trГӘs explicaГ§Гөes dominantes (poder empresarial, variedades de capitalismo, sistemas jurГӯdicos) e localiza a causa nas doutrinas profissionais que ocupam as burocracias de enforcement. Nos dois paГӯses houve, nos anos 1960, um movimento de reaГ§ГЈo contra tendГӘncias "ativistas"; nos dois houve economizaГ§ГЈo вҖ” reorientaГ§ГЈo do regime de finalidades societais amplas para objetivos puramente econГҙmicos. Mas a economizaГ§ГЈo assumiu formas opostas: nos EUA, effect-based (Chicago вҖ” bem-estar do consumidor e eficiГӘncia podem justificar menos concorrГӘncia); na Alemanha, form-based (ordoliberal вҖ” regras estruturais protegendo a "liberdade de concorrer" como fim em si). A ironia central: a reaГ§ГЈo alemГЈ foi mais radical intelectualmente e, justamente por isso, blindou o regime contra o afrouxamento americano.

O artigo Г© Open Access, saiu na Review of International Political Economy вҖ” que Г©, presumivelmente, o tipo de periГіdico que vocГӘs visam. EngajГЎ-lo nГЈo Г© sГі honestidade bibliogrГЎfica; Г© posicionamento.

Por que ele importa aqui: parente prГіximo e rival perigoso ao mesmo tempo
Isto nГЈo Г© "mais uma referГӘncia". Ergen & Kohl fazem, para EUA/Alemanha, exatamente o que vocГӘs fazem para o Brasil вҖ” mesmo objeto (mudanГ§a institucional no antitruste sem mudanГ§a legislativa), mesma famГӯlia teГіrica, mesmo nГӯvel de anГЎlise (enforcement). TrГӘs sobreposiГ§Гөes que o artigo de vocГӘs ainda nГЈo reconhece:

1. A conversГЈo jГЎ foi aplicada ao antitruste, com citaГ§ГЈo explГӯcita. E&K citam Hacker, Pierson & Thelen (2015) dizendo que instituiГ§Гөes "whose provisions are ambiguous and whose effects depend on interpretation and discretion" oferecem "fertile terrain for strategies of conversion" (linha 487) вҖ” e classificam a polГӯtica de concorrГӘncia como caso paradigmГЎtico, de "very low degree of institutional precision" (linha 537). VocГӘs derivam esse passo do zero a partir de Mahoney & Thelen (qmd:26). Um parecerista de CPE saberГЎ que jГЎ existe. Melhor citar e avanГ§ar do que reinventar.

2. Eles antecipam a rota causal do artigo вҖ” e a rejeitam. Em linhas 253вҖ“293, E&K apresentam Christophers (2016), para quem o afrouxamento do antitruste desde os anos 1980 "were essentially industrial policies, meant to hasten restructuring and revitalize American industry in international competition" вҖ” e objetam: (a) a mudanГ§a foi piecemeal, logo os interesses empresariais eram heterogГӘneos e divididos caso a caso, o que torna o empresariado "an unreliable driver of institutional change"; (b) afrouxar antitruste nГЈo Г© reaГ§ГЈo natural Г  pressГЈo econГҙmica вҖ” poucos anos antes a estratГ©gia natural era reforГ§ar o enforcement (os "Bottlenecks of Business" de Thurman Arnold), e outros paГӯses com a mesma necessidade de reestruturaГ§ГЈo nГЈo geraram movimento equivalente. "Economic pressures have to be channeled."

3. O mecanismo de pessoal jГЎ estГЎ teorizado. E&K, linhas 498вҖ“508: burocracias sГЈo "pontes" entre comunidades profissionais e o Estado; a mudanГ§a ocorre "both through ideological conversion and through personnel replacement" вҖ” normalmente quando o quadro existente "is replaced by a new generation of bureaucrats trained within a new ideational current."

Esse terceiro ponto Г© o mais importante, porque a evidГӘncia mais forte do artigo de vocГӘs estГЎ subteorizada justamente ali.

Quatro contribuiГ§Гөes concretas
I. DГЎ nome ao achado вҖ” e transforma "conversГЈo institucional" numa tese comparativa
"ConversГЈo institucional" descreve a forma da mudanГ§a. NГЈo diz para o quГӘ o regime foi convertido. E&K fornecem o conteГәdo вҖ” economizaГ§ГЈo вҖ” e uma tipologia: effect-based vs. form-based.

O que vocГӘs descrevem em qmd:102 вҖ” "emphasizing efficiency and conduct remedies over structural constraints" вҖ” Г© a transiГ§ГЈo do form-based para o effect-based, sem nome. O CADE inicial era form-based: limiar de 20% na Lei 8.884/94, cГЎlculo de participaГ§ГЈo em mercado nacional, remГ©dio estrutural (desconstituiГ§ГЈo). O CADE pГіs-1996 Г© effect-based: defesa de eficiГӘncia, mercado relevante global, remГ©dios comportamentais.

Com isso, a contribuiГ§ГЈo do artigo deixa de ser "o antitruste pode ser polГӯtica industrial" (que Г© o argumento de Christophers, jГЎ publicado e jГЎ criticado) e passa a ser:

O Brasil Г© uma terceira variedade de economizaГ§ГЈo. Nos EUA, o instrumental effect-based foi construГӯdo para tirar o Estado do caminho вҖ” Posner Г© explГӯcito de que a desconcentraГ§ГЈo o incomodava por ser "a more ambitious form of public control" (E&K:895вҖ“901). No Brasil, o mesmo instrumental foi mobilizado pelo Estado para engenheirar campeГөes nacionais. Mesmo ferramental doutrinГЎrio, economia polГӯtica invertida.

Isso Г© uma contribuiГ§ГЈo ao programa de pesquisa de E&K, nГЈo uma nota de rodapГ©. E Г© muito mais forte do que a formulaГ§ГЈo atual.

HГЎ um corolГЎrio cronolГіgico que reforГ§a: E&K datam a economizaГ§ГЈo americana de Bork (1969) Г s Merger Guidelines de Baxter (1982), com a defesa de eficiГӘncia "firmly established" nos anos 1990. A Lei 8.884/94 e a profissionalizaГ§ГЈo do CADE ocorrem exatamente aГӯ. O Brasil nГЈo sofre economizaГ§ГЈo endГіgena ao longo de duas dГ©cadas de disputa profissional вҖ” ele nasce economizado, importando o toolkit effect-based pronto e imediatamente o pondo a serviГ§o de um fim que seus autores americanos jamais pretenderam. E note que vocГӘs jГЎ registram que os novos conselheiros eram economistas de abordagem transaction-cost (qmd:98) e jГЎ citam Williamson 1975 (qmd:44) вҖ” a peГ§a estГЎ no tabuleiro, falta movГӘ-la.

Ainda: vocГӘs escrevem que a retГіrica de Jorge Gerdau contra o CADE era "a rhetorical move resembling that of Chicago School economists" (qmd:93). Com E&K, deixa de ser semelhanГ§a e passa a ser identificaГ§ГЈo: Г© a crГӯtica effect-based, reconstruГӯda por eles a partir de Bork (1969) e Posner (1979).

II. Teoriza a sua melhor evidГӘncia
A recomposiГ§ГЈo do CADE por Cardoso вҖ” troca de juristas por economistas, mais o "NГЈo me dГӘ problemas!" (qmd:98) вҖ” Г© o mecanismo de personnel replacement de E&K, com uma prova documental que eles nГЈo tГӘm para nenhum dos seus dois casos. Nos EUA a substituiГ§ГЈo foi geracional e difusa; no Brasil hГЎ data, nomes e uma frase presidencial. Hoje isso aparece no artigo como episГіdio narrativo. Deveria aparecer como confirmaГ§ГЈo вҖ” e refinamento вҖ” de um mecanismo jГЎ formulado na literatura.

Refinamento importante: E&K insistem que economizaГ§ГЈo nГЈo Г© uma coisa sГі. O tipo de economista importou. NГЈo foi qualquer economia que entrou no CADE; foi custos de transaГ§ГЈo, que traz consigo a defesa de eficiГӘncia e o ceticismo quanto a remГ©dios estruturais.

III. Fornece o caso negativo que falta вҖ” e dele sai a tese causal mais forte disponГӯvel
Hoje o artigo tem dois casos que vГЈo na mesma direГ§ГЈo (Gerdau aprovado, Ambev aprovada). Process tracing com dois casos confirmatГіrios Г© frГЎgil. E&K entregam um contraste pronto, na mesma Г©poca e sob a mesma pressГЈo de "concorrГӘncia internacional crescente":

O Bundeskartellamt nГЈo sГі recusou o argumento da competitividade como reivindicou crГ©dito ao contrГЎrio: em 1984 argumentou que aumentava a competitividade internacional das empresas alemГЈs cultivando concorrГӘncia no mercado domГ©stico вҖ” "a striking difference from US reformers' catering to the 1980s' industrial policy debates" (E&K:1259вҖ“1264).
E quando o Executivo alemГЈo de fato quis um campeГЈo nacional вҖ” Daimler/MBB, projeto Airbus, 1989 вҖ” nГЈo conseguiu fazГӘ-lo atravГ©s da autoridade antitruste. Teve de usar a Ministererlaubnis, autorizaГ§ГЈo ministerial legalmente prevista, explГӯcita e externa ao regime. Custou a renГәncia de Immenga da Monopolkommission e produziu um relatГіrio intitulado Wettbewerbspolitik oder Industriepolitik (E&K:1237вҖ“1254).
Contraste em forma explГӯcita:

Alemanha 1989 вҖ” Daimler/MBB	Brasil 1995 вҖ” Gerdau/Pains
PosiГ§ГЈo da agГӘncia	Bloqueou	Bloqueou (desconstituiГ§ГЈo parcial)
Executivo quer a fusГЈo	Sim (Airbus)	Sim
Rota utilizada	AutorizaГ§ГЈo ministerial prevista em lei, visГӯvel e contestГЎvel	Despacho ministerial contra legem (art. 50 vedava revisГЈo no Executivo) + recomposiГ§ГЈo da agГӘncia
Enquadramento	Assumidamente polГӯtica industrial	CorreГ§ГЈo de "erro tГ©cnico" sobre mercado relevante
Efeito sobre a doutrina	Nenhum. Regime intacto, doutrina ordoliberal reafirmada	ConversГЈo. EficiГӘncia e mercado relevante ressignificados; precedente para a Ambev
DaГӯ a tese вҖ” e ela Г© sua, nГЈo de E&K:

A negaГ§ГЈo discursiva nГЈo Г© cosmГ©tica; Г© causal. PolГӯtica industrial assumida deixa a doutrina antitruste intacta, porque corre por um canal externo e designado (a exceГ§ГЈo ministerial alemГЈ). PolГӯtica industrial negada precisa ser contrabandeada para dentro dos conceitos do prГіprio regime вҖ” e, ao ser contrabandeada para dentro, converte o regime. A clandestinidade Г© a condiГ§ГЈo da conversГЈo, nГЈo um detalhe retГіrico.

Hoje "covert" no artigo Г© adjetivo descritivo (eles negavam). Isso o torna explicativo. Гү, na minha leitura, o argumento mais forte que o material de vocГӘs sustenta e que ainda nГЈo estГЎ enunciado.

IV. Obriga a especificar o canal вҖ” e Г© aqui que vocГӘs emendam E&K
A objeГ§ГЈo (b) de E&K Г© a que morde. Em vГЎrios trechos (qmd:30, qmd:74, qmd:149) o argumento lГӘ-se como: restriГ§ГЈo fiscal + abertura comercial вҶ’ necessidade de competitividade вҶ’ antitruste frouxo. Isso Г© exatamente a histГіria funcionalista que E&K declaram subdeterminada. VocГӘs precisam nomear o canal.

E tГӘm um вҖ” mas ele Г© diferente do de E&K, e Г© aГӯ que estГЎ a contribuiГ§ГЈo teГіrica:

E&K constroem todo o argumento sobre conversГЈo que acontece silenciosamente: mudanГ§a "in situations of low politicization and issue salience" (linha 228), por comunidades profissionais, via "backroom strategies" e "quiet politics" de Culpepper (linha 490). Eisner: na reforma Reagan, "politics lagged behind policy"; foi "at most a coup" que apenas ratificou o que os profissionais jГЎ haviam mudado.

O caso brasileiro Г© o oposto e chega ao mesmo destino doutrinГЎrio. Foi polГӯtica ruidosa: despacho ministerial derrubando a agГӘncia na imprensa; anГәncio da fusГЈo no PalГЎcio da Alvorada com o presidente presente; entrevista de Cardoso ao Jornal do Brasil dizendo "o mundo hoje funciona em termos de oligopГіlio"; ClГіvis Carvalho oferecendo R$ 3 bilhГөes do BNDES; trГӘs campanhas publicitГЎrias da Kaiser; telefonemas de ministros e do lГӯder do governo ao presidente do CADE (depoimento de Ruy Lima). Nada disso Г© quiet politics.

O achado teГіrico, entГЈo:

ConversГЈo nГЈo exige baixa politizaГ§ГЈo. Onde nГЈo existe comunidade profissional entrincheirada вҖ” um regime regulatГіrio com dois anos de vida вҖ”, a conversГЈo pode ser feita por intervenГ§ГЈo executiva direta e substituiГ§ГЈo de pessoal, em prazo comprimido (1995вҖ“2000), em vez das duas dГ©cadas de trabalho doutrinГЎrio que os EUA levaram.

E hГЎ um sequenciamento que complementa E&K em vez de contradizГӘ-los, e que o prГіprio artigo jГЎ observa em qmd:128 ("no longer a source of acute conflict but had become part of the routine operation"): polГӯtica ruidosa instala os profissionais; os profissionais fazem o trabalho silencioso depois. Gerdau Г© o momento ruidoso; Ambev jГЎ Г© rotina. Isso Г© uma proposiГ§ГЈo sobre como a conversГЈo se estabiliza, e Г© vendГЎvel.

Sobre a objeГ§ГЈo (a) вҖ” interesses empresariais heterogГӘneos вҖ” vocГӘs tГӘm a resposta dentro do prГіprio material e nГЈo a usam: a Kaiser combateu a Ambev com trГӘs campanhas; os concorrentes reagiram. O empresariado estava dividido exatamente como E&K preveem. Isso nГЈo enfraquece o artigo вҖ” fortalece, desde que dito: o resultado nГЈo se explica por "poder empresarial", porque o empresariado estava cindido; o que decidiu foi o Estado tomar partido de uma facГ§ГЈo e o aparato interpretativo da agГӘncia acomodar essa escolha.

Onde inserir, concretamente
A. В§ "Antitrust and Industrial Policy" вҖ” Г© a casa principal. Duas correГ§Гөes e um parГЎgrafo novo:

qmd:46: substituir [@Khan2016; @Khan-Vaheesan2017; @Juhasz-etal2024] (que, como verifiquei antes, nГЈo sustentam a frase) por E&K 2019 вҖ” via Christophers (2016)/Galambos (2004) para o argumento "antitruste frouxo = polГӯtica industrial", e via o debate alemГЈo Wettbewerbspolitik oder Industriepolitik para o trade-off institucionalizado, nГЈo apenas retГіrico.
qmd:48: Dobbin (1994, ferrovias) e Prasad (2012) sГЈo um foil fraco para sustentar "these accounts have focused primarily on earlier periods". E&K Г© antitruste, comparativo, contemporГўneo e da RIPE. The user is sharing a detailed analysis of Ergen & Kohl 2019 and wants me to evaluate it criticallyвҖ”identifying what's solid, what might be overstated, and where pushback is warranted rather than just accepting it at face value.

Now I'm checking the actual text against the specific claims and quotes in this diagnostic to verify their accuracy and assess whether the interpretive moves hold up. The analysis itself is quite sophisticatedвҖ”it's proposing a three-way theoretical typology (US effect-based, Germany form-based, Brazil "developmental" economization) and making a strong causal argument that clandestinity wasn't just rhetorical window-dressing but actually the mechanism enabling Brazil's industrial policy to be absorbed into antitrust concepts, which then drove the conversion process.

The elegance of the move is that it explains why Germany's explicit, legally-sanctioned channel for industrial policy (Ministererlaubnis) left antitrust doctrine itself unchanged, whereas Brazil's forced smuggling-in of industrial policy through the back door of antitrust concepts actually transformed the doctrine. I'm verifying this against what I actually read in Ergen & KohlвҖ”confirming the rejection of business power and VoC explanations, the economization thesis in both countries, the US/Germany doctrinal split, and the paradox that Germany's more intellectually radical reaction to activist competition policy ended up immunizing its antitrust framework rather than converting it. So the user's insight is really sharp here вҖ” Ergen & Kohl's paper actually dismantles the exact causal mechanism that Nahoum-ManГ§ano is relying on, which means a RIPE reviewer familiar with that work could apply the same critique: economic pressure alone doesn't explain policy shifts without identifying the specific institutional channel that translates pressure into action. The question becomes what mechanism actually channels those competitive pressures into antitrust relaxation.

The diagnostic's answer is elegant: Brazil's paper does identify a channel, just a different one from E&K's вҖ” it's about quiet ideational drift among professionals rather than dramatic executive intervention вҖ” and that distinction itself becomes the theoretical contribution. The personnel replacement mechanism at lines 498-508 confirms this, showing how new generations of bureaucrats trained in different ideational frameworks can shift policy without dramatic political events.

For the first contribution, the economization typology reframes CADE's pre-1996 versus post-1996shift in terms of effect-based versus form-based approaches, which is a clever way to name and categorize what actually changed in enforcement practice.  I can verify this mapping: the 1994 law did establish a 20% market share threshold as a structural bright-line rule (form-based reasoning), while the Ambev case later embraced efficiency defenses, behavioral remedies, and global market definitions (effect-based reasoning). This characterization is textually grounded in what's already in the document вҖ” it's a legitimate insight, not an overreach.

The real contribution here is reframing the paper's core finding through Epstein and King's typology, then making the novel argument that Brazil represents a third variety: the same effect-based, efficiency-focused toolkit that the US deployed to constrain state power was instead mobilized by the Brazilian state itself to build national champions. It's a sharp inversion of the political economy вҖ” same doctrinal logic, opposite ideological direction.

I need to verify the specific Posner quote about deconcentration as "more ambitious form of public control" to make sure I'm representing his position accurately. Let me trace back through the text to confirm the exact framing and context.

That's a legitimate methodological advantage worth noting.

Now for the third contribution, which is the most ambitious part: introducing the German Daimler-MBB/Airbus case from 1989 as a contrasting case through the Ministererlaubnis mechanism, then building a 2x2 table to derive the claim that "clandestinity is the condition of conversion, not merely rhetorical window-dressing." It's an elegant theoretical move if it holds, so I need to check whether the Ministererlaubnis facts are actually accurate.

The core claim is compellingвҖ”that openly declared industrial policy can coexist with intact antitrust doctrine through a formal exemption channel, whereas covert industrial policy must be internalized into the regime's own concepts, thereby transforming them. It's a legitimate theoretical contribution for the discussion section, but the framing should stay appropriately cautious, positioning it as a proposition for future comparative work to test rather than an established finding. There's also a temporal and political-economy asymmetry worth examining: Germany in 1989 was a mature, wealthy, politically stable democracy with strong institutions, whereas Brazil presented a very different contextвҖ”

one where openly avowing industrial policy (like Germany's Airbus involvement) would have been politically costly precisely because Brazil was operating under Washington Consensus liberalization pressures and needed credibility signaling with international capital markets in the aftermath of hyperinflation. The diagnostic's clean "avowed versus denied" distinction doesn't fully capture this: the denial wasn't just an institutional choice but likely downstream of Brazil's specific credibility politics during liberalization. The Germany comparison needs to acknowledge that postwar West Germany's established legitimacy operated in a fundamentally different political economy context than 1990s Brazil's credibility-seeking liberalization eraвҖ”these aren't just institutional choices but reflect deeper structural differences in why each country made the decisions it did.   abstract = {Competition is a constitutive feature of capitalist societies. Social conflicts over the introduction, abolition and regulation of market organization are saturated with implicit moral arguments concerning the desirability of competition. Yet, unlike private property, exchange relations and social inequalities, economic competition has rarely been the explicit core of moral debates over capitalism. Drawing on a broad variety of social science literature, this article reconstructs, maps and systematizes ethical arguments about economic competition in capitalist societies. We discuss six contradictory rival views of economic competition and illustrate their influence by providing historical examples of the respective views in action in politicaleconomic debates. This article serves as a mapping groundwork for reviving the systematic ethical debate on economic competition. In addition, our map of rival views lends itself to use as a structuring tool in empirical research on the moral economy and ideational embeddedness of capitalist societies, markets and firms.},
```
  langid = {english},
  file = {G:\My Drive\[[1]] Kami Uploads\Ergen and Kohl - 2022 - Rival views of economic competition.pdf}
}
```

Excelente diagnГіstico вҖ” o mais forte de literatura que apareceu neste projeto atГ© agora. Antes de comentar o conteГәdo, uma correГ§ГЈo factual importante que muda a execuГ§ГЈo prГЎtica:

## CorreГ§ГЈo: Ergen & Kohl jГЎ estГЈo no `.bib`

Chequei agora вҖ” `Ergen-Kohl2019` e `Ergen-Kohl2022` **jГЎ existem** no `.bib`, com metadados completos (DOI, caminho de arquivo no Zotero, `urldate` de 2025-08-12 e 2026-07-19 respectivamente). AlguГ©m (vocГӘ ou o AndrГ©) jГЎ importou os dois antes desta conversa. Isso significa que essa parte especГӯfica da lista de pendГӘncias bibliogrГЎficas estГЎ errada вҖ” os Гәnicos genuinamente ausentes sГЈo `Christophers2016`, `Galambos2004`, `Hacker-Pierson-Thelen2015`, `Streeck-Thelen2005`, `Culpepper2011`, `Eisner1991` (confirmei um a um, zero ocorrГӘncias). Na prГЎtica isso **facilita** a execuГ§ГЈo: para citar Ergen & Kohl 2019 nГЈo precisamos do roundtrip Zotero que fizemos com Foster-Thelen вҖ” Г© sГі escrever `[@Ergen-Kohl2019]` direto.

Um adendo sobre o Ergen-Kohl 2022: o abstract dele ("Rival Views of Economic Competition") Г© sobre mapear seis visГөes morais/normativas rivais da concorrГӘncia econГҙmica вҖ” Г© um paper de sociologia econГҙmica sobre Г©tica da competiГ§ГЈo, nГЈo uma continuaГ§ГЈo empГӯrica da divergГӘncia EUA-Alemanha. Pode ser Гәtil em outro ponto (ex. na discussГЈo do discurso de Malan sobre eficiГӘncia/bem-estar), mas nГЈo assuma que ele carrega o mesmo peso argumentativo do 2019 sem ler вҖ” nГЈo li o 2022 ainda.

## Onde discordo: Khan2016/Khan-Vaheesan2017/JuhГЎsz-Lane-Rodrik2024 "nГЈo sustentam a frase"

NГЈo tenho registro de ter verificado isso nesta conversa вҖ” acho que vocГӘ estГЎ se referindo a uma checagem de outra sessГЈo, ou Г© uma leitura sua agora. Conferi as trГӘs entradas: `Juhasz-etal2024` Г© exatamente "The New Economics of Industrial Policy" (*Annual Review of Economics*) вҖ” um survey da literatura de ressurgimento de polГӯtica industrial, encaixe quase perfeito para a frase atual. `Khan-Vaheesan2017` Г© "Market Power and Inequality: The Antitrust Counterrevolution and Its Discontents" вҖ” Г© literalmente sobre a mesma virada effect-based/Chicago que Ergen & Kohl descrevem. Eu **substituiria** essas trГӘs sГі se houver um problema textual especГӯfico que eu nГЈo estou vendo; pelo que encontrei, a leitura mais defensГЎvel Г© *somar* Ergen & Kohl a elas, nГЈo trocar вҖ” Khan-Vaheesan2017 inclusive combina bem ao lado de Ergen-Kohl2019 porque ambos descrevem o mesmo fenГҙmeno (a "contrarrevoluГ§ГЈo" antitruste americana) de Гўngulos complementares (jurГӯdico-normativo vs. institucional-profissional). Se vocГӘ tem uma verificaГ§ГЈo especГӯfica de que essas trГӘs nГЈo sustentam essa frase, me mostra que eu reavalio вҖ” mas por ora eu manteria e acrescentaria.

## O que acho que estГЎ certo вҖ” e Г© o mais forte do diagnГіstico

**ContribuiГ§ГЈo I (a "terceira variedade de economizaГ§ГЈo")** Г© a ideia mais valiosa aqui, de longe. Ela resolve exatamente o problema que eu tinha apontado na minha avaliaГ§ГЈo de prontidГЈo para a RIPE (a dependГӘncia excessiva de Onto 2017 e a falta de diferenciaГ§ГЈo clara em relaГ§ГЈo a Christophers 2016, que jГЎ fez "antitruste frouxo = polГӯtica industrial" para os EUA). Em vez de "antitruste pode ser polГӯtica industrial" вҖ” argumento jГЎ feito e jГЎ criticado por E&K вҖ”, a tese fica: mesmo instrumental doutrinГЎrio (eficiГӘncia, mercado relevante, remГ©dio comportamental), usado para funГ§ГЈo polГӯtica oposta (tirar o Estado do caminho nos EUA vs. engenheirar campeГөes nacionais no Brasil). Isso Г© extensГЈo de programa de pesquisa, nГЈo nota de rodapГ©, e concordo com sua leitura do Posner (verifiquei a citaГ§ГЈo, "more ambitious form of public control", Posner 1979 p.948 n.67 вҖ” estГЎ exatamente onde vocГӘ diz).

**ContribuiГ§ГЈo II (mecanismo de personnel replacement)** e **ContribuiГ§ГЈo IV (canal ruidoso vs. silencioso + a proposiГ§ГЈo de sequenciamento Gerdau-ruidoso/Ambev-rotina)** вҖ” concordo, sГЈo de baixo risco e alto valor, e a IV em particular resolve um problema real do desenho atual (dois casos confirmatГіrios do mesmo tipo) ao reencaixГЎ-los como dois estГЎgios de um processo, nГЈo duas replicaГ§Гөes. Isso Г© bom o suficiente para eu recomendar fazer sem muita hesitaГ§ГЈo.

## Onde eu pisaria no freio: a ContribuiГ§ГЈo III (Alemanha/Daimler-MBB)

A ideia Г© elegante вҖ” "clandestinidade Г© a condiГ§ГЈo da conversГЈo" Г© uma frase que vende вҖ” mas tem trГӘs riscos que eu levaria a sГ©rio antes de escrever:

1. **"Ministererlaubnis" nГЈo Г© um termo que eu confirme ter visto no texto de E&K que li.** O mecanismo (bloqueio do Bundeskartellamt, revertido pelo MinistГ©rio da Economia por razГөes de polГӯtica industrial) estГЎ confirmado вҖ” eu li exatamente esse trecho. Mas o nome tГ©cnico especГӯfico parece conhecimento seu sobre o direito da concorrГӘncia alemГЈo (В§42 GWB), nГЈo algo que E&K nomeiem no trecho que vi. Se for usar esse termo no artigo, precisa de citaГ§ГЈo prГіpria (nГЈo pode pendurar em E&K sГі).
2. **N=1 caso-sombra sustentando uma tese causal forte.** VocГӘ mesmo already hedge isso na "disciplina 3" (tratar a Alemanha como caso-sombra ilustrativo, nГЈo comparaГ§ГЈo pareada) вҖ” concordo com o hedge, mas eu reforГ§aria: a tese deve aparecer como proposiГ§ГЈo interpretativa a testar, nГЈo como achado demonstrado. Um parecerista vai perguntar "e se eu olhar outros casos de Ministererlaubnis alemГЈ, a tese se sustenta?" вҖ” vocГӘs nГЈo tГӘm como responder com o material atual.
3. **Falta explicar por que a negaГ§ГЈo era necessГЎria no Brasil e nГЈo na Alemanha de 1989.** Isso nГЈo Г© aleatГіrio вҖ” Г© plausivelmente efeito da polГӯtica de credibilidade da liberalizaГ§ГЈo brasileira (Malan precisando sinalizar compromisso de mercado para estabilizar a economia pГіs-hiperinflaГ§ГЈo) vs. uma Alemanha do pГіs-guerra que jГЎ nГЈo precisava provar suas credenciais de mercado a ninguГ©m. O artigo jГЎ tem esse material (a fala do Malan) вҖ” eu amarraria a essa politics of credibility em vez de deixar a escolha avowed/denied como um dado institucional nГЈo explicado.

## Sobre a "disciplina 2" (replicar o mГ©todo de dicionГЎrio de E&K no corpus do CADE)

Isso Г© valioso em princГӯpio, mas Г© uma categoria de esforГ§o diferente de tudo o mais discutido aqui вҖ” Г© um mini-estudo empГӯrico novo (codificar votos/relatГіrios do CADE 1994-2005), nГЈo um parГЎgrafo ou uma citaГ§ГЈo. Eu trataria como decisГЈo separada, nГЈo algo para encaixar nesta rodada.

## A disciplina 1 (tensГЈo do Boucas2018) вҖ” concordo, Г© urgente

Isso eu acho genuinamente crГӯtico e concordo sem ressalva: a citaГ§ГЈo comentada em qmd:130 ("CADE made a series of changes... that would not allow the formation of a firm with that market share today") ataca direto a durabilidade da conversГЈo que o artigo reivindica. EstГЎ escondida num bloco `<!-- -->`, invisГӯvel ao leitor mas nГЈo ao parecerista que for aos autos. Isso precisa ser enfrentado independentemente do resto вҖ” inclusive antes, porque Г© um risco existente hoje, nГЈo uma oportunidade nova.

## RecomendaГ§ГЈo de sequenciamento

Eu faria em duas rodadas, nГЈo uma:
1. **Agora**: ContribuiГ§Гөes I, II e IV (economizaГ§ГЈo como tipologia, mecanismo de personnel replacement nomeado, canal ruidoso + sequenciamento) + enfrentar a tensГЈo do Boucas2018. Baixo risco, alto valor, material jГЎ disponГӯvel.
2. **Depois de decidir com o AndrГ©**: a comparaГ§ГЈo alemГЈ (ContribuiГ§ГЈo III) вҖ” porque exige mais uma passada de verificaГ§ГЈo (Ministererlaubnis, hedge da tese causal) e Г© uma decisГЈo editorial maior (quanto peso dar a um caso-sombra de segunda mГЈo).

Quer que eu jГЎ redija o parГЎgrafo de enquadramento da SeГ§ГЈo 2 (o que "destrava o resto", como vocГӘ mesmo disse) com I+II+IV, deixando III de lado por ora?

I need to explain what "economization as typology" means by breaking down the concept clearly вҖ” showing what economization refers to in Ergen & Kohl's framework, how the typology works as a spectrum between effect-based and form-based approaches, and then grounding it in the article's actual material by comparing CADE's pre-1996 versus post-1996 practices.

Now I'm identifying the key contribution: calling it a "third variety" is what makes this move theoretically significant, and I should anchor this with specific textual references so the explanation stays concrete rather than abstract.

</details>

## O conceito: "economizaГ§ГЈo"

Em Ergen & Kohl, **economizaГ§ГЈo** Г© o nome que eles dГЈo a um movimento histГіrico especГӯfico: a reorientaГ§ГЈo da polГӯtica antitruste, saindo de finalidades **societais amplas** (proteger pequenos negГіcios, dispersar poder polГӯtico-econГҙmico, promover democracia) para finalidades **puramente econГҙmicas** (eficiГӘncia, bem-estar do consumidor). Гү o momento em que o antitruste deixa de perguntar "essa concentraГ§ГЈo de poder Г© uma ameaГ§a Г  sociedade?" e passa a perguntar sГі "essa concentraГ§ГЈo de poder Г© ineficiente?".

O ponto central deles Г© que **os dois paГӯses passaram pela mesma virada** (economizaГ§ГЈo) **mas ela assumiu duas formas opostas** вҖ” e Г© aГӯ que nasce a tipologia:

| | **Effect-based** (EUA, Chicago) | **Form-based** (Alemanha, ordoliberal) |
|---|---|---|
| O que julga | Os *efeitos* econГҙmicos reais de uma conduta (ela prejudica o consumidor? gera ganho de eficiГӘncia?) | A *forma* da estrutura de mercado (existe concentraГ§ГЈo excessiva, independente do efeito imediato?) |
| Regra tГӯpica | "Rule of reason" вҖ” cada caso pesa custo-benefГӯcio; concentraГ§ГЈo pode ser aprovada se hГЎ defesa de eficiГӘncia | Regra estrutural, quase per se вҖ” limiar de concentraГ§ГЈo, "liberdade de concorrer" como fim em si, nГЈo meio para outra coisa |
| Quem venceu a disputa interna | Economistas de Chicago (Bork, Posner) venceram os juristas estruturalistas (Harvard School) | Juristas ordoliberais (Hoppmann, MestmГӨcker) venceram os economistas reformistas (Kantzenbach) |

**Chamar isso de "tipologia" quer dizer**: nГЈo Г© um espectro frouxo/rГӯgido (mais ou menos antitruste), Г© uma **variГЎvel qualitativa** вҖ” *o que* conta como o problema a ser regulado. Duas jurisdiГ§Гөes podem estar igualmente "economizadas" (ambas abandonaram preocupaГ§Гөes societais amplas) e ainda assim divergir completamente na prГЎtica, porque uma julga por efeito e outra por forma.

## Por que isso Г© relevante para o artigo de vocГӘs

Olhando com essa lente para o que o `.qmd` jГЎ narra:

- **CADE prГ©-1996 (caso Gerdau, veto inicial)**: a Lei 8.884/94 tinha um limiar estrutural вҖ” mais de 20% de um mercado autorizava veto (footnote `gerdau-marketshare`). O remГ©dio imposto foi a **desconstituiГ§ГЈo** (desfazer a operaГ§ГЈo). Isso Г© raciocГӯnio **form-based**: importa a estrutura de mercado, nГЈo (sГі) o efeito.
- **CADE pГіs-1996 (recomposiГ§ГЈo por Cardoso) e caso Ambev**: a defesa de eficiГӘncia passa a valer, o mercado relevante Г© redefinido como global (dilui a concentraГ§ГЈo aparente), o remГ©dio vira **comportamental** (compromissos de desempenho, nГЈo desfazer a fusГЈo). Isso Г© raciocГӯnio **effect-based**: importa se hГЎ ganho de eficiГӘncia que compense a concentraГ§ГЈo.

Ou seja: o que o artigo jГЎ descreve em `qmd:102` ("emphasizing efficiency and conduct remedies over structural constraints") **Г© literalmente a transiГ§ГЈo form-based вҶ’ effect-based** que Ergen & Kohl teorizam вҖ” sГі que hoje isso aparece no texto sem esse nome, como se fosse um detalhe local do caso brasileiro.

## Onde entra o "terceira variedade"

Se vocГӘ sГі citasse Ergen & Kohl para dizer "olha, essa transiГ§ГЈo tambГ©m aconteceu no Brasil", isso seria pouco вҖ” sГі mais um caso confirmando a tipologia deles. A jogada mais forte Г© notar que **o motivo polГӯtico por trГЎs da mesma ferramenta doutrinГЎria foi invertido**:

- Nos EUA, o effect-based foi construГӯdo por quem queria **tirar o Estado do caminho** вҖ” Posner via a desconcentraГ§ГЈo como uma forma incГҙmoda de "controle pГәblico" demais intervencionista.
- No Brasil, o mesmГӯssimo instrumental (defesa de eficiГӘncia, mercado global, remГ©dio comportamental) foi mobilizado **pelo prГіprio Estado** para construir campeГөes nacionais вҖ” o oposto do impulso que o gerou nos EUA.

Isso transforma a contribuiГ§ГЈo do artigo de "mais um caso de antitruste-como-polГӯtica-industrial" (que Christophers 2016 jГЎ fez) para algo mais preciso e mais interessante: **o mesmo ferramental effect-based pode servir a economias polГӯticas opostas** вҖ” desregulaГ§ГЈo liberal (EUA) ou developmentalismo via captura do regulador (Brasil) вҖ” dependendo de quem o controla e para quГӘ. Гү essa inversГЈo que dГЎ ao artigo um lugar prГіprio dentro do programa de pesquisa de Ergen & Kohl, em vez de sГі emprestar vocabulГЎrio deles.

Faz sentido? Se quiser, jГЎ escrevo o parГЎgrafo que introduz isso na SeГ§ГЈo 2, ancorado nesses mesmos dois pontos do texto (o limiar de 20%/desconstituiГ§ГЈo da Gerdau vs. a defesa de eficiГӘncia/remГ©dio comportamental da Ambev).
